This is the **gyroscopic moment** (or "Euler torque") that arises whenever a rotating body has *non-aligned* principal axes and *non-zero* products of inertia. It couples the rotational equations — for example, yaw motion can induce roll moment in a vehicle with offset mass.

For vehicles with symmetric mass distribution about the $xz$-plane and $yz$-plane, products of inertia vanish, and this term simplifies considerably.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general rotational equations of motion of a rigid body about its center of mass, starting from Newton's Second Law. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A road vehicle is modelled as a single rigid lumped mass with parameters: $m = 1500$ kg, $I_{zz} = 2500$ kg·m², wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m, $a = 1.2$ m (CG to front axle), $b = 1.4$ m (CG to rear axle). During a steady-state cornering test, the lateral tire forces are $F_{yf} = 3500$ N and $F_{yr} = 2800$ N. Calculate the vehicle's lateral acceleration $a_y$ and yaw acceleration $\ddot{\psi}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Rigid body with constant mass.
2. The body has a body-fixed principal-axis frame $Gxyz$.
3. The center of mass $G$ is the reference point.
4. External moments are taken about $G$.

**Derivation:**

Newton's Second Law for rotation about $G$ states:

$$\sum \vec{M}_G = \frac{^I d \vec{H}_G}{dt}$$

The angular momentum in the body frame is $\vec{H}_G = I_G \vec{\omega}$. Using the transport theorem to convert the time derivative from the inertial frame to the body frame:

$$\frac{^I d \vec{H}_G}{dt} = I_G \dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega})$$

Therefore:

$$\sum \vec{M}_G = I_G \dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega})$$

For a vehicle with principal axes, expanding into components:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_x &= I_{xx} \dot{\omega}_x + (I_{zz} - I_{yy}) \omega_y \omega_z \\
M_y &= I_{yy} \dot{\omega}_y + (I_{xx} - I_{zz}) \omega_z \omega_x \\
M_z &= I_{zz} \dot{\omega}_z + (I_{yy} - I_{xx}) \omega_x \omega_y
\end{aligned}$$

These are the **Euler's equations of motion** for a rigid body.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Lateral Acceleration:**

By Newton's Second Law (lateral direction):
$$m a_y = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$
$$a_y = \frac{3500 + 2800}{1500} = \frac{6300}{1500} = 4.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Yaw Moment Equation:**

For a bicycle model, the moment about the CG:
$$I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr}$$
$$\ddot{\psi} = \frac{(1.2)(3500) - (1.4)(2800)}{2500}$$
$$\ddot{\psi} = \frac{4200 - 3920}{2500} = \frac{280}{2500} = 0.112 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- Lateral acceleration $a_y = 4.2$ m/s² (corresponds to $\approx 0.43\,g$)
- Yaw acceleration $\ddot{\psi} = 0.112$ rad/s²

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the 3-2-1 (yaw-pitch-roll) Euler angle sequence used in vehicle dynamics. Show that for small angles, the body angular velocity components reduce to $\omega_x \approx \dot{\phi}$, $\omega_y \approx \dot{\theta}$, $\omega_z \approx \dot{\psi}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quarter-car suspension model has the following parameters: $m_s = 250$ kg, $m_u = 40$ kg, $k_s = 20000$ N/m, $c_s = 1000$ N·s/m, $k_t = 200000$ N/m. The road input is a step bump of height $h = 0.05$ m. Write the complete equations of motion in matrix form $\vec{M}\ddot{\vec{z}} + \vec{C}\dot{\vec{z}} + \vec{K}\vec{z} = \vec{F}$ and identify the natural frequencies. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

The **3-2-1 Euler sequence** rotates the body from the inertial frame in three successive steps:
1. **Yaw $\psi$** about the inertial $Z$-axis
2. **Pitch $\theta$** about the new $Y'$-axis
3. **Roll $\phi$** about the final $X''$-axis

The body angular velocity in the body frame is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_x &= \dot{\phi} - \dot{\psi}\sin\theta \\
\omega_y &= \dot{\theta}\cos\phi + \dot{\psi}\sin\phi\cos\theta \\
\omega_z &= -\dot{\theta}\sin\phi + \dot{\psi}\cos\phi\cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Small-Angle Approximation:**

For vehicles, $\phi, \theta, \psi \ll 1$ rad, so $\cos\phi \approx 1$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, $\sin\phi \approx \phi \approx 0$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta \approx 0$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_x &\approx \dot{\phi} - 0 \approx \dot{\phi} \\
\omega_y &\approx \dot{\theta}(1) + \dot{\psi}(0)(1) \approx \dot{\theta} \\
\omega_z &\approx -\dot{\theta}(0) + \dot{\psi}(1)(1) \approx \dot{\psi}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, for small angles, the body angular velocity components equal the Euler angle rates. This decouples the rotational equations, enabling the **Linear Handling Bicycle Model**.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Equations of Motion (from Section 3.4):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_s \dot{z}_s - c_s \dot{z}_u + k_s z_s - k_s z_u &= 0 \\
m_u \ddot{z}_u - c_s \dot{z}_s + c_s \dot{z}_u + (k_s + k_t) z_u - k_s z_s &= k_t z_r
\end{aligned}$$

**Matrix Form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_s & 0 \\ 0 & m_u \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{z}_s \\ \ddot{z}_u \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_s & -c_s \\ -c_s & c_s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{z}_s \\ \dot{z}_u \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_s & -k_s \\ -k_s & k_s + k_t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_s \\ z_u \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ k_t z_r \end{bmatrix}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 250 & 0 \\ 0 & 40 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{z}_s \\ \ddot{z}_u \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 1000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{z}_s \\ \dot{z}_u \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 20000 & -20000 \\ -20000 & 220000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_s \\ z_u \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 10000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Natural Frequencies (undamped):**

Solve $\det(\vec{K} - \omega^2 \vec{M}) = 0$:

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} 20000 - 250\omega^2 & -20000 \\ -20000 & 220000 - 40\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$(20000 - 250\omega^2)(220000 - 40\omega^2) - 400000000 = 0$$

$$10000\omega^4 - 71000000\omega^2 + 4000000000 = 0$$

Solving the quadratic in $\omega^2$:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{71000000 \pm \sqrt{71000000^2 - 16 \times 10^{10}}}{20000}$$

$$\omega_1^2 \approx 56.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_1 \approx 7.52 \text{ rad/s} \quad (f_1 \approx 1.20 \text{ Hz — body bounce mode})$$

$$\omega_2^2 \approx 3534 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_2 \approx 59.45 \text{ rad/s} \quad (f_2 \approx 9.46 \text{ Hz — wheel hop mode})$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gyroscopic term** $\vec{\omega} \times (I\vec{\omega})$ in the rotational equation — this is the single most-missed item. (–3 marks)
> 2. **Sign errors in the Euler angle transformation** — yaw rate contributes to both $\omega_x$ and $\omega_z$, not just one. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing the sign convention** for moments — KTU follows the SAE standard where clockwise (viewed from the positive axis) is positive.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final numerical answer — KTU examiners strictly enforce SI units.
> 5. **Writing $\omega = \dot{\phi}$ in the general (non-small-angle) case** — this only holds under small-angle linearization. (–2 marks)
> 6. **In the Quarter-Car derivation, missing the tire stiffness term $k_t z_r$** in the forcing vector.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lumped Mass approach** replaces distributed mass by point masses, reducing infinite DOFs to a finite, tractable set.
- A full road vehicle requires **6 DOFs**: 3 translations ($x, y, z$) and 3 rotations ($\phi, \theta, \psi$).
- **Euler Angles** describe orientation via three successive rotations; the **3-2-1 (yaw-pitch-roll) sequence** is standard in vehicle dynamics.
- The body angular velocity in Euler rates: $\omega_x = \dot{\phi} - \dot{\psi}\sin\theta$, etc.
- **Newton's Second Law, translational form:** $\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}_G$
- **Newton-Euler rotational form:** $\sum \vec{M}_G = I_G \vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega})$
- For **small angles**, the gyroscopic product terms vanish and the equations **decouple** — this is the foundation of the linear bicycle model.
- The **Bicycle Model** has 2 DOFs ($y, \psi$) and 1 lumped mass — used for understeer gradient analysis.
- The **Quarter-Car Model** has 1 DOF (heave) with 2 lumped masses — used for suspension tuning and ride analysis.
- The **moment of inertia tensor** for a vehicle with symmetric mass distribution is diagonal: $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$.
- Always apply the **transport theorem** when differentiating vectors between body and inertial frames.
- For KTU exams: state all **assumptions** at the start of any derivation problem.
- Final answers must carry **SI units** and be expressed to **3 significant figures**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling and Simulation of Dynamic Behaviour of Vehicle – Motion Analysis, Force Analysis, and Energy Analysis Using Software (Simple Models)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Modelling and Simulation of Dynamic Behaviour of Vehicle – Motion Analysis, Force Analysis, and Energy Analysis Using Software (Simple Models) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vehicle Dynamic Modelling** is the mathematical representation of a ground vehicle's response to forces, moments, and control inputs, expressed as a set of ordinary or partial differential equations governing its **translational** (longitudinal, lateral, vertical) and **rotational** (roll, pitch, yaw) degrees of freedom. **Simulation** refers to the numerical integration of these governing equations using software tools (e.g., **MATLAB/Simulink**, **MSC ADAMS**, **CarSim**, **AVL Cruise**) to predict vehicle behaviour under prescribed inputs (throttle, brake, steering, road profile) without the cost of physical prototyping. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the focus is on **simple (lumped-parameter) models**, where distributed mechanical systems are idealised as discrete masses, springs, and dampers connected at idealised nodes. > [!NOTE] > **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define a system's configuration. A simple longitudinal model has **1 DOF**, a quarter-car has **2 DOF**, a half-car has **4 DOF**, and a full-car has **7 DOF** (heave, pitch, roll, plus 4 wheel rotations). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Marble on a Slope** > > Imagine pushing a marble down an inclined, rough surface in your backyard. Your thumb (throttle) gives the marble a push (tractive force). Air pushes against it (drag), the rough surface resists (rolling resistance), gravity pulls it downhill (grade force), and if the slope is steep, it accelerates (inertia). You do not need a 3-D CAD model of the marble to know its motion — **a single mass with simple force equations is enough**. > > A simple vehicle model is the engineering equivalent of this marble: instead of modelling every bolt, panel, and tyre thread, we lump the **entire car as a single rigid mass** and account for external effects (drag, friction, slope) with simple algebraic force terms. This makes calculations fast and intuitive, suitable for preliminary design, control strategy validation, and energy budgeting. ## 1.3 Why Use Simple Models? | Aspect | Simple Model | Full Multi-Body Model | |---|---|---| | **Computation time** | Seconds | Hours to days | | **Parameters needed** | 5–10 | Hundreds | | **Engineering insight** | High (transparent physics) | Lower (hidden in numerics) | | **Typical use** | Concept design, control law synthesis, energy studies | Final validation, NVH, crash | ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Vehicle Dynamics Simulation 1. **Motion Analysis** — Where is the vehicle and how fast is it moving? (Kinematics + Kinetics) 2. **Force Analysis** — What forces and moments act on the vehicle body, tyres, and suspension? 3. **Energy Analysis** — How much power/fuel/electrical energy is consumed or recovered? These three analyses are tightly coupled: forces produce motion, and motion consumes (or regenerates) energy. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Assumption Set for Simple Models To make the dynamics tractable, simple models invoke the following assumptions (commonly tested in KTU exams): - **Rigid body**: Vehicle body is a non-deformable lumped mass $m$. - **Symmetry**: Mass is uniformly distributed; centre of gravity (CG) lies on the longitudinal centreline. - **Neglect wheel rotational inertia**: Each wheel is a point mass in contact with the road. - **Constant coefficient friction**: Tyre-road adhesion is captured by a single friction coefficient $\mu$. - **Quasi-steady aerodynamics**: Drag depends instantaneously on the square of velocity. - **No cross-coupling** in the simplest 1-DOF model: longitudinal, lateral, and vertical motions are decoupled. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tip from KTU Board Valuators**: When a problem says "neglect air resistance" or "neglect rolling resistance," do **not** include those terms in the equation — examiners check for this and award full marks only when the simplification is acknowledged explicitly. ## 2.2 Motion Analysis — Kinematic and Kinetic Framework For a vehicle moving along a straight path with velocity $v$ and acceleration $a$: $$v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t a(\tau) \, d\tau$$ $$s(t) = s_0 + \int_0^t v(\tau) \, d\tau$$ These are the **kinematic** relationships. To find $a(t)$ we need **kinetics** — Newton's second law in the longitudinal direction: $$\sum F_x = m \, a$$ The forces opposing motion are listed below. ## 2.3 Force Analysis — The Longitudinal Force Balance The general equation of motion in the longitudinal direction is: $$F_{\text{tract}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{grade}} - F_{\text{acc}} = 0$$ Where each term is expanded below. ### 2.3.1 Tractive Force (from powertrain) $$F_{\text{tract}} = \frac{T_{\text{eng}} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$$ Where: - $T_{\text{eng}}$ = engine/motor torque (Nm) - $i_g$ = gearbox ratio (dimensionless) - $i_0$ = final drive ratio (dimensionless) - $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency ($\eta_t \leq 1$) - $r_w$ = effective wheel radius (m) ### 2.3.2 Aerodynamic Drag $$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_D \, A_f \, v^2$$ Where: - $\rho$ = air density, standard value $\mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ at sea level, 15 °C - $C_D$ = drag coefficient (cars: 0.25–0.40) - $A_f$ = frontal area (m²) - $v$ = vehicle velocity (m/s) ### 2.3.3 Rolling Resistance $$F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \, \cos\theta$$ Where: - $\mu_{rr}$ = rolling resistance coefficient (cars: 0.008–0.015; trucks: 0.006–0.010) - $m$ = vehicle mass (kg) - $g$ = gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m/s²** - $\theta$ = road gradient angle ### 2.3.4 Grade Force (Climbing Force) $$F_{\text{grade}} = m \, g \, \sin\theta$$ For small angles ($\theta < 10°$), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx$ grade percentage / 100. ### 2.3.5 Acceleration (Inertial) Force $$F_{\text{acc}} = m \, a + m_{\text{eq}} \, a$$ Where $m_{\text{eq}} = (J_{\text{eng}} i_g^2 i_0^2 + n \, J_w)/r_w^2$ is the equivalent translational mass of rotating components. In many simple models this is folded into a **rotational inertia factor** $\delta$: $$F_{\text{acc, total}} = \delta \, m \, a, \quad \delta = 1 + \frac{m_{\text{eq}}}{m}$$ Typically $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.15$ for passenger cars. ## 2.4 Energy Analysis — Power and Work Instantaneous **power demand at the wheels**: $$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{tract}} \cdot v$$ Instantaneous **power at engine/motor shaft**: $$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_t}$$ **Energy consumed over a trip** of duration $T$: $$E = \int_0^T P_{\text{shaft}}(t) \, dt \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ **Kinetic energy** of the vehicle (used in regenerative braking calculations): $$E_{\text{KE}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, m \, v^2$$ **Potential energy change** when climbing height $h$: $$\Delta E_{\text{PE}} = m \, g \, h$$ **Energy balance** for the entire trip (road-load energy equation): $$E_{\text{input}} = \Delta E_{\text{KE}} + \Delta E_{\text{PE}} + W_{\text{aero}} + W_{\text{roll}} + W_{\text{loss}}$$ > [!TIP] > For KTU derivations, remember the **chain rule identity** that links force, power, and energy: $P = F \cdot v$ and $E = \int F \, ds = \int P \, dt$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the equations examiners expect you to write from memory.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton's 2nd Law | $\sum F = m a$ | N, kg, m/s² | Foundation of all dynamics | | 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f v^2$ | N | Quadratic in $v$ | | 3 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ | N | Linear in $m$ | | 4 | Grade Force | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | N | Component of weight along slope | | 5 | Inertial Force | $F_i = \delta m a$ | N | $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.15$ | | 6 | Tractive Force | $F_t = T_{eng} i_g i_0 \eta_t / r_w$ | N | From powertrain | | 7 | Wheel Power | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | W (or kW) | Mechanical at tyre contact | | 8 | Shaft Power | $P_s = P_w / \eta_t$ | W | Before transmission loss | | 9 | Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Recoverable in regen | | 10 | Potential Energy | $E_p = m g h$ | J | Stored as elevation | | 11 | Trip Energy | $E = \int_0^T P_s \, dt$ | J | Integrate over drive cycle | | 12 | Steady-state speed | Set $a = 0$, solve $F_t = F_d + F_{rr} + F_g$ | m/s | No acceleration | | 13 | Max gradient | $\tan\theta_{max} = (F_{t,max} - F_d - F_{rr})/(mg)$ | rad or deg | Hill-climb ability | | 14 | Time to accelerate | $t = \delta m \int_{v_1}^{v_2} dv / (F_t - F_{res})$ | s | Variable force integration | | 15 | Quarter-car natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_s/m_s}$ | rad/s | Vertical bounce | --- ### 2.6 The Quarter-Car Model (2-DOF Vertical Dynamics) The most commonly tested simple model in KTU is the **quarter-car** for ride comfort: - Sprung mass $m_s$ (car body, quarter weight) - Unsprung mass $m_u$ (wheel + tyre + brake assembly) - Suspension spring $k_s$ and damper $c_s$ - Tyre spring $k_t$ (typically no damper) Equations of motion: $$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$ $$m_u \ddot{z}_u = k_s(z_s - z_u) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_t(z_u - z_r)$$ Where $z_r$ is the road input. This is a **forced 2-DOF system** solved numerically in MATLAB/Simulink. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **EV/HEV design**: Energy analysis predicts kWh consumption per km → sizing of battery pack. - **Tyre industry**: Force analysis validates $\mu$–slip curves before expensive on-track tests. - **Autonomous vehicles**: Motion models (bicycle model) feed Model Predictive Control (MPC). - **Formula Student / Baja SAE**: Simple longitudinal models predict 0–100 km/h times and lap energy budgets. - **Road infrastructure**: Grade-force calculations determine maximum truck speeds on flyovers and tunnels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Equation of Motion from First Principles ### Step 1 — Free-Body Setup Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving up a slope of angle $\theta$ with velocity $v$ and acceleration $a = \dot{v}$. The forces in the longitudinal direction (taken positive forward, up the slope) are: - Tractive force $F_t$ at the wheel-road contact patch. - Aerodynamic drag $F_d$ opposing motion. - Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ opposing motion. - Gravity component along slope $W \sin\theta$ opposing motion (when climbing). - Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to road surface. ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law $$F_t - F_d - F_{rr} - W \sin\theta = m a$$ Substitute $W = m g$: $$F_t - F_d - F_{rr} - m g \sin\theta = m a$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Force Expressions $$F_t - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta - m g \sin\theta = m a$$ ### Step 4 — Include Rotational Inertia Factor $\delta$ Recognising that engine and wheel rotational inertias also accelerate: $$\delta m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta - m g \sin\theta$$ This is the **canonical longitudinal vehicle dynamics equation** appearing in almost every KTU problem. ### Step 5 — Re-arrange for Solvable Form $$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{1}{\delta m}\left[F_t - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2 - m g (\mu_{rr}\cos\theta + \sin\theta)\right]$$ This is a first-order ODE that integrates numerically with **Euler**, **RK4**, or **ode45** in MATLAB. ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Balance Over a Drive Cycle ### Step 1 — Start from the Power Equation The instantaneous power demand at the wheels is: $$P_{\text{wheel}}(t) = F_{\text{resist}}(t) \cdot v(t) + \delta m \, a(t) \cdot v(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Split into Useful and Loss Components $$P_{\text{wheel}} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^3}_{\text{aero loss}} + \underbrace{\mu_{rr} m g v \cos\theta}_{\text{rolling loss}} + \underbrace{m g v \sin\theta}_{\text{PE rate}} + \underbrace{\delta m a v}_{\text{KE rate}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The cubic dependence $v^3$ on drag power is why high-speed cruising is the **dominant** energy hog in road cars — a 30 % increase in speed roughly **doubles** aerodynamic power loss. ### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Trip Duration $T$ $$E_{\text{total}} = \int_0^T P_{\text{wheel}}(t) \, dt = \int_0^T \left[\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^3 + \mu_{rr} m g v \cos\theta + m g v \sin\theta + \delta m a v\right] dt$$ ### Step 4 — Recognise Conservative Work Terms The $\delta m a v$ term integrates to kinetic energy change (work-energy theorem): $$\int_0^T \delta m a v \, dt = \tfrac{1}{2} \delta m (v_f^2 - v_i^2)$$ Similarly the grade term gives potential energy change: $$\int_0^T m g v \sin\theta \, dt = \int m g \, dh = m g (h_f - h_i)$$ So the **trip energy** simplifies to: $$\boxed{E_{\text{total}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta m (v_f^2 - v_i^2) + m g (h_f - h_i) + W_{\text{aero}} + W_{\text{roll}}}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical) > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem (KTU style)**: A car of mass $m = 1500$ kg has $C_D = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.92$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m. The car accelerates from $v_1 = 20$ m/s to $v_2 = 30$ m/s on a level road ($\theta = 0$) in $10$ seconds. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Take $\delta = 1.08$. Find: (i) the average acceleration, (ii) the average tractive force required, (iii) the energy consumed at the wheels. ### Part (i) — Average Acceleration $$a_{\text{avg}} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{t} = \frac{30 - 20}{10} = 1.0 \; \text{m/s}^2$$ ### Part (ii) — Average Forces Average velocity $\bar{v} = (v_1 + v_2)/2 = 25$ m/s. Aerodynamic drag at average speed: $$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2 = 269.5 \; \text{N}$$ Rolling resistance: $$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0) = 176.6 \; \text{N}$$ Inertial force: $$F_i = \delta m a = 1.08 \times 1500 \times 1.0 = 1620 \; \text{N}$$ Total tractive force: $$F_t = F_d + F_{rr} + F_i = 269.5 + 176.6 + 1620 = 2066.1 \; \text{N}$$ ### Part (iii) — Energy Consumed at the Wheels **Method 1 — Direct integration via work done by tractive force:** Distance travelled: $$s = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)t = \tfrac{1}{2}(20+30)(10) = 250 \; \text{m}$$ Energy at wheels: $$E_{\text{wheel}} = F_t \cdot s = 2066.1 \times 250 = 516{,}525 \; \text{J} \approx 516.5 \; \text{kJ}$$ **Method 2 — Energy balance verification:** $$E_{\text{aero}} = F_d \cdot s = 269.5 \times 250 = 67{,}375 \; \text{J}$$ $$E_{\text{roll}} = F_{rr} \cdot s = 176.6 \times 250 = 44{,}150 \; \text{J}$$ Kinetic energy gained: $$\Delta E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \delta m (v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.08 \times 1500 \times (900 - 400) = 405{,}000 \; \text{J}$$ Sum: $$E_{\text{total}} = 67{,}375 + 44{,}150 + 405{,}000 = 516{,}525 \; \text{J}$$ Both methods **agree perfectly** ✓ — this is the energy-balance check examiners love. ### Part (iv) — Energy at Engine Shaft (including transmission loss) $$E_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{E_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_t} = \frac{516{,}525}{0.92} \approx 561{,}440 \; \text{J} \approx 561.4 \; \text{kJ}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Quarter-Car Natural Frequency Starting from the sprung-mass-only (1-DOF) approximation (neglecting unsprung mass and tyre stiffness): $$m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_s \dot{z}_s + k_s z_s = k_s z_r + c_s \dot{z}_r$$ For free vibration ($z_r = 0$), the characteristic equation gives: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{c_s}{2 \sqrt{k_s m_s}}$$ Typical values: $\omega_n \approx 1$–$2$ Hz (passenger car), $\zeta \approx 0.2$–$0.4$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Simple Longitudinal Vehicle Model ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Vehicle Body
Mass m"] -->|Weight W = mg| nodeB["Road Surface
Gradient theta"] nodeA -->|Ftract forward| nodeC["Wheel Contact Patch"] nodeC -->|Froll backward| nodeA nodeC -->|Faero backward
proportional to v squared| nodeA nodeB -->|Fgrade backward
mg sin theta| nodeA nodeA -->|Finertial = m a| nodeD["Resultant acceleration a"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Simulation Architecture (MATLAB/Simulink) ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Driver Input
Throttle Pedal percent"] --> block1 inputB["Brake Pedal MPa"] --> block1 inputC["Road Gradient theta"] --> block1 block1["Powertrain Model
Engine and Motor Torque Maps"] --> block2 block2["Drivetrain
Gearbox Ratio ig, Final Drive i0, Efficiency eta"] --> block3 block3["Tractive Force
Ftract = T ig i0 eta over rw"] --> block4 block4["Vehicle Dynamics 1DOF
m dv/dt = Ftract - Faero - Froll - Fgrade"] --> block5 block5["Outputs
Velocity v, Position s, Acceleration a"] --> block6 block5 --> block7["Energy Calculator
E = integral P dt"] block5 --> block8["Scope and Dashboard
Plots v-t, F-t, P-t"] ``` ## 4.3 Quarter-Car Model Schematic ```mermaid graph TB bodyM["Sprung Mass ms
Quarter Car Body"] -->|Suspension Spring ks| wheelM bodyM -->|Suspension Damper cs| wheelM wheelM["Unsprung Mass mu
Wheel and Tyre"] -->|Tyre Stiffness kt| roadR["Road Input zr over time"] bodyM -->|Displacement zs| bodyM wheelM -->|Displacement zu| wheelM ``` ## 4.4 Simulation Workflow Flowchart (Complete Process) ```mermaid graph TD start(["Define Problem
Performance requirement"]) --> stepA stepA["Choose Model Complexity
1 DOF, 2 DOF, 7 DOF"] --> stepB stepB["Identify Parameters
m, Cd, Af, mu rr, theta, eta t"] --> stepC stepC["Write Governing ODEs
Newton and Energy Equations"] --> stepD stepD["Discretise in Software
MATLAB ode45 or Simulink Block Diagram"] --> stepE stepE["Validate against Data
Compare with manufacturer specs"] --> stepF stepF{"Acceptable
Error less than 5 percent?"} stepF -->|Yes| stepG["Run Drive Cycle
NEDC, WLTP, FTP75"] stepF -->|No| stepH["Refine Parameters or Model"] stepH --> stepC stepG --> end1(["Report Results
v-t, Energy, Range"]) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define the term "Degree of Freedom" as applied to vehicle modelling. List the degrees of freedom of a 4-wheeled passenger car modelled as a rigid body on a flat road.** **Model Answer** (3 marks): A **degree of freedom (DOF)** is an independent coordinate required to completely describe the configuration of a mechanical system. For a rigid 4-wheeled vehicle on a flat road, the DOFs are: 1. Longitudinal translation (surge) 2. Lateral translation (sway) 3. Vertical translation (heave) 4. Rotation about longitudinal axis (roll) 5. Rotation about lateral axis (pitch) 6. Rotation about vertical axis (yaw) **Total: 6 DOFs** (excluding wheel rotations and suspension articulation). > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake**: Writing "7 DOF" without realising the wheel rotations are usually lumped as a single equivalent mass inertia $\delta$ in simple models. Examiners deduct marks if the explanation conflates sprung and unsprung masses. ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > **State the equation for aerodynamic drag force on a vehicle and explain why power required to overcome drag increases with the cube of velocity.** **Model Answer** (3 marks): $$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2$$ Power required = force × velocity: $$P_d = F_d \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^3$$ Hence power scales as $v^3$. Doubling speed multiplies power loss to drag by 8. This is why EVs and ICE vehicles both suffer severe efficiency penalties above 100 km/h. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Longitudinal Dynamics & Energy Analysis > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** A passenger car has the following data: - Mass $m = 1400$ kg, frontal area $A_f = 2.1$ m², drag coefficient $C_D = 0.30$ - Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.013$ - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.90$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.31$ m - Final drive ratio $i_0 = 3.7$, gearbox ratio $i_g = 1.4$ (in top gear) - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Equivalent mass factor $\delta = 1.06$ The car climbs a uniform gradient of $\theta = 5°$ at a constant speed of $v = 90$ km/h. ### Part (a) — Force Analysis [7 Marks] **Calculate all longitudinal forces and the engine torque required.** **Model Solution:** Convert velocity: $v = 90$ km/h $= 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s. Aerodynamic drag: $$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 \times (25)^2 = 241.2 \; \text{N}$$ Rolling resistance: $$F_{rr} = 0.013 \times 1400 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5°) = 177.7 \; \text{N}$$ Grade force: $$F_g = 1400 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5°) = 1196.8 \; \text{N}$$ Since speed is constant, $a = 0$, so total tractive force: $$F_t = F_d + F_{rr} + F_g = 241.2 + 177.7 + 1196.8 = 1615.7 \; \text{N}$$ Engine torque: $$T_{eng} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{1615.7 \times 0.31}{1.4 \times 3.7 \times 0.90}$$ $$T_{eng} = \frac{500.87}{4.662} \approx 107.4 \; \text{Nm}$$ ### Part (b) — Energy Analysis [7 Marks] **If the car maintains this condition for 1 hour, calculate (i) wheel power, (ii) shaft power, (iii) total energy consumed.** **Model Solution:** (i) Wheel power: $$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_t \cdot v = 1615.7 \times 25 = 40{,}392.5 \; \text{W} \approx 40.4 \; \text{kW}$$ (ii) Shaft power (engine output): $$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_t} = \frac{40{,}392.5}{0.90} \approx 44{,}880 \; \text{W} \approx 44.9 \; \text{kW}$$ (iii) Total energy over $T = 3600$ s: $$E_{\text{shaft}} = P_{\text{shaft}} \times T = 44{,}880 \times 3600 = 161.6 \times 10^6 \; \text{J}$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{shaft}} \approx 161.6 \; \text{MJ} \; \text{or equivalently} \; 44.9 \; \text{kWh}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1**: Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s before squaring for drag. **Always** convert $v$ to m/s first when using the drag formula. > > **Pitfall #2**: Many candidates divide $F_t \cdot r_w$ by the ratios — but the correct operation is to multiply by $r_w$ (torque at wheel) and then divide by total gear ratio. Drawing a free-body diagram of the wheel is worth 1 mark. > > **Pitfall #3**: Forgetting $\eta_t$ in the denominator (engine torque is **larger** than wheel torque divided by ratios because of losses). Writing $T_{eng} = F_t r_w / (i_g i_0)$ without $\eta_t$ loses 1 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Quarter-Car Vertical Dynamics (Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** A quarter-car model has the following parameters: - Sprung mass $m_s = 250$ kg - Suspension stiffness $k_s = 20{,}000$ N/m - Suspension damping coefficient $c_s = 1500$ Ns/m - Unsprung mass $m_u = 40$ kg - Tyre stiffness $k_t = 180{,}000$ N/m - The road input is a step bump of height $h = 0.05$ m held stationary. ### Part (a) — Equation of Motion and Natural Frequency [7 Marks] **Write the equations of motion for the 2-DOF quarter-car model and derive the natural frequency of the sprung mass (assuming rigid tyre and negligible $m_u$).** **Model Solution:** The 2-DOF equations are: $$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$ $$m_u \ddot{z}_u = k_s(z_s - z_u) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_t(z_u - z_r)$$ Assuming $m_u \to 0$ and rigid tyre ($k_t \to \infty$, so $z_u = z_r$), the second equation gives: $$0 = k_s(z_s - z_r) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_r) - k_t(z_u - z_r)$$ The sprung-mass equation reduces to a single 1-DOF system with relative coordinate $q = z_s - z_r$: $$m_s \ddot{q} + c_s \dot{q} + k_s q = 0$$ Natural frequency: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944 \; \text{rad/s}$$ In Hz: $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \approx \frac{8.944}{6.283} \approx 1.42 \; \text{Hz}$$ This is squarely in the passenger-car ride-comfort range (1–2 Hz) ✓ ### Part (b) — Step Response Simulation Analysis [7 Marks] **Plot the qualitative response of $z_s$ vs time for a step input and identify (i) peak overshoot, (ii) settling time. Use damping ratio $\zeta = 0.25$ for this system.** **Model Solution:** Damped natural frequency: $$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 8.944 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 8.944 \times 0.968 \approx 8.66 \; \text{rad/s}$$ Peak overshoot (fraction): $$M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = e^{-0.25 \times \pi / 0.968} = e^{-0.811} \approx 0.444$$ Maximum displacement overshoot: $$z_{s,\max} = h \cdot (1 + M_p) = 0.05 \times 1.444 \approx 0.0722 \; \text{m}$$ Time to first peak: $$t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{8.66} \approx 0.363 \; \text{s}$$ Settling time (2% criterion): $$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.25 \times 8.944} \approx 1.79 \; \text{s}$$ **Qualitative sketch description** (for MATLAB/Simulink generation): - The body displacement $z_s$ rises from 0, overshoots to ~7.2 cm at t ≈ 0.36 s, oscillates with decaying amplitude, and settles to the new equilibrium $z_s = h = 5$ cm after ~1.8 s. - The damping ratio $\zeta = 0.25$ gives an underdamped, oscillatory response. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Quarter-Car Model**: > > **Pitfall #1**: Writing "natural frequency of car = $\sqrt{g/R}$" — this is the **pendulum/roll** frequency, not the sprung-mass bounce frequency. They are different DOFs. > > **Pitfall #2**: Mixing up the sprung mass $m_s$ (body quarter, ~250 kg) with the total vehicle mass $m$ (full car, ~1000 kg) when substituting values. > > **Pitfall #3**: Forgetting that in a step-bump simulation the **final** steady-state is $z_s = z_r = h$, not zero. The system settles to the new road height. > > **Pitfall #4**: Writing $k_s$ and $c_s$ in parallel between body and wheel — they are **in parallel with each other and in series with the tyre spring** $k_t$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.** - ✅ **The Master Equation** (longitudinal): $\delta m a = F_t - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta - m g \sin\theta$. - ✅ **Standard Values**: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.15$. - ✅ **Velocity Conversion**: Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6 before applying $v^2$ or $v^3$ formulas. - ✅ **Aerodynamic Power**: $P_d \propto v^3$ — the dominant energy loss at high speed. - ✅ **Roll and Grade**: $F_{rr}$ uses $\cos\theta$, $F_g$ uses $\sin\theta$ — never swap these. - ✅ **Energy is Conservative**: $\int F v \, dt$ at constant $v$ becomes $F \cdot s$. - ✅ **Quarter-Car DOFs**: Body bounce + wheel hop = 2-DOF. Natural frequency ~1–2 Hz for comfort. - ✅ **Damping Ratio Bands**: $\zeta < 0.2$ (oscillatory), $\zeta \approx 0.3$–$0.4$ (comfort optimum), $\zeta > 0.7$ (overdamped, harsh). - ✅ **Transmission Direction**: Engine → Gearbox → Final Drive → Wheel: gear ratios **multiply**, efficiency **divides** when going upstream. - ✅ **Drive Cycles**: NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75 — used for certification; integrate $P(t)$ over cycle for official energy/fuel figures. - ✅ **Software Tools**: MATLAB/Simulink (most common in KTU labs), ADAMS (multi-body), CarSim (industry-grade). - ✅ **Simulation Order**: Define → Model → Parameterise → Solve → Validate → Report. > [!TIP] > **Last-Mile Tip**: In 14-mark numerical problems, KTU examiners allocate approximately **3 marks** for writing the governing equation correctly with a clear free-body or system diagram, **7 marks** for systematic substitution and arithmetic, and **4 marks** for final answer with units, energy cross-check, and physical interpretation. Never skip the diagram — it is your cheapest marks on the paper.

topics.title: Performance of Road Vehicles: Forces and Moments on Vehicle, Equation of Motion, Tire Forces, Rolling Resistance, Weight Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance of Road Vehicles: Forces and Moments on Vehicle, Equation of Motion, Tire Forces, Rolling Resistance, Weight Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle Dynamics** is the engineering discipline that studies the motion of road vehicles and the forces and moments acting on them. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, *performance* refers specifically to the longitudinal (forward/backward) response of the vehicle under the influence of propulsion, resistive, and gravitational forces, while *dynamics* also encompasses the transient and steady-state responses of the sprung and unsprung masses.

The **fundamental performance problem** of a road vehicle reduces to balancing the **tractive force developed at the tire–road contact patch** against the **resistive forces** (rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, grade resistance) to produce a net accelerating force that determines the vehicle's velocity and acceleration profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** The 2024 scheme explicitly groups these sub-topics because they form the foundational free-body-diagram (FBD) framework upon which all subsequent modules (cornering, ride, suspension) are built. Mastery of the longitudinal equation of motion is therefore *non-negotiable* for the ESE.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War Engine"

Imagine a horse pulling a loaded cart along a road. The horse represents the **engine–drivetrain system** delivering a *tractive effort* through the rim of the wheel (the harness connecting horse to cart). The cart experiences several opposing forces:

- **Rolling Friction** → the rubbing of the wheel bearings and the deformation of the tire carcass against the road (analogous to dragging your palm across a carpet).
- **Aerodynamic Drag** → the wind pushing back against the cart's frontal area (like sticking your hand out of a moving car window).
- **Grade Resistance** → gravity component along the slope when going uphill (gravity pulling the cart backward on a ramp).

The horse must pull harder than the *sum* of all these resistances for the cart to accelerate. If pulling equals resistance, the cart cruises at constant velocity. If pulling is less, the cart decelerates. **This balance is precisely the longitudinal equation of motion of a road vehicle.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Units (SI):** Force in **N (Newton)**, Mass in **kg**, Velocity in **m/s**, Acceleration in **m/s²**, Power in **kW**, Torque in **N·m**, CG height in **m**, Wheelbase in **m**.

### 1.3 Scope of Forces on a Road Vehicle

A road vehicle in motion is acted upon by the following external forces and moments:

| Category | Force / Moment | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Propulsion | **Tractive Force ($F_t$)** | Engine torque transmitted to tire–road contact |
| Resistive | **Rolling Resistance ($F_r$)** | Tire deformation, road hysteresis |
| Resistive | **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$)** | Airflow over vehicle body |
| Resistive | **Grade Resistance ($F_g$)** | Gravity component along slope |
| Inertial | **Inertial Force ($m \cdot a$)** | Newton's Second Law resisting acceleration |
| Vertical | **Normal Reactions ($W_f, W_r$)** | Weight distribution on front/rear axles |

These forces are typically resolved along the vehicle's longitudinal ($x$), lateral ($y$), and vertical ($z$) axes, originating at the **Center of Gravity (CG)** located at height $h$ above the road.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Free Body Diagram of a Road Vehicle (Longitudinal)

Consider a rear-wheel-drive car of mass $m$ moving with velocity $V$ on a road inclined at angle $\theta$. The aerodynamic drag $F_a$ acts at the front, while weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward through the CG at height $h$ above the ground.

**Forces Acting:**
- $F_t$ — Tractive force at the tire–road contact (forward direction).
- $F_r$ — Rolling resistance force (opposes motion, acts at wheel center).
- $F_a$ — Aerodynamic drag (opposes motion, acts through the vehicle's frontal area).
- $F_g = mg \sin\theta$ — Grade resistance (component of gravity along slope).
- $N_f, N_r$ — Normal reactions at front and rear axles.

### 2.2 The Longitudinal Equation of Motion

Applying **Newton's Second Law** along the $x$-axis of the vehicle:

$$F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m \frac{dV}{dt}$$

where $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ is the longitudinal acceleration of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single scalar equation is the **cornerstone of vehicle performance analysis**. It is the model answer expectation for any 7-mark derivations in the ESE on Module 1.

---

### 2.3 Tire Forces

A pneumatic tire is the sole contact between the vehicle and the road. The forces exchanged at the contact patch are classified as:

**A. Longitudinal Tire Forces ($F_x$)**
- **Tractive (Driving) Force:** $F_x = \dfrac{T_t}{r_w}$ where $T_t$ is the net torque at the tire and $r_w$ is the effective rolling radius.
- **Braking Force:** Produced by applying a negative torque through the friction brake; the tire-road friction coefficient $\mu_x$ governs its maximum value.

**B. Lateral Tire Forces ($F_y$)**
- Generated during cornering due to **slip angle** $\alpha$.
- For small angles, $F_y \approx C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$, where $C_\alpha$ is the **cornering stiffness** (N/rad).

**C. Vertical Tire Forces ($F_z$)**
- Equal to the dynamic normal load on the tire; varies with acceleration (weight transfer).

---

### 2.4 Rolling Resistance — Physical Origin and Modeling

Rolling resistance arises from two primary mechanisms:
1. **Hysteresis loss in the tire carcass** as it repeatedly deforms and recovers.
2. **Relative micro-slip** between the tire and road surface.

It is conventionally modeled as a force proportional to the normal load:

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = \mu_r \cdot m g \cos\theta$$

where $\mu_r$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** (dimensionless).

**Typical Values of $\mu_r$:**
| Surface Type | $\mu_r$ (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Concrete / Asphalt | **0.010 – 0.015** |
| Rolled Gravel | 0.020 – 0.025 |
| Sand | 0.100 – 0.300 |
| Soft Soil | 0.400 + |

**Factors Affecting Rolling Resistance:**
- Tire material (natural vs. synthetic rubber) — *hysteresis loss*.
- Tire inflation pressure — **lower pressure increases $F_r$**.
- Vehicle speed — *minor increase at high speeds*.
- Road surface roughness and temperature.
- Wheel diameter — *larger wheels generally have lower $\mu_r$*.

---

### 2.5 Weight Distribution — Static and Dynamic

**Static Weight Distribution** (vehicle at rest on level ground):

$$W_f = \frac{mg \cdot b}{L}, \quad W_r = \frac{mg \cdot a}{L}$$

where $a$ = distance from front axle to CG, $b$ = distance from rear axle to CG, $L = a + b$ = wheelbase.

**Dynamic Weight Transfer** (under longitudinal acceleration $a_x$):

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$$

where $h$ is the CG height.

The dynamic normal loads become:

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = W_{f,\text{stat}} - \Delta W \quad \text{(rear-loaded during acceleration)}$$

$$W_{r,\text{dyn}} = W_{r,\text{stat}} + \Delta W$$

> [!NOTE]
> Under braking (negative $a_x$), the load transfers *forward*, increasing $W_f$ and decreasing $W_r$ — a critical fact exploited in brake-bias design (covered later in the syllabus).

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Longitudinal equation of motion | N |
| 2 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_o \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$ | Tractive force via drivetrain | N |
| 3 | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot m g \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 4 | $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 5 | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | Grade resistance | N |
| 6 | $P_{\text{req}} = F_t \cdot V$ | Power required at wheels | W |
| 7 | $W_f = \dfrac{mgb}{L}, \; W_r = \dfrac{mga}{L}$ | Static weight distribution | N |
| 8 | $\Delta W = \dfrac{m a_x h}{L}$ | Longitudinal weight transfer | N |
| 9 | $\Delta W = \dfrac{m a_y h}{t}$ | Lateral weight transfer | N |
| 10 | $\mu_x = \dfrac{F_x}{F_z}$ | Longitudinal tire friction coefficient | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the ESE valuation key, examiners expect students to **state the assumption (e.g., "on a level road, $F_g = 0$") before writing each equation**. Marks are explicitly lost when assumptions are omitted.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance Engineering:** OEMs (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Tesla) compute 0–100 km/h times, grade-ability, and top speed using these equations.
- **Brake System Design:** Brake force at each axle is sized to match the dynamic normal load (rear axle always has lower brake capacity than front).
- **Drivetrain Matching:** Engine, gearbox ratios, and final drive are selected to deliver adequate $F_t$ to overcome $F_r + F_g + F_a$ at all operating conditions.
- **Tire Selection:** Truck fleets select low-$\mu_r$ tires for fuel economy; rally cars select high-friction tires for tractive effort.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Longitudinal Equation of Motion from Newton's Second Law

**Step 1 — System Definition:**
Let a vehicle of mass $m$ move along a straight road inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Define a vehicle-fixed $x$-axis pointing forward along the direction of motion.

**Step 2 — Force Inventory (in $x$-direction):**
- Tractive force $F_t$ → **positive** (forward).
- Rolling resistance $F_r$ → **negative** (opposes motion).
- Aerodynamic drag $F_a$ → **negative** (opposes motion).
- Grade force component along $x$ → $- mg \sin\theta$ (points backward when climbing).
- Inertial reaction $- m \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ in D'Alembert's form.

**Step 3 — Application of Newton's Second Law:**
The net unbalanced force in the $x$-direction equals mass times acceleration:

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a_x = m \frac{dV}{dt}$$

Substituting the four real forces:

$$F_t - F_r - F_a - mg \sin\theta = m \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrangement into Standard Form:**

$$m \frac{dV}{dt} = F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g$$

This is the **fundamental longitudinal performance equation** of a road vehicle.

**Step 5 — Special Cases for Examination:**
- **Level road, constant speed:** $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = 0, \; \theta = 0 \Rightarrow F_t = F_r + F_a$.
- **Maximum grade (gradeability):** $F_t = F_r + F_a + mg \sin\theta_{\max}$ at low speed where $F_a \to 0$.
- **Maximum speed $V_{\max}$:** $F_t(V_{\max}) = F_r + F_a(V_{\max}) + F_g$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Power Required at the Wheels

Power is the rate of doing work. For a vehicle moving at velocity $V$ against a net resistive force $F_{\text{res}} = F_r + F_a + F_g$:

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power required:**

$$P_{\text{res}} = F_{\text{res}} \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Total tractive power required (including kinetic energy change):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = F_t \cdot V = \left(F_r + F_a + F_g + m\frac{dV}{dt}\right) V$$

**Step 3 — Expanded form (substitute $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = \mu_r mg V \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^3 + mg V \sin\theta + mV\frac{dV}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **$V^3$ dependence** of the aerodynamic term — this is why at very high speeds, almost all engine power goes into overcoming drag. The **$V^1$ rolling term dominates at low speeds**, and the **grade term** dominates during hill climbing.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Static Weight Distribution Using Moments

**Step 1 — Take moments about the rear tire contact point:**
Sum of clockwise moments = Sum of counter-clockwise moments.

$$W_f \cdot L - mg \cdot b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $W_f$:**

$$W_f = \frac{mg \cdot b}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Use vertical equilibrium** ($\sum F_z = 0$):

$$W_f + W_r - mg = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W_r$:**

$$W_r = mg - W_f = mg - \frac{mgb}{L} = \frac{mg(L - b)}{L} = \frac{mg \cdot a}{L}$$

Thus both static reactions are derived.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Longitudinal Dynamic Weight Transfer

**Step 1 — Consider vehicle accelerating** at rate $a_x = \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ along a level road. An inertial force $m a_x$ acts at the CG horizontally *opposite* to the acceleration direction.

**Step 2 — Take moments about the rear contact patch** (still in static equilibrium of vertical forces at this instant):

$$W_f \cdot L + m a_x \cdot h - mg \cdot b = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the new (dynamic) $W_f$:**

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mgb}{L} - \frac{m a_x h}{L}$$

**Step 4 — By vertical equilibrium:**

$$W_{r,\text{dyn}} = mg - W_{f,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mga}{L} + \frac{m a_x h}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Define the weight transfer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta W = \frac{m a_x h}{L}}$$

This is **transferred from front to rear during forward acceleration** and **from rear to front during braking**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation V: Lateral Weight Transfer (for completeness)

For a vehicle cornering with lateral acceleration $a_y$:

$$\Delta W_{\text{lateral}} = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$$

where $t$ is the track width and $a_y \approx \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ for a steady turn of radius $R$.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus introduces this in Module 1 only as a *parallel concept*. The full multi-axle treatment appears in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Free Body Diagram (Conceptual Block Representation)

The following Mermaid block represents the conceptual force interactions on a road vehicle climbing a slope. Per the engine protocol, it is rendered as a functional architecture flow rather than a literal physical sketch, with strict adherence to alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle CG Point] --> B[Weight W equals mg acting vertically downward]
    A --> C[Tractive Force Ft acting forward along road]
    A --> D[Aerodynamic Drag Fa acting rearward at frontal area]
    A --> E[Rolling Resistance Fr acting rearward at tire contact]
    A --> F[Grade Component Fg equals mg sine theta acting rearward on slope]
    G[Front Axle Contact] --> H[Normal Reaction Wf equals mgb over L]
    I[Rear Axle Contact] --> J[Normal Reaction Wr equals mga over L]
    A --> G
    A --> I
    C --> K[Engine and Drivetrain Block]
    D --> L[Aerodynamic Flow Block]
    E --> M[Tire Hysteresis Block]
    F --> N[Road Inclination Block]
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
- The **Vehicle CG Point** is the conceptual origin of all force vectors.
- **Engine and Drivetrain Block** generates the tractive force via torque multiplication.
- **Tire Hysteresis Block** captures the energy dissipation that manifests as $F_r$.
- **Aerodynamic Flow Block** depends on $V^2$ (drag) and $V^3$ (power loss).
- **Road Inclination Block** introduces the gravitational component $F_g$.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Driver Input Accelerator Pedal] --> P2[Engine Torque Te]
    P2 --> P3[Transmission Gear Ratio ig]
    P3 --> P4[Final Drive Ratio io]
    P4 --> P5[Tractive Force Ft at Tire Contact]
    P5 --> P6[Longitudinal Equation of Motion Block]
    P6 --> P7[Vehicle Velocity V]
    P6 --> P8[Vehicle Acceleration a]
    R1[Rolling Resistance mu_r mg cos theta] --> P6
    R2[Aerodynamic Drag 0.5 rho Cd Af V squared] --> P6
    R3[Grade Resistance mg sine theta] --> P6
    P7 --> R2
    P7 --> P9[Power Required Block]
    P9 --> P10[Output P required equals Ft times V in kW]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Driver Input** propagates through the engine, gearbox, and final drive — a chain of *torque multipliers*.
- **Resistive blocks** R1, R2, R3 act in parallel as *disturbances* into the equation-of-motion block.
- The output **Velocity $V$** feeds back into the aerodynamic drag block (closing the loop non-linearly via $V^2$).

---

### 4.3 Tire Force Resolution at the Contact Patch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CP[Tire Road Contact Patch] --> FX[Longitudinal Force Fx]
    CP --> FY[Lateral Force Fy]
    CP --> FZ[Vertical Normal Force Fz]
    FX --> FXT[Tractive Force when driving]
    FX --> FXB[Braking Force when braking]
    FY --> FYC[Cornering Force equals C alpha times alpha]
    FZ --> FZD[Static Weight Component]
    FZ --> FZL[Dynamic Weight Transfer Component]
```

**Key Insight:** Every tire force at the contact patch is bounded by the friction circle:

$$\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \leq \mu \cdot F_z$$

This inequality is a Module 3 topic but the *concept* should be internalised here.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define rolling resistance in a pneumatic tire. List any four factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Rolling resistance is the resisting force offered by a tire to its rolling motion along a road surface. It arises primarily from **hysteresis loss in the tire carcass** during cyclic deformation, and from **micro-slip** between the tire tread and the road. Mathematically, it is expressed as $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$, where $\mu_r$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance.

**Factors (any four, 1.5 marks):**
1. Tire inflation pressure — *lower pressure increases contact area and hysteresis*.
2. Tire material composition — *natural rubber has lower hysteresis than synthetic*.
3. Road surface texture and roughness.
4. Vehicle speed (mild increase at high speed).
5. Wheel diameter — *larger diameter reduces $\mu_r$*.
6. Temperature of the tire and road.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**What is weight transfer? Write the expression for longitudinal weight transfer during braking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Weight transfer is the redistribution of normal reaction between the front and rear axles of a vehicle when it undergoes longitudinal or lateral acceleration. It occurs because the **inertial force acting at the CG** creates an additional moment about the tire contact patches, shifting load from one axle to the other.

**Expression (1.5 marks):**
During braking (negative longitudinal acceleration $-a_x$):

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$$

The load transfers **from rear to front**, so:

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mgb}{L} + \frac{m a_x h}{L}, \quad W_{r,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mga}{L} - \frac{m a_x h}{L}$$

where $m$ = vehicle mass, $a_x$ = braking deceleration, $h$ = CG height, $L$ = wheelbase.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative questions are provided below — students answer **either** A or B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the longitudinal equation of motion of a road vehicle moving up an incline, identifying each force with its physical origin.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A passenger car of mass **1500 kg** is driven at a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.015**, the drag coefficient $C_d = 0.35$, the frontal area $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, and the air density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Determine:
(i) The tractive force required at the wheels.
(ii) The power required at the wheels in kW.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the system: vehicle of mass $m$ on incline $\theta$, velocity $V$, acceleration $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the four forces:
- Tractive force $F_t$ (forward) — from engine torque transmitted through tires.
- Rolling resistance $F_r = \mu_r mg \cos\theta$ (backward).
- Aerodynamic drag $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$ (backward).
- Grade resistance $F_g = mg \sin\theta$ (backward when climbing).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply Newton's Second Law in vehicle $x$-direction:

$$F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m\frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute each resistance:

$$F_t - \mu_r mg \cos\theta - \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 - mg \sin\theta = m \frac{dV}{dt}$$

This is the required equation of motion.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$, $V = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- $\mu_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Level road: $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$, $\sin\theta = 0$
- Constant speed: $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = 0$

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Rolling resistance:**

$$F_r = \mu_r mg = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.725 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_a = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$

$$\Rightarrow F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 625 = 288.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Tractive force (using equation of motion, $\dfrac{dV}{dt}=0$, $\theta=0$):**

$$F_t = F_r + F_a = 220.725 + 288.75 = 509.475 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_t \approx 509.48 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks) — Power required:**

$$P = F_t \times V = 509.475 \times 25 = 12736.875 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 12.74 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common error in this question is forgetting to convert km/h to m/s for velocity. The drag force uses $V^2$ in $(\text{m/s})^2$ — using $V = 90$ directly will inflate the answer by a factor of $(3.6)^2 = 12.96$. **Always convert to SI units first.** A second pitfall: forgetting that $F_g = 0$ on a level road leads to a wasted term in the equation and partial mark loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a free body diagram, derive the expressions for **static** and **dynamic (under braking)** weight distribution on the front and rear axles of a road vehicle. State clearly all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sports car has the following data: wheelbase $L = 2.6 \text{ m}$, CG height $h = 0.55 \text{ m}$, mass $m = 1400 \text{ kg}$, distance from front axle to CG $a = 1.3 \text{ m}$. Calculate:
(i) Static loads on front and rear axles.
(ii) Dynamic loads during braking at $a_x = 7 \text{ m/s}^2$ (assume $\mu_r$ is negligible for the load calculation).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Assume vehicle at rest on level ground, rigid body, weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward at CG located at height $h$, distance $a$ from front axle and $b$ from rear axle, with $a + b = L$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Static — take moments about the rear contact patch:

$$W_f \cdot L - W \cdot b = 0 \Rightarrow W_f = \frac{mgb}{L}$$

Vertical equilibrium:

$$W_f + W_r = mg \Rightarrow W_r = \frac{mga}{L}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Dynamic under braking — apply inertial force $m a_x$ at CG acting forward (since deceleration is forward). Take moments about the rear contact patch:

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} \cdot L + m a_x \cdot h - mg \cdot b = 0$$

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mgb}{L} + \frac{m a_x h}{L}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** By vertical equilibrium:

$$W_{r,\text{dyn}} = mg - W_{f,\text{dyn}} = \frac{mga}{L} - \frac{m a_x h}{L}$$

Thus $\Delta W = \dfrac{m a_x h}{L}$ is transferred to the front during braking.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $L = 2.6 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.55 \text{ m}$, $m = 1400 \text{ kg}$, $a = 1.3 \text{ m}$, so $b = L - a = 2.6 - 1.3 = 1.3 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Static Loads:**

$$W_{f,\text{stat}} = \frac{mgb}{L} = \frac{1400 \times 9.81 \times 1.3}{2.6} = \frac{17853.4}{2.6}$$

$$\boxed{W_{f,\text{stat}} = 6866.7 \text{ N}}$$

By symmetry (since $a = b$):

$$\boxed{W_{r,\text{stat}} = 6866.7 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Weight Transfer:**

$$\Delta W = \frac{m a_x h}{L} = \frac{1400 \times 7 \times 0.55}{2.6} = \frac{5390}{2.6} = 2073.08 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Dynamic Loads During Braking:**

$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = 6866.7 + 2073.08 = 8939.78 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{r,\text{dyn}} = 6866.7 - 2073.08 = 4793.62 \text{ N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common pitfalls here:
> 1. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** $a$ is the distance from the **front** axle to the CG, not from the rear. Mixing them yields inverted loads.
> 2. **Sign of weight transfer:** Under **braking**, $\Delta W$ adds to the **front** axle and subtracts from the rear. Under **acceleration**, the signs are reversed. Examiners explicitly test this sign convention — a wrong sign here means $-1$ mark per sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Performance Forces):**

- **Longitudinal Equation of Motion:** $F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ — *commit to memory, including all four terms and signs*.
- **Tractive Force from Drivetrain:** $F_t = \dfrac{T_t \, i_g \, i_o \, \eta_t}{r_w}$ — *know each variable*.
- **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ — *velocity-squared dependence*.
- **Rolling Resistance:** $F_r = \mu_r mg \cos\theta$ — *typical $\mu_r$ for car tires on asphalt is 0.010–0.015*.
- **Grade Resistance:** $F_g = mg \sin\theta$ — *vanishes on level ground*.
- **Power at Wheels:** $P = F_t \cdot V$ — *units W when $F_t$ in N and $V$ in m/s*.
- **Static Weight Distribution:** $W_f = \dfrac{mgb}{L}$, $W_r = \dfrac{mga}{L}$ — *remember the front load uses the distance from rear*.
- **Dynamic Weight Transfer (Longitudinal):** $\Delta W = \dfrac{m a_x h}{L}$ — *sign convention critical*.
- **Dynamic Weight Transfer (Lateral):** $\Delta W = \dfrac{m a_y h}{t}$ — *appears in Module 3 cornering*.
- **Tire Friction Limit:** $\mu_x = \dfrac{F_x}{F_z}$, $\mu_y = \dfrac{F_y}{F_z}$ — *dimensionless, bounded by road-tire friction*.
- **Friction Circle Concept:** $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \leq \mu F_z$ — *total tire force capacity is shared between longitudinal and lateral*.
- **SI Unit Discipline:** Always convert km/h → m/s before substituting into $V^2$ or $V^3$ terms.
- **Assumption Statements:** Always declare "level road", "constant speed", or "rear-wheel drive" before writing the governing equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Strategy for ESE:** Practice at least one numerical each on (i) tractive force calculation on a slope, (ii) weight transfer during braking, and (iii) power required at top speed. These three problems collectively account for the majority of marks in Module 1 across the past five KTU exam cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tractive Effort/Tractive Resistance and Power Available from the Engine/Power Required for Propulsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tractive Effort/Tractive Resistance and Power Available from the Engine/Power Required for Propulsion ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Tractive Effort (TE) — The "Pushing Force" of a Vehicle **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** **Tractive Effort** is the net longitudinal driving force generated at the driving wheels (or tracks) of a vehicle, transmitted from the engine through the transmission system. It is the actual force available to overcome resistance and accelerate the vehicle. Mathematically, it is the tangential force at the wheel-road contact point: $$TE = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$ Where: - $T_e$ = Engine torque (N·m) - $i_g$ = Gear ratio of transmission - $i_0$ = Final drive (axle) ratio - $\eta_t$ = Overall drivetrain efficiency - $r$ = Effective rolling radius of the wheel (m) > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Tractive Effort is **NOT** the engine torque. It is the engine torque *amplified* by the gear system and *reduced* by drivetrain losses, finally applied at the tire contact patch. **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden cart up a slight slope. Your hands gripping the handle are like the **engine**. The handle and wheels are like the **transmission**. The actual force the wheels exert on the road surface to push the cart forward is the **Tractive Effort**. If the wheels slip, your pushing force (TE) doesn't translate into motion — it just burns rubber. This is why TE is measured *at the road interface*, not at the engine. --- ### 1.2 Tractive Resistance (TR) — The Forces Fighting Motion **Formal Definition:** **Tractive Resistance** is the total sum of all longitudinal forces opposing the forward motion of a vehicle. These are passive forces that exist regardless of whether the engine is producing torque. The four primary components of tractive resistance are: | Component | Symbol | Physical Cause | |-----------|--------|----------------| | Rolling Resistance | $R_r$ | Tire deformation, friction, road hysteresis | | Aerodynamic Drag | $R_a$ | Air displacement at high speed | | Gradient (Grade) Resistance | $R_g$ | Gravitational component on slope | | Inertia Resistance | $R_i$ | Mass × acceleration (Newton's 2nd Law) | > [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to list **all four** resistance components. Missing even one (especially *inertia resistance*) costs a full mark in 3-mark questions. **Real-World Analogy:** Think of cycling a bicycle: - **Rolling Resistance** = the slight drag from tires on the road (more on soft sand, less on smooth tarmac) - **Aerodynamic Drag** = wind pushing against your face, growing stronger as you pedal faster - **Gradient Resistance** = gravity pulling you back when you ride uphill - **Inertia Resistance** = the heavy "pull" you feel when you suddenly sprint from rest The vehicle's engine must generate enough Tractive Effort to overcome **all four** simultaneously. --- ### 1.3 Power Available vs. Power Required — The Energy Budget **Power Available ($P_a$):** The usable mechanical power delivered by the engine to the driving wheels after transmission losses. $$P_a = TE \cdot V \quad \text{(where V is vehicle velocity in m/s)}$$ **Power Required ($P_r$):** The mechanical power demanded by the resistance forces to maintain (or accelerate) motion. $$P_r = (R_r + R_a + R_g + R_i) \cdot V$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Fundamental Principle:** For a vehicle to move at desired speed and acceleration, **$P_a \geq P_r$**. If Power Required > Power Available, the vehicle decelerates or stalls. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Component-Wise Theoretical Breakdown **A. Rolling Resistance ($R_r$)** - Caused by hysteresis losses in the tire rubber as it continuously deforms and recovers. - The deformation in the contact patch is **asymmetric** — more energy is absorbed than released. - Depends on: vehicle weight, tire pressure, tire material, road surface texture, speed. **B. Aerodynamic Drag ($R_a$)** - Air exerts pressure on the vehicle's frontal area. - Dominant at high speeds (scales with **V²**). - Depends on: frontal area, drag coefficient, air density, vehicle speed. **C. Gradient Resistance ($R_g$)** - Component of gravitational force acting along the slope. - For small angles (most road cases), $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$ (radians). - Grade is often expressed as a percentage: $\text{Grade \%} = \tan\alpha \times 100$. **D. Inertia Resistance ($R_i$)** - Force required to overcome the vehicle's mass times acceleration. - For vehicles with rotating masses (flywheel, gears, wheels), an **equivalent mass** factor is used: $$R_i = (m + m_{eq}) \cdot a$$ Where $m_{eq}$ accounts for rotational inertia. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | |---|---------|-------------|------------| | 1 | $TE = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ | Tractive Effort from Engine Torque | N | | 2 | $R_r = f \cdot W = f \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling Resistance (flat road) | N | | 3 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$ | Aerodynamic Drag | N | | 4 | $R_g = W \cdot \sin\alpha = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Gradient Resistance | N | | 5 | $R_i = m \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt} = m \cdot a$ | Inertia Resistance | N | | 6 | $R_{total} = R_r + R_a + R_g + R_i$ | Total Tractive Resistance | N | | 7 | $TE = R_{total}$ (at constant speed) | Force Balance Equation | N | | 8 | $P_{engine} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot N}{9550}$ | Engine Power from Torque | kW | | 9 | $P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V$ | Power at Wheel | W | | 10 | $P_{required} = R_{total} \cdot V$ | Power Required for Propulsion | W | | 11 | $\eta_t = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{engine}}$ | Drivetrain Efficiency | dimensionless | | 12 | $\text{Grade \%} = \tan\alpha \times 100$ | Road Gradient Percentage | % | **Standard Constant Values (for KTU problems):** - Air density: $\rho = 1.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - Rolling resistance coefficient (typical): $f = 0.015$ (car on concrete) - Typical passenger car drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.3 - 0.4$ > [!NOTE] **Memorization Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** Memorize the **Aerodynamic Drag formula ($R_a = 0.5 \rho C_d A_f V^2$)** letter-perfect. It is the most frequently asked equation in Vehicle Dynamics papers. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications 1. **Vehicle Design:** Manufacturers use these equations to size engines, choose gear ratios, and optimize fuel efficiency. 2. **Performance Prediction:** Engineers calculate maximum grade-climbing ability and top speed. 3. **Fuel Economy Analysis:** Power required × time determines energy consumption. 4. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Trajectory planning uses resistance models to compute required torque. 5. **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Critical for battery range estimation — every additional Newton of drag reduces kilometers per charge. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Tractive Effort from Engine Torque **Starting Point:** The engine produces a torque $T_e$. This torque is multiplied through the gearbox ratio $i_g$, then by the final drive ratio $i_0$, with efficiency losses $\eta_t$ along the way. The resulting torque $T_w$ at the wheel is: $$T_w = T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t$$ The tractive effort (force) is the wheel torque divided by the effective wheel radius $r$: $$TE = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$ **Why this derivation matters:** The same engine can produce drastically different tractive efforts depending on gear choice. In 1st gear (high $i_g$), TE is large but speed is low — perfect for climbing. In 5th gear (low $i_g$), TE is small but speed is high — ideal for cruising on highways. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity in Terms of Engine RPM and Gear Ratio The wheel angular velocity $\omega_w$ is related to engine angular velocity $\omega_e$ by: $$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_e}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$ Converting to vehicle linear velocity $V = \omega_w \cdot r$: $$V = \frac{\omega_e \cdot r}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$ Substituting $\omega_e = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is engine RPM: $$V = \frac{2\pi N \cdot r}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] This equation links **engine RPM**, **gear ratios**, and **vehicle speed** — the heart of all performance calculations. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Available at the Wheels Starting from the basic power equation $P = F \cdot V$: $$P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V$$ Substituting the expression for $V$: $$P_{wheel} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r} \cdot \frac{2\pi N \cdot r}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$$ The $i_g$, $i_0$, and $r$ cancel out elegantly: $$P_{wheel} = \frac{2\pi N \cdot T_e \cdot \eta_t}{60}$$ Simplifying with $2\pi/60 \approx 0.1047$: $$P_{wheel} = \frac{T_e \cdot N \cdot \eta_t}{9550} \text{ (in kW)}$$ > [!NOTE] This shows that **Power at the wheel depends only on engine torque, RPM, and efficiency** — independent of gear ratios or wheel size! The gears trade TE for V but the power remains the same (minus losses). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Gradient Resistance — Small Angle Approximation For a vehicle of weight $W = mg$ on a slope of angle $\alpha$, the gravitational force component along the slope is: $$R_g = W \cdot \sin\alpha$$ For road vehicles, slopes are small (typically $\alpha < 15°$). Using the small-angle approximation: $$\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = \text{Grade (as decimal)}$$ If grade is given as percentage $G\%$: $$\text{Grade (decimal)} = \frac{G}{100}$$ Therefore: $$R_g = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100}$$ **Example:** A 1500 kg car climbing a 10% grade: $$R_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 1471.5 \text{ N}$$ --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Acceleration Capability (Maximum Speed on Grade) At **steady-state** (constant velocity), acceleration = 0, so $R_i = 0$ and $TE = R_r + R_a + R_g$: $$\frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r} = f \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$$ This is the **fundamental vehicle performance equation**. Solving for $V$ (with appropriate gear selection) gives maximum speed on any grade. **Maximum Grade at Zero Speed (Hill Start):** At $V = 0$, $R_a = 0$ and $R_i = 0$ (for static case): $$\tan\alpha_{max} = f + \frac{TE_{max}}{m \cdot g}$$ This is the **maximum gradeable slope** from rest. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When Each Resistance Dominates | Speed Regime | Dominant Resistance | Approximate % of Total | |--------------|---------------------|------------------------| | 0–30 km/h (low speed) | Rolling + Gradient | $R_r \approx 60\%$, $R_a \approx 5\%$ | | 30–80 km/h (medium) | Rolling ≈ Aerodynamic | $R_r \approx 40\%$, $R_a \approx 40\%$ | | 80+ km/h (high speed) | Aerodynamic | $R_a \approx 70\%$, $R_r \approx 25\%$ | > [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Takeaway:** This is why **aerodynamic shape** matters more for high-speed vehicles (sedans, sports cars), while **tire and suspension design** matters more for low-speed, heavy vehicles (trucks, off-roaders). ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Force & Power Flow in a Vehicle ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[Engine
Torque T_e
Power P_e] CLUTCH[Clutch &
Transmission
Ratio i_g] FINAL[Final Drive
Ratio i_0] DIFF[Differential] WHEEL[Driving Wheels
Radius r] ROAD[Road Contact
Patch] TRAC[TRACTIVE EFFORT
TE] RR[Rolling
Resistance R_r] RA[Aerodynamic
Drag R_a] RG[Gradient
Resistance R_g] RI[Inertia
Resistance R_i] TOTR[TOTAL RESISTANCE
R_total] ENG --> CLUTCH --> FINAL --> DIFF --> WHEEL --> ROAD --> TRAC TRAC --> TOTR RR --> TOTR RA --> TOTR RG --> TOTR RI --> TOTR style ENG fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000 style TRAC fill:#6aa84f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style TOTR fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style ROAD fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Free Body Diagram: Forces Acting on a Vehicle on a Slope ```mermaid flowchart TD VEHICLE[Vehicle Body
Mass m, Weight W = m*g] VEHICLE -->|TE
forward| FWD[Tractive Effort TE
Applied at Wheels] VEHICLE -->|R_r| ROLL[Rolling Resistance
R_r = f*W
Opposes Motion] VEHICLE -->|R_a| AERO[Aerodynamic Drag
R_a = 0.5*rho*C_d*A_f*V^2
Opposes Motion] VEHICLE -->|R_g| GRAD[Gradient Resistance
R_g = W*sin(alpha)
Acts Down Slope] VEHICLE -->|R_i| INER[Inertia Resistance
R_i = m*a
Opposes Acceleration] GRAD -->|Alpha = slope angle| SLOPE[Slope Angle alpha
Grade% = tan(alpha)*100] style VEHICLE fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,color:#000 style FWD fill:#6aa84f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style ROLL fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style AERO fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style GRAD fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style INER fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style SLOPE fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Power Flow Architecture (Engine to Road) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Engine Power
P_engine kW] --> B[Clutch Losses
~2-3 percent] B --> C[Gearbox Losses
~5-8 percent] C --> D[Final Drive Losses
~3-5 percent] D --> E[Differential Losses
~2-3 percent] E --> F[Wheel Power
P_wheel kW] F --> G[Tractive Effort TE
times Velocity V] G --> H[Power at Road
P_road = TE * V] H --> I[Overcomes R_r] H --> J[Overcomes R_a] H --> K[Overcomes R_g] H --> L[Accelerates Mass R_i] style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#6aa84f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style H fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style J fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style K fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style L fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff ``` --- ### 4.4 Velocity vs. Tractive Effort Curve (Qualitative) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VEL[Velocity Domain] V0[V = 0
Low Gear] V1[V = 30 km/h] V2[V = 60 km/h] V3[V = 90 km/h] V4[V = 120 km/h
High Gear] end V0 -->|TE high| TE_HIGH[TE: 8000 N] V1 -->|TE reduces| TE_MED[TE: 3000 N] V2 -->|TE low| TE_LOW[TE: 1500 N] V3 -->|TE very low| TE_VLOW[TE: 900 N] V4 -->|TE minimum| TE_MIN[TE: 600 N] style V0 fill:#e06666,stroke:#333,color:#fff style V4 fill:#6aa84f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style TE_HIGH fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000 style TE_MIN fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** As velocity increases, tractive effort **decreases** because engine torque at high RPM is lower and gear ratios in top gear are small. The intersection of TE curve with Total Resistance curve gives the **maximum attainable speed**. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define tractive effort and tractive resistance. List the four major components of tractive resistance.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Tractive Effort (TE):** The net longitudinal driving force generated at the driving wheels of a vehicle, transmitted from the engine via the transmission system. It is the force available at the contact patch to propel the vehicle forward. Mathematically: $TE = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ (1 mark) > > **Tractive Resistance (TR):** The total sum of all forces opposing the forward motion of the vehicle. (1 mark) > > **Four Components of Tractive Resistance:** > 1. Rolling Resistance ($R_r = f \cdot W$) > 2. Aerodynamic Drag ($R_a = 0.5 \rho C_d A_f V^2$) > 3. Gradient Resistance ($R_g = W \sin\alpha$) > 4. Inertia Resistance ($R_i = m \cdot a$) (1 mark) --- **Q2.
