[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *Write the expression for aerodynamic drag force on a vehicle and explain the significance of each term.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$R_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$ | Term | Symbol | Significance | |------|--------|--------------| | Air density | $\rho$ | Mass of air per unit volume (typically 1.25 kg/m³ at sea level) | | Drag coefficient | $C_d$ | Shape efficiency of vehicle body (lower = more aerodynamic) | | Frontal area | $A_f$ | Projected cross-section facing airflow (m²) | | Velocity | $V$ | Vehicle speed relative to air (m/s) | (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for each 2 terms explained, 1 mark for units/significance) --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **A car has the following specifications:** - Mass (m) = 1200 kg - Engine torque ($T_e$) = 180 N·m at 3000 RPM - Gear ratio ($i_g$) = 3.5 - Final drive ratio ($i_0$) = 4.1 - Drivetrain efficiency ($\eta_t$) = 0.88 - Wheel radius (r) = 0.32 m - Rolling resistance coefficient (f) = 0.018 - Frontal area ($A_f$) = 2.2 m² - Drag coefficient ($C_d$) = 0.35 - Air density ($\rho$) = 1.25 kg/m³ - Slope angle ($\alpha$) = 5° - $g = 9.81$ m/s² #### **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the Tractive Effort available and the total Tractive Resistance at a vehicle speed of 60 km/h on the given slope. #### **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Determine the Power Available at the wheels and Power Required for propulsion. State whether the vehicle can maintain 60 km/h on this slope. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Tractive Effort (TE)** (3 marks) $$TE = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$ Substituting values: $$TE = \frac{180 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.88}{0.32}$$ Computing the numerator: $$180 \times 3.5 = 630$$ $$630 \times 4.1 = 2583$$ $$2583 \times 0.88 = 2273.04$$ $$TE = \frac{2273.04}{0.32} = 7103.25 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Individual Resistance Forces** (3 marks) Converting velocity: $V = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$ *Rolling Resistance:* $$R_r = f \cdot m \cdot g = 0.018 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 211.896 \text{ N}$$ *Aerodynamic Drag:* $$R_a = 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A_f \times V^2$$ $$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.25 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2$$ $$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.25 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 277.89$$ $$R_a = 0.48125 \times 2.2 \times 277.89$$ $$R_a = 1.05875 \times 277.89 = 294.21 \text{ N}$$ *Gradient Resistance:* $$R_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 1200 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5°)$$ $$\sin(5°) = 0.0872$$ $$R_g = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 = 1026.59 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3: Total Resistance** (1 mark) $$R_{total} = R_r + R_a + R_g = 211.90 + 294.21 + 1026.59 = 1532.70 \text{ N}$$ --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1: Power Available at Wheels** (3 marks) $$P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V = 7103.25 \times 16.67$$ $$P_{wheel} = 118410.97 \text{ W} \approx 118.41 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2: Power Required for Propulsion** (3 marks) $$P_{required} = R_{total} \cdot V = 1532.70 \times 16.67$$ $$P_{required} = 25550.5 \text{ W} \approx 25.55 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Conclusion** (1 mark) Since $P_{wheel} (118.41 \text{ kW}) \gg P_{required} (25.55 \text{ kW})$, the vehicle has **sufficient power** to maintain 60 km/h on the 5° slope. The excess power (92.86 kW) can be used for acceleration. > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make these mistakes: > 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** — Always divide by 3.6 when using SI units. > 2. **Using degrees instead of radians** in $\sin\alpha$ — Most calculators default to degrees; verify mode. > 3. **Forgetting $\eta_t$ in TE calculation** — Drivetrain losses are NOT optional; examiners deduct 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing $R_r = f \cdot W$ with $R_r = f \cdot m$** — Weight (W) includes gravity, mass (m) does NOT. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — KTU Internal Option Pattern]** **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the equation for tractive effort from engine torque. A truck has an engine torque of 450 N·m at 2000 RPM, gear ratio 4.2, final drive ratio 5.0, efficiency 0.85, and wheel radius 0.5 m. Calculate the tractive effort. **(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle of mass 1500 kg travels at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a 4% gradient. If $C_d = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.5$ m², $f = 0.015$, $\rho = 1.25$ kg/m³, find (i) total tractive resistance, (ii) power required, and (iii) tractive effort required. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Derivation** (3 marks): Starting from engine torque $T_e$, the torque transmitted to the driving wheels after gear multiplication and efficiency losses is: $$T_w = T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t$$ Since tractive effort is the tangential force at the wheel contact patch: $$TE = \frac{T_w}{r} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$ **Numerical Calculation** (4 marks): $$TE = \frac{450 \times 4.2 \times 5.0 \times 0.85}{0.5}$$ Numerator computation: $$450 \times 4.2 = 1890$$ $$1890 \times 5.0 = 9450$$ $$9450 \times 0.85 = 8032.5$$ $$TE = \frac{8032.5}{0.5} = 16065 \text{ N} = 16.065 \text{ kN}$$ --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** $m = 1500$ kg, $V = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, Grade $= 4\% = 0.04$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.5$ m², $f = 0.015$, $\rho = 1.25$ kg/m³. **Step 1: Calculate each resistance component** (3 marks) *Rolling Resistance:* $$R_r = f \cdot m \cdot g = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.725 \text{ N}$$ *Aerodynamic Drag:* $$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.25 \times 0.32 \times 2.5 \times (25)^2$$ $$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.25 \times 0.32 \times 2.5 \times 625$$ $$R_a = 0.5 \times 1.25 = 0.625$$ $$0.625 \times 0.32 = 0.20$$ $$0.20 \times 2.5 = 0.50$$ $$0.50 \times 625 = 312.5 \text{ N}$$ *Gradient Resistance:* $$\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = 0.04$$ $$R_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 588.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Total Tractive Resistance** (1 mark) $$R_{total} = 220.73 + 312.5 + 588.6 = 1121.83 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3: Power Required** (1.5 marks) $$P_{required} = R_{total} \cdot V = 1121.83 \times 25 = 28045.75 \text{ W} \approx 28.05 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Tractive Effort Required** (1.5 marks) At constant speed, $TE = R_{total}$: $$TE = 1121.83 \text{ N} \approx 1.122 \text{ kN}$$ > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** > 1. **Forgetting inertia resistance** when the problem states "constant speed" — $R_i = 0$ only at constant velocity. > 2. **Using Grade% directly as $\sin\alpha$** — correct only for small angles; verify before substituting. > 3. **Unit mismatch** — Always express final answers in consistent SI units (N, m/s, W or kW). > 4. **Losing marks for missing the "constant speed" condition** — explicitly state $R_i = 0$. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):** - [ ] **Tractive Effort Formula:** $TE = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ — gear ratios amplify torque. - [ ] **Four Resistances:** Rolling ($f \cdot mg$), Aerodynamic ($0.5 \rho C_d A V^2$), Gradient ($mg \sin\alpha$), Inertia ($ma$). - [ ] **Power Equation:** $P = F \cdot V$ — works for both Available and Required. - [ ] **Engine Power Formula:** $P = \dfrac{T \cdot N}{9550}$ kW (with T in N·m and N in RPM). - [ ] **Steady State:** TE = Total Resistance; acceleration = 0; $R_i = 0$. - [ ] **Maximum Speed:** Solve $TE(V) = R_{total}(V)$ in highest gear. - [ ] **Small Angle Approx:** $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha$ for $\alpha < 15°$ — Grade% × W ≈ Gradient Resistance. - [ ] **Drivetrain Efficiency:** $\eta_t$ typically 0.85–0.92 for cars, 0.80–0.88 for trucks. - [ ] **Unit Conversions:** 1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s; 1 kW = 1000 W; 1 HP ≈ 0.746 kW. - [ ] **Order of Dominance:** At low V, rolling dominates. At high V, aerodynamic dominates. - [ ] **Vehicle Performance Equation:** $\dfrac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r} = fmg + 0.5\rho C_d A V^2 + mg\sin\alpha + ma$ - [ ] **For EVs:** Same equations apply, but $T_e$ is replaced by motor torque characteristics (flat curve). - [ ] **Common Mistake:** Mixing up "engine power" with "wheel power" — wheel power = engine power × $\eta_t$. - [ ] **Memorize Constants:** $\rho = 1.25$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², typical $f = 0.015$, typical $C_d = 0.3$–0.4. - [ ] **Examiner's Favorite:** Questions linking engine RPM to vehicle speed via gear ratios and wheel radius.

topics.title: Road Performance Curves – Acceleration, Grade Ability, Drawbar Pull and the Problems Related to These Terms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Road Performance Curves – Acceleration, Grade Ability, Drawbar Pull and the Problems Related to These Terms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Road Performance Curves — The Formal Definition

**Road Performance Curves** are graphical representations that describe how a vehicle performs under real-world operating conditions. They plot key performance parameters — namely **Maximum Acceleration**, **Grade Ability (Grade Climbing)**, and **Drawbar Pull** — as a function of vehicle **speed** or sometimes **gear ratio**. These curves are derived from the **tractive force–speed characteristics** of the engine combined with the **resistive forces** acting on the moving vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Road Performance Curves are central to Module 1 because they integrate the **Engine Power Curve**, **Gear Train Kinematics**, and **Vehicle Resistances** into a unified engineering tool used by automotive designers to predict vehicle behavior *before* physical prototyping.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Road Performance

| # | Performance Parameter | Real-World Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Acceleration (a)** | How fast a vehicle can increase its speed | m/s² |
| 2 | **Grade Ability (θ or %)** | The steepest slope the vehicle can climb at steady speed | degrees or % |
| 3 | **Drawbar Pull (F_db)** | Net towing force available at the coupling point | N |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug-of-War" Picture

Imagine a **horse pulling a loaded cart** up a muddy, windy hill:

- The **horse's pulling effort** is the *Tractive Force (F_t)*.
- The **mud dragging the wheels back** is the *Rolling Resistance (F_r)*.
- The **wind pushing against the cart** is the *Aerodynamic Drag (F_a)*.
- The **gravity pulling the cart downhill** is the *Grade Resistance (F_g)*.
- Whatever effort remains after fighting mud, wind, and gravity is used to **accelerate** the cart (pick up speed) or **climb the slope** (fight gravity).
- If a smaller cart is hitched *behind* the first, the remaining effort becomes the **Drawbar Pull** — the force available to tow another vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** The Tractive Force at the wheels must first *defeat* all resistances. **Whatever force is left over** is the *useful performance* — used either to accelerate the vehicle itself, climb a hill, or tow a load. This is the foundational idea of road performance analysis.

### 1.4 Why These Curves Matter in Engineering

- They allow engineers to **select appropriate gear ratios** and **engine power** for a target vehicle class.
- They help in **designing transmission systems** (number of gears, ratio spacing).
- They predict **off-road capability** for trucks, tractors, and military vehicles.
- They form the **design contract** between chassis engineers and powertrain engineers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Force Balance (Newton's Second Law for a Vehicle)

For a vehicle of **equivalent mass** $M_e$ (also called *effective mass*, which includes rotational inertia of rotating parts), moving on a road:

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = M_e \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Tractive force at the **tire–road contact patch** (N)
- $F_{rr}$ = Rolling resistance force (N)
- $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic drag force (N)
- $F_{gr}$ = Grade resistance force (N)
- $M_e$ = Equivalent mass of the vehicle (kg)
- $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ = Longitudinal acceleration (m/s²)

### 2.2 Component-Wise Breakdown

#### (a) Tractive Force at the Wheels
Derived from the engine torque $T_e$ transmitted through the gearbox ($i_g$), final drive ($i_0$), with overall drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$, applied at the effective wheel radius $r_d$:

$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **rear-wheel-drive** or **4WD** vehicle, $F_t$ is split between the driven axles. For a **two-wheeler**, $F_t$ acts on the single driven wheel.

#### (b) Rolling Resistance
Caused by tire deformation hysteresis on the road:

$$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta \approx f_r \cdot W \quad (\text{for small }\theta)$$

Where $f_r$ is the **rolling resistance coefficient** (typically 0.01 to 0.02 for car tires on asphalt) and $W = M \cdot g$ is the vehicle weight.

#### (c) Aerodynamic Drag
Caused by air pushing against the vehicle's frontal area $A$:

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$$

Where $\rho_a \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (air density at sea level), $C_d$ is the drag coefficient, $A$ is frontal area (m²), and $V$ is vehicle speed (m/s).

#### (d) Grade Resistance
Caused by gravity acting on a vehicle on a slope $\theta$:

$$F_{gr} = W \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot \theta \quad (\text{for small angles in radians})$$

### 2.3 The Three Performance Parameters — Detailed Logic

#### A. Maximum Acceleration
At any given speed, the maximum acceleration is the net force available divided by the effective mass:

$$a_{max} = \frac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr}}{M_e}$$

- At **low speeds**, $F_t$ is high and $F_{ad}$ is low → **high acceleration**.
- At **high speeds**, $F_t$ drops (engine power-limited) and $F_{ad}$ rises sharply ($\propto V^2$) → **acceleration drops to zero** at top speed.

#### B. Grade Ability
The **maximum grade** the vehicle can climb at a *steady speed* (acceleration = 0) is found by setting $\frac{dV}{dt} = 0$ and solving for $\theta$:

$$F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = W \cdot \sin\theta_{max}$$

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{F_{t,max} - f_r W - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2}{W}$$

The grade is often expressed as a **percentage**:

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan\theta \times 100$$

#### C. Drawbar Pull
For a **tractor-trailer system** or any vehicle pulling a load, the **Drawbar Pull** is the net force available at the coupling *after* overcoming all self-resistances of the towing vehicle *and* pulling the towed vehicle:

$$F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr,towing} - M_{trailer} \cdot g \cdot f_{r,trailer} - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d,t} A_t V^2 - M_{trailer} g \sin\theta$$

For a **single vehicle** (no trailer) at constant speed, the drawbar pull reduces to the *reserve tractive force*:

$$F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Tractive Force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \, i_g \, i_0 \, \eta_t}{r_d}$ | Units: **N** |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot W$ | $f_r \in [0.01, 0.02]$ |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = 0.5 \, \rho_a \, C_d \, A \, V^2$ | $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³ |
| 4 | Grade Resistance | $F_{gr} = W \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ in **radians** |
| 5 | Newton's 2nd Law (Vehicle) | $F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = M_e a$ | $M_e$ = effective mass |
| 6 | Maximum Acceleration | $a_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr}}{M_e}$ | Units: **m/s²** |
| 7 | Maximum Grade (steady) | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{W}$ | Reported in **%** |
| 8 | Drawbar Pull (no trailer, accel = 0) | $F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr}$ | Units: **N** |
| 9 | Equivalent Mass | $M_e = M + \dfrac{I_e i_g^2 i_0^2 \eta_t + I_w}{r_d^2}$ | Includes rotational inertia |
| 10 | Top Speed Condition | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ at $V = V_{max}$ | Grade = 0 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Trucks:** Drawbar pull curves dictate what load a truck can tow on a given highway gradient.
- **Tractors (Agricultural):** Drawbar pull at the hitch point is the *rated* metric (e.g., "50 kN drawbar pull").
- **Passenger Cars:** Acceleration and grade ability curves become the **marketing specs** (0–100 km/h time, 30% gradeability).
- **Off-Road / Military Vehicles:** The 60% grade climb capability is a *design requirement* written into procurement contracts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Acceleration as a Function of Speed

**Step 1 — Start with the longitudinal force balance** for a vehicle on a level road ($\theta = 0$, so $F_{gr} = 0$):

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = M_e \cdot a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the resistance expressions**:

$$\frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r \cdot W - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 = M_e \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Solve explicitly for acceleration**:

$$a = \frac{1}{M_e} \left[ \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the limit cases:**

- At $V = 0$: $F_{ad} = 0$ → maximum starting acceleration:

$$a_{start} = \frac{1}{M_e} \left[ \frac{T_{e,max} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W \right]$$

- At $V = V_{max}$: $a = 0$ → tractive force equals drag:

$$\frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} = f_r W + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V_{max}^2$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Grade Ability at Constant Speed

**Step 1 — Set acceleration to zero** (steady-state climb):

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand all terms**:

$$\frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W \cos\theta - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 - W \sin\theta = 0$$

**Step 3 — For small slopes**, $\cos\theta \approx 1$:

$$\frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 = W \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the grade angle**:

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{1}{W}\left[ \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Convert to grade percentage**:

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan\theta_{max} \times 100 \approx \theta_{max} \times 57.296 \quad (\theta \text{ in rad})$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Drawbar Pull for a Tractor–Trailer System

**Step 1 — Consider a tractor of mass $M_1$ towing a trailer of mass $M_2$** on a level road, moving at constant speed $V$.

**Step 2 — The tractor's tractive force must overcome**:
- Tractor's own rolling resistance: $f_{r1} \cdot M_1 g$
- Tractor's aerodynamic drag: $\frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d1} A_1 V^2$
- Trailer's rolling resistance: $f_{r2} \cdot M_2 g$
- Trailer's aerodynamic drag: $\frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d2} A_2 V^2$

**Step 3 — The drawbar pull is the net force transmitted** from tractor to trailer (i.e., the force the trailer experiences at its coupling):

$$F_{db} = F_t - f_{r1} M_1 g - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d1} A_1 V^2$$

**Step 4 — Equivalently, the drawbar pull is the force needed to move the trailer** at constant speed:

$$F_{db} = f_{r2} M_2 g + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d2} A_2 V^2$$

**Step 5 — Equating both expressions** gives the **load-carrying capacity** of the tractor:

$$f_{r2} M_2 g + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d2} A_2 V^2 = F_t - f_{r1} M_1 g - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d1} A_1 V^2$$

Solving for trailer mass:

$$M_2 = \frac{F_t - f_{r1} M_1 g - \frac{1}{2}\rho_a (C_{d1} A_1 + C_{d2} A_2) V^2}{f_{r2} g}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem — Worked Example

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A passenger car has the following data:
- Curb mass: $M = 1500$ kg
- Effective mass factor: $M_e = 1.05 M$
- Engine torque $T_e = 200$ N·m (assumed constant for simplification)
- Gear ratio $i_g = 3.0$, final drive $i_0 = 3.5$, efficiency $\eta_t = 0.9$
- Tire radius $r_d = 0.32$ m
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.015$
- Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², drag coefficient $C_d = 0.35$
- Air density $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Compute the maximum acceleration at $V = 20$ m/s, and the maximum grade climbable at the same speed.**

**Solution — Step 1: Tractive Force**

$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} = \frac{200 \times 3.0 \times 3.5 \times 0.9}{0.32}$$

$$F_t = \frac{1890}{0.32} = 5906.25 \text{ N}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Weight and Rolling Resistance**

$$W = M g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{rr} = f_r W = 0.015 \times 14715 = 220.725 \text{ N}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Aerodynamic Drag at $V = 20$ m/s**

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (20)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 400$$

$$F_{ad} = 184.8 \text{ N}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Maximum Acceleration at $V = 20$ m/s** (level road, so $F_{gr} = 0$)

$$a = \frac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{M_e} = \frac{5906.25 - 220.725 - 184.8}{1.05 \times 1500}$$

$$a = \frac{5500.725}{1575} = 3.492 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Solution — Step 5: Maximum Grade Climbable at $V = 20$ m/s (steady speed)**

$$W \sin\theta_{max} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 5500.725 \text{ N}$$

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{5500.725}{14715} = 0.3738$$

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.3738) = 21.94°$$

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan(21.94°) \times 100 = 40.35\%$$

**Conclusion:** At 20 m/s (~72 km/h), this vehicle can theoretically climb a **~40% grade** at steady speed and accelerate at **~3.5 m/s²** on a level road.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Road Performance Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **analytical workflow** used by a vehicle dynamics engineer to construct road performance curves. It traces the data path from engine maps to the final performance graphs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque Map Te vs RPM] --> B[Apply Gear Train]
    C[Gearbox Ratios ig] --> B
    D[Final Drive i0] --> B
    E[Efficiency eta_t] --> B
    F[Tire Radius rd] --> B
    B --> G[Tractive Force Ft at Wheel]
    G --> H[Subtract Resistances]
    H --> I[Net Force Available]
    I --> J[Divide by Effective Mass Me]
    J --> K[Acceleration Curve a vs V]
    I --> L[Set Net Force = 0 for Grade]
    L --> M[Grade Ability Curve theta vs V]
    G --> N[Subtract Towing Vehicle Resistances]
    O[Trailer Mass M_t] --> N
    N --> P[Drawbar Pull Fdb vs V]
    Q[KTU Note Ft must exceed all resistances first] --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every block represents a *mathematical operation*. The tractive force $F_t$ is the **central node**; all three performance curves (acceleration, grade, drawbar) are derived by *subtracting the appropriate resistances* and then normalizing by the vehicle mass.

### 4.2 Schematic: Force Balance at the Tire–Road Contact Patch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Torque Te] -->|ig i0 eta_t| B[Tractive Force Ft at Contact Patch]
    B --> C[Net Force at CG]
    D[Rolling Resistance Frr] --> C
    E[Aero Drag Fad] --> C
    F[Grade Resistance Fgr] --> C
    C --> G{Motion Type}
    G -->|a > 0| H[Accelerating Vehicle]
    G -->|a = 0 on slope| I[Climbing at Steady Speed]
    G -->|trailer coupled| J[Drawbar Pull Fdb]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping Table

| Diagram Block | Equation | Performance Output |
|---------------|----------|--------------------|
| B → H | $a = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr}) / M_e$ | **Acceleration Curve** |
| B → I | $\sin\theta = (F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}) / W$ | **Grade Ability Curve** |
| B → J | $F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr,tractor} - F_{ad,tractor}$ | **Drawbar Pull Curve** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term **Drawbar Pull** as used in vehicle dynamics. State the equation for drawbar pull when a tractor pulls a trailer at constant speed on a level road.

**Model Answer:**
Drawbar Pull is the net horizontal force available at the **coupling point** of a towing vehicle (tractor) to pull a trailed vehicle. For a tractor–trailer system moving at constant speed $V$ on a level road:

$$F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr,tractor} - F_{ad,tractor} = f_{r,trailer} \cdot M_{trailer} g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_{d,trailer} A_{trailer} V^2$$

It represents the **useful towing capacity** of the vehicle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
List the **three major resistive forces** acting on a vehicle moving up a gradient. Write the equation for each in terms of speed $V$ and grade angle $\theta$.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Rolling Resistance:** $F_{rr} = f_r W \cos\theta$ (acts opposite to motion)
2. **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$ (acts opposite to motion)
3. **Grade Resistance:** $F_{gr} = W \sin\theta$ (acts opposite to motion on a slope)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **maximum grade climbable** by a vehicle at a given constant speed $V$ on a road with grade angle $\theta$. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A truck has a gross vehicle weight of **18,000 N**, tractive effort at the wheels of **9,000 N**, rolling resistance coefficient **0.02**, frontal area **5 m²**, drag coefficient **0.6**, and travels at **20 m/s** on a level road. Calculate the **maximum grade** the truck can climb at this speed. Take $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The force balance for a vehicle climbing a slope at constant speed ($a = 0$):

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W \cos\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 - W \sin\theta = 0$$

**Assumptions:**
- Constant vehicle speed (steady state)
- No acceleration
- No headwind/tailwind
- Small grade angle: $\cos\theta \approx 1$
- No wheel slip

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{1}{W}\left[\frac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 \right]$$

Expressing in percentage:

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan\theta_{max} \times 100$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $W = 18000$ N, $F_t = 9000$ N, $f_r = 0.02$, $A = 5$ m², $C_d = 0.6$, $V = 20$ m/s, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³.

Compute $F_{rr}$:

$$F_{rr} = f_r W = 0.02 \times 18000 = 360 \text{ N}$$

Compute $F_{ad}$:

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 5 \times (20)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 5 \times 400 = 720 \text{ N}$$

Apply the force balance at steady speed:

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = W \sin\theta_{max}$$

$$9000 - 360 - 720 = 18000 \sin\theta_{max}$$

$$7920 = 18000 \sin\theta_{max}$$

$$\sin\theta_{max} = 0.44$$

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.44) = 26.1°$$

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan(26.1°) \times 100 \approx 48.9\%$$

**Answer:** The truck can climb a **26.1° slope (≈ 48.9% grade)** at 20 m/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **longitudinal force balance** acting on a vehicle moving up a gradient. Derive the **equation of motion** in terms of tractive force, resistances, and effective mass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car of mass **1200 kg** has a maximum tractive force at the wheels of **5500 N**, rolling resistance coefficient **0.015**, drag coefficient **0.4**, frontal area **2 m²**, and air density **1.2 kg/m³**. Determine: (i) the **maximum acceleration** at **30 m/s** on a level road, and (ii) the **maximum drawbar pull** available at the same speed on the same level road. Use effective mass $M_e = 1.08 M$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the force balance schematic in **Section 4.2**. The longitudinal equation of motion is:

$$\boxed{\frac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} - f_r W \cos\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2 - W \sin\theta = M_e \frac{dV}{dt}}$$

All terms are as previously defined. The LHS is the net force available; the RHS is the inertia force.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $M = 1200$ kg, $M_e = 1.08 \times 1200 = 1296$ kg, $F_t = 5500$ N, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.4$, $A = 2$ m², $V = 30$ m/s, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³.

Weight: $W = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772$ N

Rolling resistance:

$$F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 11772 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.4 \times 2 \times (30)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.4 \times 2 \times 900 = 432 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Maximum Acceleration at $V = 30$ m/s, level road:**

$$a_{max} = \frac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{M_e} = \frac{5500 - 176.58 - 432}{1296}$$

$$a_{max} = \frac{4891.42}{1296} = 3.77 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(ii) Maximum Drawbar Pull at $V = 30$ m/s, level road (acceleration = 0):**

For drawbar pull at constant speed, the net force is the force available to tow:

$$F_{db} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 5500 - 176.58 - 432 = 4891.42 \text{ N}$$

**Answers:** (i) $a_{max} = 3.77$ m/s², (ii) $F_{db} = 4891.42$ N

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $F_{rr}$ and $F_{ad}$ from $F_t$** before computing drawbar pull — *most common 2-mark loss.*
> 2. **Mixing up effective mass $M_e$ with curb mass $M$** in acceleration problems — careful: $M_e > M$ due to rotating inertia.
> 3. **Expressing $\theta$ in degrees inside $\sin$ or $\tan$** — always convert to **radians** or use the inverse function correctly.
> 4. **Conflating grade % with grade angle** — Grade $\% = \tan\theta \times 100$, **not** $\theta \times 100$.
> 5. **Forgetting $g = 9.81$ m/s²** when weight $W$ is needed — KTU paper data usually gives mass in kg, not weight in N.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** — KTU examiners allot 1–2 marks for assumption statements (small angle, constant speed, no wind).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Road Performance Curves** are graphs of acceleration, grade ability, and drawbar pull plotted against vehicle speed.
- The **central equation** of vehicle longitudinal dynamics is:

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{gr} = M_e a$$

- **Tractive Force** is engine torque amplified by the gear train: $F_t = T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t / r_d$.
- **Rolling Resistance** is proportional to vehicle weight: $F_{rr} = f_r W$.
- **Aerodynamic Drag** grows with the **square of speed**: $F_{ad} \propto V^2$.
- **Grade Resistance** is the gravity component along the slope: $F_{gr} = W \sin\theta$.
- **Maximum acceleration** is the net force divided by effective mass.
- **Maximum grade** is found by setting acceleration to zero and solving for $\theta$.
- **Drawbar pull** is the residual force at the hitch point after self-resistances are overcome.
- **Effective mass $M_e$** > **curb mass $M$** because of rotational inertia of engine, gears, and wheels.
- **Top speed** occurs when $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ (level road).
- **Grade %** is **not** the angle in degrees — convert using $\tan$.
- All three performance metrics **decrease with speed** because $F_t$ drops and $F_{ad}$ rises.
- **KTU-favorite numerical pattern:** Given $M$, $F_t$, $f_r$, $C_d$, $A$, $V$ → compute $a$ and $\theta$ (or $F_{db}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Maximum Acceleration, Braking Torque, Braking Force, Brake Proportioning, Braking Efficiency, Stopping Distance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Maximum Acceleration, Braking Torque, Braking Force, Brake Proportioning, Braking Efficiency, Stopping Distance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maximum Acceleration

### Formal Definition
**Maximum Acceleration** of a vehicle is the highest achievable longitudinal acceleration under given operating conditions. It is fundamentally constrained by two limits: the **engine power limit** (tractive effort available) and the **tire-road friction limit** (maximum force that can be transmitted through the contact patch). The vehicle can only accelerate as fast as the *smaller* of these two limits allows.

$$a_{max} = \frac{F_t - (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g)}{m}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Tractive force (either from engine or limited by $\mu \cdot W_d$)
- $F_{rr}$ = Rolling resistance force
- $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic drag force
- $F_g$ = Grade resistance force
- $m$ = Vehicle mass
- $W_d$ = Weight on driven wheels
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of adhesion between tire and road

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to derive $a_{max}$ using the free-body diagram approach (Newton's second law) and clearly distinguish between the *power-limited* regime and the *friction-limited* regime.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pushing a heavy wooden block on a rough floor. No matter how hard you push, the block will not slide faster than a certain speed — that is the friction limit. Similarly, a car cannot accelerate harder than what the tires can "grab" from the road. The tires are like rubber fingers trying to grip the asphalt — if they slip, no more acceleration is possible, no matter how powerful the engine.

---

## 1.2 Braking Torque

### Formal Definition
**Braking Torque** ($T_b$) is the frictional torque generated at the brake interface (disc or drum) when the brake mechanism is actuated. It is the rotational equivalent of the braking force at the wheel center and is the primary input that decelerates the wheel.

$$T_b = F_{c} \cdot \mu_{b} \cdot r_{eff}$$

Where:
- $F_c$ = Actuation force (hydraulic/electric from caliper or shoe)
- $\mu_b$ = Brake friction coefficient (between pad/linings and rotor/drum)
- $r_{eff}$ = Effective brake radius

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The braking torque is converted to a **tangential braking force at the tire contact patch** via $F_b = T_b / r_w$, where $r_w$ is the wheel radius. This is the force that actually decelerates the vehicle.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a brake disc like a giant clamp grabbing a spinning plate (the rotor). The harder the clamp squeezes, and the grippier the clamp surface, the more torque resists the rotation. That torque, multiplied by the wheel radius, becomes the deceleration force at the ground.

---

## 1.3 Braking Force

### Formal Definition
**Braking Force** ($F_b$) is the total longitudinal retarding force at the tire-road interface, generated as a reaction to the braking torque applied at the wheel. For a vehicle with front and rear brakes:

$$F_{b,total} = F_{bf} + F_{br} = \frac{T_{bf}}{r_w} + \frac{T_{br}}{r_w}$$

This force is responsible for decelerating the vehicle mass and overcoming inertial effects:

$$F_{b,total} = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point**: The braking force cannot exceed $\mu \cdot W$ (friction limit). Beyond this, the wheel **locks up** and slides, leading to skidding and loss of lateral stability — hence ABS.

---

## 1.4 Brake Proportioning

### Formal Definition
**Brake Proportioning** refers to the deliberate distribution of total braking force between the **front axle ($F_{bf}$)** and the **rear axle ($F_{br}$)** to optimize stopping performance while maintaining directional stability. The objective is to keep the front-rear braking force ratio as close as possible to the **Ideal Braking Force Distribution Curve (I-curve)**.

$$F_{bf} + F_{br} = F_{b,total}$$

The **Ideal Proportion** satisfies the condition that **both front and rear wheels are at the same utilization ratio** of their adhesion potential:

$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = z$$

Where $z$ is the deceleration in units of $g$.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two people pulling back on a sled with ropes — one at the front and one at the back. If the front-puller pulls too hard, the sled nose-dives (front wheel lock); if the rear-puller pulls too hard, the sled spins (rear-wheel lock). The ideal is when both pull in exactly the right ratio to match the load they are responsible for.

---

## 1.5 Braking Efficiency

### Formal Definition
**Braking Efficiency** ($\eta_b$) is a dimensionless measure of how effectively the brake system utilizes the available tire-road friction. It compares the actual deceleration achieved to the theoretical maximum deceleration (the friction limit).

$$\eta_b = \frac{a_{actual}}{a_{max,possible}} = \frac{a_{actual}}{\mu \cdot g}$$

Or equivalently, expressed in terms of forces:

$$\eta_b = \frac{F_{b,actual}}{F_{b,max}} = \frac{F_{b,actual}}{\mu \cdot W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Braking efficiency = 100% means the vehicle is using the full available friction (any more would lock the wheels). Efficiency < 100% means under-utilized friction (poor brake balance or insufficient brake torque).

---

## 1.6 Stopping Distance

### Formal Definition
**Stopping Distance** ($s$) is the total distance traveled by a vehicle from the instant the driver perceives a need to stop until the vehicle comes to a complete halt. It has two components:

1. **Perception-Reaction Distance** ($s_{pr}$): Distance covered during the driver's reaction time.
2. **Braking Distance** ($s_b$): Distance covered during actual braking.

$$s = s_{pr} + s_b = V \cdot t_r + \frac{V^2}{2 \cdot a_{decel}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Initial vehicle speed
- $t_r$ = Perception-reaction time (typically **1.5 to 2.5 seconds**)
- $a_{decel}$ = Average deceleration during braking

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Formula (SI units)**: When $V$ is in m/s and $a$ in m/s²:
> $$s_b = \frac{V^2}{2 \cdot a}$$
> When $V$ is in km/h and $\mu$ is used directly:
> $$s_b \approx \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot \mu}$$ (where 254 comes from $\frac{1}{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1000/3600)^2}$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free-Body Diagram of an Accelerating Vehicle

For a rear-wheel-drive (RWD) vehicle accelerating on a flat road:

### Forces Acting on Vehicle (Longitudinal Direction):
1. **Tractive Force at driven wheels**: $F_t$ — pushes vehicle forward (acts at contact patch)
2. **Rolling Resistance**: $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot W$ — opposes motion
3. **Aerodynamic Drag**: $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ — opposes motion
4. **Grade Resistance**: $F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot \theta$ (for small angles) — opposes or assists motion depending on slope direction
5. **Inertial Force**: $F_i = m \cdot a$ — fictitious force in accelerating reference frame

### Newton's Second Law (Accelerating on Level Ground):
$$F_t = m \cdot a + F_{rr} + F_{ad}$$

### Maximum Acceleration — Two Cases:

**Case 1: Friction-Limited Acceleration** (when engine power is sufficient but road adhesion is the limit)
$$a_{max} = \frac{\mu \cdot W_d - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m}$$

**Case 2: Power-Limited Acceleration** (when road adhesion is sufficient but engine cannot deliver more force)
$$a_{max} = \frac{\eta_t \cdot P_{engine}/V - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m}$$

Where:
- $\eta_t$ = Drivetrain efficiency
- $P_{engine}$ = Engine power output
- $V$ = Vehicle velocity

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: At low speeds, friction is the limit (Case 1). At high speeds, power becomes the limit (Case 2). The transition occurs at a characteristic speed.

---

## 2.2 Free-Body Diagram of a Braking Vehicle

### Forces on a Braking Vehicle (Level Ground, Forward Direction Positive):
1. **Inertial force** $m \cdot a$ acting forward (tendency to continue moving)
2. **Braking force at front**: $F_{bf}$ acting backward
3. **Braking force at rear**: $F_{br}$ acting backward
4. **Rolling resistance**: $F_{rr}$ (small, often neglected)
5. **Aerodynamic drag**: $F_{ad}$ (adds to braking, usually included in $\mu$ for conservative design)

### Dynamic Weight Transfer During Braking:

When a vehicle decelerates with deceleration $a$, an inertial load transfers from rear to front:

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

Where:
- $h_g$ = Height of center of gravity
- $L$ = Wheelbase

**New vertical loads:**
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$
$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a_f}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

Where:
- $b$ = Horizontal distance from CG to front axle
- $a_f$ (wheelbase term) = Horizontal distance from CG to rear axle
- $L = b + a_f$

---

## 2.3 Ideal Braking Force Distribution (I-curve)

### Derivation of the I-Curve:
For *ideal* braking, both front and rear tires must reach the limit of adhesion simultaneously. This means:

$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \frac{a}{g}$$

Substituting the dynamic load expressions:

$$\frac{F_{bf}}{\left(\frac{W \cdot b}{L} + \frac{W \cdot a \cdot h_g}{g \cdot L}\right)} = \frac{F_{br}}{\left(\frac{W \cdot a_f}{L} - \frac{W \cdot a \cdot h_g}{g \cdot L}\right)} = \frac{a}{g}$$

Solving for $F_{bf}$ in terms of $a$ (and noting $F_{bf} + F_{br} = m \cdot a$):

$$\boxed{F_{bf} = \frac{W}{L} \left(b + \frac{a \cdot h_g}{g}\right) \cdot \frac{a}{g} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{simplified}}}$$

A more elegant form (front and rear separately):

$$F_{bf,ideal} = \frac{W \cdot (b + a \cdot h_g / g)}{L} \cdot \frac{a}{g}$$

$$F_{br,ideal} = \frac{W \cdot (a_f - a \cdot h_g / g)}{L} \cdot \frac{a}{g}$$

When $a = 0$ (static): $F_{bf,ideal} = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}$ (matches static load on front).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**: The I-curve is a *parabola* in the $F_{bf}$ vs $F_{br}$ plane. Real vehicles use a *fixed proportioning valve* (linear split), so they deviate from the ideal. Modern vehicles use **EBD (Electronic Brake Distribution)** to dynamically follow the I-curve.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Max. Friction-Limited Acceleration | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ (neglecting resistance) | $\mu$ = adhesion coefficient, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | Tractive Force Limit | $F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot W_d$ | $W_d$ = Weight on driven wheels (N) |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot W$ | $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient (0.01–0.02) |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_d$ = drag coeff., $A$ = frontal area (m²), $V$ = velocity (m/s) |
| 5 | Grade Resistance | $F_g = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = road slope angle |
| 6 | Dynamic Weight Transfer | $\Delta W = m \cdot a \cdot h_g / L$ | $h_g$ = CG height (m), $L$ = wheelbase (m) |
| 7 | Braking Force on Front Tire | $F_{bf} = T_{bf} / r_w$ | $T_{bf}$ = front brake torque (N·m), $r_w$ = wheel radius (m) |
| 8 | Braking Force on Rear Tire | $F_{br} = T_{br} / r_w$ | $T_{br}$ = rear brake torque (N·m) |
| 9 | Deceleration | $a_{decel} = (F_{bf} + F_{br}) / m$ | $m$ = vehicle mass (kg) |
| 10 | Ideal Front Braking Force | $F_{bf,ideal} = (W \cdot (b + a h_g/g)) / L \cdot (a/g)$ | $b$ = CG to front axle (m) |
| 11 | Ideal Rear Braking Force | $F_{br,ideal} = (W \cdot (a_f - a h_g/g)) / L \cdot (a/g)$ | $a_f$ = CG to rear axle (m) |
| 12 | Braking Efficiency | $\eta_b = a_{actual} / (\mu \cdot g)$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Braking Distance (SI) | $s_b = V^2 / (2 a)$ | $V$ = m/s, $a$ = m/s², $s_b$ = m |
| 14 | Stopping Distance | $s = V \cdot t_r + V^2/(2a)$ | $t_r$ = reaction time (s) |
| 15 | Stopping Distance (km/h, μ) | $s \approx V^2 / (254 \mu)$ | $V$ = km/h, $\mu$ = tire-road friction |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes used in table cells to prevent markdown parser breakage** — the brake proportioning equation uses fraction notation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where This Knowledge Is Used |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| **ABS Tuning** | Engineers use the I-curve to program ABS threshold pressures |
| **EBD Systems** | Electronic brake distribution approximates the I-curve in real-time |
| **Brake Bias Setup (Motorsport)** | Race engineers adjust front/rear brake bias to follow I-curve for given track conditions |
| **NCAP Crash Ratings** | Stopping distance is a key metric in Euro NCAP and Bharat NCAP testing |
| **EV Regenerative Braking** | Regen torque must be coordinated with friction braking to follow ideal proportioning |
| **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** | Algorithm must compute stopping distance in real-time for collision avoidance |

---

## 2.6 Step-by-Step Logic of Braking Process

1. **Driver Input**: Pedal force → master cylinder pressure $P_m$
2. **Hydraulic Transmission**: Pressure amplified/lost through brake lines → wheel cylinder pressure $P_w$
3. **Actuation Force**: $F_c = P_w \cdot A_c$ (where $A_c$ is caliper piston area)
4. **Frictional Engagement**: Brake pads clamp rotor with friction force $\mu_b \cdot F_c$
5. **Braking Torque**: $T_b = \mu_b \cdot F_c \cdot r_{eff}$ (for disc brake)
6. **Braking Force at Tire**: $F_b = T_b / r_w$
7. **Vehicle Deceleration**: $a = F_b / m$ (Newton's second law)
8. **Lock-up Check**: If $F_b > \mu \cdot W$ → wheel locks → skid → ABS activates
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Acceleration on Level Road

### Setup:
Consider a rear-wheel-drive vehicle of mass $m$ on a flat road, accelerating from rest. Let $W_d$ = weight on driven wheels = $W \cdot (b/L)$ (where $b$ is CG-to-front distance, $L$ is wheelbase, $W = mg$).

### Free-Body Equation (Longitudinal):

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = m \cdot a$$

### Friction Limit on Tractive Force:
The maximum force the tires can transmit without slipping is:

$$F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot W_d = \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{b}{L}$$

### Case 1A: Engine Capable of Saturating Friction (Low Speed)

When the engine produces enough torque to spin the wheels (no slip), the tractive force is bounded by friction. Substituting the maximum:

$$\mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{b}{L} - f_r \cdot W - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2 = m \cdot a_{max}$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\mu \cdot g \cdot \frac{b}{L} - f_r \cdot g - \frac{\rho C_d A V^2}{2m} = a_{max}$$

$$\boxed{a_{max} = g \left[\mu \cdot \frac{b}{L} - f_r\right] - \frac{\rho C_d A V^2}{2m}}$$

### Case 1B: Aerodynamic Drag Negligible (Conservative Approximation)

If we neglect $F_{ad}$ (valid for low-speed analysis):

$$a_{max} \approx g \left(\mu \cdot \frac{b}{L} - f_r\right)$$

### Worked Example:
Given: $m = 1500$ kg, $L = 2.5$ m, $b = 1.2$ m, $\mu = 0.8$, $f_r = 0.015$.

Step 1: Compute $W_d$ fraction:
$$\frac{b}{L} = \frac{1.2}{2.5} = 0.48$$

Step 2: Apply friction-limited acceleration formula:
$$a_{max} = 9.81 \times (0.8 \times 0.48 - 0.015)$$
$$a_{max} = 9.81 \times (0.384 - 0.015) = 9.81 \times 0.369$$
$$\boxed{a_{max} \approx 3.62 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.37g}$$

Step 3: Convert to time to reach 100 km/h (27.78 m/s):
$$t = \frac{V}{a_{max}} = \frac{27.78}{3.62} \approx 7.67 \text{ seconds}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Braking Deceleration (General Case)

### Setup:
A vehicle of mass $m$ decelerates with deceleration $a$. Brake forces $F_{bf}$ and $F_{br}$ act at front and rear contact patches. Aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance contribute positively to deceleration but are conservatively neglected in basic KTU problems.

### Longitudinal Equilibrium (Braking):

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$

Taking forward as positive and noting braking force acts backward:

$$-(F_{bf} + F_{br}) = -m \cdot a_{decel}$$

Or, expressing deceleration magnitude:

$$\boxed{a_{decel} = \frac{F_{bf} + F_{br}}{m}}$$

### Including Resistance Forces (Drag and Rolling):

In a more realistic scenario where aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance also assist deceleration:

$$F_{bf} + F_{br} + F_{rr} + F_{ad} = m \cdot a_{decel}$$

### Static Load Distribution (Before Braking):

Static weight on each axle (when $a = 0$):

$$W_{f,static} = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}, \quad W_{r,static} = \frac{W \cdot a_f}{L}$$

### Dynamic Load During Braking:

Applying moment equilibrium about the rear contact patch:

$$W_f \cdot L = W \cdot b - m \cdot a \cdot h_g$$

Solving for $W_f$:

$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b - m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L} = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

Wait — this gives a *reduction* in front load during braking? Let me re-derive carefully. The sign convention: when braking, inertia acts *forward* on the CG, creating a moment that **increases** load on the front wheels.

**Corrected derivation:**

Taking moments about the rear contact patch (counterclockwise positive):

$$-W_f \cdot L + W \cdot b + m \cdot a \cdot h_g = 0$$

(Here $m \cdot a \cdot h_g$ is the moment from the inertial force, which acts forward at the CG)

$$W_f \cdot L = W \cdot b + m \cdot a \cdot h_g$$

$$\boxed{W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}}$$

$$\boxed{W_r = W - W_f = \frac{W \cdot a_f}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This confirms the **dynamic weight transfer to the front** during braking. As $a$ increases, more load moves to the front axle — meaning front brakes have higher friction capacity available.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ideal Brake Proportioning (I-Curve)

### Condition for Ideal Braking:
Both axles reach the friction limit simultaneously. This means:

$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \frac{a_{decel}}{g}$$

### Solving for $F_{bf}$ in Terms of $a$:

From $F_{bf} = W_f \cdot a/g$, substitute the dynamic load expression:

$$F_{bf} = \frac{a}{g} \left[ \frac{W \cdot b}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L} \right]$$

$$F_{bf} = \frac{W \cdot b \cdot a}{g \cdot L} + \frac{m \cdot a^2 \cdot h_g}{g \cdot L}$$

Since $W = m \cdot g$:

$$F_{bf} = \frac{m \cdot b \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a^2 \cdot h_g}{g \cdot L}$$

$$\boxed{F_{bf,ideal} = \frac{m}{L}\left(b \cdot a + \frac{a^2 \cdot h_g}{g}\right)}$$

Similarly for the rear:

$$\boxed{F_{br,ideal} = \frac{m}{L}\left(a_f \cdot a - \frac{a^2 \cdot h_g}{g}\right)}$$

### Parabolic Form:
The I-curve in the $F_{bf}$ vs $F_{br}$ plane (or vs $a$) is a **parabola**:

$$F_{bf} = \frac{m \cdot b \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot h_g}{g \cdot L} \cdot a^2$$

This is of the form $y = c_1 x + c_2 x^2$ — a parabola opening in the direction of the second term's sign.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Braking Efficiency

### Definition Setup:
Braking efficiency compares actual deceleration to the theoretical maximum. The maximum deceleration possible is when the total available friction $\mu \cdot W$ is fully used:

$$a_{max,theoretical} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{m} = \mu \cdot g$$

### Actual Deceleration:
$$a_{actual} = \frac{F_{bf,actual} + F_{br,actual}}{m}$$

### Efficiency Formula:
$$\boxed{\eta_b = \frac{a_{actual}}{a_{max,theoretical}} = \frac{F_{bf,actual} + F_{br,actual}}{\mu \cdot W}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

**Given:**
- $m = 1200$ kg, $W = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772$ N
- $L = 2.4$ m, $b = 1.1$ m, $a_f = 1.3$ m, $h_g = 0.55$ m
- $\mu = 0.7$
- Actual brake forces: $F_{bf} = 5500$ N, $F_{br} = 2500$ N

**Step 1: Total actual braking force**
$$F_{b,total} = 5500 + 2500 = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Actual deceleration**
$$a_{actual} = \frac{8000}{1200} = 6.667 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Maximum theoretical deceleration**
$$a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4: Braking efficiency**
$$\eta_b = \frac{6.667}{6.867} = 0.9708 \approx 97.1\%$$

**Step 5: Identify wheel lock-up risk**
Compute front utilization:
$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{5500}{\frac{11772 \times 1.1}{2.4} + \frac{1200 \times 6.667 \times 0.55}{2.4}} = \frac{5500}{5395.5 + 1833.4} = \frac{5500}{7228.9} \approx 0.761$$

Since $0.761 > \mu = 0.7$ → **Front wheels would lock** under this brake distribution.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Stopping Distance

### Step 1: Kinematic relation for constant deceleration

From $V_f^2 = V_i^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot s$, with $V_f = 0$:

$$0 = V^2 - 2 a s$$

Solving for $s$:

$$\boxed{s_b = \frac{V^2}{2 a}}$$

### Step 2: Adding perception-reaction distance

During reaction time $t_r$, the vehicle travels at constant velocity:

$$s_{pr} = V \cdot t_r$$

### Step 3: Total stopping distance

$$\boxed{s_{total} = V \cdot t_r + \frac{V^2}{2 a}}$$

### Step 4: When friction coefficient $\mu$ is given (and reaction time neglected)

If $a = \mu \cdot g$ (ideal braking with $\eta_b = 1$):

$$s_b = \frac{V^2}{2 \mu g}$$

### Step 5: Unit conversion for km/h

Convert $V$ from km/h to m/s: $V_{m/s} = V_{km/h} / 3.6$

$$s_b = \frac{(V/3.6)^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{V^2}{25.92 \cdot \mu g}$$

With $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$s_b = \frac{V^2}{25.92 \times 9.81 \times \mu} = \frac{V^2}{254.27 \cdot \mu} \approx \frac{V^2}{254 \mu}$$

### Worked Stopping Distance Example:

**Given:** $V = 90$ km/h, $\mu = 0.7$, $t_r = 2.5$ s

**Step 1: Convert speed**
$$V = 90/3.6 = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Deceleration**
$$a = \mu g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Reaction distance**
$$s_{pr} = 25 \times 2.5 = 62.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Braking distance**
$$s_b = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 6.867} = \frac{625}{13.734} = 45.51 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Total stopping distance**
$$\boxed{s_{total} = 62.5 + 45.51 \approx 108.0 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — How Topics Connect

| Concept | Inputs Required | Outputs Produced | Connects To |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|-------------|
| Max Acceleration | $\mu$, $W_d$, $f_r$, $C_d A$, $V$ | $a_{max}$, $F_t$ | Tractive effort curve, gradeability |
| Brake Torque | $F_c$, $\mu_b$, $r_{eff}$ | $T_b$ | Braking force, deceleration |
| Braking Force | $T_b$, $r_w$ | $F_b$ | Total deceleration, wheel slip |
| Brake Proportioning | $W_f$, $W_r$, $a$, $h_g$, $L$ | $F_{bf}$, $F_{br}$ | Stability, I-curve deviation |
| Braking Efficiency | $a_{actual}$, $\mu$, $g$ | $\eta_b$ | ABS tuning, brake design |
| Stopping Distance | $V$, $a$, $t_r$ | $s$ | Safety regulations, AEB systems |

---

## 3.7 Edge Case: Rear-Wheel-Only Braking Limit

If a vehicle brakes *only* on the rear wheels (hypothetical extreme):

The friction limit on rear gives maximum deceleration:
$$a_{max,rear-only} = \frac{\mu \cdot W_r}{m} = \mu \cdot g \cdot \frac{a_f}{L}$$

For a typical sedan with $a_f/L \approx 0.45$:
$$a_{max,rear-only} = 0.45 \times \mu g \approx 0.45 \times 0.8 \times 9.81 \approx 3.5 \text{ m/s}^2$$

This is why **brake proportioning heavily favors the front** — front brakes must provide ~60–70% of total braking force on a typical passenger car.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define braking efficiency. A vehicle of mass 1200 kg is braked from 80 km/h to rest in 50 m on a level road. Calculate the braking efficiency, given $\mu = 0.7$.**

**Model Answer:**

Braking efficiency is the ratio of actual deceleration achieved to the maximum possible deceleration (friction-limited):

$$\eta_b = \frac{a_{actual}}{\mu \cdot g}$$

**Step 1: Compute actual deceleration**
Using $V_f^2 = V_i^2 - 2 a s$:
$$V = 80/3.6 = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$
$$0 = 22.22^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot 50$$
$$a = \frac{22.22^2}{100} = \frac{493.83}{100} = 4.94 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2: Maximum theoretical deceleration**
$$a_{max} = \mu g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Braking efficiency**
$$\eta_b = \frac{4.94}{6.867} = 0.7194 \approx 71.9\%$$

---

### Question A2: Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the ideal brake proportioning? Why is the actual brake force distribution in passenger cars biased towards the front wheels?**

**Model Answer:**

**Ideal Brake Proportioning** is the distribution of braking force between front and rear axles such that both axles reach the limit of tire-road adhesion simultaneously. Mathematically:

$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \frac{a}{g}$$

**Why front bias?** During braking, dynamic weight transfer shifts load from the rear to the front axle (because inertia acts forward at the CG, creating a nose-down moment). The front axle thus has:
1. **Higher vertical load** → higher friction capacity ($\mu \cdot W_f$ is greater)
2. **Greater steering control** → must maintain lateral grip for stability
3. **Higher energy absorption** → typically 60–70% of total braking energy is dissipated at the front

Therefore, brake systems are designed with a **front bias** of about 60:40 to 70:30 to follow the I-curve approximately and maintain stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A passenger car has the following specifications:**
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m
- Distance from CG to front axle $b = 1.2$ m
- Height of CG $h_g = 0.6$ m
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
- Tire-road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.75$

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Determine the **maximum deceleration** possible during braking without locking any wheel, assuming ideal brake proportioning. Also calculate the dynamic weight on each axle at this deceleration. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If the actual brake proportioning is fixed at $F_{bf}/F_{br} = 3:1$ and the total braking force is 9000 N, calculate the **braking efficiency** and the **stopping distance from 90 km/h** (neglecting reaction time). Identify which axle locks first. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Static load distribution**
$$W = m g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{f,static} = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} = \frac{14715 \times 1.2}{2.6} = 6791.5 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{r,static} = \frac{W \cdot a_f}{L} = \frac{14715 \times 1.4}{2.6} = 7923.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Maximum deceleration under ideal proportioning**
The maximum deceleration without locking is determined by the front-rear adhesion limit simultaneously. For ideal proportioning, both axles reach $\mu W$ simultaneously:

$$a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.75 \times 9.81 = 7.36 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Dynamic weight transfer**
$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L} = \frac{1500 \times 7.36 \times 0.6}{2.6} = 2547.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Dynamic axle loads**
$$W_f = W_{f,static} + \Delta W = 6791.5 + 2547.7 = 9339.2 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = W_{r,static} - \Delta W = 7923.5 - 2547.7 = 5375.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Verification**
$$\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \mu \Rightarrow F_{bf} = 0.75 \times 9339.2 = 7004.4 \text{ N}$$
$$\frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \mu \Rightarrow F_{br} = 0.75 \times 5375.8 = 4031.85 \text{ N}$$
$$\text{Total} = 7004.4 + 4031.85 = 11036.25 \text{ N}$$
$$a = 11036.25 / 1500 = 7.357 \approx 7.36 \text{ m/s}^2 \checkmark$$

**Final Answers (a):** $a_{max} = 7.36 \text{ m/s}^2$, $W_f = 9339$ N, $W_r = 5376$ N.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Distribute braking force with 3:1 ratio**
Total = 9000 N, ratio $F_{bf}:F_{br} = 3:1$
$$F_{bf} = \frac{3}{4} \times 9000 = 6750 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{br} = \frac{1}{4} \times 9000 = 2250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute actual deceleration**
$$a_{actual} = \frac{9000}{1500} = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Maximum theoretical deceleration**
$$a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 7.36 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4: Braking efficiency**
$$\eta_b = \frac{a_{actual}}{a_{max}} = \frac{6.0}{7.36} = 0.815 \approx 81.5\%$$

**Step 5: Stopping distance from 90 km/h**
$$V = 90/3.6 = 25 \text{ m/s}$$
$$s_b = \frac{V^2}{2 a_{actual}} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 6.0} = \frac{625}{12} = 52.08 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Identify first-locking axle**
Dynamic loads at $a = 6.0$ m/s²:
$$\Delta W = \frac{1500 \times 6.0 \times 0.6}{2.6} = 2076.9 \text{ N}$$
$$W_f = 6791.5 + 2076.9 = 8868.4 \text{ N}$$
$$W_r = 7923.5 - 2076.9 = 5846.6 \text{ N}$$

Front utilization: $\mu_{f,used} = \frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{6750}{8868.4} = 0.761$
Rear utilization: $\mu_{r,used} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \frac{2250}{5846.6} = 0.385$

Since front utilization (0.761) > $\mu = 0.75$, the **FRONT wheels lock first**.

**Final Answers (b):** $\eta_b = 81.5\%$, $s_b = 52.08$ m, **Front wheels lock first**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A sports car weighing 18 kN with the following parameters is analyzed:**
- Wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m
- Distance from CG to rear axle $a_f = 1.25$ m
- CG height $h_g = 0.5$ m
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Engine maximum torque $T_e = 350$ N·m at 3000 rpm
- First gear ratio $i_g = 3.5$, Final drive $i_0 = 4.1$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.9$
- Tire-road friction $\mu = 0.85$

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Compute the **maximum acceleration** the vehicle can achieve in first gear on a level road. State whether it is power-limited or friction-limited. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If the driver brakes from 100 km/h to a complete stop with a constant deceleration of 0.6g, calculate the **total stopping distance** including a 2-second reaction time. Also compute the **braking torque at each wheel** if 65% of braking is done by the front axle and the vehicle has four equal wheels. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Vehicle mass and weight**
$$m = \frac{W}{g} = \frac{18000}{9.81} = 1834.86 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Friction-limited tractive force (RWD, weight on rear wheels)**
$$W_d = W \cdot \frac{b}{L} = 18000 \cdot \frac{(2.5 - 1.25)}{2.5} = 18000 \times 0.5 = 9000 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{t,max,friction} = \mu \cdot W_d = 0.85 \times 9000 = 7650 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Power-limited tractive force at wheel**
$$T_w = T_e \times i_g \times i_0 \times \eta_t = 350 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.9 = 4520.25 \text{ N·m}$$
$$F_{t,max,power} = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{4520.25}{0.33} = 13697.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Comparison**
$$F_{t,max,friction} = 7650 \text{ N} < F_{t,max,power} = 13697.7 \text{ N}$$

Friction is the limiting factor. **The vehicle is friction-limited.**

**Step 5: Neglecting resistance forces for simplicity**
$$a_{max} = \frac{F_{t,max,friction}}{m} = \frac{7650}{1834.86} = 4.17 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a_{max} \approx 4.17 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (friction-limited)}}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Convert speed and compute deceleration**
$$V = 100 \text{ km/h} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$$
$$a = 0.6g = 0.6 \times 9.81 = 5.886 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2: Reaction distance**
$$s_{pr} = V \times t_r = 27.78 \times 2.0 = 55.56 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Braking distance**
$$s_b = \frac{V^2}{2 a} = \frac{27.78^2}{2 \times 5.886} = \frac{771.73}{11.772} = 65.56 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Total stopping distance**
$$\boxed{s_{total} = 55.56 + 65.56 = 121.12 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Total braking force**
$$F_{b,total} = m \times a = 1834.86 \times 5.886 = 10799.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Front and rear brake forces**
$$F_{bf} = 0.65 \times 10799.9 = 7019.9 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{br} = 0.35 \times 10799.9 = 3780.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Force per wheel (4 equal wheels)**
$$F_{bf,per\,wheel} = \frac{7019.9}{2} = 3510.0 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{br,per\,wheel} = \frac{3780.0}{2} = 1890.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Braking torque per wheel**
$$T_{bf,per\,wheel} = F_{bf,per\,wheel} \times r_w = 3510.0 \times 0.33 = 1158.3 \text{ N·m}$$
$$T_{br,per\,wheel} = F_{br,per\,wheel} \times r_w = 1890.0 \times 0.33 = 623.7 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{bf,per\,wheel} = 1158.3 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{br,per\,wheel} = 623.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Students forget to convert km/h to m/s in stopping distance formulas. KTU strictly deducts 0.5–1 mark per unit error.
>
> 2. **Static vs Dynamic Load Confusion**: In braking problems, ALWAYS compute dynamic loads using the actual deceleration, NOT the static loads. Using static loads when computing friction limits is a **fatal conceptual error**.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors**: Forgetting that braking force and inertia force are in opposite directions. A common mistake is writing $F_b = m \cdot a$ (sign issue) — should be $F_b = m \cdot |a_{deceleration}|$.
>
> 4. **I-curve vs Fixed Proportioning**: When asked "is the brake distribution ideal?", students often answer yes/no without computing the utilization ratio $F/W$ at each axle.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Reaction Time**: KTU questions often explicitly say "include reaction time" or "neglect reaction time" — read carefully. Default is to **include** $t_r = 2.5$ s if not stated.
>
> 6. **Lock-up Direction**: When asked "which wheel locks first?", students sometimes write "rear" without showing the calculation. ALWAYS compute $\mu_{used} = F/W$ for both axles and compare with $\mu$.
>
> 7. **Formula for I-curve**: Some students write the I-curve equation incorrectly. The correct form involves a **parabolic** relationship, not linear.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary

- **Maximum acceleration** is limited by **$\mu \cdot W_d$** (friction) or **$\eta_t P/V$** (power), whichever is smaller.
- **Rolling resistance** $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot W$ with $f_r \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ for car tires.
- **Aerodynamic drag** $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A V^2$ becomes dominant at high speeds.
- **Dynamic weight transfer during braking** shifts load forward: $\Delta W = m a h_g / L$.
- **Braking force** $F_b = T_b / r_w$ at the tire contact patch.
- **Ideal brake proportioning (I-curve)** requires $\frac{F_{bf}}{W_f} = \frac{F_{br}}{W_r} = \frac{a}{g}$.
- **I-curve is a parabola**: $F_{bf} = \frac{m b a}{L} + \frac{m h_g a^2}{g L}$.
- **Real vehicles have front bias** of 60:40 to 70:30 to maintain stability.
- **Braking efficiency** $\eta_b = a_{actual} / (\mu g)$; a value of 1.0 means maximum friction utilization.
- **Wheel lock-up** occurs when $F_b > \mu W$ at that axle — ABS prevents this.
- **Stopping distance** $s = V t_r + V^2 / (2 a)$; typical $t_r = 1.5$ to $2.5$ s.
- **Braking distance in km/h and μ**: $s_b \approx V^2 / (254 \mu)$ — handy quick formula.

### Key Numerical Values to Remember

| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Air density $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Typical reaction time $t_r$ | **2.5 s** (design standard) |
| Typical car $\mu$ (dry asphalt) | **0.7 to 0.9** |
| Typical car $\mu$ (wet asphalt) | **0.4 to 0.6** |
| Typical car $\mu$ (ice) | **0.1 to 0.2** |
| Typical $C_d A$ (sedan) | **0.6 to 0.8 m²** |
| Conversion factor | **254** (for $V^2/254\mu$ formula) |

### Final Exam Tips

- Always draw a **clear free-body diagram** before starting any calculation.
- Label all forces with direction arrows (forward/backward).
- State assumptions explicitly (level road, neglect drag, etc.).
- In multi-part questions, **carry forward** computed values — don't recompute and risk arithmetic errors.
- Final answers should include **units**.
- For 14-mark questions, show **all intermediate steps** — partial marks depend on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Distribution (Three Wheeled and Four Wheeled Vehicles)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Distribution (Three Wheeled and Four Wheeled Vehicles)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Load Distribution** in a vehicle refers to the static and dynamic apportionment of the total vehicle weight (curb weight + payload) among the contact patches of the tires with the road surface. It is governed by the spatial location of the **Centre of Gravity (CG)** relative to the wheelbase and track width, and is dynamically altered by longitudinal acceleration/deceleration and lateral acceleration during cornering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Load distribution is the ratio of the normal reaction forces (load) carried by each axle (or each wheel) of the vehicle, expressed as a function of vehicle weight, CG coordinates, and the applied inertial accelerations.

## 1.2 Classification of Vehicles Considered

| Vehicle Class | Front Axle | Rear Axle | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type A** (Auto-rickshaw style) | **1 wheel** | **2 wheels** | Bajaj RE, Piaggio Ape |
| **Type B** (Scooter with sidecar style) | **2 wheels** | **1 wheel** | Morgan 3-Wheeler, vintage cars |
| **Type C** (Standard Car) | **2 wheels** | **2 wheels** | All passenger cars, SUVs, trucks |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Seesaw on a Boat":**
> Imagine a small wooden boat floating on still water. You and your friend sit at opposite ends. The boat tilts so that the heavier person sits lower. The "down-force" on each end of the boat is essentially the **load distribution**.
> - If your friend (heavier) sits closer to the middle, the load on that end is enormous, while the far end nearly lifts out of the water.
> - **This is exactly how vehicle load distribution works!** The Centre of Gravity (CG) of the vehicle is the "balance point." The axle closest to the CG carries more weight.
> - Now imagine the boat suddenly turns (cornering) — water rushes to one side, and you feel pushed sideways. The boat tilts **more** on the outside of the turn. This represents **dynamic load transfer**, where inertial forces shift weight from the inner wheels to the outer wheels.

## 1.4 Key Coordinate System

To standardise analysis, every vehicle is assigned a right-handed coordinate system (per ISO 8855 and SAE J670):

- **x-axis:** Longitudinal (positive forward)
- **y-axis:** Lateral (positive to the left of driver)
- **z-axis:** Vertical (positive upward)
- **CG Height (h):** Distance of mass centre from ground — typically **h = 0.45 m to 0.55 m** for a passenger car
- **Wheelbase (L):** Distance between front and rear axles — typically **L = 2.4 m to 3.0 m**
- **Track Width (t):** Distance between left and right wheels on the same axle — typically **t = 1.5 m to 1.6 m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gravitational acceleration g = 9.81 m/s²** is used throughout KTU derivations. Some numerical problems may round this to **g = 10 m/s²** for simplicity — always check the given data in the question paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Load Distribution — Theory

When a vehicle is **stationary on a level road**, the load distribution is purely **static**, determined by the location of the CG. Two perpendicular force balances must be satisfied:

1. **Vertical equilibrium:** Sum of normal reactions = Total weight (W)
2. **Moment equilibrium:** Sum of moments about any point = 0

### 2.1.1 Three-Wheeled Vehicle — Type A (1 front, 2 rear)

Let:
- **W** = Total vehicle weight (N or kgf)
- **a** = Longitudinal distance of CG from **front axle**
- **b** = Longitudinal distance of CG from **rear axle**
- **L = a + b** = wheelbase

Taking moments about the rear axle:
$$W \cdot b = W_f \cdot L$$

Solving for the front (single) wheel normal load:
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}$$

Taking moments about the front axle:
$$W \cdot a = W_r \cdot L$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}$$

Since the rear has **2 wheels**, each rear tire carries:
$$W_{r, \text{each}} = \frac{W_r}{2} = \frac{W \cdot a}{2L}$$

### 2.1.2 Three-Wheeled Vehicle — Type B (2 front, 1 rear)

By symmetry of analysis (swapping a ↔ b):
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} \quad \text{(total on front axle)}$$
$$W_{f, \text{each}} = \frac{W \cdot b}{2L}$$
$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} \quad \text{(total on rear single wheel)}$$

### 2.1.3 Four-Wheeled Vehicle (2 front, 2 rear)

Identical axle loads as the three-wheeler, but each axle load is split equally between two wheels (assuming symmetric lateral CG placement):
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}, \quad W_{f, \text{each}} = \frac{W \cdot b}{2L}$$
$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}, \quad W_{r, \text{each}} = \frac{W \cdot a}{2L}$$

## 2.2 Dynamic Load Distribution — Theory

When the vehicle **accelerates, brakes, or corners**, inertial forces acting at the CG produce **load transfer** between axles and across an axle.

### 2.2.1 Longitudinal Load Transfer (Acceleration/Braking)

When a longitudinal acceleration $a_x$ (positive forward) or deceleration $j$ acts:
- An inertial force $F_i = (W/g) \cdot a_x$ acts at CG, directed **opposite** to acceleration
- This creates a moment about the road contact patches
- **Longitudinal Load Transfer (ΔW_long):**
$$\Delta W_{\text{long}} = \frac{W \cdot a_x \cdot h}{g \cdot L}$$

**Sign convention for KTU problems:**
- **Braking (deceleration):** Weight shifts **forward** → Front axle load increases
- **Acceleration:** Weight shifts **rearward** → Rear axle load increases (in rear-wheel-drive)
- **Braking dynamic front load:** $W_{f, \text{dyn}} = W_f + \Delta W_{\text{long}}$
- **Braking dynamic rear load:** $W_{r, \text{dyn}} = W_r - \Delta W_{\text{long}}$

### 2.2.2 Lateral Load Transfer (Cornering)

When lateral acceleration $a_y$ acts during a turn:
- Inertial force $F_{iy} = (W/g) \cdot a_y$ acts at CG
- **Lateral Load Transfer (ΔW_lat):**
$$\Delta W_{\text{lat}} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{g \cdot t}$$

where **t** = track width of the axle being considered.
- **Outer wheel** (away from turn centre) gains load: $W_{\text{outer}} = W_{\text{static}}/2 + \Delta W_{\text{lat}}/2$
- **Inner wheel** (toward turn centre) loses load: $W_{\text{inner}} = W_{\text{static}}/2 - \Delta W_{\text{lat}}/2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** If lateral load transfer exceeds the static load on the inner wheel, the **inner wheel lifts off the ground** (zero normal load). This condition is called **wheel lift-off** and is a key limit-handling criterion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Front axle static load (3-wheel Type A) | $W_f = W \cdot b/L$ | N | 1 front, 2 rear |
| 2 | Rear axle static load (3-wheel Type A) | $W_r = W \cdot a/L$ | N | 1 front, 2 rear |
| 3 | Front axle static load (4-wheel) | $W_f = W \cdot b/L$ | N | 2 front, 2 rear |
| 4 | Rear axle static load (4-wheel) | $W_r = W \cdot a/L$ | N | 2 front, 2 rear |
| 5 | Load per wheel (4-wheel, sym.) | $W_{\text{wheel}} = W_f/2$ or $W_r/2$ | N | Symmetric CG |
| 6 | Longitudinal load transfer | $\Delta W_{\text{long}} = W \cdot a_x \cdot h / (g \cdot L)$ | N | During braking/accel |
| 7 | Lateral load transfer | $\Delta W_{\text{lat}} = W \cdot a_y \cdot h / (g \cdot t)$ | N | During cornering |
| 8 | Dynamic front axle load (braking) | $W_{f,\text{dyn}} = W_f + \Delta W_{\text{long}}$ | N | $a_x$ = deceleration |
| 9 | Dynamic rear axle load (braking) | $W_{r,\text{dyn}} = W_r - \Delta W_{\text{long}}$ | N | $a_x$ = deceleration |
| 10 | Outer wheel load (cornering) | $W_{\text{outer}} = W/4 + \Delta W_{\text{lat}}/2$ | N | For 4-wheel vehicle |
| 11 | Inner wheel load (cornering) | $W_{\text{inner}} = W/4 - \Delta W_{\text{lat}}/2$ | N | For 4-wheel vehicle |
| 12 | Wheel lift-off condition | $\Delta W_{\text{lat}} \geq W_{\text{static, axle}}$ | N | Inner wheel lifts |
| 13 | Total weight | $W = m \cdot g$ | N | m = total mass (kg) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Load distribution analysis is foundational for:
> - **Suspension design** (spring stiffness tuning)
> - **Brake design** (front-rear brake force proportioning — EBD systems)
> - **Tire sizing** (front tires are typically larger in rear-wheel-drive sports cars)
> - **Stability control (ESC)** algorithms in modern vehicles

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Load on Three-Wheeled Vehicle (Type A: 1 Front, 2 Rear)

### Free Body Setup

Consider a three-wheeled vehicle with one front wheel (at distance **L** ahead of the rear axle) and two rear wheels mounted on a common rear axle. The CG lies at:
- Height **h** above ground
- Longitudinal distance **b** ahead of the rear axle
- Longitudinal distance **a** behind the front axle
- So $L = a + b$

```
       W_f (front single wheel)
            |
            ↓
            ●  ← Front wheel contact
            |
            |---L---|
            |       |
            |       b
            |       |
            ●———————●  CG
            ↑       ↑
         W_r/2    W_r/2  (two rear wheels)
```

### Step 1 — Write Equilibrium Equations

**Vertical Force Equilibrium:**
The sum of vertical forces must equal zero (taking upward as positive, and W acting downward at CG):
$$W_f + W_r - W = 0$$

**Moment Equilibrium about Rear Axle (taking counterclockwise positive):**
The weight W acts at the CG, located at horizontal distance b from the rear axle. The front reaction $W_f$ acts at horizontal distance L from the rear axle.
$$W_f \cdot L - W \cdot b = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Front Wheel Load $W_f$

From the moment equation:
$$W_f \cdot L = W \cdot b$$

$$\boxed{W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Rear Axle Load $W_r$

From the vertical equilibrium equation:
$$W_r = W - W_f = W - \frac{W \cdot b}{L} = W \cdot \frac{L - b}{L}$$

Since $L - b = a$:
$$\boxed{W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Load on Each Rear Wheel

Since the rear axle load is shared by **two wheels**:
$$W_{r, \text{each}} = \frac{W_r}{2} = \frac{W \cdot a}{2L}$$

### Step 5 — Verification

Check: $W_f + W_r = \frac{Wb}{L} + \frac{Wa}{L} = \frac{W(a+b)}{L} = \frac{W \cdot L}{L} = W$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When the CG is closer to the rear axle (b small, a large), $W_f$ becomes small and the front wheel carries less load. When the CG is far back, the rear wheels carry most of the weight. This is why sports cars with rear-mounted engines have huge rear tires — to handle this large $W_r$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Load Transfer During Braking (4-Wheeled Vehicle)

### Free Body Setup

A four-wheeled vehicle of weight **W** is braking with deceleration **j** (m/s²). The CG is at height **h** above the ground and at distance **b** ahead of the rear axle.

### Step 1 — Identify the Inertial Force (D'Alembert's Principle)

By D'Alembert's principle, the vehicle can be analysed as a static system if we apply a pseudo-force at the CG, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the acceleration:
$$F_{\text{inertia}} = m \cdot j = \frac{W \cdot j}{g}$$

This pseudo-force acts **horizontally forward** (since the vehicle decelerates, the inertia "throws" the load forward).

### Step 2 — Identify the Resulting Moment

The inertial force, acting at height **h** above the ground, creates an **overturning moment** about the wheel contact patches:
$$M_{\text{inertia}} = F_{\text{inertia}} \cdot h = \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g}$$

### Step 3 — Decompose the Moment into Axle Load Changes

This overturning moment must be balanced by a **redistribution** of vertical loads between the front and rear axles, separated by wheelbase **L**:
$$\Delta W_f \cdot L = M_{\text{inertia}} = \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g}$$

$$\Delta W_f = \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L}$$

By force equilibrium:
$$\Delta W_r = -\Delta W_f = -\frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L}$$

The negative sign on $W_r$ indicates that the rear axle **loses** an equal amount of load.

### Step 4 — Total Dynamic Axle Loads During Braking

$$\boxed{W_{f,\text{dyn}} = W_f + \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L}}$$

$$\boxed{W_{r,\text{dyn}} = W_r - \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L}}$$

### Step 5 — Full Aligned Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{f,\text{dyn}} &= W_f + \Delta W_f \\[4pt]
