&= \frac{W \cdot b}{L} + \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W}{L} \left( b + \frac{j \cdot h}{g} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{r,\text{dyn}} &= W_r - \Delta W_f \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W \cdot a}{L} - \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W}{L} \left( a - \frac{j \cdot h}{g} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Case — Rear Wheel Lift-off During Braking:** If $j \cdot h / g = a$ (very heavy braking or very rearward CG), then $W_{r,\text{dyn}} = 0$. This means the rear wheels completely lift off the ground — a **forward flip** is imminent. This is exactly why anti-lock braking systems (ABS) and Electronic Stability Control (ESC) exist in modern vehicles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Load Transfer During Cornering (4-Wheeled Vehicle)

### Free Body Setup

The vehicle takes a turn with lateral acceleration $a_y$. The CG is at height **h** above ground, and the track width is **t**.

### Step 1 — Inertial Force in Lateral Direction

The lateral inertial force acting at CG:
$$F_{\text{lat}} = m \cdot a_y = \frac{W \cdot a_y}{g}$$

This force acts **outward** (away from the turn centre).

### Step 2 — Overturning Moment about Roll Axis

For a single axle (front or rear), the roll axis is approximately at the road contact patches. The lateral load transfer on that axle:
$$\Delta W_{\text{lat}} = \frac{F_{\text{lat}} \cdot h}{t} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{g \cdot t}$$

### Step 3 — Distribution Between Inner and Outer Wheels

Each axle load is split equally between left and right wheels, so each wheel's share of load transfer is half:
$$\Delta W_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{\Delta W_{\text{lat}}}{2} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{2 \cdot g \cdot t}$$

### Step 4 — Final Wheel Loads

**Outer Wheel (away from turn centre):**
$$W_{\text{outer}} = \frac{W_{\text{axle}}}{2} + \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{2 \cdot g \cdot t}$$

**Inner Wheel (toward turn centre):**
$$W_{\text{inner}} = \frac{W_{\text{axle}}}{2} - \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{2 \cdot g \cdot t}$$

### Step 5 — Wheel Lift-off Condition

Setting $W_{\text{inner}} = 0$ for the front axle (which is usually the critical axle in lift-off):
$$\frac{W_f}{2} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{2 \cdot g \cdot t}$$

Solving for the critical lateral acceleration:
$$a_{y,\text{crit}} = \frac{W_f \cdot g \cdot t}{W \cdot h} = \frac{b \cdot g \cdot t}{L \cdot h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reducing **h** (lowering CG) and widening **t** (wider track) significantly increases the cornering capability before wheel lift-off. This is exactly why sports cars have very low ride heights and wide tracks — e.g., a Ferrari 488 has $h \approx 0.45$ m and $t \approx 1.67$ m.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A four-wheeled car has total mass **m = 1500 kg**, wheelbase **L = 2.5 m**, track width **t = 1.5 m**, CG height **h = 0.55 m**. The CG is located at **a = 1.0 m** behind the front axle and **b = 1.5 m** ahead of the rear axle. The car brakes with deceleration **j = 5 m/s²** while also cornering with lateral acceleration **a_y = 4 m/s²**. Find:
1. Static loads on front and rear axles.
2. Load transfer due to braking and due to cornering.
3. Dynamic load on each front wheel (left and right).

**Take g = 10 m/s²**

### Solution

**Step 1 — Total weight:**
$$W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 10 = 15000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Static axle loads:**
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} = \frac{15000 \times 1.5}{2.5} = 9000 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{15000 \times 1.0}{2.5} = 6000 \text{ N}$$

Check: $W_f + W_r = 15000$ N ✓

**Step 3 — Longitudinal load transfer (braking):**
$$\Delta W_{\text{long}} = \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L} = \frac{15000 \times 5 \times 0.55}{10 \times 2.5} = \frac{41250}{25} = 1650 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Lateral load transfer (front axle):**
$$\Delta W_{\text{lat, front}} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{g \cdot t} = \frac{15000 \times 4 \times 0.55}{10 \times 1.5} = \frac{33000}{15} = 2200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic load per front wheel:**

Dynamic front axle load (with braking):
$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = W_f + \Delta W_{\text{long}} = 9000 + 1650 = 10650 \text{ N}$$

Each front wheel:
$$\frac{W_{f,\text{dyn}}}{2} = 5325 \text{ N}$$

Outer front wheel (with cornering):
$$W_{f,\text{outer}} = 5325 + \frac{2200}{2} = 5325 + 1100 = 6425 \text{ N}$$

Inner front wheel (with cornering):
$$W_{f,\text{inner}} = 5325 - \frac{2200}{2} = 5325 - 1100 = 4225 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Static $W_f$ | **9000 N** |
| Static $W_r$ | **6000 N** |
| $\Delta W_{\text{long}}$ | **1650 N** |
| $\Delta W_{\text{lat, front}}$ | **2200 N** |
| Front outer wheel | **6425 N** |
| Front inner wheel | **4225 N** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Distribution Analysis Workflow

Since load distribution involves physical free-body diagrams best drawn on paper, the following Mermaid block diagram maps the **analytical procedure** for solving load distribution problems, aligned with the KTU examination problem-solving approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle Geometry and Loading Data] --> B{Identify Vehicle Type}
    B --> B1[Type A: 3-Wheel 1F 2R]
    B --> B2[Type B: 3-Wheel 2F 1R]
    B --> B3[Type C: 4-Wheel Standard]

    A --> C[Compute Total Weight W = m x g]
    C --> D[Locate CG: distance a, b, height h]

    D --> E[Step 1: Static Equilibrium]
    E --> E1[Sum of vertical forces = 0]
    E --> E2[Moment balance about rear axle]
    E1 --> F[Compute Static Axle Loads Wf and Wr]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Split into per-wheel loads]

    G --> H{Driving Condition Specified?}
    H --> H1[No: Static Result is Final]
    H --> H2[Yes: Proceed to Dynamic Analysis]

    H2 --> I[Compute Longitudinal Load Transfer deltaW_long = W x j x h / g x L]
    H2 --> J[Compute Lateral Load Transfer deltaW_lat = W x ay x h / g x t]

    I --> K[Update Axle Loads: Wf dyn = Wf plus deltaW_long]
    J --> L[Update Wheel Loads: Outer plus deltaW_lat slash 2, Inner minus deltaW_lat slash 2]

    K --> M[Check Wheel Lift-off Condition]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Lift-off Detected?}
    N --> N1[No: Report Final Wheel Loads]
    N --> N2[Yes: Flag Instability, Note Critical ay or j]

    N1 --> O[End: Final Load Distribution Output]
    N2 --> O

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Force and Moment Components in Load Distribution

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Total Weight W]
        I2[Wheelbase L]
        I3[Track Width t]
        I4[CG Height h]
        I5[Longitudinal distance a, b]
        I6[Acceleration ax]
        I7[Lateral Acceleration ay]
    end

    subgraph StaticAnalysis
        S1[Vertical Equilibrium]
        S2[Moment Equilibrium]
        S3[Static Axle Loads Wf and Wr]
        S4[Per-Wheel Static Loads]
    end

    subgraph DynamicAnalysis
        D1[Inertial Force Computation]
        D2[Overturning Moment]
        D3[Longitudinal Load Transfer deltaW_long]
        D4[Lateral Load Transfer deltaW_lat]
        D5[Dynamic Axle Loads]
        D6[Dynamic Per-Wheel Loads]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Final Wheel Loads - 4 Wheels]
        O2[Stability Check - Lift-off]
        O3[Brake Force Distribution Reference]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S2
    I5 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4

    I6 --> D1
    I7 --> D1
    I1 --> D1
    I4 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    I2 --> D3
    I3 --> D4
    D3 --> D5
    D4 --> D6
    S3 --> D5
    D5 --> D6

    D6 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    S4 --> O3

    style StaticAnalysis fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style DynamicAnalysis fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style Outputs fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — CG Influence on Load Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CG Far Forward: a large, b small] --> A1[Front Axle carries MORE Load Wf large]
    B[CG Central: a equals b] --> B1[Axle Loads EQUAL Wf equals Wr]
    C[CG Far Rearward: a small, b large] --> C1[Rear Axle carries MORE Load Wr large]

    A1 --> A2[Use: Front-Wheel-Drive Cars, City Buses]
    B1 --> B2[Use: Balanced Sports Cars, Ideal Configuration]
    C1 --> C2[Use: Rear-Engine Cars, Heavy Cargo Trucks]

    style A1 fill:#FFCCCB
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style C1 fill:#FFCCCB
    style B2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always draw the **free-body diagram** with arrows for $W_f$, $W_r$, and $W$. Mark the distances **a, b, L, h, t** explicitly. Examiners award 2-3 marks just for a clean diagram in 14-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Define load distribution in a vehicle. Why is it important for vehicle design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Load distribution refers to the apportionment of the total vehicle weight among the tire contact patches of the front and rear axles (and laterally between left and right wheels). It is determined by the location of the Centre of Gravity (CG) and is critical for vehicle design because it directly affects:
1. **Braking performance** — front-rear brake force proportioning depends on dynamic load distribution during braking.
2. **Steering and handling** — lateral load transfer during cornering influences understeer/oversteer behavior.
3. **Tire wear** — unequal loading causes uneven tire wear.
4. **Suspension design** — spring stiffness is tuned based on per-wheel static and dynamic loads.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: With the help of neat sketches, explain the static load distribution on a three-wheeled vehicle having one front wheel and two rear wheels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a three-wheeled vehicle with one front wheel and two rear wheels, the static load distribution is derived using static equilibrium.

Let W = total weight, a = CG distance from front axle, b = CG distance from rear axle, L = a + b = wheelbase.

Taking moments about the rear axle:
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}$$

Taking moments about the front axle:
$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}$$

Load on each rear wheel:
$$W_{r,\text{each}} = \frac{W \cdot a}{2L}$$

**Diagram (Neat Sketch Required — 1 mark):**
```
        W_f
        ↓
        ●  ← Front single wheel
        |
        |--L--|
        |     |←--b--|
        |     ● CG → W (downward)
        |
        ●———————●  Rear axle
        ↑       ↑
       W_r/2   W_r/2
```

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Load Transfer:** Students often forget that braking shifts load **forward** (so $W_f$ **increases**, $W_r$ **decreases**). Losing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Track Width:** Many students confuse the term "track width" with "wheelbase." Track width **t** is left-right on a single axle; wheelbase **L** is front-rear.
> 3. **Forgetting Half-Factor:** In a 4-wheeled vehicle, each axle load is shared by **2 wheels**. Many students forget to divide by 2 for per-wheel loads.
> 4. **Units Inconsistency:** Use **N** (Newtons) for weight if mass is in kg × g. Mixing kgf and N is a common error.
> 5. **Missing Negative Signs:** In dynamic equations, the rear axle shows **minus** $\Delta W$. Writing only positive values loses 1 mark.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]

**Q: A three-wheeled delivery vehicle has the following specifications:**

- **Total mass** m = 800 kg
- **Wheelbase** L = 2.0 m
- **CG distance from front axle** a = 0.8 m
- **CG distance from rear axle** b = 1.2 m
- **CG height** h = 0.6 m
- **Track width** t = 1.2 m (rear axle only, since single front wheel)
- **Configuration:** 1 front wheel, 2 rear wheels

The vehicle is travelling on a level road and applies brakes with deceleration **j = 4 m/s²**.

**(a)** Determine the **static load distribution** on the front and rear wheels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **dynamic loads** on the front and rear wheels during braking. Also, find the **maximum deceleration** before rear-wheel lift-off occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Take g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Static Load Distribution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Weight:**
$$W = m \cdot g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Front Wheel Static Load** (taking moments about rear axle):
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} = \frac{7848 \times 1.2}{2.0} = \frac{9417.6}{2.0} = 4708.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Rear Axle Static Load** (taking moments about front axle):
$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{7848 \times 0.8}{2.0} = \frac{6278.4}{2.0} = 3139.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Per Rear Wheel Load** (split equally between two wheels):
$$W_{r,\text{each}} = \frac{W_r}{2} = \frac{3139.2}{2} = 1569.6 \text{ N}$$

**Verification:** $W_f + W_r = 4708.8 + 3139.2 = 7848$ N = W ✓

| Wheel | Static Load |
|---|---|
| Front wheel | **4708.8 N** |
| Each rear wheel | **1569.6 N** |

---

**Part (b) — Dynamic Loads and Maximum Deceleration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Load Transfer During Braking:**
$$\Delta W_{\text{long}} = \frac{W \cdot j \cdot h}{g \cdot L} = \frac{7848 \times 4 \times 0.6}{9.81 \times 2.0}$$

$$\Delta W_{\text{long}} = \frac{18835.2}{19.62} = 960 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Front Wheel Load:**
$$W_{f,\text{dyn}} = W_f + \Delta W_{\text{long}} = 4708.8 + 960 = 5668.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Rear Axle Load:**
$$W_{r,\text{dyn}} = W_r - \Delta W_{\text{long}} = 3139.2 - 960 = 2179.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Load on Each Rear Wheel:**
$$W_{r,\text{each,dyn}} = \frac{2179.2}{2} = 1089.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Deceleration Before Rear Lift-off:**

For rear axle lift-off, set $W_{r,\text{dyn}} = 0$:
$$W_r - \Delta W_{\text{long,max}} = 0$$
$$\Delta W_{\text{long,max}} = W_r = 3139.2 \text{ N}$$

$$\frac{W \cdot j_{\text{max}} \cdot h}{g \cdot L} = 3139.2$$

$$j_{\text{max}} = \frac{3139.2 \times g \times L}{W \times h} = \frac{3139.2 \times 9.81 \times 2.0}{7848 \times 0.6}$$

$$j_{\text{max}} = \frac{61597.55}{4708.8} = 13.08 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\Delta W_{\text{long}}$ | **960 N** |
| Dynamic front wheel | **5668.8 N** |
| Dynamic rear wheel (each) | **1089.6 N** |
| $j_{\text{max}}$ (rear lift-off) | **13.08 m/s²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A deceleration of 13.08 m/s² corresponds to about **1.33 g** — this is the theoretical limit. In practice, tire-road friction limits braking to about **0.8–1.0 g** on dry asphalt, so rear lift-off is rarely reached on level roads. However, on slippery surfaces, the friction limit drops below this, and rear lift-off becomes a real risk.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]

**Q: A four-wheeled passenger car has the following data:**

- **Kerb weight** = 1200 kgf
- **Wheelbase** L = 2.4 m
- **Track width** (front and rear, equal) t = 1.4 m
- **CG height** h = 0.5 m
- **CG distance from front axle** a = 1.0 m
- **CG distance from rear axle** b = 1.4 m

**(a)** Calculate the **static load** on each of the four wheels, assuming the CG is on the longitudinal centreline of the car. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The car negotiates a curve of radius **R = 50 m** at a constant speed of **v = 72 km/h**. Determine the **lateral load transfer** on the front and rear axles. Also compute the **load on each wheel** of the front and rear axles, and check whether the **inner rear wheel** lifts off the ground. **(7 marks)**

**Take g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Static Per-Wheel Loads (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Weight:**
$$W = 1200 \text{ kgf} = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Static Axle Loads:**
$$W_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} = \frac{11772 \times 1.4}{2.4} = \frac{16480.8}{2.4} = 6867 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} = \frac{11772 \times 1.0}{2.4} = \frac{11772}{2.4} = 4905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Per-Wheel Loads (assuming symmetric lateral CG):**
$$W_{f,\text{each}} = \frac{W_f}{2} = \frac{6867}{2} = 3433.5 \text{ N}$$

$$W_{r,\text{each}} = \frac{W_r}{2} = \frac{4905}{2} = 2452.5 \text{ N}$$

**Verification:** $W_f + W_r = 6867 + 4905 = 11772$ N = W ✓

| Wheel | Static Load |
|---|---|
| Each front wheel | **3433.5 N** |
| Each rear wheel | **2452.5 N** |

---

**Part (b) — Lateral Load Transfer During Cornering (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Speed in m/s:**
$$v = 72 \text{ km/h} = \frac{72 \times 1000}{3600} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lateral Acceleration:**
$$a_y = \frac{v^2}{R} = \frac{(20)^2}{50} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Lateral Load Transfer on Front Axle:**
$$\Delta W_{\text{lat, front}} = \frac{W \cdot a_y \cdot h}{g \cdot t} = \frac{11772 \times 8 \times 0.5}{9.81 \times 1.4}$$

$$\Delta W_{\text{lat, front}} = \frac{47088}{13.734} = 3428.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Lateral Load Transfer on Rear Axle:**
(Same formula, since track widths are equal for front and rear axles)
$$\Delta W_{\text{lat, rear}} = 3428.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Per-Wheel Loads on Each Axle:**

**Front Axle:**
- Outer front wheel: $W_{f,\text{outer}} = 3433.5 + \frac{3428.4}{2} = 3433.5 + 1714.2 = 5147.7 \text{ N}$
- Inner front wheel: $W_{f,\text{inner}} = 3433.5 - 1714.2 = 1719.3 \text{ N}$

**Rear Axle:**
- Outer rear wheel: $W_{r,\text{outer}} = 2452.5 + 1714.2 = 4166.7 \text{ N}$
- Inner rear wheel: $W_{r,\text{inner}} = 2452.5 - 1714.2 = 738.3 \text{ N}$

**Step 6 — Lift-off Check for Inner Rear Wheel:**
Since $W_{r,\text{inner}} = 738.3 \text{ N} > 0$, **the inner rear wheel does NOT lift off** the ground.

**Step 7 — Critical Lateral Acceleration for Rear Lift-off:**
For $W_{r,\text{inner}} = 0$:
$$\frac{W_r}{2} = \frac{W \cdot a_{y,\text{crit}} \cdot h}{2 \cdot g \cdot t}$$

$$a_{y,\text{crit}} = \frac{W_r \cdot g \cdot t}{W \cdot h} = \frac{4905 \times 9.81 \times 1.4}{11772 \times 0.5} = \frac{67357.7}{5886} = 11.44 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Since the actual $a_y = 8 \text{ m/s}^2 < 11.44 \text{ m/s}^2$, the vehicle is operating within safe limits.

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $a_y$ (lateral acceleration) | **8 m/s²** |
| $\Delta W_{\text{lat, front}}$ | **3428.4 N** |
| $\Delta W_{\text{lat, rear}}$ | **3428.4 N** |
| Front outer / inner wheel | **5147.7 N / 1719.3 N** |
| Rear outer / inner wheel | **4166.7 N / 738.3 N** |
| Lift-off status | **SAFE (no lift-off)** |
| $a_{y,\text{crit}}$ (limit) | **11.44 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The inner rear wheel is the most lightly loaded during cornering, so it is the first to lift off. The margin of $11.44 - 8 = 3.44$ m/s² represents the safety buffer. To increase this margin, designers lower **h** or widen **t**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision for KTU Exam Hall:**

- [x] **Static load on front axle:** $W_f = Wb/L$ (always; whether 1 or 2 front wheels — the *axle* load is the same)
- [x] **Static load on rear axle:** $W_r = Wa/L$
- [x] **For 4-wheeled vehicles, divide each axle load by 2 to get per-wheel load.**
- [x] **For 3-wheeled Type A (1F + 2R),** $W_f$ is on **one** wheel; $W_r$ is split between **two** wheels.
- [x] **For 3-wheeled Type B (2F + 1R),** $W_r$ is on **one** wheel; $W_f$ is split between **two** wheels.
- [x] **Longitudinal load transfer** during braking: $\Delta W = W \cdot j \cdot h / (g \cdot L)$ — always positive magnitude.
- [x] **Braking increases $W_f$ and decreases $W_r$.**
- [x] **Acceleration (in rear-wheel-drive) increases $W_r$ and decreases $W_f$.**
- [x] **Lateral load transfer:** $\Delta W_{\text{lat}} = W \cdot a_y \cdot h / (g \cdot t)$
- [x] **Outer wheels gain load; inner wheels lose load** during cornering.
- [x] **Lift-off condition:** inner wheel load reaches **zero** when $\Delta W_{\text{lat}} = W_{\text{axle, static}}$.
- [x] **Critical lateral acceleration:** $a_{y,\text{crit}} = b \cdot g \cdot t / (L \cdot h)$ (for front axle).
- [x] **Critical braking deceleration:** $j_{\text{crit}} = a \cdot g \cdot L / h$ (for rear lift-off during braking).
- [x] **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the problem specifies g = 10 m/s².
- [x] **Units:** Weight in **N** (Newtons), mass in **kg**, distances in **m**, accelerations in **m/s²**.
- [x] **Always draw a labelled free-body diagram** before applying equations — KTU awards 1-2 marks for a clean diagram.
- [x] **Sum of all axle loads = Total weight W** (sanity check).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When the problem asks "load on each wheel," you **must** divide the axle load by the number of wheels on that axle. Skipping this division is the **single most common mistake** in KTU Vehicle Dynamics exams, costing students up to **2 marks** per question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Acceleration, Tractive Effort and Reactions for Different Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Acceleration, Tractive Effort and Reactions for Different Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | Calculation of Acceleration, Tractive Effort and Reactions for Different Drives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tractive Effort (TE)** is the net longitudinal force generated at the driving wheel–road contact patch, transmitted from the engine via the transmission and final drive. It is the only external longitudinal force a vehicle can apply to the road to overcome resistances and produce acceleration.

The **longitudinal dynamics equation** governing vehicle motion on a road is:

$$\boxed{m \cdot a = F_t - F_r \pm F_g - F_a}$$

where every term is a force along the direction of motion (positive forward).

**Reactions for Different Drives** refers to the normal loads carried by the front and rear axles when the vehicle is accelerating or decelerating. Because the centre of gravity (CG) is above ground, any longitudinal acceleration creates a couple that transfers weight between axles, directly altering the maximum usable tractive force.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping trolley."**
> - You push at the handle (the engine pushes at the wheels).
> - Friction between trolley wheels and floor is what actually moves the trolley — push harder than friction can hold, and the wheels **slip**.
> - The normal force on the wheels = how much weight sits on them. Heavier wheels = more friction available.
> - Now imagine tipping the trolley backwards while pushing (this mimics acceleration — weight shifts to the rear). The rear wheels can now push harder without slipping.
> - This is exactly why **rear-wheel-drive cars accelerate better** than front-wheel-drive cars of the same weight, especially when launching.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of road adhesion | **μ** | 0.8 – 1.0 (dry), 0.3 – 0.5 (wet) | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | **f_r** | 0.012 – 0.018 (car tyre) | dimensionless |
| Air density at sea level | **ρ** | **1.25** | kg/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient | **C_d** | 0.30 – 0.45 (passenger car) | dimensionless |
| Frontal area | **A** | 1.8 – 2.5 | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 explicitly requires students to evaluate acceleration capability by considering **the drive layout** (Front / Rear / Four-wheel). The same vehicle can have *different* maximum accelerations purely because of which axle is driven.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forces Acting on a Moving Vehicle

### 2.1.1 Tractive Effort (F_t)

The tractive effort generated at the wheel–road interface has **two physical limits**:

1. **Engine-limited tractive effort** — limited by power available at the wheel:

$$F_{t,eng} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_o \cdot \eta_t}{r}$$

where:
- $T_e$ = engine torque (N·m)
- $i_g$ = transmission gear ratio
- $i_o$ = final drive (axle) ratio
- $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency (typically 0.85 – 0.92)
- $r$ = effective rolling radius of tyre (m)

2. **Adhesion-limited tractive effort** — limited by tyre–road friction:

$$F_{t,\max} = \mu \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the **normal reaction at the driven axle(s)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **effective** tractive effort is the **minimum** of these two limits:
> $$F_t = \min(F_{t,eng},\ \mu \cdot N)$$
> During hard acceleration from rest, the adhesion limit is usually reached first.

### 2.1.2 Rolling Resistance (F_r)

$$F_r = f_r \cdot W$$

where $f_r$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance and $W = m g$ is the vehicle weight.

### 2.1.3 Gradient / Hill-Climbing Resistance (F_g)

$$F_g = W \sin\theta \approx W \tan\theta = W \cdot G$$

where $\theta$ is the slope angle and $G$ is the grade (in decimal, e.g., 0.10 = 10%).

### 2.1.4 Aerodynamic Drag (F_a)

$$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, V^2$$

### 2.1.5 Inertia Force (m·a)

By Newton's second law, the net forward force accelerates the equivalent mass:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a$$

## 2.2 Static & Dynamic Normal Reactions

### Static case (vehicle at rest, level road)

Take moments about the rear contact patch:

$$N_f = \frac{W \cdot b}{L}, \quad N_r = \frac{W \cdot a}{L}$$

where:
- $a$ = horizontal distance from **front axle to CG**
- $b$ = horizontal distance from **rear axle to CG**
- $L = a + b$ = wheelbase
- $h_g$ = height of CG above ground

### Dynamic case during acceleration (a > 0)

When the vehicle accelerates forward, an inertial force $m \cdot a$ acts **backward** through the CG. This creates a moment that **transfers weight from the front axle to the rear axle**.

**Dynamic weight transfer:**

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

**Reactions during forward acceleration:**

$$N_f^{dyn} = \frac{W \cdot b}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

$$N_r^{dyn} = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_g}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> During **braking** (deceleration), the signs reverse — weight transfers forward, increasing the front-axle load.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Tractive effort (engine-limited) | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \, i_g \, i_o \, \eta_t}{r}$ | N |
| Tractive effort (adhesion-limited) | $F_t \le \mu \, N_{driven}$ | N |
| Rolling resistance | $F_r = f_r \, W$ | N |
| Gradient resistance | $F_g = W \sin\theta$ | N |
| Aerodynamic drag | $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$ | N |
| Equation of motion | $m a = F_t - F_r \pm F_g - F_a$ | kg·m/s² |
| Static front reaction | $N_f = \dfrac{W \, b}{L}$ | N |
| Static rear reaction | $N_r = \dfrac{W \, a}{L}$ | N |
| Weight transfer during acceleration | $\Delta W = \dfrac{m \, a \, h_g}{L}$ | N |
| Dynamic front reaction (FWD driven) | $N_f = \dfrac{Wb}{L} + \dfrac{mah_g}{L}$ | Use **+** sign for FWD |
| Dynamic rear reaction (RWD driven) | $N_r = \dfrac{Wa}{L} + \dfrac{mah_g}{L}$ | Use **+** sign for RWD |
| Maximum acceleration (RWD) | $a_{\max} = \dfrac{\mu (W a + m a_{\max} h_g)/L - F_r - F_g - F_a}{m}$ | Solved iteratively or implicitly |
| Maximum acceleration (FWD) | $a_{\max} = \dfrac{\mu (W b + m a_{\max} h_g)/L - F_r - F_g - F_a}{m}$ | CG shift aids RWD |
| Maximum acceleration (4WD) | $a_{\max} \le \mu \, g$ (theoretical, no resistances) | Uses **all** weight |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This analysis is critical for:
- **Performance car design**: Selecting drive layout (RWD vs AWD) for launch performance.
- **Truck design**: Maximum gradability depends on driven-axle load.
- **Brake system design**: Dynamic weight transfer dictates front/rear brake-force distribution to prevent rear-wheel lockup.
- **Traction control (TCS/ESC)**: Algorithm estimates normal force at each axle and limits slip ratio accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Acceleration for a Rear-Wheel-Drive Vehicle

### Step 1: Free-Body Setup

A vehicle of mass $m$ accelerating on a level road with rear-wheel-drive. The driven axle is the **rear axle**. We want to find the maximum possible acceleration limited by tyre-road adhesion.

### Step 2: Write the Adhesion Limit on the Driven Axle

The maximum force the rear tyres can transmit without slipping is:

$$F_{t,\max} = \mu \cdot N_r^{dyn}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Dynamic Rear Reaction

From Section 2.2, during forward acceleration the rear-axle normal reaction is:

$$N_r^{dyn} = \frac{W \cdot a}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a_{\max} \cdot h_g}{L}$$

Substituting $W = m g$:

$$N_r^{dyn} = \frac{m g a}{L} + \frac{m \, a_{\max} \, h_g}{L} = \frac{m}{L}\left(g a + a_{\max} h_g\right)$$

### Step 4: Equate Adhesion Limit to Available Force

The maximum tractive force available equals what is needed to overcome all resistances plus accelerate the mass. Neglecting resistances for the maximum theoretical acceleration:

$$F_{t,\max} = m \cdot a_{\max}$$

So:

$$\mu \cdot N_r^{dyn} = m \cdot a_{\max}$$

$$\mu \cdot \frac{m}{L}\left(g a + a_{\max} h_g\right) = m \, a_{\max}$$

### Step 5: Cancel Mass and Rearrange

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$\frac{\mu}{L}\left(g a + a_{\max} h_g\right) = a_{\max}$$

$$\mu \, g a + \mu \, a_{\max} \, h_g = L \, a_{\max}$$

$$\mu \, g \, a = a_{\max}\left(L - \mu \, h_g\right)$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{RWD} = \frac{\mu \, g \, a}{L - \mu \, h_g}}$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

- Larger $a$ (CG farther forward) → larger $a_{\max}$ for RWD. This is why mid/rear-engined sports cars launch so well.
- Smaller $L - \mu h_g$ in the denominator → easier to reach the **tipping limit** (wheel lift at front).

## 3.2 Derivation for a Front-Wheel-Drive Vehicle

Following identical steps, but using $N_f^{dyn}$:

$$N_f^{dyn} = \frac{W b}{L} - \frac{m \, a_{\max} \, h_g}{L}$$

Note the **negative sign** in front of the inertia term — under forward acceleration, FWD **unloads** the front axle.

Setting $\mu \cdot N_f^{dyn} = m \cdot a_{\max}$:

$$\mu \cdot \frac{m}{L}\left(g b - a_{\max} h_g\right) = m \, a_{\max}$$

$$\mu g b - \mu a_{\max} h_g = L \, a_{\max}$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{FWD} = \frac{\mu \, g \, b}{L + \mu \, h_g}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Rear-Wheel Drive | Front-Wheel Drive | Four-Wheel Drive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driven normal load (forward acc.) | **Increases** (+) | **Decreases** (−) | Uses **all** weight |
| Formula coefficient | $\dfrac{\mu g a}{L - \mu h_g}$ | $\dfrac{\mu g b}{L + \mu h_g}$ | $\dfrac{\mu g L}{L} = \mu g$ |
| Launch performance | **Best** if $a > b$ | Weaker (especially with high $h_g$) | **Theoretical maximum** |
| Sensitivity to $h_g$ | **High** (small denom.) | Lower (adds in denom.) | None |
| Typical vehicle application | Sports cars, trucks, buses | Economy hatchbacks, sedans | SUVs, rally, supercars |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU students:** Notice that for RWD, the maximum acceleration can in principle exceed $\mu g$ (i.e., exceed the apparent friction limit) because dynamic weight transfer **augments** the rear load. Conversely for FWD, the maximum acceleration is *always* less than $\mu g$. This is a classic KTU viva / written question!

### 3.4 Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A 1500 kg car has $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, $L = 2.6$ m, $h_g = 0.55$ m. $\mu = 0.9$, $f_r = 0.015$. Find maximum acceleration on level road for RWD and FWD.

**RWD:**
$$a_{\max}^{RWD} = \frac{0.9 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.6 - 0.9 \times 0.55} = \frac{10.5948}{2.105} \approx 5.03\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**FWD:**
$$a_{\max}^{FWD} = \frac{0.9 \times 9.81 \times 1.4}{2.6 + 0.9 \times 0.55} = \frac{12.3606}{3.095} \approx 3.99\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Conclusion:** RWD produces ~26 % greater theoretical launch acceleration than FWD for this vehicle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Longitudinal Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque Te] --> B[Transmission Ratio ig]
    B --> C[Final Drive Ratio io]
    C --> D[Wheel Radius r]
    D --> E[Engine Limited Tractive Effort Fte]
    F[Driven Axle Normal N] --> G[Adhesion Limit mu times N]
    E --> H{Minimum Selector}
    G --> H
    H --> I[Net Tractive Force Ft]
    I --> J[Subtract Rolling Resistance Fr]
    J --> K[Subtract Gradient Resistance Fg]
    K --> L[Subtract Aerodynamic Drag Fa]
    L --> M[Net Force m times a]
    M --> N[Vehicle Acceleration a]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dynamic Reaction Resolution by Drive Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Mass m] --> B[Static Weight W equals mg]
    B --> C[Static Reaction at Front Nf equals Wb over L]
    B --> D[Static Reaction at Rear Nr equals Wa over L]
    E[Acceleration a] --> F[Weight Transfer Delta W equals m a h g over L]
    F --> G{Driven Axle}
    G --> H[Front Wheel Drive: Front Reaction Nf plus Delta W]
    G --> I[Rear Wheel Drive: Rear Reaction Nr plus Delta W]
    G --> J[All Wheel Drive: Sum of Front plus Rear reactions]
    H --> K[Adhesion Limited TE for FWD]
    I --> L[Adhesion Limited TE for RWD]
    J --> M[Adhesion Limited TE for 4WD]
    K --> N[Max Acceleration Computation]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Maximum Acceleration Comparative Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Drive Configuration] --> B[RWD]
    A --> C[FWD]
    A --> D[4WD]
    B --> E[amax RWD equals mu g a over L minus mu hg]
    C --> F[amax FWD equals mu g b over L plus mu hg]
    D --> G[amax 4WD equals mu g]
    E --> H{Compare Magnitudes}
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Select Configuration Based on Required Performance]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Weight Transfer During Forward Acceleration

```
                    ↑  Nf (decreases during acceleration)
                    │
            ┌───────┴───────┐
            │               │
   ┌────────┴───┐     ┌─────┴────┐
   │            │     │          │
   │  Front     │     │  Rear    │
   │  Wheel     │     │  Wheel   │
   │   ⊙        │     │    ⊙     │
   └────────────┘     └──────────┘
                    │           
                    │           ← Weight transfer ΔW = m·a·h/L
                    ↓            (moves from front to rear)
                    │           
                  N_r (increases during acceleration)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define tractive effort. State the two physical limits that bound it.**

**Model Answer:**
Tractive effort (TE) is the net longitudinal force at the driving wheel–road contact patch, transmitted from the engine through the transmission and used to overcome resistances and accelerate the vehicle. It is bounded by:
1. **Engine limit:** $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \, i_g \, i_o \, \eta_t}{r}$
2. **Adhesion limit:** $F_t \le \mu \, N_{driven}$
The usable tractive effort is the minimum of these two. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does a rear-wheel-drive car accelerate faster than a front-wheel-drive car of identical mass and CG location?**

**Model Answer:**
During forward acceleration, inertial force $m a$ through the CG at height $h_g$ transfers weight from the front to the rear axle by $\Delta W = m a h_g / L$. For RWD, this transfer **adds to** the rear-axle normal load $N_r$, increasing the adhesion-limited tractive force. For FWD, the front axle **loses** load, reducing available traction. Hence $a_{\max}^{RWD} > a_{\max}^{FWD}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A car of mass **1500 kg** has the following data: wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m, CG located $a = 1.3$ m from the front axle and $b = 1.2$ m from the rear axle, CG height $h_g = 0.6$ m. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.85$, rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.015$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(a) Compute the static and dynamic normal reactions at both axles when the vehicle accelerates at 3 m/s² on a level road.**

**(b) Calculate the maximum theoretical acceleration possible on a level road if the car is (i) rear-wheel-drive, (ii) front-wheel-drive, (iii) all-wheel-drive. Compare and comment.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Static reactions (level road):**
$$N_f = \frac{W b}{L} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.5} = 7063.2\ \text{N}$$
$$N_r = \frac{W a}{L} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.3}{2.5} = 7651.8\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Weight transfer during $a = 3$ m/s²:**
$$\Delta W = \frac{m \, a \, h_g}{L} = \frac{1500 \times 3 \times 0.6}{2.5} = 1080\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic reactions:**
$$N_f^{dyn} = N_f - \Delta W = 7063.2 - 1080 = 5983.2\ \text{N}$$
$$N_r^{dyn} = N_r + \Delta W = 7651.8 + 1080 = 8731.8\ \text{N}$$

**Verification:** $N_f^{dyn} + N_r^{dyn} = 14715 = m g$ ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Maximum theoretical acceleration** (resistances neglected as a conservative lower bound):

**Case (i) — RWD:**
$$a_{\max}^{RWD} = \frac{\mu \, g \, a}{L - \mu \, h_g} = \frac{0.85 \times 9.81 \times 1.3}{2.5 - 0.85 \times 0.6} = \frac{10.840}{1.99}$$
$$a_{\max}^{RWD} \approx 5.45\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Case (ii) — FWD:**
$$a_{\max}^{FWD} = \frac{\mu \, g \, b}{L + \mu \, h_g} = \frac{0.85 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.5 + 0.85 \times 0.6} = \frac{10.006}{3.01}$$
$$a_{\max}^{FWD} \approx 3.32\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Case (iii) — 4WD:**
$$a_{\max}^{4WD} = \mu \, g = 0.85 \times 9.81 \approx 8.34\ \text{m/s}^2$$
(Assumes all weight is utilized; subject to wheel-lift stability.)

**Comparison comment:**
- RWD yields **64 % higher** max acceleration than FWD on this vehicle.
- 4WD yields the highest **theoretical** acceleration.
- In real vehicles, resistance forces reduce all values by ~0.5–1 m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative choice)*

A truck of mass **8000 kg** with $L = 4$ m, $a = 1.6$ m, $b = 2.4$ m, $h_g = 1.2$ m. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.7$, rolling resistance $f_r = 0.02$. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(a) Derive the expressions for maximum acceleration of a RWD and FWD vehicle on level road, clearly stating your assumptions.**

**(b) For the given truck, compute the maximum acceleration in RWD configuration, considering rolling resistance. Also compute the maximum gradient (in %) it can climb at constant speed using only the maximum tractive effort.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions:**
1. Level road, no aerodynamic drag considered.
2. Engine can supply unlimited torque (adhesion-limited case).
3. Rigid body, CG at height $h_g$.
4. Longitudinal acceleration $a$ constant.

**Derivation for RWD:**
Maximum tractive force = adhesion limit on rear axle:
$$F_{t,\max} = \mu \, N_r^{dyn} = \mu\left(\frac{W a}{L} + \frac{m a_{\max} h_g}{L}\right)$$

Setting this equal to $m \, a_{\max}$ (net force, no resistances):
$$\mu \, g \, a = a_{\max}\left(L - \mu \, h_g\right)$$
$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{RWD} = \frac{\mu \, g \, a}{L - \mu \, h_g}}$$ **(7 marks)**

*(Full FWD derivation follows identically with $b$ and $+$ sign in denominator — students should write it out.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Including rolling resistance:** Equation of motion becomes:
$$m \, a_{\max} = \mu \, N_r^{dyn} - F_r$$

where $F_r = f_r \, W = 0.02 \times 8000 \times 9.81 = 1569.6$ N.

**Maximum adhesion force on rear:**
$$N_r^{dyn} = \frac{W a}{L} + \frac{m \, a_{\max} \, h_g}{L} = \frac{8000 \times 9.81 \times 1.6}{4} + \frac{8000 \times a_{\max} \times 1.2}{4} = 31392 + 2400 \, a_{\max}$$

So:
$$F_{t,\max} = 0.7 \left(31392 + 2400 \, a_{\max}\right) = 21974.4 + 1680 \, a_{\max}$$

Force balance:
$$8000 \, a_{\max} = 21974.4 + 1680 \, a_{\max} - 1569.6$$
$$8000 \, a_{\max} - 1680 \, a_{\max} = 20404.8$$
$$6320 \, a_{\max} = 20404.8$$
$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{RWD} \approx 3.23\ \text{m/s}^2}$$

**Maximum gradient at constant speed (a = 0):**
At $a = 0$: $F_{t,\max} = F_r + W \sin\theta$
For level road $a_{\max} = 3.23$, so $F_{t,\max} \approx 21974.4 + 1680 \times 3.23 = 27400$ N

Setting $F_{t,\max} = F_r + W \sin\theta$:
$$27400 = 1569.6 + 8000 \times 9.81 \times \sin\theta$$
$$25830.4 = 78480 \, \sin\theta$$
$$\sin\theta = 0.3292 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 19.2°$$

**Grade %:**
$$G = \tan\theta = \tan(19.2°) \approx 0.348 \Rightarrow \boxed{34.8\%}$$

**Comment:** This is an extreme theoretical limit; practical gradability is much lower due to gear-shift constraints and driveline strength. **(7 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
> 1. **Sign error in weight transfer** — Students often write $N_r^{dyn} = N_r - \Delta W$. Under *acceleration*, rear load *increases* (+); under *braking*, it decreases. Losing 2 marks per error.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$ in $W$** — Writing $m g$ as just $m$ in static reactions is a frequent slip.
> 3. **Mixing $a$ and $b$** — Some students confuse the front-rear distances. **Remember:** the *front* reaction uses the *rear* CG distance $b$, and vice-versa. *(Moment principle.)*
> 4. **Ignoring rolling resistance** in maximum-acceleration derivations — KTU often gives a vehicle moving at constant velocity (so $a = 0$) and asks for max gradient; students forget $F_r$ in the force balance.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing $m/s²$ with $g$ as multiplier without converting. Always use SI units consistently.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tractive Effort** has two physical limits: **engine** and **adhesion** — always take the **minimum**.
- **Equation of motion** is the master relation: $m a = F_t - F_r \pm F_g - F_a$.
- **Static reactions:** $N_f = Wb/L$, $N_r = Wa/L$ — front uses rear distance, rear uses front distance.
- **Weight transfer** during forward acceleration: $\Delta W = m a h_g / L$, **from front to rear**.
- **RWD advantage:** Dynamic transfer **increases** the driven-axle load → $a_{\max}^{RWD} = \dfrac{\mu g a}{L - \mu h_g}$ (denominator smaller → bigger result).
- **FWD disadvantage:** Dynamic transfer **decreases** the driven-axle load → $a_{\max}^{FWD} = \dfrac{\mu g b}{L + \mu h_g}$ (denominator larger → smaller result).
- **4WD** utilizes the entire vehicle weight → theoretical max acceleration $\mu g$.
- **Braking** reverses the sign of $\Delta W$ — front axle load increases, important for brake design.
- **Rolling resistance** always opposes motion: $F_r = f_r W$.
- **Aerodynamic drag** is velocity-squared: $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$.
- **Gradient resistance:** $F_g = W \sin\theta \approx W \tan\theta$ for small slopes.
- **Tipping limit:** If $\mu h_g \to L$, RWD formula denominator → 0 (wheel lift occurs); informs vehicle design.
- **Practical rule:** Real cars achieve $a_{\max} \approx 0.5 \mu g$ to $0.7 \mu g$ due to resistive losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of a Vehicle on Slope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability of a Vehicle on Slope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

The **stability of a vehicle on a slope** is defined as the ability of a stationary vehicle, parked on an inclined surface (with its longitudinal axis aligned to the slope direction), to remain in **static equilibrium** indefinitely without undergoing any of the following failure modes:

1. **Sliding instability** — the vehicle translating down the slope due to gravitational pull overcoming the available tyre-road friction.
2. **Overturning (tipping) instability** — the vehicle rotating about the contact point of its lower wheel(s) when the line of action of its weight passes outside the wheelbase.

Two characteristic angles govern the stability:

- **Critical Sliding Angle ($\theta_s$)** — the steepest slope angle beyond which the vehicle begins to slide down even with brakes applied.
- **Critical Overturning Angle ($\theta_o$)** — the steepest slope angle beyond which the vehicle begins to tip over (rearward or forward) about the lower wheel contact.

A vehicle is **completely stable** only when the slope angle $\theta$ satisfies:
$$\theta < \min(\theta_s, \theta_o)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For a four-wheeled vehicle, the relevant geometry uses the **wheelbase** $L$, the **CG height** $h$ from the ground, and the **horizontal distance $a$** of the CG projection from the rear axle. The student must always resolve the weight vector along and perpendicular to the slope, **not** along the horizontal/vertical axes.

## Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a tall bookshelf standing on a ramp:

- **Sliding**: A gentle push would make it slide down (overcoming friction between the base and the ramp). The steeper the ramp, the easier it slides.
- **Tipping**: If you somehow stuck the bookshelf to the ramp (infinite friction), it would still eventually fall over backwards because its weight line would no longer pass through its base.

A vehicle behaves identically. If the ramp is **less** steep, both effects are prevented by friction and the geometry of the wheelbase. As the slope steepens, **one of these two failure modes will occur first**, depending on the **coefficient of friction** $\mu$ versus the geometric ratio **$a/h$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Rule (Quick Comparison):**
> - If $\mu > a/h$ → the vehicle **overturns first** (friction is "too good", holding the car in place until tipping is unavoidable).
> - If $\mu < a/h$ → the vehicle **slides first** (typical for normal cars on dry tarmac where friction is the limiting factor).

## Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of static friction (tyre-road) | **0.7 – 0.9** (dry asphalt), **0.1 – 0.3** (wet/icy) |
| $h$ | Height of CG above ground | **0.5 – 0.9 m** (passenger cars) |
| $a, b$ | CG distances from rear / front axles | $a + b = L$ |
| $L$ | Wheelbase | **2.3 – 3.2 m** (passenger cars) |
| $\theta$ | Slope angle | Limited by road grade (max ~ 8% ≈ 4.6° for highways) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Free Body Setup

When a vehicle of weight $W = mg$ is parked on a slope of angle $\theta$ (facing uphill, longitudinal axis parallel to slope), the forces acting on it are:

1. **Weight $W$** — acting vertically downward through the CG.
2. **Normal reactions** $N_f$ (front wheel) and $N_r$ (rear wheel) — perpendicular to the slope surface.
3. **Friction forces** $F_f$ (front) and $F_r$ (rear) — parallel to slope surface, opposing the tendency of motion.

### Resolution of Weight

The weight $W$ is resolved into two components along axes parallel and perpendicular to the slope:

$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta \quad \text{(acting down the slope)}$$

$$W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta \quad \text{(acting into the slope surface)}$$

## Sliding Condition (Translational Equilibrium Limit)

The vehicle is on the verge of sliding when the **friction force reaches its maximum static value** $f_{\max} = \mu N$:

$$W \sin\theta = \mu W \cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\tan\theta_s = \mu \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_s = \arctan(\mu)}$$

- For $\theta < \theta_s$ → vehicle does not slide.
- For $\theta \geq \theta_s$ → vehicle slides down.

## Overturning Condition (Rotational Equilibrium Limit)

The vehicle is on the verge of overturning about the lower (rear) wheel when the **normal reaction at the upper (front) wheel becomes zero** ($N_f = 0$). At this exact instant, all the weight is carried by the rear wheel, and the line of action of $W$ passes through the rear wheel contact point.

For a vehicle with CG at height $h$ above the slope surface and distance $a$ from the rear axle (measured along slope):

$$\boxed{\tan\theta_o = \dfrac{a}{h} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_o = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right)}$$

- For $\theta < \theta_o$ → vehicle does not tip.
- For $\theta \geq \theta_o$ → vehicle tips rearward about the rear wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symmetry:** If the vehicle faces **downhill** on the slope, the front wheel becomes the lower pivot. The relevant distance is then $b$ (CG distance from front axle), and $\tan\theta_o = b/h$.

## Combined Stability Criterion

The vehicle remains stationary on the slope **only if both** sliding and overturning are prevented:

$$\boxed{\theta < \min(\theta_s, \theta_o) = \min\!\left[\arctan(\mu),\ \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right)\right]}$$

## Effect of Brake Application

When all four wheel brakes are applied:
- The wheels are locked and cannot rotate.
- The tyre-road interface now operates under **static friction** (coefficient $\mu_s$) instead of rolling resistance.
- The governing sliding condition **remains** $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$.
- However, the brake torque $T_b$ at each wheel must be reacted by a friction force $f = T_b/r$ at the contact patch. This must not exceed $\mu N_{\text{wheel}}$; otherwise, the wheel slips and the brake "fades".

**Stability with brakes applied:**
$$\theta \leq \arctan(\mu_s)$$
$$\text{and for each wheel: } \quad \dfrac{T_{b,i}}{r} \leq \mu_s N_i$$

In most practical KTU problems, a single equivalent coefficient $\mu$ is used and the per-wheel analysis is omitted.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU University Exams (ESE). Memorize units and the conditions under which each applies.

| Formula | Description | Conditions / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta$ | Weight component along slope (down-slope) | Newtons (N) |
| $W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta$ | Weight component perpendicular to slope | Newtons (N) |
| $\tan\theta_s = \mu$ | Critical sliding angle condition | Dimensionless |
| $\theta_s = \arctan(\mu)$ | Critical sliding angle | Degrees / Radians |
| $\tan\theta_o = a/h$ | Critical overturning angle condition | Dimensionless |
| $\theta_o = \arctan(a/h)$ | Critical overturning angle (CG behind front wheel) | Degrees / Radians |
| $\theta_o = \arctan(b/h)$ | Critical overturning angle (CG ahead of rear wheel) | Degrees / Radians |
| $\theta < \min(\theta_s, \theta_o)$ | Complete stability criterion | Both conditions must hold |
| $\mu_s \geq \tan\theta$ | Stability condition with brakes locked | Static friction limit |
| $F_{b,\text{wheel}} \leq \mu N_{\text{wheel}}$ | Brake force limit per wheel | Avoids brake-fade |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Road Design Standards:** Maximum highway gradient (~ 6–8 %) is set well below both $\theta_s$ and $\theta_o$ to ensure parked and braked vehicles remain safe.
- **Heavy Vehicle Design:** Truck manufacturers specify **maximum parking gradient** (typically 20–30 %) on the nameplate, governed by the parking brake capacity and CG location.
- **Off-Road & Construction Equipment:** Low CG and wide track width are deliberately designed to maximize $a/h$ ratio and hence stability on rough terrain.
- **Anti-Roll Bars & CG Optimization:** Automakers lower the CG of SUVs and sports cars to increase the overturning limit, reducing rollover risk on cambered roads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Critical Sliding Angle

**Statement:** Determine the maximum slope angle at which a vehicle of weight $W$ can remain stationary on an inclined road without sliding, given a coefficient of static friction $\mu$ between the tyres and the road.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the vehicle along and perpendicular to the slope.**

Along the slope:
- Component of weight pulling the vehicle downhill: $W \sin\theta$
- Friction force (acting up the slope, opposing impending motion): $f$

Perpendicular to the slope:
- Component of weight pressing the vehicle into the slope: $W \cos\theta$
- Normal reaction from the slope: $N$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium perpendicular to the slope (no acceleration in this direction).**

For the vehicle to remain stationary, the net force perpendicular to the slope must be zero:

$$N - W \cos\theta = 0$$

$$N = W \cos\theta$$

This identifies the normal reaction that determines the maximum available friction.

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium along the slope (impending sliding condition).**

At the verge of sliding, the friction force reaches its limiting value, given by Coulomb's law of static friction:

$$f = \mu N$$

Substituting $N$ from Step 2:

$$f = \mu W \cos\theta$$

For impending motion (down the slope), the friction force must balance the down-slope component of weight:

$$W \sin\theta = \mu W \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $W$ on both sides and solve for the critical angle.**

$$W \sin\theta = \mu W \cos\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $W \cos\theta$ (assuming $\cos\theta \neq 0$):

$$\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \mu$$

$$\tan\theta = \mu$$

Therefore, the **critical sliding angle** is:

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \arctan(\mu)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

For slope angles $\theta < \theta_s$, the available friction exceeds the down-slope gravitational pull, and the vehicle remains stationary. For $\theta \geq \theta_s$, the gravitational pull exceeds friction and the vehicle slides down.

## Derivation 2: Critical Overturning Angle

**Statement:** Determine the slope angle at which a vehicle, with CG located at height $h$ above the slope surface and at horizontal distance $a$ from the rear axle (measured on flat ground), begins to tip rearward about the rear wheel contact point.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**

Place the origin at the **rear wheel contact point** on the slope.
- $x$-axis: horizontal, positive up the slope.
- $y$-axis: vertical, positive upward.

The vehicle body is tilted by the slope angle $\theta$ with respect to horizontal.

**Step 2 — Express the position of the CG in world coordinates.**

In the vehicle's body frame (before tilting), the CG is located at coordinates $(a, h)$ relative to the rear axle. When the body rotates by angle $\theta$ about the rear axle, the CG position in world coordinates (origin at rear wheel contact) becomes:

$$x_{cg} = a \cos\theta - h \sin\theta$$

$$y_{cg} = a \sin\theta + h \cos\theta$$

This follows from the rotation transformation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_{cg} \\ y_{cg} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a \\ h \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} a \cos\theta - h \sin\theta \\ a \sin\theta + h \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the overturning condition.**

The vehicle is on the verge of overturning when the line of action of the weight $W$ (a vertical line passing through the CG) coincides with the rear wheel contact point. In our coordinate system, this means the **horizontal distance** from the rear contact point to the CG line of action is zero.

The horizontal distance is exactly $x_{cg}$, so the condition becomes:

$$x_{cg} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the critical angle.**

Substituting the expression for $x_{cg}$:

$$a \cos\theta - h \sin\theta = 0$$

$$a \cos\theta = h \sin\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $h \cos\theta$:

$$\dfrac{a}{h} = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta$$

Therefore, the **critical overturning angle** is:

$$\boxed{\theta_o = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

- For $\theta < \theta_o$: $x_{cg} > 0$, the weight line lies within the wheelbase, so the vehicle is rotationally stable.
- For $\theta > \theta_o$: $x_{cg} < 0$, the weight line passes behind the rear wheel, producing an unbalanced moment that causes rearward overturning.
- The overturning angle depends purely on the geometry ($a/h$) and **not** on the vehicle's weight or the friction coefficient.

## Derivation 3: Stability with Brakes Applied

**Statement:** Analyze the stability of a vehicle on a slope when all four wheel brakes are locked.

**Step 1 — Identify the additional constraint imposed by brake lockup.**

When brakes are applied, the wheels cannot rotate. The vehicle behaves like a single rigid block resting on the slope. The contact patch therefore operates under **pure sliding (static) friction** with coefficient $\mu_s$.

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium along the slope.**

The vehicle is in static equilibrium along the slope when the friction force (acting up the slope) balances the down-slope weight component:

$$f = W \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Friction limit.**

The maximum friction that can be developed is:

$$f_{\max} = \mu_s N = \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

For the vehicle to remain stationary:

$$W \sin\theta \leq \mu_s W \cos\theta$$

$$\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\theta \leq \arctan(\mu_s)}$$

**Step 4 — Brake torque constraint at each wheel.**

For each wheel, the brake applies a torque $T_{b,i}$. This must be reacted by a friction force $f_i$ at the tyre-road interface:

$$f_i = \dfrac{T_{b,i}}{r}$$

To prevent the wheel from slipping (and the brake from fading), this must satisfy:

$$\dfrac{T_{b,i}}{r} \leq \mu_s N_i$$

where $N_i$ is the normal load on wheel $i$. For a typical symmetric vehicle on a flat surface, $N_i = W/4$. On a slope, weight transfer shifts load between front and rear axles.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The mathematical condition for no-sliding with brakes applied is identical to the no-brake case, except that $\mu$ is replaced by the **static friction coefficient** $\mu_s$. In typical KTU numerical problems, the symbol $\mu$ is used directly without distinction between static and kinetic.

## Derivation 4: Combined Stability Criterion

**Statement:** Determine the maximum slope angle for complete stability of the vehicle.

**Step 1 — Combine the two independent conditions.**

Stability requires **both** no-sliding and no-tipping:

$$\theta \leq \theta_s \quad \text{AND} \quad \theta \leq \theta_o$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{\theta \leq \min(\theta_s, \theta_o)}$$

**Step 2 — Case Analysis.**

**Case A: $\mu < a/h$ (i.e., $\theta_s < \theta_o$)** — Sliding is the limiting mode.

The vehicle will begin to slide at $\theta_s$ before it has a chance to tip. Maximum safe slope:
$$\theta_{\max} = \theta_s = \arctan(\mu)$$

This is the common case for cars on dry roads with low CG.

**Case B: $\mu > a/h$ (i.e., $\theta_s > \theta_o$)** — Overturning is the limiting mode.

The friction is "too good" — the vehicle holds its position until the slope steepens enough to cause tipping. Maximum safe slope:
$$\theta_{\max} = \theta_o = \arctan(a/h)$$

This case is rare and is usually encountered only with extremely high friction (e.g., rubber tyres on sticky surfaces) or with vehicles having a high CG (tall, narrow vehicles).

**Step 3 — Boundary condition.**

When $\mu = a/h$, both failure modes occur simultaneously at the same slope angle.

## Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg, so $W = mg = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715$ N
- Wheelbase $L = 2.4$ m
- CG height $h = 0.55$ m
- Distance of CG from rear axle: $a = 1.2$ m, hence $b = 1.2$ m (CG at mid-wheelbase)
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.7$
- Slope angle $\theta = 15°$

**Step A — Critical sliding angle:**

$$\theta_s = \arctan(0.7) = 34.99° \approx 35°$$

**Step B — Critical overturning angle:**

$$\theta_o = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{1.2}{0.55}\right) = \arctan(2.182) = 65.4°$$

**Step C — Compare with operating slope:**

$$\theta = 15° < 35° < 65.4°$$

The vehicle is **completely stable** at 15°. Sliding is the limiting mode.

**Step D — Maximum safe parking slope:**

$$\theta_{\max} = \min(\theta_s, \theta_o) = 35°$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical free-body sketches of vehicles on inclines are difficult to render in pure Mermaid syntax. The diagrams below present a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that captures the same physical relationships, force interactions, and decision logic used in textbook schematics.

## Diagram 1: Block Architecture of the Stability Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Parameters]
        P1[theta - Slope angle]
        P2[h - CG height above slope]
        P3[a - CG distance from rear axle]
        P4[mu - Coefficient of static friction]
        P5[L - Total wheelbase]
        P6[m - Vehicle mass]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Stability Computation Module]
        W[Compute Weight W equals m times g]
        WP[Resolve Weight along slope: W sin theta]
        WN[Resolve Weight normal to slope: W cos theta]
        SLD[Sliding Check Module: compare W sin theta with mu W cos theta]
        TIP[Tipping Check Module: compare tan theta with a over h]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Decision Outputs]
        OS[Output: STABLE]
        OSD[Output: VEHICLE SLIDES DOWN]
        OOV[Output: VEHICLE OVERTURNS]
    end

    P6 --> W
    P1 --> WP
    P1 --> WN
    W --> WP
    W --> WN
    WP --> SLD
    WN --> SLD
    P4 --> SLD
    P1 --> TIP
    P2 --> TIP
    P3 --> TIP
    SLD --> OS
    TIP --> OS
    SLD --> OSD
    TIP --> OOV
```

## Diagram 2: Force Interaction Map on a Slope (Logical Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Slope Surface at angle theta] --> B[Rear Wheel Contact Point P1]
    A --> C[Front Wheel Contact Point P2]
    D[Center of Gravity CG] --> E[Weight W: vertical downward]
    D --> F[W sin theta: down slope component]
    D --> G[W cos theta: into slope component]
    F --> H[Friction force f: up slope opposing motion]
    G --> I[Normal reaction N: outward from slope]
    H --> J{Check f versus mu N}
    I --> J
    J -->|f less than mu N| K[No sliding]
    J -->|f greater than mu N| L[Sliding occurs]
    K --> M{Check x_cg greater than 0}
    M -->|Yes| N[Stable configuration]
    M -->|No| O[Tipping imminent]
```

## Diagram 3: Decision Flow for Mode of Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vehicle parked at slope angle theta]) --> Compare{Compare theta_s and theta_o}
    Compare -->|theta_s smaller than theta_o| CaseA[Friction limit reached first]
    Compare -->|theta_o smaller than theta_s| CaseB[Geometric tipping first]
    Compare -->|theta_s equals theta_o| CaseC[Both occur simultaneously]
    CaseA --> LimitA[Max safe slope equals theta_s equals arctan mu]
    CaseB --> LimitB[Max safe slope equals theta_o equals arctan a over h]
    CaseC --> LimitC[Max safe slope equals theta_s equals theta_o]
    LimitA --> End1([Vehicle slides if theta exceeds theta_s])
    LimitB --> End2([Vehicle overturns if theta exceeds theta_o])
    LimitC --> End3([Either failure at same slope])
```

## Diagram 4: Geometry Definition Block (Textual Schematic Equivalent)

```
     y (vertical, up)
     ^
     |
     |        CG
     |        *
     |       /|
     |      / |
     |     /  |  h  (perpendicular to slope)
     |    /   |
     |   /    |
     |  /  a  |         Slope surface (angle theta with horizontal)
     | /______|______________________________> x (horizontal)
     | P1 \   theta
     |     \___________
     |                  \   P2
     |                   \
     |                    \
     |  L (wheelbase along slope)
     |
```

Where:
- **P1** = rear wheel contact point (lower on slope)
- **P2** = front wheel contact point (higher on slope)
- **CG** = center of gravity
- **$a$** = distance of CG from rear axle along slope
- **$h$** = perpendicular height of CG above slope
- **$L$** = wheelbase along slope
- **$\theta$** = slope angle with horizontal

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
Define the terms **(i) Critical Sliding Angle** and **(ii) Critical Overturning Angle** of a vehicle on an inclined surface.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Critical Sliding Angle ($\theta_s$):** It is the maximum angle of inclination of the slope with the horizontal at which a vehicle, when parked on the slope, just begins to slide down due to gravity overcoming the static friction between the tyres and the road surface. Mathematically, $\theta_s = \arctan(\mu)$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction.

**(ii) Critical Overturning Angle ($\theta_o$):** It is the maximum angle of inclination of the slope at which the vehicle is on the verge of tipping over (rearward) about its lower wheel contact point. Mathematically, $\tan\theta_o = a/h$, where $a$ is the horizontal distance of the CG from the rear axle and $h$ is the CG height above the road surface.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
State the conditions under which a vehicle will **(a)** slide down a slope and **(b)** overturn (tip over) on a slope.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sliding condition:** The vehicle slides down when the down-slope component of its weight exceeds the maximum available static friction force at the tyre-road interface. This occurs when:
$$\tan\theta > \mu$$

**(b) Overturning condition:** The vehicle overturns about the lower wheel contact point when the line of action of its weight passes outside the wheelbase. This occurs when:
$$\tan\theta > \dfrac{a}{h}$$

where $a$ is the distance of CG from the rear axle and $h$ is the CG height.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert degrees to radians** (or vice versa) in $\arctan$ calculations. Show both numerical input and final angle in degrees explicitly.
> - A common error is using **kinetic friction $\mu_k$** instead of **static friction $\mu_s$** when brakes are applied. Use the value explicitly given in the problem.
> - Do **not** confuse wheelbase $L$ with $a$ or $b$. The overturning formula uses the CG distance from the relevant (lower) wheel — typically $a$ from the rear axle if the vehicle faces uphill.
> - Always state **which failure mode occurs first** in your conclusion, with a clear numerical comparison.
> - Marks are often lost for **not drawing a free body diagram** in derivation problems. Always include a labeled FBD.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat free body diagram, derive the expressions for the critical sliding angle and critical overturning angle for a four-wheeled vehicle parked on an inclined road. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Free Body Diagram:**
A vehicle of weight $W$ rests on a slope of angle $\theta$. The weight $W$ acts through the CG (height $h$ above slope, distance $a$ from rear axle). The slope exerts normal reactions $N_f$ (front), $N_r$ (rear) and friction forces $F_f$, $F_r$ on the wheels. The weight is resolved into $W\sin\theta$ (down slope) and $W\cos\theta$ (into slope).

**Derivation — Critical Sliding Angle:**

For equilibrium perpendicular to slope:
$$N_f + N_r = W \cos\theta$$

For impending sliding along slope, total friction reaches its maximum:
$$F_f + F_r = \mu(N_f + N_r) = \mu W \cos\theta$$

The down-slope component of weight is balanced by total friction:
$$W \sin\theta = \mu W \cos\theta$$

Dividing by $W \cos\theta$:
$$\tan\theta_s = \mu \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\theta_s = \arctan(\mu)}$$

**Derivation — Critical Overturning Angle:**

The vehicle is on the verge of overturning when the front (upper) wheel just loses contact, i.e., $N_f \to 0$. Taking moments about the rear wheel contact point, the line of action of $W$ must pass through this pivot.

Using the geometry established in Section 3 (Derivation 2), the horizontal distance from the rear contact to the CG line of action is:
$$x_{cg} = a \cos\theta - h \sin\theta$$

Setting $x_{cg} = 0$ for the critical overturning condition:
$$a \cos\theta = h \sin\theta$$
$$\tan\theta_o = \dfrac{a}{h} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\theta_o = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right)}$$

#### (b) A car has the following specifications: mass = 1200 kg, wheelbase = 2.5 m, distance of CG from rear axle = 1.3 m, height of CG above ground = 0.55 m, and coefficient of friction between tyres and road = 0.7. The car is parked on an inclined road with brakes applied. Determine: **(i) Critical sliding angle**, **(ii) Critical overturning angle**, and **(iii) which failure mode occurs first**. **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1200$ kg, $W = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772$ N
- $L = 2.5$ m
- $a = 1.3$ m (CG from rear axle)
- $h = 0.55$ m
- $\mu = 0.7$

**Solution:**

**(i) Critical sliding angle:**

Applying the sliding condition:
$$\tan\theta_s = \mu = 0.7$$
$$\theta_s = \arctan(0.7)$$
$$\boxed{\theta_s = 34.99° \approx 35°}$$

**(ii) Critical overturning angle:**

Applying the overturning condition:
$$\tan\theta_o = \dfrac{a}{h} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.55} = 2.364$$
$$\theta_o = \arctan(2.364)$$
$$\boxed{\theta_o = 67.08° \approx 67.1°}$$

**(iii) Comparison of failure modes:**

$$\theta_s = 35° < \theta_o = 67.1°$$

Since $\theta_s < \theta_o$, the vehicle reaches the **sliding limit first** as the slope angle increases. Therefore, the car will **slide down the slope before it can overturn**. The maximum safe parking slope is $\theta_{\max} = 35°$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the various forces acting on a vehicle parked on an inclined plane. Discuss how the application of brakes affects the stability of the vehicle on the slope. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Forces Acting on a Vehicle Parked on an Inclined Plane:**

When a vehicle of weight $W$ is parked on a slope of angle $\theta$ (facing uphill), the following forces act on it:

1. **Weight $W$** — Acts vertically downward through the CG. Resolved into:
   - $W\sin\theta$ parallel to the slope (tending to slide the vehicle downhill).
   - $W\cos\theta$ perpendicular to the slope (pressing the vehicle into the surface).

2. **Normal Reactions $N_f$ and $N_r$** — Act perpendicular to the slope surface at the front and rear wheel contact patches, respectively.

3. **Friction Forces $F_f$ and $F_r$** — Act parallel to the slope surface, opposing the tendency of motion. For a stationary vehicle, these act **up the slope**.

**Effect of Brake Application:**

When brakes are applied to all four wheels:
- The wheels are locked and **cannot rotate**.
- The contact patches operate under pure **static friction** with coefficient $\mu_s$.
- The vehicle behaves like a rigid block sliding on the slope.
- The condition for stability remains mathematically identical: $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$.

However, brakes introduce an additional constraint. Each brake applies a torque $T_b$ on its wheel, which must be reacted by a tangential friction force $f = T_b/r$ at the contact patch. This force must satisfy:
$$f = \dfrac{T_b}{r} \leq \mu_s N_{\text{wheel}}$$

If the slope is too steep, the required brake force exceeds $\mu_s N$ at one or more wheels, leading to **brake fade** (wheel slip despite applied brake). This causes the vehicle to slide despite brake application.

**Key Insight:** Brakes do **not** increase the maximum friction available at the tyre-road interface — the limit is still $\mu N$. They merely ensure that the wheels do not rotate, converting a rolling situation into a sliding situation governed by static friction.

#### (b) A truck weighing 2500 kg is parked on a slope of 20° with all four wheels braked. The coefficient of static friction is 0.65. The truck has a wheelbase of 3.0 m, with the CG located 1.6 m from the rear axle and 0.85 m above the ground. Determine: **(i) Whether the truck will remain stationary, slide, or overturn**, and **(ii) the maximum safe slope angle for parking**. **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 2500$ kg, $W = 2500 \times 9.81 = 24525$ N
- $\theta = 20°$ (operating slope)
- $\mu = 0.65$ (static friction)
- $L = 3.0$ m
- $a = 1.6$ m (CG from rear axle)
- $h = 0.85$ m

**Solution:**

**(i) Stability check at $\theta = 20°$:**

Compute the critical angles:
$$\theta_s = \arctan(\mu) = \arctan(0.65) = 33.02°$$

$$\theta_o = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{1.6}{0.85}\right) = \arctan(1.882) = 61.99°$$

Compare with operating slope:
$$\theta = 20° < \theta_s = 33.02° < \theta_o = 61.99°$$

**Conclusion:** The truck **remains stationary** at 20°. Neither sliding nor overturning occurs because the operating slope is less than both critical angles.

**(ii) Maximum safe parking slope:**

$$\theta_{\max} = \min(\theta_s, \theta_o) = \min(33.02°, 61.99°) = 33.02°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} \approx 33°}$$

The truck can be safely parked on any slope up to about 33° with brakes applied. Beyond this angle, it would begin to slide down the slope.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**

- [ ] **Stability** on a slope requires **both** no-sliding AND no-tipping.
- [ ] **Sliding condition:** $\tan\theta \leq \mu$. Critical angle: $\theta_s = \arctan(\mu)$.
- [ ] **Overturning condition:** $\tan\theta \leq a/h$. Critical angle: $\theta_o = \arctan(a/h)$.
- [ ] **$a$** = distance of CG from the **rear axle** (measured along slope / horizontal projection on flat ground). **$h$** = vertical height of CG above ground.
- [ ] **Wheelbase** $L = a + b$; do not confuse $L$ with $a$ in the overturning formula.
- [ ] **Weight resolution** is along/perpendicular to the **slope**, NOT to the horizontal/vertical.
- [ ] **Normal force** on slope: $N = W \cos\theta$ (not $W$).
- [ ] **Friction force** maximum: $f_{\max} = \mu N = \mu W \cos\theta$.
- [ ] **Comparison rule:** If $\mu < a/h$ → slides first (typical); if $\mu > a/h$ → overturns first.
- [ ] **Brake application** locks the wheels. The sliding condition becomes $\tan\theta \leq \mu_s$, with per-wheel brake torque constraint $T_b/r \leq \mu_s N_{\text{wheel}}$.
- [ ] **Maximum safe slope:** $\theta_{\max} = \min(\theta_s, \theta_o)$.
- [ ] For a vehicle facing **downhill**, the front wheel becomes the pivot, and use $b$ instead of $a$ in $\tan\theta_o = b/h$.
- [ ] **Common error to avoid:** Using the wheelbase $L$ in place of $a$ — this gives an incorrect (too-high) overturning angle.
- [ ] **Units:** Angles in degrees for final answer; trigonometric ratios are dimensionless.
- [ ] **Real-world:** Road gradient limits (~6–8%) ensure $\theta \ll \theta_s, \theta_o$ for all parked vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Transfer during Acceleration, Cornering and Braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Transfer during Acceleration, Cornering and Braking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weight Transfer (Load Transfer):** Weight transfer, more rigorously termed **load transfer**, is the redistribution of the normal (vertical) reaction forces acting on a vehicle's tires caused by inertial forces generated during **longitudinal** (acceleration/braking) and **lateral** (cornering) dynamic maneuvers. Since the total vertical load on the tires must remain equal to the vehicle weight **W = mg**, any shift in load from one axle/wheel to another is referred to as weight transfer.

Mathematically, for a vehicle of mass **m**, gravitational acceleration **g**, wheelbase **L**, and height of center of gravity **h**, the longitudinal load transfer during longitudinal acceleration **a_x** is:

$$\Delta W_x = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h}{L}$$

The lateral load transfer during cornering acceleration **a_y** over a track width **t** is:

$$\Delta W_y = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$$

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are standing in a bus.** When the bus suddenly brakes, you feel a "push" forward. Your body wants to keep moving due to inertia (Newton's First Law). In the same way, when a car brakes, the entire car body — including the engine, passengers, and cargo — wants to keep moving forward. Because the tires grip the road, the car stops, but the inertia of the body **dives forward**, pressing more weight onto the front tires and **lifting** weight off the rear tires. This is *longitudinal* weight transfer during braking. During acceleration, the opposite happens — weight shifts to the rear. During a sharp turn, the body "leans outward," pushing more load onto the outer wheels and unloading the inner wheels — this is *lateral* weight transfer during cornering.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical CG height (passenger car):** **h ≈ 0.45–0.55 m**
- **Typical CG height (SUV/truck):** **h ≈ 0.7–0.9 m**
- **Performance car braking limit:** ≈ **0.9g to 1.2g**
- **Maximum lateral adhesion on dry asphalt:** ≈ **1.0g**
- **Static weight distribution:** Often given as **% front / % rear** (e.g., 50/50, 53/47)
- **Roll center height (h_r):** Typically **0 to 150 mm** above ground for a passenger car

### 1.4 Why Weight Transfer Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A vehicle's tires can only generate longitudinal and lateral force proportional to the **vertical load** they carry (up to the friction limit). Therefore, shifting load from one tire to another directly affects the **maximum braking, traction, and cornering capability** of each tire. Weight transfer is the single most fundamental concept that explains why a braking car loses rear grip first, why understeer/oversteer is influenced by load shift, and why anti-roll bars and suspension geometry are designed the way they are.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vehicle Reference Frame and Key Parameters

To analyze weight transfer rigorously, we define a vehicle-fixed coordinate system:

- **x-axis:** longitudinal, pointing forward
- **y-axis:** lateral, pointing to the driver's left
- **z-axis:** vertical, pointing upward
- **Origin:** typically the projected CG onto the ground plane

**Geometric parameters used throughout:**
- **L** = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- **t** = track width (distance between left and right wheels on the same axle)
- **a** = longitudinal distance from CG to front axle
- **b** = longitudinal distance from CG to rear axle
- **L = a + b**
- **h** = height of the CG above the ground
- **h_r** = height of the roll center above the ground
- **W = mg** = total vehicle weight
- **m** = vehicle mass (sprung + unsprung)

### 2.2 Longitudinal Weight Transfer — Conceptual Logic Steps

1. When a vehicle decelerates at **a_x** (positive magnitude), the entire vehicle mass experiences an inertial force **m·a_x** acting **forward** at the CG.
