## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Motor (Full Order)

**Step 1 — Electrical sub-system (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around armature loop).**

Let $V_a$ be the applied armature voltage, $R_a$ the armature resistance, $L_a$ the armature inductance, $i_a$ the armature current, and $e_b = K_b \omega$ the back-EMF.

$$V_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

Substituting $e_b = K_b \omega$:

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_b \, \omega(t)$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \, \Omega(s) \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical sub-system (Newton's second law on rotor inertia).**

Motor torque $T_m = K_t i_a$ drives the rotor inertia $J$ against viscous damping $B$ and external load $T_L$:

$$T_m(t) - T_L(t) = J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t)$$

Laplace transform:

$$K_t I_a(s) - T_L(s) = (J s + B) \Omega(s) \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $I_a(s)$.** From (2):

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t}$$

Substitute into (1):

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t} + K_b \Omega(s)$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms in $\Omega(s)$ and $T_L(s)$.**

$$V_a(s) = \frac{R_a + L_a s}{K_t} (J s + B) \Omega(s) + \frac{R_a + L_a s}{K_t} T_L(s) + K_b \Omega(s)$$

Factor out $\Omega(s)$:

$$V_a(s) = \left[\frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b\right] \Omega(s) + \frac{R_a + L_a s}{K_t} T_L(s)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Omega(s)/V_a(s)$ with $T_L = 0$.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $K_t$:

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) = L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + R_a B$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t)} \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 6 — Obtain $\Theta(s)/V_a(s)$ by integrating (3).** Because $\Theta(s) = \Omega(s)/s$:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t)\right]}$$

This is the **full third-order** model.

**Step 7 — Neglect $L_a$ (valid when $\tau_e = L_a/R_a \ll \tau_m$).** With $L_a \to 0$:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[R_a J s + (R_a B + K_b K_t)\right]}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $(R_a B + K_b K_t)$:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / (R_a B + K_b K_t)}{s \left[\dfrac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_t} s + 1\right]}$$

Recognising the definitions of $K_m$ and $\tau_m$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}}$$

This is the **standard first-order position servo** form used in nearly all KTU 2024 board derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Hydraulic Natural Frequency $\omega_h$

**Step 1 — Continuity equation in the cylinder chamber.** The net flow into the cap-end (volume $V_1$) minus the flow out of the rod-end (volume $V_2$) plus the compression of the fluid equals zero:

$$Q_L - A \dot{x} = \frac{V_1}{\beta_e} \dot{P}_1 \quad \text{and} \quad Q_L - A \dot{x} = -\frac{V_2}{\beta_e} \dot{P}_2$$

**Step 2 — Symmetric cylinder with $V_1 = V_2 = V_t/2$ and load pressure $P_L = P_1 - P_2$.** Adding the two continuity equations:

$$2 \dot{x}_{avg} = \frac{V_t}{2 \beta_e} \dot{P}_L \Rightarrow \dot{P}_L = \frac{4 \beta_e}{V_t}\left(Q_L - A \dot{x}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q_L = K_q x_v - K_c P_L$:**

$$\dot{P}_L = \frac{4 \beta_e}{V_t}\left(K_q x_v - K_c P_L - A \dot{x}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Force balance on the piston (load is purely inertial $M \ddot{x}$):**

$$A P_L = M \ddot{x}$$

Thus $\dot{x} = \dfrac{1}{A} \int P_L \, dt$ — an integrating plant.

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace transform of the force balance** and use it to eliminate $x$ in the pressure equation. After algebraic elimination (full algebra in KTU 2024 Module 2 recommended reading, e.g. Merritt), the characteristic equation of the linearised third-order system is:

$$s^3 + \frac{4 \beta_e K_c}{V_t} s^2 + \frac{4 \beta_e A^2}{V_t M} s + \frac{4 \beta_e A K_q}{V_t M} x_v(s) = 0$$

**Step 6 — The undamped natural frequency** (when $K_c = 0$, i.e. ideal valve) is the coefficient of $s$ divided by the coefficient of $s^3$ (under the square root) when normalised:

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta_e A^2}{V_t M}}$$

This is the **hydraulic resonance frequency** — a parameter students are *required* to know.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Combined Electrical + Hydraulic Model (State-Space Form)

Combining the armature circuit, the valve torque motor, and the cylinder dynamics, the state vector is commonly chosen as:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_v \\ \dot{x}_v \\ P_L \\ \dot{x} \end{bmatrix}, \quad u = V_a$$

The state-space form is $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A \mathbf{x} + B u$, where:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1/\tau_v & K_{tv}/J_v & 0 \\
0 & 0 & -\dfrac{4\beta_e K_c}{V_t} & \dfrac{4\beta_e A}{V_t} \\
0 & 0 & A/M & 0
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ K_{av}/J_v \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

with $K_{tv}$ = torque-motor pressure gain, $K_{av}$ = torque-motor voltage gain, $J_v$ = valve spool inertia, $\tau_v$ = valve damping time-constant.

This 4-state model is the **standard KTU 2024 question** for "derive the state-space model of an EHSA used in active suspension".

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Actuator Model by VDC Application

| VDC Subsystem | Dominant Actuator | Order of Model | Bandwidth Required | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS / ESC brake modulator | EHSA (solenoid valve) | 1st order + delay | 10–15 Hz | $\tau_v \approx 5$ ms |
| Active suspension strut | Hydraulic cylinder | 3rd order (Merritt) | 5–10 Hz | $\omega_h \approx 80$ rad/s |
| Electric Power Steering (EPS) | DC motor + rack | 2nd order (neglecting $L_a$) | 20–30 Hz | $\tau_m \approx 30$ ms |
| eThrottle-by-Wire | DC motor + gear | 1st order | 5–8 Hz | $\tau_m \approx 50$ ms |
| Active anti-roll bar | DC motor / EHA | 2nd order | 3–5 Hz | $\tau_m \approx 80$ ms |
| Air suspension (CV) | Pneumatic cylinder | 2nd order | 1–3 Hz | $K_g \approx 20$ kN/m |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic VDC Actuator in Closed-Loop

The following Mermaid diagram captures the closed-loop architecture of a typical VDC system with explicit modelling of the actuator block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference Input r] --> sum1((Sum + -))
    fb[Feedback y] --> sum1
    sum1 --> ctrl[Controller C of s]
    ctrl --> sat[Saturation Block]
    sat --> dz[Dead Zone Block]
    dz --> act[Actuator G sub a of s]
    act --> plant[Vehicle Plant G sub p of s]
    plant --> out[Measured Output y]
    out --> fb
    plant --> dist[Disturbance d]
    dist --> out
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **controller** $C(s)$ computes a command $u_c$ based on error $e = r - y$.
- $u_c$ passes through **non-linearities** (saturation + dead-zone) before reaching the **actuator** $G_a(s)$.
- The actuator output drives the **vehicle plant** $G_p(s)$ (e.g., suspension, brake, steering).
- The output $y$ is fed back. A disturbance $d$ (road input, wind gust) injects at the plant.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EHSA (Active Suspension Strut)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cmd[ECU Command Voltage V sub a] --> amp[Power Amplifier]
    amp --> tm[Torque Motor]
    tm --> spool[Valve Spool x sub v]
    spool --> flw[Valve Flow Q sub L]
    flw --> cyl[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    cyl --> frc[Output Force F]
    frc --> mass[Vehicle Mass M]
    mass --> fb[Position / Acceleration Sensor]
    fb --> ecu[ECU Feedback y]
    ecu --> cmd
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The ECU closes the loop by comparing desired vs. measured body acceleration.
- The torque motor (TM) drives a flapper-nozzle or jet-pipe servo valve.
- The valve meters oil into the cylinder, generating force $F = A P_L$ on the sprung mass $M$.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — DC Motor Actuator with Saturation and Dead-Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uc[Controller Output u sub c] --> dz[Dead Zone Non Linearity]
    dz --> sat[Saturation Block]
    sat --> gain[Gain K sub m]
    gain --> int[Integrator 1 over s]
    int --> taulag[First Order Lag 1 over tau sub m s plus 1]
    taulag --> theta[Output Position theta]
    theta --> fb[Feedback]
    fb --> err[Error e]
    err --> uc
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The linearised DC motor block is $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ — one pole at $s=-1/\tau_m$ and one pole at the origin (integrator).
- Dead-zone and saturation sit **before** the motor gain, modelling valve overlap / voltage limits.
- Feedback is typically position or velocity.

### 4.4 State-Flow Diagram of the Electro-Hydraulic Servo Actuator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SlewUp: Command exceeds dead zone
    SlewUp --> ActiveTracking: Pressure builds omega sub h reached
    ActiveTracking --> SlewDown: Command drops below dead zone
    SlewDown --> Idle: Pressure bled down
    ActiveTracking --> Saturation: Command exceeds P sub s
    Saturation --> ActiveTracking: Command within bounds
    ActiveTracking --> [*]: System shutdown
```

**Reading the state diagram:**
- The actuator cycles through **Idle → Slew → Active → Saturation** states.
- Saturation is a *safety* state, not a fault — it represents the EHSA delivering maximum available pressure.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part A = 2 × 3 = 6 marks. Part B = 1 × 14 marks (with internal choice between Question A and Question B). Total = 20 marks for the module question.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Explain the role of an actuator in a closed-loop Vehicle Dynamic Control (VDC) system. Why is the dynamic model of the actuator preferred over an ideal (unity-gain) model in high-bandwidth VDC loops?**

**Model Answer:**

An actuator is the final control element in a VDC loop. It receives a low-power command signal from the ECU and converts it into a mechanical output (force/torque/pressure/displacement) that influences the vehicle state.

**Why dynamic model is preferred:**

1. **Finite bandwidth:** Every practical actuator has a dominant time-constant (e.g., 5–100 ms). At frequencies approaching this bandwidth, the actuator attenuates and phase-shifts the command signal, reducing the achievable closed-loop bandwidth and phase margin.
2. **Non-linearities:** Dead-zone causes steady-state error; saturation causes integrator wind-up; rate-limit adds extra phase lag.
3. **Stability:** If the controller is designed assuming a unity-gain actuator, the *real* closed-loop system may become unstable once the actuator's unmodelled pole is included (the so-called "hidden pole" problem).
4. **Performance verification:** A dynamic model allows time-domain (step response, settling time) and frequency-domain (Bode plot) verification of the controller before deployment.

Hence, the actuator is modelled as a **first-order lag** $G_a(s) = K_a/(\tau_a s + 1)$ (or second-order for hydraulic systems) and is *cascaded* with the plant in the simulation model.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**List and briefly explain any three non-linearities commonly present in automotive VDC actuators.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Saturation:** Every actuator has a maximum deliverable output — a DC motor cannot exceed its rated torque, a hydraulic cylinder cannot exceed its supply pressure. Mathematically: $y(t) = \text{sat}(u(t)) = \text{sgn}(u) \cdot \min(\vert u \vert, u_{max})$. Effect: integrator wind-up in PI controllers.

2. **Dead-zone:** A small command range around zero produces *no* output. Caused by valve spool overlap, brush friction in DC motors, or seal stiction. Effect: steady-state error and possible limit-cycle oscillation.

3. **Hysteresis:** The output for a given input differs between increasing and decreasing commands, forming a loop. Caused by magnetic hysteresis in motor laminations and seal friction in hydraulic cylinders. Effect: position error in steer-by-wire systems and a "sticky" feel in throttle-by-wire.

(Other acceptable non-linearities: **rate-limit / slew-rate**, **Coulomb friction**, **backlash in gearbox**.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Writing $G_a(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ without specifying whether the output is **position**, **velocity**, or **force** — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unit/dimensional ambiguity.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to **neglect $L_a$** in the DC motor model — examiners want a justification (electrical time-constant $\ll$ mechanical time-constant).
> - **Common mistake 3:** In hydraulic cylinder derivation, students often confuse $V_1$, $V_2$ and $V_t$ — examiners expect the symmetric-cylinder assumption $V_1 = V_2 = V_t/2$ to be **explicitly stated**.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In state-space, the $B$ matrix is often written incorrectly — ensure the input enters at the correct state (typically the valve voltage enters via the torque-motor current, which drives $\dot{x}_v$).
> - **Common mistake 5:** Skipping the sign convention in the force balance — examiners expect $\sum F = m \ddot{x}$ with all forces (including friction and load) explicitly listed.

---

#### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$ of an armature-controlled DC motor used as an electromechanical actuator in an Electric Power Steering (EPS) system. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same DC motor, with parameters $R_a = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 1 \text{ mH}$, $K_t = 0.05 \text{ N·m/A}$, $K_b = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $J = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$, $B = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m·s/rad}$, compute:
   (i) the electrical time-constant $\tau_e$,
   (ii) the mechanical time-constant $\tau_m$,
   (iii) the motor gain $K_m$,
   (iv) the simplified first-order transfer function. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation** — *See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes (Derivation A).*

Final result (full order):

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t)\right]}$$

Assumptions:
- Armature reaction neglected (constant field).
- Linear magnetic circuit ($K_t$ and $K_b$ are constants; $K_t = K_b$ in SI).
- Viscous friction only (Coulomb friction neglected).
- Constant temperature ($R_a$ constant).
- Rigid shaft (no torsional compliance).

**(b) Numerical computation:**

(i) **Electrical time-constant:**

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

(ii) **Mechanical time-constant:**

$$\tau_m = \frac{J \, R_a}{R_a B + K_b K_t} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-4})(1.0)}{(1.0)(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.05)(0.05)}$$

Denominator:

$$R_a B + K_b K_t = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_m = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0385 \text{ s} \approx 38.5 \text{ ms}$$

(iii) **Motor gain:**

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t} = \frac{0.05}{2.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 19.23 \text{ rad/s/V}$$

(iv) **Simplified transfer function** (neglecting $L_a$ since $\tau_e = 1 \text{ ms} \ll \tau_m = 38.5 \text{ ms}$):

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)} = \frac{19.23}{s(0.0385 s + 1)}$$

---

#### Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the construction and working of an Electro-Hydraulic Servo Actuator (EHSA) used in an automotive active-suspension system. Identify the energy-conversion stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from first principles (continuity equation in the cylinder and force balance on the piston), derive the expression for the **hydraulic natural frequency** $\omega_h$ of a symmetric double-acting cylinder of piston area $A$, moving mass $M$, effective bulk modulus $\beta_e$, and total trapped volume $V_t$. Comment on how increasing each of these parameters individually affects $\omega_h$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Construction and Working of EHSA:**

An EHSA is a closed-loop hydraulic position/force actuator comprising four sub-systems:

1. **Torque Motor (Electrical → Mechanical):** A permanent-magnet DC or voice-coil actuator that converts the ECU's voltage command $V_a$ into a small rotary/linear force on the valve spool.
2. **Servo Valve (Mechanical → Hydraulic):** A flapper-nozzle, jet-pipe, or direct-drive proportional valve that meters hydraulic fluid from the high-pressure supply $P_s$ to the cylinder chambers as a function of spool displacement $x_v$.
3. **Hydraulic Cylinder (Hydraulic → Mechanical):** A double-acting piston-cylinder that converts the pressure differential $P_L$ across the piston into a force $F = A P_L$ and piston motion $x$.
4. **Displacement Sensor (Mechanical → Electrical):** An LVDT or rotary encoder providing feedback of the actual piston position to the ECU.

**Energy conversion chain:** Electrical (ECU) → Mechanical (torque motor) → Hydraulic (valve + oil) → Mechanical (piston) → Vehicle body.

**Working principle:** A small error voltage drives the torque motor, displacing the valve spool by $x_v$. This meters oil at flow $Q_L$ into the cap-end, building pressure $P_1$ and relieving pressure $P_2$ on the rod-end. The resulting $\Delta P = P_L$ accelerates the piston (and the vehicle mass it supports) according to $A P_L = M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x}$. The piston position is fed back, closing the loop.

**(b) Derivation of $\omega_h$** — *See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation (Derivation B).*

Final result:

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta_e A^2}{V_t M}}$$

**Parameter sensitivity:**

- **Increase $A$ (larger piston):** $\omega_h \uparrow$ proportionally to $A$. Larger pistons deliver more force but are mechanically stiffer.
- **Increase $M$ (heavier sprung mass):** $\omega_h \downarrow$ as $1/\sqrt{M}$. Heavier vehicles have lower suspension bandwidth.
- **Increase $\beta_e$ (stiffer fluid, less entrained air):** $\omega_h \uparrow$. Removing air from hydraulic oil is critical — even 1% entrained air can reduce $\beta_e$ by an order of magnitude.
- **Increase $V_t$ (longer hoses, larger cylinder):** $\omega_h \downarrow$ as $1/\sqrt{V_t}$. This is why active-suspension struts are mounted close to the hydraulic supply.

---

### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Vehicle Dynamic Control – Modelling of Actuators):**

- ☐ An actuator is the **final control element** in a VDC loop — it converts a command into a mechanical output that influences vehicle state.
- ☐ Always distinguish between **ideal actuator** (unity gain, infinite bandwidth) and **practical actuator** (finite bandwidth, non-linear).
- ☐ The **hydraulic natural frequency** is $\omega_h = \sqrt{4 \beta_e A^2 / (V_t M)}$ — a key parameter for active-suspension design.
- ☐ The **DC motor transfer function** (full order) is **third order** in $s$ for $\Theta/V_a$. After neglecting $L_a$, it reduces to $K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]$.
- ☐ Justification for neglecting $L_a$: $\tau_e = L_a/R_a \ll \tau_m = J R_a / (K_b K_t)$. Always state this in the exam.
- ☐ The five non-linearities to remember: **Saturation, Dead-zone, Rate-limit, Hysteresis, Coulomb friction**. Each can be represented by a block between the controller and the plant.
- ☐ Polytropic index $n$ for pneumatic: $n = 1$ (slow/isothermal) to $n = 1.4$ (fast/adiabatic).
- ☐ EHSAs use a **four-stage energy-conversion chain**: Electrical → Mechanical (TM) → Hydraulic (valve) → Mechanical (piston).
- ☐ State-space models use state vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_v, \dot{x}_v, P_L, \dot{x}]^T$ for a complete EHSA model.
- ☐ **Bandwidth rule of thumb:** Actuator bandwidth should be **5–10× the desired closed-loop bandwidth** to avoid phase-margin erosion.
- ☐ **Memorise the typical time-constants:** ABS valve ≈ 5 ms, EPS motor ≈ 30 ms, eThrottle motor ≈ 50 ms, active-suspension strut ≈ 12 ms.
- ☐ In board answers, **always state the units** of $K_m$, $\tau_m$, $\omega_h$, and $\zeta$ to avoid losing 1 mark for dimensional ambiguity.
- ☐ The Merritt reduced model of an EHSA: $\dfrac{X_p(s)}{X_v(s)} = \dfrac{K_v/A}{s(s^2/\omega_h^2 + 2\zeta_h s/\omega_h + 1)}$ — a guaranteed question topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors for Automobile Control and Sensors for Detecting Vehicle Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUTUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors for Automobile Control and Sensors for Detecting Vehicle Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sensors for Automobile Control — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Automobile control sensors are electromechanical/electronic transducers that continuously monitor the kinematic, dynamic, thermodynamic, and chemical state variables of the vehicle's powertrain, chassis, body, and drivetrain subsystems, and convert these physical quantities into standardized electrical signals (analog voltage/current or digital protocols such as CAN, LIN, SPI) that are interpreted by the Electronic Control Unit (ECU) for closed-loop and open-loop control of vehicle functions.*

In the modern automobile, **every dynamic subsystem is now sensor-driven**. These sensors form the *afferent (input) nervous system* of the vehicle, replacing the purely mechanical/hydraulic linkages that dominated pre-1990s vehicles. They enable Engine Control Units (ECU), Transmission Control Units (TCU), Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) modules, Electronic Stability Control (ESC) modules, and Body Control Modules (BCM) to execute real-time control laws.

### 1.2 Sensors for Detecting Vehicle Environment — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Perception sensors (or exteroceptive sensors) are devices that detect and characterize objects, road conditions, infrastructure, and other agents in the external environment surrounding the vehicle. They provide the raw spatio-temporal data — range, bearing, velocity, and class — required for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), Autonomous Driving (AD), and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication frameworks.*

These are the sensors that allow the vehicle to "see," "hear," and "feel" the world outside its body shell.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Vehicle as a Human Body**
> Imagine a human driving a car. The driver's *inner ear* (vestibular system + proprioception) tells the brain the body's orientation and acceleration — these are exactly like the **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** sensors in a vehicle (accelerometers, gyroscopes). The driver's *eyes and ears* warn about pedestrians, other cars, and traffic lights — these are analogous to **Radar, Lidar, and Cameras** (the vehicle's exteroceptive sensors). The driver's *skin pressure* on the steering wheel and pedals tells them the steering angle and brake pedal travel — these are like the **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** and **Brake Pedal Position Sensor**.
> Without these senses, the brain (ECU) cannot issue commands to the arms (steering actuator) or legs (brake/throttle actuators). A modern vehicle is therefore a **cybernetic extension of the driver**, augmented by hundreds of micro-sensors.

### 1.4 Categorical Map of Automotive Sensors

Sensors are taxonomically classified along two axes: **functional location** and **physical principle of transduction**.

| **Axis** | **Categories** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| Functional | Powertrain | MAP, MAF, O₂, CKP, CMP, Knock |
| Functional | Chassis / Dynamics | Wheel Speed, Yaw Rate, Steering Angle, Lng/Lat Accelerometer |
| Functional | Body / Comfort | Light, Rain, Cabin Temperature, Seat Occupancy |
| Functional | Environment (Exteroceptive) | Radar, Lidar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GNSS, V2X |
| Principle | Resistive | TPS, ECT (NTC thermistor) |
| Principle | Piezoelectric | Knock, Accelerometer |
| Principle | Hall-effect | CKP/CMP, Wheel Speed |
| Principle | Optical / Photonic | Camera, Lidar, Light Sensor |
| Principle | Electromagnetic | Radar (24/77 GHz), GNSS |

### 1.5 Core Physical Constants & Standards

- **Speed of light**: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- **Automotive radar bands**: $24\ \text{GHz}$ (narrowband/ultra-wideband) and **77–81 GHz** (long-range, $76\text{–}77\ \text{GHz}$ being the K-band allocation per ETSI EN 302 264)
- **GNSS carrier frequencies**: L1 = **1575.42 MHz**, L5 = **1176.45 MHz**
- **CAN bus data rate**: Classical CAN — **1 Mbit/s**, CAN-FD — up to **5 Mbit/s**
- **Standard supply voltage**: $V_{cc} = 5\ \text{V}$ (legacy) or **3.3 V/12 V** (modern)
- **Operating temperature range**: $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$ (under-hood: up to $150^{\circ}\text{C}$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sensors for Automobile Control (Interoceptive Sensors)

#### 2.1.1 Engine / Powertrain Sensors

**1. Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor**
- *Function*: Measures absolute pressure inside the intake manifold.
- *Working Principle*: A silicon micromachined diaphragm flexes under pressure, changing the resistance of a Wheatstone bridge (piezoresistive effect).
- *Use*: ECU calculates air mass entering cylinders using the speed-density equation: $\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot p_{man} \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{man}}$.

**2. Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor**
- *Function*: Directly measures the mass of air entering the engine.
- *Principle*: Hot-film anemometer — a heated element is cooled by incoming air; the current required to maintain constant temperature is proportional to mass flow.
- *Equation*: $I^2 \cdot R \propto \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.

**3. Oxygen (λ / O₂) Sensor**
- *Function*: Measures the air–fuel ratio in the exhaust via the Nernst equation.
- *Output*: Voltage steps between $\approx 0.1\ \text{V}$ (lean) and $\approx 0.9\ \text{V}$ (rich) at stoichiometric point ($\lambda = 1$).
- *Nernst EMF*: $E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2, \text{cathode}}}{p_{O_2, \text{anode}}}\right)$, where $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$ is Faraday's constant.

**4. Crankshaft Position (CKP) & Camshaft Position (CMP) Sensors**
- *Principle*: Variable Reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect.
- *Function*: Provides engine RPM ($N$) and top-dead-centre (TDC) reference for sequential fuel injection and ignition timing.

**5. Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**
- *Principle*: Potentiometric (resistive) or Hall-effect non-contact.
- *Output*: $0.5\ \text{V}$ (idle) to $4.5\ \text{V}$ (WOT) — duty cycle input to ECU.

**6. Knock Sensor**
- *Principle*: Piezoelectric crystal (PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate).
- *Detection*: Detects vibration frequencies in the **5–15 kHz** band characteristic of engine knock.

**7. Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) & Intake Air Temperature (IAT)**
- *Principle*: Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor: $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$, where $\beta$ is the material constant ($\approx 3435$ for typical automotive NTC).

#### 2.1.2 Chassis & Vehicle Dynamics Sensors

These are central to the **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**, **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)**, and **Electric Power Steering (EPS)**.

**1. Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS)**
- *Variants*: (a) Passive VR — AC voltage output, frequency proportional to wheel speed; (b) Active Hall-effect — digital square wave, AMR (Anisotropic Magnetoresistive) in modern units.
- *Function*: Backbone of ABS/ESC; allows slip ratio $\lambda = \frac{v_{\text{wheel}} - v_{\text{veh}}}{v_{\text{veh}}}$ calculation.

**2. Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS)**
- *Principle*: Coriolis-effect vibrating-ring gyroscope or MEMS tuning-fork.
- *Output*: Angular velocity $\omega_z$ about the vertical axis (deg/s or rad/s).
- *Equation*: Coriolis force $F_c = 2 m \vec{v} \times \vec{\omega}$.

**3. Steering Angle Sensor (SAS)**
- *Principle*: Optical encoder (Gray code) or Hall-based resolver.
- *Output*: Steering wheel angle $\delta_{sw}$ and steering wheel angular velocity $\dot{\delta}_{sw}$.

**4. Lateral & Longitudinal Accelerometer**
- *Principle*: MEMS capacitive or piezoelectric.
- *Output*: Specific force $\vec{a} - \vec{g}$; used to estimate sideslip angle $\beta$ and vehicle speed indirectly.

**5. Ride-Height / Suspension Position Sensor**
- *Principle*: LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) or Hall-effect linear.
- *Use*: Active suspension, headlight auto-leveling.

#### 2.1.3 Body, Comfort & Safety Sensors

- **Airbag Crash Sensor**: Piezoelectric accelerometer; deploys airbag if $\Delta v >$ threshold within time window.
- **Seat Occupancy Sensor**: Capacitive mat, weight-based strain gauge.
- **Light Sensor (Auto Headlamp)**: Photodiode/phototransistor.
- **Rain Sensor**: Optical IR reflectance on windshield.
- **TPMS (Tyre Pressure Monitoring System)**: Piezoresistive inside each wheel; transmits via 433 MHz RF.

### 2.2 Sensors for Detecting Vehicle Environment (Exteroceptive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Perception Sensor Stack Hierarchy**
> Modern ADAS and AD systems use a **heterogeneous sensor fusion** approach, combining complementary strengths of different modalities.

**1. Automotive Radar (RAdio Detection And Ranging)**
- *Bands*: **24 GHz** (short/medium range, SRR/MRR, 0.2–30 m) and **77 GHz** (long range, LRR, up to 250 m).
- *Principle*: FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave). Transmitted chirp frequency: $f_t(t) = f_c + S \cdot t$, where $S = \Delta f / T_c$ is chirp slope.
- *Beat frequency for range*: $f_b = \frac{2 R S}{c} \implies R = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2 S}$.
- *Doppler frequency for radial velocity*: $f_D = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$.

**2. Lidar (LIght Detection And Ranging)**
- *Wavelength*: Typically **905 nm** (silicon-compatible) or **1550 nm** (eye-safe, longer range).
- *Principle*: Time-of-Flight (ToF): $R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$.
- *Mechanism*: Mechanical spinning, MEMS mirror, or Optical Phased Array (OPA) — solid-state.
- *Point cloud*: Up to ~3 million points/second (e.g., Velodyne, Hesai, Luminar).

**3. Camera (Mono, Stereo, Surround)**
- *Sensor*: CMOS image sensor (rolling/global shutter).
- *Functions*: Lane detection (LDW/LKA), traffic sign recognition (TSR), object classification, semantic segmentation.
- *Pinhole model*: $x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$, $y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$, with focal length $f$.

**4. Ultrasonic Sensor**
- *Range*: **0.1 – 5 m** (parking aid).
- *Principle*: Piezoelectric transmitter/receiver pair; ToF: $R = \frac{c_{\text{air}} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$.
- *Note*: $c_{\text{air}} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ at 20 °C.

**5. GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)**
- *Systems*: GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), IRNSS/NavIC (India), BeiDou (China).
- *Accuracy*: Standalone ~3–5 m; with RTK corrections **<2 cm**.
- *Position fix*: Trilateration using pseudoranges.

**6. V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) Communication**
- *Protocols*: **DSRC** (802.11p, 5.9 GHz) and **C-V2X** (3GPP Release 14+, 5.9 GHz).
- *Use*: Cooperative awareness messages (CAM), hazard warnings, GLOSA (Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory).

**7. IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) — Bridging Categories**
- *Composition*: 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope + (often) 3-axis magnetometer.
- *Function*: Used in *both* interoceptive (dead reckoning) and exteroceptive (sensor fusion with GPS) modes.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table — Critical Equations**

| **#** | **Quantity / Sensor** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed-Density (MAP) | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\, p_{man}\, V_d\, N}{2 R\, T_{man}}$ | $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ for air |
| 2 | NTC Thermistor | $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | $\beta \approx 3435\ \text{K}$ |
| 3 | Nernst (λ Sensor) | $E = \dfrac{R T}{n F}\ln\!\dfrac{p_{O_2}^{\text{cath}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{an}}}$ | $n=4,\ F=96485\ \text{C/mol}$ |
| 4 | Coriolis (Yaw Gyro) | $F_c = 2 m\, (\vec{v} \times \vec{\omega})$ | Vector triple product |
| 5 | Radar Range (FMCW) | $R = \dfrac{c\, f_b}{2\, S}$ | $S = \Delta f / T_c$ chirp slope |
| 6 | Radar Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{c\, f_D}{2 f_c}$ | Doppler shift relation |
| 7 | Lidar / Ultrasonic ToF | $R = \dfrac{c\, \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3\times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ (Lidar) |
| 8 | Speed of Sound | $c_{\text{air}} = 331 + 0.6\, T\ (^{\circ}\text{C})$ | $\approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ at 20 °C |
| 9 | Pinhole Camera | $\dfrac{1}{Z} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{Z'}$ | Thin-lens imaging |
| 10 | Slip Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R - v}{\max(\omega R, v)}$ | ABS / traction control |
| 11 | Vehicle Dynamic Equation (Steady-State Yaw) | $\dfrac{\omega_z}{\delta} = \dfrac{v/L}{1 + K\, v^{2}}$ | $K = \dfrac{m}{2 L^{2}}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{C_{\alpha r}} - \dfrac{b}{C_{\alpha f}}\right)$ |
| 12 | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{cc}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | Sensor signal conditioning |
| 13 | Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G\,\varepsilon$ | $G \approx 2.0$ for constantan |
| 14 | Pinhole FOV | $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2 f}\right)$ | $d$ = sensor width |
| 15 | Free-Space Path Loss (Radar) | $\text{FSPL} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | Friis equation in far field |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In radar range equation, students frequently forget the factor of **2** in the denominator. Remember — the wave travels to the target *and back*, hence $R = c \Delta t / 2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety compliance**: Euro NCAP 2025+ mandates **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** with pedestrian detection — impossible without radar+camera fusion.
- **Emission control**: λ sensor closed-loop control enables three-way catalytic converter efficiency >95 %.
- **Fuel economy**: MAP + MAF + IAT fusion improves volumetric efficiency estimation, giving **5–15 %** fuel savings.
- **Autonomy**: SAE Level 3+ requires sensor redundancy across modalities — if LiDAR fails, radar + camera must compensate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Radar Range and Velocity Resolution

**Given:** An FMCW radar transmits a chirp with starting frequency $f_c$, bandwidth $\Delta f$, and chirp duration $T_c$. The chirp slope is:

$$S = \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} \quad \left[\text{Hz/s}\right]$$

The instantaneous transmitted frequency is therefore:

$$f_t(t) = f_c + S\,t$$

The reflected signal from a stationary target at range $R$ arrives after a round-trip delay:

$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

The received frequency is:

$$f_r(t) = f_c + S\,(t - \tau)$$

The mixer output (after multiplying $f_t$ and $f_r$ and low-pass filtering) yields a **beat frequency**:

$$f_b = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = S\,\tau = S \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

Therefore, the **range equation** follows directly:

$$R = \frac{c\, f_b}{2\, S}$$

This derivation is essential for KTU short-answer derivations. Each line must be justified.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 1 — MAP Sensor Speed-Density Calculation

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine has displacement $V_d = 2.0\ \text{L}$, runs at $N = 3000\ \text{rpm}$. The MAP sensor reads $p_{man} = 60\ \text{kPa}$, IAT reads $T_{man} = 333\ \text{K}$, and volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.88$. Calculate the mass air flow rate. Assume $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ for air.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing speed-density equation:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_{vol}\, p_{man}\, V_d\, N}{2\, R\, T_{man}}$$

**Step 2 — Note that $N$ is in rpm but the formula expects revolutions per second, so divide by 60. Also, for a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once per 2 revolutions, hence the factor of 2 in the denominator. Here the formula already encodes the 2-stroke/4-stroke conversion via the 2 in the denominator, so we plug $N$ in rev/s:**

$$N = 3000\ \text{rpm} = 50\ \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute all numerical values:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{0.88 \times 60\,000\ \text{Pa} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 \times 50\ \text{rev/s}}{2 \times 287\ \text{J/kg·K} \times 333\ \text{K}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.88 \times 60\,000 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 = 0.88 \times 60\,000 \times 0.1 = 5280$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator:**

$$2 \times 287 \times 333 = 191\,142$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{5280}{191\,142} \approx 0.0276\ \text{kg/s} \approx 27.6\ \text{g/s}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 2 — NTC Thermistor Resistance

> **Problem:** A coolant temperature sensor has $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298\ \text{K}$ and $\beta = 3435\ \text{K}$. Find its resistance at $T = 363\ \text{K}$ (90 °C).

**Step 1 — Write the NTC equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the bracket term:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{363} - \frac{1}{298}$$

$$= \frac{298 - 363}{363 \times 298} = \frac{-65}{108\,174} = -6.009 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $\beta$:**

$$\beta \times (-6.009 \times 10^{-4}) = 3435 \times (-6.009 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.064$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**

$$\exp(-2.064) \approx 0.127$$

**Step 5 — Final resistance:**

$$R_T = 10\ \text{k}\Omega \times 0.127 \approx 1.27\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The resistance drops from 10 kΩ to ~1.27 kΩ as the temperature rises from 25 °C to 90 °C — a clear *negative* temperature coefficient, as the name implies. This non-linear behaviour is exactly why the ECU uses a lookup table rather than a linear interpolation.

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 3 — Radar Range from Beat Frequency

> **Problem:** An automotive 77 GHz FMCW radar has chirp bandwidth $\Delta f = 200\ \text{MHz}$ and chirp duration $T_c = 40\ \mu\text{s}$. If the IF (beat) frequency is measured as $f_b = 256\ \text{kHz}$, determine the range $R$ to the target.

**Step 1 — Compute chirp slope:**

$$S = \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Hz/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply range equation:**

$$R = \frac{c\, f_b}{2\, S} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 256 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{12}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$3 \times 10^{8} \times 256 \times 10^{3} = 7.68 \times 10^{13}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$2 \times 5 \times 10^{12} = 10^{13}$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$R = \frac{7.68 \times 10^{13}}{10^{13}} = 7.68\ \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** With $\Delta f = 200\ \text{MHz}$, the theoretical range resolution is $\Delta R = c / (2 \Delta f) = 0.75\ \text{m}$, meaning two targets must be at least 0.75 m apart to be distinguished. This is why modern radars use 1–2 GHz bandwidth (resolution down to ~10 cm) for finer discrimination.

### 3.5 Analytical Problem 4 — Ultrasonic Parking Distance

> **Problem:** A car reverses at $v = 2\ \text{m/s}$. Rear ultrasonic sensors detect an obstacle at time-of-flight $\Delta t = 5.85\ \text{ms}$. Take $c_{\text{air}} = 343\ \text{m/s}$. Find (a) the obstacle distance, (b) the time-to-collision if the car continues at $v$, (c) whether ABS-style automatic braking should engage if the system threshold is **1.2 s**.

**Step 1 — Distance via ToF:**

$$R = \frac{c_{\text{air}} \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.00655}{2} \approx 1.003\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Time-to-collision:**

$$\text{TTC} = \frac{R}{v} = \frac{1.003}{2} \approx 0.50\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold comparison:**

$$\text{TTC} = 0.50\ \text{s} < 1.2\ \text{s}$$

Since TTC is below the 1.2 s threshold, the system **should issue automatic braking**.

### 3.6 Analytical Problem 5 — Camera Intrinsic Parameters & FOV

> **Problem:** A forward-facing ADAS camera has focal length $f = 6\ \text{mm}$ and image sensor width $d = 8.5\ \text{mm}$. Calculate (a) the horizontal field of view and (b) the angular resolution per pixel for a 1920-pixel-wide sensor.

**Step 1 — FOV formula:**

$$\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2 f}\right) = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{8.5}{2 \times 6}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.7083)$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$\arctan(0.7083) \approx 35.3^{\circ} \implies \text{FOV} \approx 70.6^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Per-pixel resolution:**

$$\theta_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{\text{FOV}}{N_{\text{pixels}}} = \frac{70.6^{\circ}}{1920} \approx 0.0368^{\circ} \approx 2.2\ \text{arcmin}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** At $R = 100\ \text{m}$, one pixel subtends $100 \times \tan(0.0368^{\circ}) \approx 6.4\ \text{cm}$. A pedestrian is typically ~0.5 m wide → only ~8 pixels wide, which is borderline for robust detection. This is why long-range cameras often use higher focal length / telephoto lenses, or why LiDAR is preferred for fine angular discrimination.

### 3.7 Slip Ratio & ABS Decision Logic (Short Analytical Table)

| **Operating State** | **Wheel Angular Speed $\omega$** | **Vehicle Speed $v$** | **Slip Ratio $\lambda$** | **ABS Action** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free Rolling | $\omega R_{eff} = v$ | $v$ | $\lambda = 0$ | None |
| Braking Hard | $\omega R_{eff} < v$ | $v$ | $\lambda > 0$ (locking) | Release pressure |
| Accelerating Hard | $\omega R_{eff} > v$ | $v$ | $\lambda < 0$ (spinning) | Reduce torque |

Typical ABS slip target: $\lambda^{*} \approx 0.10$–$0.20$ (peak $\mu$ region of the tire-road friction curve).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Vehicle Sensor Architecture & ECU Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *complete signal flow* from physical phenomena through sensors, signal conditioning, ECU processing, and finally to actuators — the canonical automotive sensor loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph env["EXTEROCEPTIVE SENSORS - Environment Detection"]
        rad["Automotive Radar<br>24 77 GHz FMCW"]
        lid["Lidar 905 1550 nm ToF"]
        cam["Camera Mono Stereo<br>Surround CMOS"]
        ult["Ultrasonic 40 58 kHz"]
        gnss["GNSS RTK Receiver"]
        v2x["V2X Modem<br>DSRC C-V2X"]
    end

    subgraph intf["SENSOR INTERFACE"]
        cond["Signal Conditioning<br>Amplification Filtering"]
        adc["ADC Resolution 12 16 bit"]
        can["CAN Bus CAN-FD<br>Ethernet SOME IP"]
    end

    subgraph fus["DATA FUSION LAYER"]
        fusUnit["Sensor Fusion ECU<br>Kalman Filter<br>Object Tracking"]
    end

    subgraph ecus["VEHICLE ECUs"]
        ecu1["Powertrain ECU<br>Engine and Transmission"]
        ecu2["Chassis ECU<br>ABS ESC EPS"]
        ecu3["Body ECU BCM<br>Lighting Climate"]
        ecu4["ADAS ECU<br>ACC AEB LKA"]
    end

    subgraph intsens["INTEROCEPTIVE SENSORS - Vehicle State"]
        map["MAP MAF Sensors"]
        oxy["Oxygen Lambda Sensor"]
        ckp["CKP CMP Hall Sensors"]
        tps["Throttle Position Sensor"]
        wss["Wheel Speed Sensor"]
        yaw["Yaw Rate Gyroscope"]

    subgraph act["ACTUATORS"]
        engAct["Engine Injector Ignition"]
        brkAct["Brake Hydraulic Modulator"]
        strAct["Steering Motor EPS"]

    end

    env --> cond
    intsens --> cond
    cond --> adc
    adc --> can
    can --> fusUnit
    can --> ecu1
    can --> ecu2
    can --> ecu3
    can --> ecu4
    fusUnit --> ecu4
    ecu1 --> engAct
    ecu1 --> trnAct
    ecu2 --> brkAct
    ecu2 --> strAct
    ecu3 --> strAct
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Radar Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    antTX["TX Antenna<br>77 GHz Chirp"] --> propT["Free Space Propagation"]
    propT --> tgt["Target Vehicle Pedestrian"]
    tgt --> propR["Reflected Echo"]
    propR --> antRX["RX Antenna Array<br>MIMO NxM"]
    antRX --> mix["Mixer Multiply fTX fRX"]
    mix --> lpf["Low Pass Filter<br>Extract Beat fB"]
    lpf --> adcR["ADC Sampling"]
    adcR --> fftR["Range FFT<br>2D FFT"]
    fftR --> cfar["CFAR Detection<br>Cell Averaging"]
    cfar --> doa["Angle Estimation<br>MUSIC or Beamforming"]
    doa --> track["Tracker<br>Kalman Filter"]
    track --> out["Object List<br>Range Velocity Angle"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Sensor Classification by Transduction Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["AUTOMOTIVE SENSORS"]
    root --> intP["INTEROCEPTIVE"]
    root --> extP["EXTEROCEPTIVE"]

    intP --> power["Powertrain"]
    intP --> chas["Chassis Dynamics"]
    intP --> body["Body and Safety"]

    power --> p1["MAP MAF O2 IAT ECT CKP CMP TPS Knock"]
    chas --> c1["Wheel Speed Yaw Rate SAS Accel Ride Height"]

    extP --> radar["Radar 24 77 GHz"]
    extP --> lidar["Lidar"]
    extP --> cam["Camera"]

    extP --> gnss["GNSS V2X"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop ABS Sensor-Actuator Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wheel["Wheel rotating with omega"] --> wss["Wheel Speed Sensor"]
    vss["Vehicle Speed from ESP"] --> comp["Comparator ECU"]
    wss --> comp
    comp --> slip["Compute Slip Lambda"]
    slip --> ctrl["PID Fuzzy Controller<br>Target Lambda 0.15"]
    ctrl --> valve["Inlet and Outlet<br>Solenoid Valves"]

    ctrl --> diag["Diagnostic and<br>Fault Memory"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Interpretation:** The arrows in these diagrams represent the *direction of information flow*, not physical wiring. In a real vehicle, the CAN bus is a *shared* medium — every ECU "listens" to every message and filters based on the CAN-ID. This bus architecture is why sensor data is intrinsically broadcast rather than point-to-point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any **three** interoceptive sensors used in an Electronic Stability Program (ESP), stating their physical principle and the variable they measure.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS)** — Hall-effect / VR type — measures individual wheel angular velocity $\omega_w$ [rad/s] — enables per-wheel slip ratio computation.
2. **Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS)** — MEMS Coriolis gyroscope — measures vehicle angular velocity about vertical axis $\omega_z$ [deg/s] — used to compare actual vs. desired yaw rate.
3. **Steering Angle Sensor (SAS)** — Optical encoder / Hall resolver — measures steering wheel angle $\delta_{sw}$ [deg] and rate — provides driver intent input.

(Any three correctly mapped sensors earn full marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between a **MAP sensor** and a **MAF sensor**. Why are both used together in some modern engines?*

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **MAP Sensor** | **MAF Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| Measures | Manifold absolute pressure (kPa) | Mass air flow rate (kg/s) |
| Principle | Piezoresistive silicon diaphragm | Hot-film anemometer |
| Inference type | Indirect (speed-density) | Direct |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Response | Fast pressure pulse tracking | Slower, intake lag |

**Why combined:** MAP corrects MAF transient errors (back-purge, reversion), and MAF provides barometric-pressure-independent mass flow. Modern **speed-density + MAF fusion** yields best accuracy across transients and steady states — improves fuel trim and emissions compliance.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Sensors for Automobile Control*

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric knock sensor**. How is the ECU able to differentiate knock from normal combustion noise?*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- A **piezoelectric crystal** (typically Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT) is mounted on a seismic mass inside a steel housing.
- The crystal is sandwiched between two electrodes connected to a coaxial shielded cable.
- The sensor is bolted into the engine block between cylinders (often cylinder 2 and 3 for inline-4), picking up structural vibration.

**Working:**
- During knock, high-frequency pressure oscillations in the combustion chamber (typically **5–15 kHz**, occasionally up to 25 kHz) excite the engine block.
- These vibrations mechanically stress the PZT crystal, generating a charge proportional to acceleration: $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$, where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient [C/N].
- The output voltage is amplified and fed to the ECU's knock-IC.

**Discrimination logic:**
- The ECU performs **real-time FFT** of the vibration signal.
- Normal combustion has a **smooth, broad-band** signature; knock produces a **sharp, narrow peak** at the engine's characteristic knock frequency $f_k$, which depends on bore diameter $D$ and cylinder geometry: $f_k \approx \dfrac{c_{\text{sound, gas}}}{2 D}$ (for end-gas resonance).
- When the FFT magnitude at $f_k$ exceeds a calibrated threshold, the ECU **retards ignition timing** by typically 2°–5° in 0.5° steps until the peak disappears.

**Sketch (description):** Engine block → threaded mounting stud → seismic mass → PZT disc with electrodes → coaxial output lead → ECU.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
*A wheel speed sensor of Hall-effect type produces a digital square wave with frequency $f = 800\ \text{Hz}$ when the wheel rotates. The wheel has effective rolling radius $R_{eff} = 0.32\ \text{m}$ and there are $N = 48$ tone-wheel teeth. If the vehicle speed is $v = 80\ \text{km/h}$, calculate:*
*(i) the wheel angular velocity in rad/s.*
*(ii) the corresponding wheel speed in rpm.*
*(iii) the slip ratio if ABS identifies locking tendency.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i):** Frequency $f$ relates to wheel rotation by:

$$f = \frac{N \cdot \omega_w}{2\pi}$$

Solving for $\omega_w$:

$$\omega_w = \frac{2\pi f}{N} = \frac{2\pi \times 800}{48} = \frac{5026.55}{48} \approx 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step (ii):** Convert to rpm:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{\omega_w \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{104.72 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{6283.2}{6.2832} \approx 1000\ \text{rpm}$$

(Alternative direct check: $f = N_{rpm} \times N / 60 \Rightarrow N_{rpm} = 60 f / N = 60 \times 800 / 48 = 1000$ rpm.)

**Step (iii):** Compute vehicle speed in m/s:

$$v = 80\ \text{km/h} = \frac{80\,000}{3600} = 22.22\ \text{m/s}$$

Wheel peripheral speed:

$$v_w = \omega_w R_{eff} = 104.72 \times 0.32 = 33.51\ \text{m/s}$$

Since $v_w > v$, the wheel is **spinning** (driving force > friction), so this is an **accelerating slip**, not a braking slip:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_w - v}{\max(v_w, v)} = \frac{33.51 - 22.22}{33.51} = \frac{11.29}{33.51} \approx 0.337$$

$$\lambda \approx 33.7\ \%$$

For traction control intervention (not ABS), the target slip is $\lambda^{*} \approx 0.10$–$0.15$, so TC would **reduce engine torque**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Sensors for Detecting Vehicle Environment*

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
*Explain the principle of an **FMCW automotive radar**. Derive the relationship between target range $R$ and beat frequency $f_b$. Mention two applications in ADAS.*

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
- A Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar transmits a *continuous* chirp signal whose frequency ramps linearly from $f_c$ to $f_c + \Delta f$ over chirp duration $T_c$.
- The transmitted and reflected signals are mixed in a homodyne receiver. The mixer output yields a beat frequency that encodes target range and Doppler (velocity).

**Derivation (already covered in Section 3.1 — re-derive here concisely):**

Transmitted instantaneous frequency:

$$f_t(t) = f_c + S\,t,\quad S = \frac{\Delta f}{T_c}$$

Received signal delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$f_r(t) = f_c + S\,(t - \tau)$$

Beat frequency (after mixer + LPF):

$$f_b = S\,\tau = \frac{2 S R}{c}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{c\, f_b}{2 S} = \frac{c\, T_c\, f_b}{2\, \Delta f}$$

**Two ADAS Applications:**
1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — long-range radar (77 GHz, up to 250 m) maintains headway to preceding vehicle.
2. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — medium-range radar monitors adjacent lanes for overtaking vehicles.
3. *(Optional)* **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — short/medium radar triggers braking when collision imminent.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
*A forward-looking ADAS sensor suite comprises a **camera, a Lidar, and a radar**. Justify why all three are used in a fused manner rather than relying on a single modality. Tabulate the comparative strengths.*

**Model Solution:**

**Justification (Sensor Fusion Rationale):**
Each modality has *complementary* strengths and weaknesses. Relying on any single sensor creates a *single point of failure* and limits operational design domain (ODD).

| **Property** | **Camera** | **Lidar** | **Radar** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 0–250 m | 0–250 m | 0–250 m |
| Range Resolution | Poor (depth ambiguity) | Excellent (cm-level) | Good (~10 cm @ 1 GHz BW) |
| Angular Resolution | Excellent (~0.04°/px) | Excellent (0.1°–0.4°) | Moderate (~1°) |
| Object Classification | Excellent (CNN-based) | Poor (geometric only) | Poor |
| Works in Rain/Fog/Snow | Degraded | Degraded | Robust |
| Works in Darkness | No (needs IR/headlamp) | Yes | Yes |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Velocity Measurement | Indirect (optical flow) | Indirect (multi-frame) | Direct (Doppler) |
| Detectability of metal | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Detectability of soft tissue | Yes | Partial | Weak |
| Latency | ~50–100 ms | ~50–100 ms | ~20–40 ms |

**Conclusion:** Camera provides *semantic understanding* (lanes, signs, lights); Lidar provides *precise 3D geometry*; Radar provides *robust velocity and all-weather operation*. Their fusion, typically via **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **deep-learning-based fusion networks**, yields *robustness, redundancy, and accuracy* unattainable by any single modality.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting factor of 2 in radar/lidar range** — Always remember the wave travels to *and* from the target.
> 2. **Confusing MAP vs. MAF principles** — MAP is *pressure-based speed-density*; MAF is *direct mass flow*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing these.
> 3. **Stating units incorrectly** — Yaw rate in deg/s vs. rad/s, pressure in kPa vs. bar, frequency in GHz vs. MHz. Always write the unit explicitly.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers** — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a labelled sketch even if the explanation is partial. Always draw a block diagram.
> 5. **Confusing 24 GHz vs. 77 GHz radar** — 24 GHz is for short range; 77 GHz for long range. Students frequently swap these.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivations** — When deriving radar range, explicitly state: "assuming stationary target, narrowband chirp, no Doppler-induced range migration."
> 7. **Not mentioning sensor fusion** — In any ADAS question (even partial), reference to *multi-sensor fusion* is a high-yield bonus point.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensors for Vehicle Dynamics (Module 2)**

**Powertrain / Interoceptive Sensors:**
- **MAP** (piezoresistive, kPa) — indirect air mass via speed-density.
- **MAF** (hot-film, kg/s) — direct air mass.
- **O₂ / Lambda** (Nernst, V) — closed-loop air-fuel trim.
- **CKP / CMP** (Hall/VR) — engine speed and phase.
- **TPS** (potentiometric/Hall) — driver throttle demand.
- **Knock** (piezoelectric, 5–15 kHz band) — ignition retard feedback.
- **ECT / IAT** (NTC thermistor) — temperature compensation.

**Chassis / Dynamics Sensors:**
- **Wheel Speed Sensor** — Hall or VR; foundation of ABS/ESC.
- **Yaw Rate Sensor** — Coriolis MEMS gyro.
- **Steering Angle Sensor** — optical encoder.
- **Lateral / Longitudinal Accelerometer** — MEMS capacitive.
- **Ride-Height Sensor** — LVDT / Hall linear.

**Body / Safety Sensors:**
- **Crash sensor** (front + side) — piezoelectric, airbag deployment.
- **TPMS** — in-wheel piezoresistive + 433 MHz RF.
- **Rain/Light sensor** — optoelectronic.

**Environment / Exteroceptive Sensors:**
- **Radar** — FMCW, 24 GHz SRR, 77 GHz LRR. Range $R = c f_b / (2S)$, Velocity $v_r = c f_D / (2 f_c)$.
- **Lidar** — 905/1550 nm ToF; $R = c \Delta t / 2$; gives dense 3-D point cloud.
- **Camera** — CMOS, pinhole model; semantic + lane + sign recognition.
- **Ultrasonic** — 40–58 kHz; short range (0.1–5 m) for parking.
- **GNSS** — L1/L5; RTK gives cm-level accuracy.
- **V2X** — DSRC (802.11p) / C-V2X; cooperative awareness.
- **IMU** — 3-axis accel + gyro (± mag); dead reckoning.

**Universal Equations (must memorize):**
- Speed-density: $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\, p_{man}\, V_d\, N}{2 R\, T_{man}}$
- NTC: $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$
- Nernst: $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(p_{\text{cath}}/p_{\text{an}})$
- Radar range: $R = \dfrac{c\, f_b}{2\, S}$
- Radar velocity: $v_r = \dfrac{c\, f_D}{2\, f_c}$
- ToF range: $R = \dfrac{c\, \Delta t}{2}$
- Slip ratio: $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R_{eff} - v}{\max(\omega R_{eff}, v)}$
- FOV camera: $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$

**Engineering Rule of Thumb:**
> *No single sensor modality is sufficient for Level 3+ autonomy. Always fuse.*

**End of Module 2 Notes — Sensors for Automobile Control and Environment Detection**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Central Tyre Inflation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Central Tyre Inflation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Central Tyre Inflation System (CTIS)** is an integrated, on-board pneumatic management subsystem that enables the driver (or an automated controller) to **remotely monitor, regulate, and adjust the inflation pressure of each pneumatic tyre** of a vehicle — individually or simultaneously — while the vehicle is **either stationary or in motion**, without requiring the operator to exit the cabin or manually approach the wheels.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBMUT604 – Vehicle Dynamics, Module 2), CTIS is treated as a **terrain-adaptive suspension support technology** that actively modifies the **vertical tyre stiffness ($K_t$)**, the **contact patch footprint ($A_c$)**, and consequently the **mobility-resistance parameters** of an off-road or military vehicle. The system fundamentally alters the **ground pressure ($\sigma_g$)** exerted by the tyre, defined as:

$$\sigma_g = \frac{W_{w}}{A_c}$$

where $W_w$ is the dynamic wheel load and $A_c$ is the tyre–ground contact area. By varying $\sigma_g$ in real time, the CTIS optimises traction, ride comfort, fuel economy, and soil-trafficability simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBMUT604 – Module 2):** CTIS is studied under the umbrella of *active chassis systems for off-highway vehicles*. Students must be able to **(i)** identify the functional architecture, **(ii)** explain the working principle, and **(iii)** evaluate the influence of inflation pressure on vehicle dynamics parameters such as rolling resistance, tractive effort, and ride frequency.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **modern home central air-conditioning system with individual room thermostats** that you can adjust from a single wall-mounted control panel — without walking to each room. The *Central Tyre Inflation System* is the **automotive equivalent of this for tyres**:

- The **driver's cabin-mounted control panel** acts as the "thermostat zone controller."
- The **on-board air compressor** acts as the "compressor unit" that supplies pressurised air.
- The **solenoid-operated valve bank + air lines running to each wheel** acts as the "ductwork."
- The **rotary unions at the wheel hubs** act as the "room vents," allowing stationary air to enter a spinning wheel without leakage.
- The **electronic control unit (ECU)** acts as the "smart thermostat," continuously reading pressure sensors and commanding inflation/deflation.

So just as you would set your bedroom to **24 °C in summer and 28 °C in winter** without opening a window, the CTIS lets a soldier or off-road driver set the front-left tyre to **220 kPa for highway cruising** and the rear tyres to **110 kPa for soft sand** — all from inside the cabin, even while the vehicle is moving.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does it matter for vehicle dynamics?** Because tyre pressure is the **single most influential controllable parameter** affecting the vertical stiffness, damping characteristic, and footprint geometry of a pneumatic tyre. Lowering pressure widens the contact patch (better traction on soft ground), while raising pressure reduces rolling resistance (better fuel economy on hard terrain).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Military 4×4) | Typical Value (Agricultural) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Highway Inflation Pressure | $P_{hwy}$ | 250 – 350 | 180 – 220 | kPa |
| Off-Road Inflation Pressure | $P_{off}$ | 100 – 180 | 80 – 120 | kPa |
| Compressor Delivery Pressure | $P_{comp}$ | 800 – 1000 | 600 – 800 | kPa |
| Compressor Flow Rate | $Q_{comp}$ | 30 – 60 | 20 – 40 | L/min |
| System Response Time (Full Inflation) | $t_{resp}$ | 30 – 90 | 60 – 120 | s |
| Rotary Union Leakage Rate | $Q_{leak}$ | **< 5** | **< 5** | mL/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions:** All pressures are measured **gauge** (i.e., above atmospheric). Industry convention uses **bar** in Europe and **psi** in North America; KTU 2024 standardises on **kPa** with **1 bar = 100 kPa** and **1 psi ≈ 6.895 kPa**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of CTIS

The CTIS can be decomposed into **five functional subsystems**, each with a distinct engineering role. Understanding the **division of labour** among these subsystems is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations.

### 2.1.1 Air Supply Subsystem
- An **engine-driven or electric air compressor** (typically reciprocating or scroll-type) draws filtered ambient air through an intake.
- A **receiver tank** (volume $V_{rec}$ ≈ 6 – 15 L) stores pressurised air at $P_{comp}$ to meet peak demand and dampen pulsations.
- A **pressure regulator** maintains the system at a stable delivery pressure irrespective of engine RPM fluctuations.
- A **dehydrator/desiccant filter** removes moisture to prevent corrosion in the wheel-end hardware.

### 2.1.2 Control Subsystem
- A **microprocessor-based ECU** accepts inputs from the driver's panel, wheel-mounted **pressure transducers**, vehicle speed sensor (VSS), and ABS wheel-speed sensors.
- The ECU executes **closed-loop pressure control** using a **proportional-integral (PI)** algorithm.
- Output signals drive the **solenoid valve bank** to inflate, deflate, or hold each tyre individually.

### 2.1.3 Distribution Subsystem
- A **manifold of solenoid valves** (one inflate valve + one deflate valve per wheel, plus isolation valves) routes air to/from the correct tyre.
- **Nylon or rubber reinforced air lines** (typically 6 mm OD) carry the air to each wheel-end.
- **Quick-disconnect couplings** are sometimes provided for emergency manual inflation.

### 2.1.4 Wheel-End Subsystem
- A **rotary union (slip ring with sealed air passage)** transfers stationary pressurised air to the rotating wheel hub without leakage — this is the *most mechanically critical* component.
- A **tyre valve stem adaptor** couples the rotary union to the tyre's inflation valve.
- Optional **in-wheel pressure sensor** transmits real-time data back to the ECU.

### 2.1.5 Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Subsystem
- A **cabin-mounted control panel** with discrete mode buttons (HIGHWAY, CROSS-COUNTRY, SAND, MUD, EMERGENCY) and a **digital LCD/LED display** showing per-tyre pressures.
- **Audible/visual alarms** for low pressure, high pressure, and system faults.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The CTIS operates in a **closed feedback loop**. The control sequence per KTU reference is:

1. **Driver Command:** Driver selects a target mode (e.g., "SAND") from the HMI panel. The ECU maps the mode to a **pre-programmed target pressure set-point** $P_{set}$.
2. **Sensor Polling:** The ECU reads the **current pressure $P_{meas}$** from each wheel's transducer (sampling rate ≈ 10 Hz).
3. **Error Computation:** The pressure error is computed as $e(t) = P_{set} - P_{meas}(t)$.
4. **Valve Actuation Decision:**
   - If $e(t) > +e_{th}$ → **Inflate valve opens** → compressor air flows into the tyre.
   - If $e(t) < -e_{th}$ → **Deflate valve opens** → tyre air vents to atmosphere through a silencer.
   - If $\vert e(t) \vert \le e_{th}$ → both valves **closed**, tyre is **isolated** (no flow).
5. **PI Control Law:** The ECU computes the valve open-time using a discrete PI law:
   $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \sum_{k=0}^{t} e(k) \cdot \Delta t$
6. **Re-sensing & Convergence:** After valve closure, the system waits a **dwell time $t_{dwell}$ ≈ 1 – 3 s** for the pressure to stabilise (thermal equilibration of the wheel-end air) and re-samples.
7. **HMI Update:** The display is refreshed with the new measured pressure, giving the driver a closed-loop visual confirmation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:** Students must know that the system uses **hysteresis-based dead-band control** (threshold $e_{th}$ ≈ 2 – 5 kPa) to **prevent valve chattering** (rapid open/close cycles) which would damage the solenoid coils and cause pressure oscillations.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for CTIS that have appeared in previous KTU university examinations and model question papers. Master this before any numerical problem.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description / Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_g = \dfrac{W_w}{A_c}$ | Ground pressure — the key variable CTIS optimises | kPa |
| 2 | $A_c \approx k_1 \cdot \dfrac{W_w}{P_i}$ | Contact patch area is inversely proportional to inflation pressure | m² |
| 3 | $K_t = C_k \cdot P_i$ | Vertical tyre stiffness is approximately linear in inflation pressure | N/m |
| 4 | $t_{inf} = \dfrac{V_{tyre}(P_{set} - P_{init})}{\eta_{comp} \cdot Q_{comp} \cdot P_{atm}} \cdot P_{comp}$ | Time to inflate a tyre from $P_{init}$ to $P_{set}$ | s |
| 5 | $t_{def} = \dfrac{V_{tyre}}{C_d \cdot A_v} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho_{air}}{P_{init}}} \cdot \left[ \sqrt{P_{init}} - \sqrt{P_{set}} \right]$ | Time to deflate a tyre through a vent of area $A_v$ | s |
| 6 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot W \cdot \left(1 + \kappa \cdot \dfrac{P_i}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | Rolling resistance as a function of inflation pressure | N |
| 7 | $\mu_{long} = \mu_0 \cdot \left[ 1 - \beta \left( 1 - \dfrac{P_i}{P_{ref}} \right)^2 \right]$ | Longitudinal friction coefficient vs. pressure (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t + K_w}{m_{u}}}$ | Wheel-hop natural frequency (ride) | Hz |
| 9 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{\text{Useful air delivered to tyre}}{\text{Air drawn by compressor}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{leak}}{Q_{comp}}$ | System thermodynamic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{comp,req} = P_i \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{atm}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Compressor delivery pressure accounting for adiabatic temperature rise | kPa |

Where the symbols used throughout the KTU CTIS formulation denote:

- $W_w$ = dynamic wheel load (N)
- $A_c$ = contact patch area (m²)
- $P_i$ = tyre inflation pressure (kPa, gauge)
- $P_{set}, P_{init}$ = target and initial pressures
- $P_{comp}$ = compressor delivery pressure
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure ≈ 101.325 kPa
- $V_{tyre}$ = tyre internal volume (m³)
- $Q_{comp}$ = compressor volumetric flow (m³/s)
- $C_d, A_v$ = discharge coefficient and vent area for deflation
- $\rho_{air}$ = density of air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats for air ≈ 1.4
- $C_{rr}$ = rolling resistance coefficient
- $\mu_0, \mu_{long}$ = reference and longitudinal friction coefficients
- $K_w$ = suspension spring rate, $m_u$ = unsprung mass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation (3)** is the *most important relationship* for the KTU Module 2 paper — it directly links inflation pressure to the **vertical stiffness** of the tyre, which in turn determines the **wheel-hop natural frequency $f_n$** (Equation 8). Reducing pressure to 50% of $P_{ref}$ typically halves $f_n$, which is why off-road vehicles prefer lower pressures for ride comfort.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Benefit Achieved via CTIS |
|---|---|---|
| **Military (e.g., HMMWV, JLTV)** | Adjust for desert, mud, snow, and urban combat in real time | Reduced soil erosion, lower thermal signature, faster mission re-tasking |
| **Agricultural Tractors** | Switch between road and field without leaving the cab | 8 – 15% reduction in soil compaction → higher crop yield |
| **Heavy Mining Trucks** | Lower pressure in load bays, raise on haul roads | 4 – 7% fuel saving per cycle |
| **Fire & Rescue (Wildland)** | Adjust for asphalt vs. forest floor | Improved traction, faster response |
| **Logistics (Cross-Country Convoys)** | Pre-set pressure for anticipated terrain | Reduced driver workload, lower tyre wear |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern CTIS units, such as those by **FPT Industrial (IVECO)** and **STEMCO**, integrate with **ABS/EBS** and **Adaptive Cruise Control** to pre-set tyre pressure based on GPS-mapped terrain data — an early form of *terrain-aware autonomous driving*.
