topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability of a Rigid Vehicle and Equations of Motion of a Rigid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability of a Rigid Vehicle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Rigid Vehicle** is defined as the inherent tendency of a vehicle to return to its original steady-state equilibrium path (straight-line or steady-turning condition) when subjected to a small external disturbance, *without requiring any corrective steering input from the driver*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **rigid vehicle** is modeled as a single rigid body (the so-called *bicycle model* or *two-degree-of-freedom model*) in which the vehicle's chassis is assumed to be perfectly rigid, suspensions are neglected in their full kinematic complexity, and motion is restricted to the horizontal (ground) plane. The vehicle has only **two degrees of freedom (2-DOF)**: lateral translation (sideslip) and yaw rotation (heading change).

### 1.1.1 Steady-State Stability vs. Transient Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Steady-State Stability** describes the vehicle's behavior at constant speed and constant steer angle after all transient oscillations have died out. It is characterized by the *understeer/oversteer gradient* K.
>
> **Transient Stability** describes the response of the vehicle during the initial few seconds after a steering input — does the yaw rate overshoot, oscillate, or build up smoothly?

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Speed (V_ch)** is the forward speed at which the vehicle transitions from a stable (understeering) regime to an unstable (oversteering) regime. It is given by:
> $$V_{ch} = \sqrt{\frac{C_{\alpha f} \cdot C_{\alpha r} \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot (C_{\alpha r} \cdot b - C_{\alpha f} \cdot a)}}$$

## 1.2 Equations of Motion of a Rigid Vehicle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations of Motion (EOM)** of a rigid vehicle are the two coupled second-order ordinary differential equations that mathematically describe the lateral (sideslip) and yaw (rotational) dynamics of a vehicle in the horizontal plane. They are derived by applying **Newton's Second Law** for translation in the lateral direction and **Euler's Equation** for rotation about the vertical (yaw) axis.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Model

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bicyclist on a Wet Floor Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are teaching a small child to ride a bicycle at very slow speed on a slippery floor. When the child tilts the steering slightly, the bicycle turns predictably and recovers when the child straightens the wheel. This is like a **stable (understeering) vehicle**.
>
> Now imagine the same child tries to ride fast on the same slippery floor. With just a tiny twitch of the handlebar, the bicycle suddenly spins out — the rear wheel slides outward, and the rider falls. This is the behavior of an **unstable (oversteering) vehicle**.
>
> The **critical speed** is the exact speed at which the bicycle's behavior changes from "predictable and recoverable" to "wobbly and dangerous."

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|--------|----------|--------------|------------------|
| $m$ | Total vehicle mass | **kg** | Total inertia resisting lateral acceleration |
| $I_z$ | Yaw moment of inertia | **kg·m²** | Resistance to rotation about vertical axis |
| $L$ | Wheelbase | **m** | Distance between front and rear axles |
| $a$ | Distance from CG to front axle | **m** | **Typically 1.0–1.5 m** |
| $b$ | Distance from CG to rear axle | **m** | **Typically 1.2–1.6 m** |
| $C_{\alpha f}$ | Front cornering stiffness | **N/rad** | Cornering force per unit slip angle at front tires |
| $C_{\alpha r}$ | Rear cornering stiffness | **N/rad** | Cornering force per unit slip angle at rear tires |
| $\delta$ | Steering angle at front wheels | **rad** | Driver's steering input |
| $v_x$ | Longitudinal (forward) velocity | **m/s** | Constant cruise speed |
| $v_y$ | Lateral (sideways) velocity | **m/s** | Sideslip component at CG |
| $r$ | Yaw rate | **rad/s** | Rate of heading change |
| $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$ | Front and rear slip angles | **rad** | Angle between tire heading and velocity vector |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bicycle Model — Conceptual Foundation

The vehicle is reduced to a **two-wheeled "bicycle"** representing the combined left/right wheels at each axle. The justifications are:

1. **Symmetry**: Most road vehicles are symmetric about their longitudinal centerline.
2. **Small-angle assumption**: During normal handling maneuvers, lateral velocity and yaw rate are small enough that left/right wheels experience similar slip angles.
3. **Reduced complexity**: 2-DOF is sufficient for studying directional dynamics without the heavy mathematical burden of a 7-DOF or 14-DOF full-vehicle model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The vehicle coordinate system** used in the KTU syllabus places the **x-axis pointing forward**, the **y-axis pointing to the driver's left (lateral)**, and the **z-axis pointing upward**. The CG origin makes this a *body-fixed* (moving) reference frame.

## 2.2 Slip Angle — The Cornerstone of Tire-Force Generation

> [!NOTE]
> A tire generates a **lateral (cornering) force** only when its **slip angle** is non-zero. The slip angle is the angle between the tire's heading (the direction the wheel is pointing) and the tire's actual velocity vector.

**Why does slip angle exist?**
Because the tire is a viscoelastic object. When you turn the wheel, the tire contact patch cannot instantaneously align with the new heading — it lags behind, and the tread rubber is sheared sideways. This shear deformation is what produces the cornering force.

### Front Slip Angle:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \theta_v$$
where $\theta_v = \arctan\left(\dfrac{v_y + a \cdot r}{v_x}\right)$ is the angle of the velocity vector at the front axle.

### Rear Slip Angle:
$$\alpha_r = -\theta_{v,r}$$
where $\theta_{v,r} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{v_y - b \cdot r}{v_x}\right)$ is the velocity vector angle at the rear axle.

Using the **small-angle assumption** ($v_y \ll v_x$ and $ar, br \ll v_x$):
$$\alpha_f \approx \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a \cdot r}{v_x}$$

$$\alpha_r \approx -\dfrac{v_y - b \cdot r}{v_x}$$

## 2.3 Linear Tire Model — Cornering Force Law

For small slip angles (linear region), the lateral force at each axle is **directly proportional** to the slip angle:

$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f \quad \text{(Front lateral force)}$$

$$F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r \quad \text{(Rear lateral force)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The linear tire model is valid only for $|\alpha| < 4°$ to $6°$. Beyond this, the tire enters the **nonlinear saturation region**, and forces no longer grow linearly.

## 2.4 The Two Coupled Equations of Motion

Applying **Newton's Second Law** in the lateral direction (sum of lateral forces = mass × lateral acceleration in body frame):
$$m a_y = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$$

For small $\delta$, $\cos\delta \approx 1$:
$$m(\dot{v}_y + v_x \cdot r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

Applying **Euler's Rotational Equation** about the z-axis (sum of yaw moments = $I_z \times \dot{r}$):
$$I_z \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} \cos\delta - b \cdot F_{yr}$$

For small $\delta$:
$$I_z \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} - b \cdot F_{yr}$$

## 2.5 Substituting Slip Angles and Tire Forces

Substituting the linear tire model:
$$m(\dot{v}_y + v_x \cdot r) = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a \cdot r}{v_x}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b \cdot r}{v_x}\right)$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = a \cdot C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a \cdot r}{v_x}\right) - b \cdot C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b \cdot r}{v_x}\right)$$

## 2.6 Standard Compact Matrix Form

After algebraic simplification, the equations take the standard state-space form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & I_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{v}_y \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} & \dfrac{a \cdot C_{\alpha f} - b \cdot C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} - m v_x \\ \dfrac{a \cdot C_{\alpha f} - b \cdot C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} & \dfrac{a^{2} C_{\alpha f} + b^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ r \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{\alpha f} \\ a \cdot C_{\alpha f} \end{bmatrix} \delta$$

In compact notation:
$$M \dot{x} + C(v_x) x = D \cdot \delta$$

where:
- $x = \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ r \end{bmatrix}$
- $M = \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & I_z \end{bmatrix}$ (Inertia matrix)
- $C(v_x)$ = Velocity-dependent damping/coupling matrix
- $D = \begin{bmatrix} C_{\alpha f} \\ a \cdot C_{\alpha f} \end{bmatrix}$ (Input vector)

## 2.7 Steady-State Solution (Constant Yaw Rate Turning)

For steady-state cornering, $\dot{v}_y = 0$ and $\dot{r} = 0$. The solution yields the steady-state yaw rate gain:

$$\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{v_x / L}{1 + K \cdot v_x^{2}}$$

where **K is the understeer gradient**:
$$K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot b}{L \cdot C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot a}{L \cdot C_{\alpha r}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{rad}}{\text{m/s}^2}\right]$$

## 2.8 Classification of Vehicle Stability

| Condition | Sign of K | Behavior | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Understeering** | $K > 0$ | **STABLE** | Vehicle needs MORE steer as speed increases |
| **Neutral Steer** | $K = 0$ | Marginal | Yaw rate gain is constant, speed-independent |
| **Oversteering** | $K < 0$ | **UNSTABLE** | Vehicle needs LESS steer as speed increases |

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}$ | Front slip angle (small-angle) |
| 2 | $\alpha_r = -\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}$ | Rear slip angle (small-angle) |
| 3 | $F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f$ | Linear front cornering force |
| 4 | $F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r$ | Linear rear cornering force |
| 5 | $m(\dot{v}_y + v_x r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$ | Lateral force equilibrium |
| 6 | $I_z \dot{r} = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr}$ | Yaw moment equilibrium |
| 7 | $\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{v_x/L}{1 + K v_x^2}$ | Steady-state yaw rate gain |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$ | Understeer gradient |
| 9 | $V_{ch} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}}$ | Critical speed (oversteer case) |
| 10 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} L^{2}}{m I_z v_x^{2}}}$ | Natural frequency of yaw oscillation |
| 11 | $a + b = L$ | Geometric constraint |
| 12 | $m g \cdot b = W_f \cdot L$ | Static weight on front axle |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The bicycle model and the resulting understeer gradient form the **backbone of every modern EPS (Electric Power Steering) calibration, ESC (Electronic Stability Control) algorithm, and autonomous vehicle path planner**. Without this 2-DOF formulation, engineers would have no computationally cheap way to predict whether a vehicle will spin out at high speed during an evasive maneuver.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Two Equations of Motion

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram and Sign Convention

Consider a vehicle moving forward at velocity $v_x$ along its body-fixed x-axis. The CG is located distance $a$ behind the front axle and $b$ ahead of the rear axle. The vehicle undergoes combined lateral translation $v_y$ and yaw rotation $r$ about the CG.

The lateral forces $F_{yf}$ and $F_{yr}$ act at the front and rear axles respectively, perpendicular to the wheelbase in the small-angle regime.

### Step 2: Velocity Components at Each Axle

The lateral velocity at the front axle is the sum of the lateral velocity at the CG plus the contribution due to yaw rotation:
$$v_{y,f} = v_y + a \cdot r$$

The lateral velocity at the rear axle is:
$$v_{y,r} = v_y - b \cdot r$$

### Step 3: Slip Angles via Small-Angle Approximation

Since the vehicle is moving forward at $v_x$ and laterally at $v_y$, the velocity vector at the front axle makes an angle $\theta_f$ with the x-axis:
$$\theta_f = \arctan\left(\dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right)$$

Using $\arctan(x) \approx x$ for small $x$:
$$\theta_f \approx \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}$$

The front wheel is steered by $\delta$ from the x-axis. The slip angle is the difference between the wheel heading and the velocity heading:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \theta_f = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}$$

For the rear axle, the wheel heading is fixed at 0 (no rear steer):
$$\alpha_r = 0 - \theta_r = -\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}$$

### Step 4: Linear Tire Forces

Substituting into the linear tire model:
$$F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right)$$

$$F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \left(-\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}\right)$$

### Step 5: Lateral Force Equation (Newton's Second Law)

The total lateral force equals mass times lateral acceleration in the body-fixed frame. The body-fixed lateral acceleration is:
$$a_y^{body} = \dot{v}_y + v_x \cdot r$$

(The $v_x r$ term is the centripetal component due to rotation of the body-fixed axes.)

Summing lateral forces:
$$m(\dot{v}_y + v_x r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

Substituting:
$$m(\dot{v}_y + v_x r) = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}\right)$$

### Step 6: Yaw Moment Equation (Euler's Equation)

The yaw moment about the CG is produced by the lateral forces multiplied by their moment arms:
$$I_z \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} - b \cdot F_{yr}$$

Substituting:
$$I_z \dot{r} = a \cdot C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right) - b \cdot C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}\right)$$

### Step 7: Expanding Both Equations

**Lateral Equation (expanded):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m \dot{v}_y + m v_x r &= C_{\alpha f} \delta - \dfrac{C_{\alpha f} v_y}{v_x} - \dfrac{C_{\alpha f} a r}{v_x} - \dfrac{C_{\alpha r} v_y}{v_x} + \dfrac{C_{\alpha r} b r}{v_x}
\end{aligned}$$

**Yaw Equation (expanded):**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_z \dot{r} &= a C_{\alpha f} \delta - \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} v_y}{v_x} - \dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} r}{v_x} + \dfrac{b C_{\alpha r} v_y}{v_x} - \dfrac{b^2 C_{\alpha r} r}{v_x}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8: Collecting Terms in Standard Matrix Form

Rearranging into $\dot{x}$ on the left and $x, \delta$ on the right:

$$\begin{aligned}
m \dot{v}_y &= -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} v_y + \left(m v_x - \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{v_x}\right) r + C_{\alpha f} \delta
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_z \dot{r} &= -\dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} v_y - \dfrac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} r + a C_{\alpha f} \delta
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9: Final Compact Matrix Form

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & I_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{v}_y \\ \dot{r} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} & \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} - m v_x \\[2mm] \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} & \dfrac{a^{2} C_{\alpha f} + b^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{v_x} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} v_y \\ r \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{\alpha f} \\ a C_{\alpha f} \end{bmatrix} \delta
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Yaw Rate Gain

### Step 1: Set Derivatives to Zero

For steady-state, $\dot{v}_y = 0$ and $\dot{r} = 0$.

From the lateral equation:
$$m v_x r = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f + C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$$

From the yaw equation:
$$0 = a C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f - b C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$$

### Step 2: Solve the Yaw Equation for $\alpha_f$ and $\alpha_r$ Ratio

$$a C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f = b C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r \implies \alpha_r = \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f}}{b C_{\alpha r}} \alpha_f$$

### Step 3: Use the Geometric Slip Angle Relationship

From the kinematic definitions:
$$\alpha_f - \alpha_r = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x} + \dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x} = \delta - \dfrac{(a+b) r}{v_x} = \delta - \dfrac{L r}{v_x}$$

Therefore:
$$r = \dfrac{v_x}{L}(\delta - \alpha_f + \alpha_r)$$

### Step 4: Solve the System Simultaneously

Substituting and after algebraic manipulation (omitted for brevity but standard in textbooks):
$$\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{v_x / L}{1 + K v_x^{2}}$$

where:
$$K = \dfrac{m}{L}\left(\dfrac{b}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{a}{C_{\alpha r}}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Speed (Oversteer Case)

When $K < 0$ (oversteer), the denominator $1 + K v_x^2$ passes through zero at:
$$1 + K v_x^2 = 0 \implies v_x^2 = -\dfrac{1}{K}$$

Therefore:
$$V_{ch} = \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{K}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}{m (C_{\alpha r} b - C_{\alpha f} a)}}$$

Above this speed, the steady-state yaw rate becomes **negative** (or undefined), indicating the vehicle can no longer maintain a stable turn — it spins out.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Classification

| Property | Understeer ($K>0$) | Neutral ($K=0$) | Oversteer ($K<0$) |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Yaw rate $r_{ss}$ vs $v_x$ | Decreasing | Constant | Increasing, diverges at $V_{ch}$ |
| Steady-state behavior | Stable at all speeds | Marginally stable | Stable only below $V_{ch}$ |
| Transient response | Non-oscillatory, well-damped | Oscillatory, neutral damping | Oscillatory, divergent above $V_{ch}$ |
| Characteristic equation roots | Real, negative | Pure imaginary | Complex with positive real part |

## 3.5 Characteristic Equation (Eigenvalue Analysis)

The homogeneous part of the matrix equation has characteristic equation:
$$\lambda^{2} + 2\zeta \omega_n \lambda + \omega_n^{2} = 0$$

where:
$$\omega_n^{2} = \dfrac{C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} L^{2}}{m I_z v_x^{2}}$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \dfrac{I_z(C_{\alpha f}+C_{\alpha r}) + m(a^{2} C_{\alpha f} + b^{2} C_{\alpha r})}{m I_z v_x}$$

Stability requires $\zeta > 0$, i.e., **all coefficients positive**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define slip angle and cornering stiffness of a tire. How are they related in the linear region?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Slip Angle (α)**: The angle between the direction in which a wheel is pointing and the direction in which the tire is actually moving. For a steered wheel, $\alpha = \delta - \theta_v$; for a non-steered wheel, $\alpha = -\theta_v$.
- **Cornering Stiffness (Cα)**: The slope of the lateral force vs. slip angle curve at the origin. Units: **N/rad**. It represents the tire's ability to generate lateral force per unit slip angle.
- **Linear Relationship**: In the linear region ($|\alpha| \leq 4°$ to $6°$), the lateral force is given by $F_y = C_{\alpha} \cdot \alpha$. Beyond this region, the force saturates and the relationship becomes nonlinear.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between understeer and oversteer. What is the role of the understeer gradient K?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Understeer**: Vehicle tends to turn less than the steering input commands as speed increases. The driver must increase the steering angle with rising speed to maintain the same turn radius. $K > 0$.
- **Oversteer**: Vehicle turns more than commanded as speed increases. $K < 0$. Beyond the critical speed $V_{ch}$, the vehicle becomes directionally unstable.
- **Neutral Steer**: $K = 0$. Yaw rate gain is independent of speed.
- **Role of K**: K quantifies the change in steady-state yaw rate gain per unit speed squared. It determines whether the vehicle is stable ($K > 0$) or unstable ($K < 0$) above a critical speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the two coupled equations of motion for a rigid vehicle (bicycle model) in the lateral and yaw directions. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a passenger car with $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $L = 2.6$ m, $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 90{,}000$ N/rad, determine:
(i) The understeer gradient K.
(ii) The critical speed $V_{ch}$ if oversteer condition holds.
(iii) Comment on the stability of the vehicle.

**Model Solution to (a):**

Refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1 through 9 for the complete derivation. The key assumptions are:
1. Vehicle is symmetric about the longitudinal axis (bicycle model).
2. Small steering and slip angles ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$).
3. Constant forward speed $v_x$ (no longitudinal dynamics).
4. Linear tire model valid ($|\alpha| < 4°$–$6°$).
5. Motion is restricted to the horizontal plane (no roll/pitch).
6. Vehicle is rigid (no suspension compliance).

The final two equations are:

$$\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{v}_y + v_x r) &= C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_z \dot{r} &= a C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{v_x}\right) - b C_{\alpha r}\left(-\dfrac{v_y - b r}{v_x}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the static axle loads.**
The static weight on each axle is:
$$W_f = \dfrac{m g b}{L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.4}{2.6} = 7924.6 \text{ N}$$

$$W_r = \dfrac{m g a}{L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{2.6} = 6792.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute the understeer gradient K.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} \\
&= \dfrac{7924.6}{80000} - \dfrac{6792.7}{90000} \\
&= 0.0991 - 0.0755 \\
&= 0.0236 \text{ rad/(m/s)}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Critical speed check.**

Since $K > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering**. The critical speed formula applies only to oversteer case ($K < 0$). Therefore, $V_{ch}$ is **not physically defined** for this vehicle — it is stable at all speeds.

**Step 4: Stability comment.**

The vehicle exhibits **understeer** behavior ($K = +0.0236$ rad/(m/s)² > 0). It will require progressively more steering input as speed increases to maintain a given turn radius. The vehicle is **directionally stable at all forward speeds**, which is the desirable design outcome for passenger cars.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the steady-state yaw rate gain of a rigid vehicle? Derive the expression $\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{v_x/L}{1 + K v_x^{2}}$ and explain its physical significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle has the following parameters: $m = 1800$ kg, $a = 1.3$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 70{,}000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 75{,}000$ N/rad.
(i) Calculate the understeer gradient K.
(ii) Determine the steady-state yaw rate gain $\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta}$ at $v_x = 20$ m/s when $\delta = 0.05$ rad.
(iii) What is the actual turn radius and cornering acceleration?

**Model Solution to (a):**

Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes. The key steps are:
1. Set $\dot{v}_y = \dot{r} = 0$ for steady-state.
2. From yaw equation: $a C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f = b C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$, giving $\alpha_f$ and $\alpha_r$ ratio.
3. Use kinematic relation: $\alpha_f - \alpha_r = \delta - L r / v_x$.
4. Solve the two equations simultaneously.

The final expression:
$$\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{v_x/L}{1 + K v_x^{2}}$$

**Physical Significance**:
- The numerator $v_x/L$ represents the **Ackermann-geometric** ideal yaw rate (low-speed bicycle behavior).
- The denominator $1 + K v_x^2$ is the **tire-compliance correction** that accounts for tire slip angles at higher speeds.
- For $K > 0$ (understeer): gain decreases with speed → safer behavior.
- For $K = 0$ (neutral): gain is constant → ideal racing response.
- For $K < 0$ (oversteer): gain increases and **diverges** at $V_{ch}$ → instability.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the understeer gradient.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{m}{L}\left(\dfrac{b}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{a}{C_{\alpha r}}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{1800}{2.8}\left(\dfrac{1.5}{70000} - \dfrac{1.3}{75000}\right) \\
&= 642.86 \times (2.143 \times 10^{-5} - 1.733 \times 10^{-5}) \\
&= 642.86 \times 0.410 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 2.636 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/(m/s)}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Steady-state yaw rate gain at $v_x = 20$ m/s.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} &= \dfrac{v_x/L}{1 + K v_x^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{20/2.8}{1 + 2.636 \times 10^{-3} \times 400} \\
&= \dfrac{7.143}{1 + 1.0544} \\
&= \dfrac{7.143}{2.0544} \\
&= 3.477 \text{ (rad/s)/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $r_{ss} = 3.477 \times 0.05 = 0.1739$ rad/s.

**Step 3: Turn radius and cornering acceleration.**

Turn radius (Ackermann formula, modified):
$$R = \dfrac{v_x}{r_{ss}} = \dfrac{20}{0.1739} = 115.0 \text{ m}$$

Centripetal (cornering) acceleration:
$$a_y = \dfrac{v_x^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{20^{2}}{115.0} = 3.478 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Or equivalently:
$$a_y = v_x \cdot r_{ss} = 20 \times 0.1739 = 3.478 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Most students lose marks on:
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle assumption** when computing slip angles (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Sign errors in the yaw moment equation** — the rear cornering force produces a **negative (nose-left)** yaw moment, while the front force produces a **positive (nose-right)** moment. Getting these signs wrong will flip the stability classification.
> 3. **Mixing up the understeer gradient formula** — K uses $W_f/C_{\alpha f}$ (front weight over front stiffness) **minus** $W_r/C_{\alpha r}$ (rear weight over rear stiffness). Reversing this gives the wrong sign.
> 4. **Forgetting that $V_{ch}$ exists only for oversteer** ($K<0$). For understeer vehicles, students write $V_{ch} = \sqrt{-1/K}$ and end up with an imaginary number — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Not checking units** — K is in **rad/(m/s)²**, not just rad.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stability of Rigid Vehicle & Equations of Motion**

- ⭐ The bicycle model reduces a 4-wheeled vehicle to a **2-DOF system**: lateral translation ($v_y$) and yaw rotation ($r$).
- ⭐ Slip angles are the **fundamental link** between tire forces and vehicle motion: $\alpha_f = \delta - (v_y + ar)/v_x$ and $\alpha_r = -(v_y - br)/v_x$.
- ⭐ Linear tire model: $F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f$ and $F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$ — valid for $|\alpha| \leq 4°$–$6°$.
- ⭐ Two EOMs in compact matrix form: $M\dot{x} + C(v_x)x = D\delta$.
- ⭐ The lateral acceleration in body frame is $a_y = \dot{v}_y + v_x r$ — this is the **centripetal plus translational** component.
- ⭐ Understeer gradient $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$ in **rad/(m/s)²**.
- ⭐ Steady-state yaw rate gain: $r_{ss}/\delta = (v_x/L)/(1 + K v_x^2)$.
- ⭐ **$K > 0$ → Understeer → Stable at all speeds** (most road cars).
- ⭐ **$K < 0$ → Oversteer → Unstable above $V_{ch} = \sqrt{-1/K}$**.
- ⭐ **$K = 0$ → Neutral steer → Marginal**.
- ⭐ The characteristic equation of yaw oscillation: $\lambda^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n \lambda + \omega_n^2 = 0$.
- ⭐ Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} L^2/(m I_z v_x^2)}$ — decreases with speed.
- ⭐ Geometric constraint: $a + b = L$ — always verify in problems.
- ⭐ Static axle load: $W_f = mgb/L$ and $W_r = mga/L$.
- ⭐ The bicycle model is the **foundation** for ESC, EPS, and autonomous vehicle controllers.
- ⭐ For KTU exam, **always show assumptions** before starting the derivation.
- ⭐ **Sign convention**: positive $v_y$ to the left, positive $r$ counter-clockwise (left turn).
- ⭐ The 2-DOF bicycle model cannot predict roll dynamics — a more complex 3-DOF or 7-DOF model is needed for that.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Wind Handling and the Problems Related to These Terms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross Wind Handling and the Problems Related to These Terms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Crosswind** in the context of vehicle dynamics refers to a wind component acting **perpendicular** (lateral) to the direction of vehicle travel. It represents an aerodynamic disturbance whose velocity vector is not aligned with the vehicle's longitudinal axis of motion. The response of the vehicle to this disturbance is governed by **aerodynamic side force**, **aerodynamic yaw moment**, and the resulting **sideslip angle** at the vehicle's center of gravity.

Mathematically, if a vehicle travels with longitudinal velocity $V_x$ and encounters a true wind vector $\vec{V}_w$ approaching at an angle $\theta$ relative to the vehicle's longitudinal axis, the **crosswind component** is resolved as:

$$V_{cw} = V_w \sin\theta$$

The **resultant relative wind** acting on the vehicle becomes the **vector sum** of the vehicle velocity and the negative of the true wind velocity. The angle between the resultant relative wind and the vehicle's longitudinal axis is called the **apparent yaw angle** $\psi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly emphasizes how crosswind handling affects **directional stability**, **driver workload**, and **path-following accuracy** of road vehicles. Students must master the relationship between aerodynamic coefficients ($C_S$, $C_Y$, $C_N$, $C_L$) and crosswind-induced vehicle behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle along a straight road on a calm day. Suddenly, a strong gust of wind blows from your left, pushing the bicycle sideways. To keep traveling in a straight line, you must instinctively lean into the wind and steer slightly to the right to **counteract** the lateral push. This is **crosswind handling** in its simplest form.

For a car traveling at high speed on a highway:
- The wind exerts a **lateral force** pushing the car sideways.
- Because this force typically does not act exactly at the center of gravity, it also creates a **rotating moment** (yaw moment) trying to twist the car.
- The driver must make continuous, small steering corrections to keep the car in the lane — this is called **crosswind compensation**.

Think of the car as an **arrow** being shot through a **crosswind**. Just as the arrow is deflected from its intended path, so is the vehicle — but unlike the arrow, the vehicle has **steering control** and the driver can actively correct the trajectory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in real engineering?** Crosswind sensitivity is a critical safety parameter for:
> - **High-sided vehicles** (buses, trucks, caravans, tall vans)
> - **Lightweight vehicles** (motorcycles, sports cars)
> - **Race cars** negotiating banked circuits
> - **Road design** (exposed bridges, mountain passes, coastal highways)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level, ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | Standard benchmark value |
| Vehicle frontal area | $A$ | **m²** | Projected frontal area |
| Wheelbase | $l$ | **m** | Distance between axles |
| Aerodynamic side force coefficient | $C_S$ | dimensionless | Lateral force / (½ρV²A) |
| Aerodynamic yaw moment coefficient | $C_N$ | dimensionless | About z-axis / (½ρV²Al) |
| Crosswind velocity | $V_{cw}$ | **m/s** | Lateral component of wind |
| Sideslip angle | $\beta$ | **radians / degrees** | Angle between v and x-axis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Wind Vector

When a vehicle moving at velocity $V_x$ in the $X$-direction experiences a wind of magnitude $V_w$ at an angle $\theta$ to the longitudinal axis:

**Longitudinal component** of wind (headwind/tailwind component):

$$V_{long} = V_w \cos\theta$$

**Lateral component** of wind (true crosswind):

$$V_{cw} = V_w \sin\theta$$

The **resultant relative air velocity** magnitude seen by the vehicle is:

$$V_{rel} = \sqrt{(V_x + V_{long})^2 + V_{cw}^2}$$

The **apparent yaw angle** $\psi$ of the relative wind with respect to the vehicle's longitudinal axis is:

$$\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{cw}}{V_x + V_w \cos\theta}\right)$$

## 2.2 Aerodynamic Forces and Moments in Crosswind

In the presence of crosswind, three primary aerodynamic effects act on the vehicle:

1. **Aerodynamic Side Force ($F_S$)** — pushes the car laterally.
2. **Aerodynamic Yaw Moment ($N$)** — tends to rotate the car about its vertical (yaw) axis.
3. **Aerodynamic Rolling Moment ($L_R$)** — tends to roll the car about its longitudinal axis.

These are expressed as:

$$F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_{rel}^{\,2}\, A\, C_S(\psi)$$

$$N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_{rel}^{\,2}\, A\, l\, C_N(\psi)$$

$$L_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_{rel}^{\,2}\, A\, b\, C_{LR}(\psi)$$

where $b$ is a characteristic length (often the wheelbase $l$) and the aerodynamic coefficients $C_S$, $C_N$, $C_{LR}$ are functions of the apparent yaw angle $\psi$.

> [!NOTE]
> For small yaw angles (a good engineering first approximation), the coefficients are linearized:
> $C_S \approx C_{S\beta}\,\beta$ and $C_N \approx C_{N\beta}\,\beta$, where $C_{S\beta}$ and $C_{N\beta}$ are **stability derivatives**.

## 2.3 Effects on the Vehicle — Physical Phenomena

The crosswind-induced side force and yaw moment produce the following observable effects on the vehicle:

- **Sideways displacement** of the vehicle's center of gravity from the intended path.
- **Yaw rotation** of the vehicle body — the heading angle deviates from the road direction.
- **Sideslip angle $\beta$** build-up at the C.G. — the velocity vector is no longer aligned with the body.
- **Load transfer** between left and right wheels due to the crosswind-induced rolling moment, reducing tire grip on the leeward side.
- **Steering correction** required from the driver to maintain lane discipline.

## 2.4 Crosswind Sensitivity Index

A widely used metric in automotive engineering to quantify how vulnerable a vehicle is to crosswind is the **Crosswind Sensitivity Index (CSI)**:

$$\boxed{\text{CSI} = \frac{C_{N\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} \cdot \frac{v}{l}}$$

where $v$ is the vehicle speed and $l$ is the wheelbase. A **lower CSI** indicates better crosswind stability (the vehicle is less likely to be yawed by a side gust).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crosswind component | $V_{cw} = V_w \sin\theta$ | Resolving wind into lateral component |
| 2 | Apparent yaw angle | $\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{cw}}{V_x + V_w\cos\theta}\right)$ | Angle at which air meets vehicle |
| 3 | Aerodynamic side force | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A\, C_S$ | Lateral push on vehicle |
| 4 | Aerodynamic yaw moment | $N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} A\, l\, C_N$ | Rotational disturbance about z-axis |
| 5 | Linearized $C_S$ | $C_S \approx C_{S\beta}\,\beta$ | Small-angle approximation |
| 6 | Linearized $C_N$ | $C_N \approx C_{N\beta}\,\beta$ | Small-angle approximation |
| 7 | Crosswind sensitivity index | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{C_{N\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} \cdot \dfrac{v}{l}$ | Quantifying disturbance susceptibility |
| 8 | Sideslip at C.G. | $\beta = \tan^{-1}(v/u)$ | Vehicle body slip angle |
| 9 | Yaw rate response (steady) | $r_{ss} = \dfrac{N}{m\, v}$ | Equilibrium rotation rate |
| 10 | Driver correction steering angle | $\delta_c \approx -\dfrac{l\, N}{C_{\alpha f} \cdot l_f \cdot v^2}$ | Counter-steer required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** in formula #10 reflects the **counter-steering action** — to oppose a yaw disturbance, the driver must steer *into* the wind (negative relative to vehicle orientation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Yaw Rate in a Steady Crosswind

**Problem setup:** A vehicle travels at constant forward speed $u$ on a straight road. A steady crosswind of magnitude $V_{cw}$ (purely lateral, $\theta = 90°$) acts on it. We wish to determine the steady-state yaw rate $r_{ss}$ produced.

**Assumptions:**
- Vehicle is a rigid body with mass $m$ and yaw moment of inertia $I_z$.
- Small-angle linearization applies.
- The vehicle reaches equilibrium after initial transient.

### Step 1 — Compute the apparent yaw angle $\psi$

Since the crosswind is purely lateral, $V_w \cos\theta = 0$. So:

$$\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{cw}}{u + 0}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{cw}}{u}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute aerodynamic side force and yaw moment

Using the linearized coefficient model:

$$C_S = C_{S\beta}\,\psi, \qquad C_N = C_{N\beta}\,\psi$$

The side force is:

$$F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(u^2 + V_{cw}^2)\,A\,C_{S\beta}\,\psi$$

The yaw moment is:

$$N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(u^2 + V_{cw}^2)\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,\psi$$

### Step 3 — Apply small-angle approximation

For typical road vehicles with $V_{cw} \ll u$:

$$\psi \approx \frac{V_{cw}}{u}, \qquad u^2 + V_{cw}^2 \approx u^2$$

Therefore:

$$F_S \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^{2}\,A\,C_{S\beta}\,\frac{V_{cw}}{u} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u\,A\,C_{S\beta}\,V_{cw}$$

$$N \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^{2}\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,\frac{V_{cw}}{u} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,V_{cw}$$

### Step 4 — Equate aerodynamic yaw moment to inertial resistance

In steady state, the aerodynamic yaw moment is balanced by the inertia of the vehicle's rotational motion. The **centripetal** requirement for steady cornering gives:

$$N = m\,u\,r_{ss}\,\Delta x$$

where $\Delta x$ is the distance between the C.G. and the **center of pressure** of the side force (typically slightly ahead of or behind the C.G.). For a first-order engineering estimate where the aerodynamic moment is balanced by the gyroscopic-style inertia term $m\,u\,r\,x_{cp}$:

### Step 5 — Solve for steady-state yaw rate

Rearranging:

$$r_{ss} = \frac{N}{m\,u\,\Delta x}$$

Substituting the expression for $N$:

$$\boxed{\,r_{ss} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,V_{cw}}{m\,\Delta x}\,}$$

This expression shows that the **steady-state yaw rate** in a crosswind is:
- **Proportional** to the crosswind speed $V_{cw}$
- **Proportional** to the yaw moment derivative $C_{N\beta}$
- **Inversely proportional** to the vehicle mass $m$ (lighter vehicles are more disturbed).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²
- Wheelbase $l = 2.6$ m
- Forward speed $u = 30$ m/s (~108 km/h)
- Crosswind speed $V_{cw} = 10$ m/s (lateral)
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $C_{S\beta} = 0.8$, $C_{N\beta} = 0.05$
- Center of pressure offset from C.G.: $\Delta x = -0.3$ m (behind C.G.)

**Compute aerodynamic side force:**

$$F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)(2.2)(0.8)(10)$$

$$F_S = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 30 \times 2.2 \times 0.8 \times 10$$

$$F_S = 323.4\ \text{N}$$

**Compute aerodynamic yaw moment:**

$$N = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)(2.2)(2.6)(0.05)(10)$$

$$N = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 30 \times 2.2 \times 2.6 \times 0.05 \times 10$$

$$N = 52.55\ \text{N·m}$$

**Compute steady-state yaw rate:**

$$r_{ss} = \frac{N}{m\,u\,\Delta x} = \frac{52.55}{1500 \times 30 \times (-0.3)}$$

$$r_{ss} = \frac{52.55}{-13500} = -0.00389\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\vert r_{ss} \vert \approx 0.223°/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The vehicle experiences a continuous yaw rotation of roughly **0.22 degrees per second** due to the crosswind. Over a 10-second exposure, this would amount to ~2.2° of heading misalignment — substantial enough to push the vehicle a full lane width on a highway if uncorrected.

## 3.3 Driver Steering Compensation

To hold the vehicle on a straight path against this yaw rate, the driver applies a **counter-steer** $\delta_c$. In a linear bicycle model, the steady-state correction is:

$$\delta_c \approx -\frac{r_{ss} \cdot l}{u}$$

Using our numerical values:

$$\delta_c = -\frac{(-0.00389)(2.6)}{30} = +0.000337\ \text{rad} = +0.0193°$$

This is a small but **continuous** steering input — a real driver would constantly adjust, and over long highway stretches this causes significant **driver fatigue**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect Summary

| Crosswind Effect | Root Cause | Primary Vehicle Response | Driver Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lateral drift | Side force $F_S$ | Sideslip $\beta$ build-up | Hold steering against drift |
| Yaw rotation | Yaw moment $N$ | Yaw rate $r$ | Counter-steer |
| Body roll | Rolling moment $L_R$ | Lateral load transfer | None (passive suspension) |
| Speed loss/gain | Longitudinal component $V_w\cos\theta$ | Drag/thrust change | None |
| Trailer sway (towed vehicles) | Disturbed yaw | Pendulum oscillation of trailer | Counter-steer + throttle modulation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Crosswind Disturbance Path

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full functional architecture of how a crosswind disturbance propagates through a vehicle system, including driver feedback compensation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENVIRONMENT CROSSWIND Vcw] --> B[WIND VECTOR RESOLUTION]
    B --> C[APPARENT YAW ANGLE psi]
    C --> D[AERODYNAMIC COEFFICIENTS CS and CN]
    D --> E[SIDE FORCE Fs]
    D --> F[YAW MOMENT N]
    E --> G[VEHICLE RIGID BODY DYNAMICS]
    F --> G
    G --> H[SIDESLIP ANGLE beta]
    G --> I[YAW RATE r]
    G --> J[BODY ROLL phi]
    H --> K[PATH DEVIATION dy]
    I --> K
    K --> L[DRIVER PERCEPTION]
    L --> M[STEERING CORRECTION deltaC]
    M --> N[FRONT TIRE LATERAL FORCE]
    N --> G
    J --> O[SUSPENSION LOAD TRANSFER]
    O --> P[TIRE GRIP ASYMMETRY]
    P --> G
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Crosswind Mitigation Strategy Map

This diagram outlines the engineering countermeasures applied at each stage of the disturbance response chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DISTURBANCE [DISTURBANCE ORIGIN]
        D1[Crosswind Gust Vcw]
    end
    DISTURBANCE --> S1[STAGE 1 AERODYNAMIC DESIGN]
    S1 --> M1[Streamlined Body Shape]
    S1 --> M2[Rear Spoiler and Side Skirts]
    S1 --> M3[Aerodynamic Center Near C.G.]
    M1 --> S2[STAGE 2 VEHICLE CONFIGURATION]
    M2 --> S2
    M3 --> S2
    S2 --> M4[Low Center of Pressure Offset]
    S2 --> M5[Wide Track and Low C.G.]
    S2 --> M6[Yaw Damping Control YDC]
    M4 --> S3[STAGE 3 CHASSIS DYNAMICS]
    M5 --> S3
    M6 --> S3
    S3 --> M7[Active Rear Steering]
    S3 --> M8[Electronic Stability Control ESC]
    S3 --> M9[Sensor Fusion Camera and IMU]
    M7 --> OUT[IMPROVED CROSSWIND STABILITY]
    M8 --> OUT
    M9 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Side Force and Yaw Moment Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Crosswind Pressure Distribution on Body] --> Q1[Front Axle Side Force]
    P --> Q2[Rear Axle Side Force]
    P --> Q3[Yaw Moment About C.G.]
    Q1 --> R1[Front Tire Slip Angle alphaF]
    Q2 --> R2[Rear Tire Slip Angle alphaR]
    R1 --> S[Vehicle Sideslip beta]
    R2 --> S
    Q3 --> T[Yaw Rate r]
    S --> U[Path Curvature kappa]
    T --> U
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define crosswind sensitivity. What is the role of the aerodynamic center of pressure in crosswind behavior of a vehicle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Crosswind sensitivity is the quantitative measure of how strongly a vehicle responds to lateral wind disturbances. It is captured by the **Crosswind Sensitivity Index (CSI)**, defined as $\text{CSI} = (C_{N\beta}/C_{S\beta})(v/l)$. A lower CSI indicates a more crosswind-stable vehicle.
- **Role of aerodynamic center (1.5 marks):** The aerodynamic center of pressure is the point on the vehicle where the resultant side force effectively acts. Its **longitudinal distance from the C.G.** governs the magnitude of the induced yaw moment. If the center of pressure is behind the C.G., the yaw moment tends to align the vehicle with the wind (aerodynamically stabilizing); if ahead of the C.G., the yaw moment amplifies the disturbance (destabilizing). Thus, designers aim to place the aerodynamic center near the C.G. to minimize the moment arm.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**List any three problems faced by road vehicles due to crosswind.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lane deviation / path drift** — the lateral aerodynamic force pushes the vehicle off its intended lane, requiring continuous steering correction.
2. **Yaw disturbance** — the aerodynamic yaw moment rotates the vehicle's heading, making directional control difficult.
3. **Driver fatigue** — sustained counter-steering on long highway journeys in crosswind increases cognitive load and physical effort.
*(Optionally: rolling moment causing load transfer, reduced tire grip on leeward side, lateral instability of trailers/towed vehicles, sudden gusts causing loss of control.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the steady-state aerodynamic side force and yaw moment acting on a road vehicle subjected to a steady crosswind. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car of mass $m = 1400$ kg, frontal area $A = 2.1$ m², and wheelbase $l = 2.5$ m travels at $u = 25$ m/s. It is subjected to a steady lateral crosswind of $V_{cw} = 8$ m/s. Using $C_{S\beta} = 0.75$, $C_{N\beta} = 0.04$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, and center of pressure located $0.25$ m behind the C.G., compute: (i) the apparent yaw angle, (ii) the steady-state yaw rate, and (iii) the required driver counter-steering angle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Steady-state, constant forward speed.
- Small yaw angles so linearized aerodynamic coefficients apply.
- Rigid vehicle body.
- Flat, level road; no gradient.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The crosswind velocity $V_{cw}$ is purely lateral, so the apparent yaw angle is:

$$\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{cw}}{u}\right)$$

For small $\psi$:

$$\psi \approx \frac{V_{cw}}{u}$$

Using the linearized aerodynamic coefficients:

$$C_S = C_{S\beta}\,\psi, \qquad C_N = C_{N\beta}\,\psi$$

The aerodynamic side force is:

$$F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^{2}\,A\,C_{S\beta}\,\psi$$

Substituting $\psi \approx V_{cw}/u$:

$$\boxed{F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u\,A\,C_{S\beta}\,V_{cw}}$$

The aerodynamic yaw moment is:

$$N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^{2}\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,\psi$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u\,A\,l\,C_{N\beta}\,V_{cw}}$$

These two equations describe the steady-state aerodynamic loading on the vehicle. The side force produces lateral drift; the yaw moment produces heading rotation. Together they require driver correction for safe path-keeping.

---

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**(i) Apparent yaw angle (2 marks):**

$$\psi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{8}{25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.32)$$

$$\psi \approx 17.74° = 0.3096\ \text{rad}$$

**(ii) Steady-state yaw rate (3 marks):**

$$N = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(25)(2.1)(2.5)(0.04)(8)$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$N = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 25 \times 2.1 \times 2.5 \times 0.04 \times 8$$

$$N = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 25 = 15.3125$$

$$N = 15.3125 \times 2.1 = 32.156$$

$$N = 32.156 \times 2.5 = 80.39$$

$$N = 80.39 \times 0.04 = 3.2157$$

$$N = 3.2157 \times 8 = 25.73\ \text{N·m}$$

Steady-state yaw rate using $\Delta x = -0.25$ m:

$$r_{ss} = \frac{N}{m\,u\,\Delta x} = \frac{25.73}{1400 \times 25 \times (-0.25)}$$

$$r_{ss} = \frac{25.73}{-8750} = -0.00294\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\vert r_{ss} \vert \approx 0.169°/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Required counter-steer (2 marks):**

$$\delta_c = -\frac{r_{ss}\cdot l}{u} = -\frac{(-0.00294)(2.5)}{25}$$

$$\delta_c = +\frac{0.00735}{25} = +0.000294\ \text{rad} = +0.0168°$$

This tiny but **continuous** counter-steering is what leads to driver fatigue on long highway drives.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the crosswind sensitivity index? Explain the physical significance of $C_{S\beta}$ and $C_{N\beta}$. Why is the longitudinal location of the center of pressure critical for crosswind stability? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various problems encountered by road vehicles in crosswind. Briefly describe two engineering design strategies used to improve crosswind stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Crosswind Sensitivity Index and stability derivatives (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Crosswind Sensitivity Index quantifies how strongly a vehicle yaws in response to a side wind. It is defined as:

$$\text{CSI} = \frac{C_{N\beta}}{C_{S\beta}} \cdot \frac{v}{l}$$

A **low CSI** corresponds to high crosswind stability — even a strong gust produces only a small yaw disturbance. A **high CSI** indicates the vehicle is easily rotated by lateral wind, which is dangerous.

**Physical significance of derivatives (2 marks):**
- $C_{S\beta}$ — the rate of change of aerodynamic side force coefficient per unit sideslip/yaw angle. Higher $C_{S\beta}$ means the wind produces a larger lateral push.
- $C_{N\beta}$ — the rate of change of aerodynamic yaw moment coefficient per unit sideslip. Higher $C_{N\beta}$ means the wind produces a larger rotational disturbance.

**Role of center of pressure location (3 marks):** The aerodynamic center of pressure is the point where the net side force effectively acts. Its **longitudinal offset from the C.G.** creates the moment arm that produces the aerodynamic yaw moment $N$. Specifically, if the pressure center is at a longitudinal distance $x_{cp}$ from the C.G.:

$$N = F_S \cdot x_{cp}$$

- If $x_{cp} > 0$ (ahead of C.G.): the moment tends to yaw the vehicle further into the wind, amplifying the disturbance — **aerodynamically unstable**.
- If $x_{cp} < 0$ (behind C.G.): the moment aligns the vehicle with the wind, **aerodynamically stabilizing** (weathervane effect).
- The ideal design places $x_{cp}$ at or very near the C.G., minimizing the moment arm and hence the yaw disturbance.

---

**(b) Problems and design strategies (7 marks):**

**Problems (4 marks):**
1. **Lateral path deviation:** Side force pushes the car sideways, causing it to leave its lane — particularly hazardous on narrow roads, bridges, and tunnels.
2. **Yaw disturbance and instability:** The yaw moment can rotate the vehicle unexpectedly, leading to spin-out in extreme cases (especially for high-sided vans, trucks, and motorcycles).
3. **Driver fatigue:** Sustained counter-steering over long distances causes cognitive and muscular fatigue, reducing safety.
4. **Trailer sway (towed vehicles):** Crosswind excites pendulum-like oscillations of trailers, which can grow to dangerous amplitudes.
5. **Load transfer and tire grip loss:** The aerodynamic rolling moment shifts weight from the leeward tires, reducing grip precisely when the vehicle most needs it.
6. **Sudden gust response:** Abrupt wind changes produce transient overshoots in sideslip and yaw that are harder to control than steady crosswind.

**Design strategies (3 marks):**
1. **Aerodynamic body shaping:** Streamlining the body so that side force coefficients are minimized and the aerodynamic center of pressure is located near the C.G. (e.g., tapered rear ends, side skirts, careful A-pillar design).
2. **Active yaw control / Electronic Stability Control (ESC):** Sensors detect unwanted yaw rate and apply differential braking or steering corrections automatically to suppress crosswind-induced rotations. Active rear steering is also used in luxury and high-performance vehicles.

*(Other acceptable strategies: low center of gravity, wide track, yaw dampers on trailers, driver-assist lane-keeping systems with crosswind compensation algorithms.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in crosswind problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to linearize** — students often write $C_S$ and $C_N$ as constants without explaining the small-angle assumption. Examiners expect you to state $C_S \approx C_{S\beta}\psi$ explicitly.
> 2. **Sign confusion on yaw rate** — the sign of $\Delta x$ (center of pressure location) determines whether the vehicle yaws *with* or *against* the wind. Always mark the convention.
> 3. **Mixing units** — degrees vs. radians in $\psi$ or $r$ leads to numerical errors of a factor of ~57. Be consistent.
> 4. **Ignoring the longitudinal wind component** — for $\theta \neq 90°$, do not drop $V_w \cos\theta$ from the denominator of $\psi$.
> 5. **Writing only the final answer in numerical problems** — KTU examiners award marks for *each algebraic step*. Always show intermediate calculations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Crosswind** is the lateral component of wind acting on a vehicle: $V_{cw} = V_w \sin\theta$.
- The **apparent yaw angle** $\psi$ determines how the oncoming air meets the vehicle.
- The two key aerodynamic loads are the **side force** $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u A C_{S\beta} V_{cw}$ and the **yaw moment** $N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u A l\, C_{N\beta} V_{cw}$.
- The **center of pressure location** relative to the C.G. dictates whether the yaw moment is stabilizing (behind C.G.) or destabilizing (ahead of C.G.).
- **Crosswind Sensitivity Index** $\text{CSI} = (C_{N\beta}/C_{S\beta})(v/l)$ — lower is better.
- **Key vehicle responses:** sideslip $\beta$, yaw rate $r$, lateral drift, body roll, load transfer.
- **Driver correction** is small in magnitude but continuous, causing fatigue on long drives.
- **High-risk vehicles:** tall vans, trucks, buses, motorcycles, towed trailers, lightweight race cars.
- **Design countermeasures:** streamlined body shape, locating aerodynamic center near C.G., ESC, active rear steering, yaw damping, low C.G., wide track.
- **Linearization** ($C_S = C_{S\beta}\psi$, $C_N = C_{N\beta}\psi$) is valid only for small yaw angles — a standard assumption in KTU problems.
- **Standard air density at sea level** is $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ — used unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MATLAB Simulation for Lateral Dynamic Response during Cornering of Automotive System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MATLAB Simulation for Lateral Dynamic Response during Cornering of Automotive System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lateral Dynamic Response during Cornering** is defined as the transient, time-dependent behavior of a road vehicle's lateral (sideways) motion and rotational (yawing) motion when subjected to a steering input, evaluated at a constant longitudinal speed using a numerical simulation framework such as the MATLAB/Simulink environment on a reduced-order vehicle model (typically the **Bicycle Model**).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMUT604), the term "MATLAB Simulation" refers to the **computational procedure** used to:
- Formulate the governing Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) of the vehicle.
- Represent them in **state-space canonical form** ($\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$).
- Numerically integrate them to obtain the **time history** of sideslip angle ($\beta$) and yaw rate ($r$).
- Validate **handling and stability** characteristics of the vehicle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trailing Shopping Cart"

Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart straight down an aisle and then suddenly twist the handle to the right by a fixed angle (a **step steer input**).

- The **handle (front wheels)** immediately points in the new direction.
- The **body of the cart (vehicle body)** does NOT instantly follow — it **wobbles, oscillates**, and gradually settles into the new path.
- The **time it takes** to settle and the **number of wobbles** before settling are the **lateral dynamic response**.

> [!NOTE]
> In a passenger car, this wobble is a safety-critical phenomenon. A simulation in MATLAB mathematically replicates this wobble for hundreds of "what-if" steering scenarios in seconds, without crashing real cars.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities and Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude (Passenger Car) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Vehicle mass | **1200 – 2000 kg** |
| $I_z$ | Yaw moment of inertia | **2000 – 3500 kg·m²** |
| $a$, $b$ | CG-to-front, CG-to-rear distance | **1.0 – 1.5 m** |
| $L = a+b$ | Wheelbase | **2.5 – 3.0 m** |
| $C_{\alpha f}$, $C_{\alpha r}$ | Cornering stiffness (front/rear) | **40 000 – 100 000 N/rad** |
| $u$ | Longitudinal speed | **10 – 35 m/s** (36 – 126 km/h) |
| $r$ | Yaw rate | **0.0 – 0.6 rad/s** |
| $\beta$ | Sideslip angle at CG | **0 – 5 deg** |
| $\delta$ | Steering angle (input) | **0 – 0.1 rad** (≈ 0 – 5.7°) |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Exists in the KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **derive** the state-space matrices, **interpret** the transient plots, and **classify** the vehicle as **understeer / neutral steer / oversteer** using the stability factor $K$. This is a **core CO3 (Apply)** and **CO4 (Analyze)** expectation under the 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Logic of the Simulation (Step-by-Step Logic Flow)

The simulation is built upon **5 sequential engineering pillars**. Each must be understood before the next.

### Pillar 1 — The Bicycle Model Assumption
> [!NOTE]
> The two front wheels are lumped into a single front "virtual" wheel, and the two rear wheels are lumped into a single rear "virtual" wheel. This reduces a complex 7-DOF system into a manageable **2-DOF** system: lateral translation $v_y$ and yaw rotation $r$.

### Pillar 2 — Linear Tire Cornering Force
For small slip angles (typically $|\alpha| < 4°$), the tire's lateral force is proportional to the slip angle:

$$F_{yi} = C_{\alpha i} \cdot \alpha_i$$

where $C_{\alpha i}$ is the **cornering stiffness** (N/rad). This is the "Hooke's Law" of tire mechanics.

### Pillar 3 — Slip Angle Geometry
The slip angle is the angle between the wheel's heading and its actual velocity vector.

- **Front slip angle:** $\alpha_f = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a \cdot r}{u}$
- **Rear slip angle:** $\alpha_r = -\dfrac{v_y - b \cdot r}{u}$

The numerators ($v_y + ar$ and $v_y - br$) are the **lateral velocities at the front and rear axles**.

### Pillar 4 — Equations of Motion (Newton–Euler)
Summing forces in the lateral direction and moments about the CG:

$$m \cdot (\dot{v}_y + u \cdot r) = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f + C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r$$

$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f - b \cdot C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r$$

### Pillar 5 — State-Space Canonical Form
Defining state vector $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ r \end{bmatrix}$ and input $u_{in} = \delta$, the simulation reduces to:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\delta$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the **system matrix** (2×2) and $\mathbf{B}$ is the **input matrix** (2×1).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** high-yield equations tested in KTU ESE and Module Internal exams. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Equation | Symbolic Form | Purpose in Simulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Front slip angle | $\alpha_f = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{u}$ | Geometric coupling |
| 2 | Rear slip angle | $\alpha_r = -\dfrac{v_y - b r}{u}$ | Geometric coupling |
| 3 | Lateral force balance | $m(\dot{v}_y + ur) = C_{\alpha f}\alpha_f + C_{\alpha r}\alpha_r$ | Newton’s 2nd law (lateral) |
| 4 | Yaw moment balance | $I_z \dot{r} = a C_{\alpha f}\alpha_f - b C_{\alpha r}\alpha_r$ | Euler’s rotational equation |
| 5 | A-matrix element [1,1] | $-\dfrac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m u}$ | Velocity damping |
| 6 | A-matrix element [1,2] | $-1 - \dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{m u^{2}}$ | Centripetal & restoring term |
| 7 | A-matrix element [2,1] | $-\dfrac{a C_{\alpha f} - b C_{\alpha r}}{I_z}$ | Yaw moment from sideslip |
| 8 | A-matrix element [2,2] | $-\dfrac{a^{2} C_{\alpha f} + b^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{I_z u}$ | Yaw damping |
| 9 | B-matrix | $\begin{bmatrix} C_{\alpha f}/(m u) \\ a C_{\alpha f}/I_z \end{bmatrix}$ | Steering input gain |
| 10 | Stability factor (rad·s²/m²) | $K = \dfrac{m(b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f})}{L^{2} C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$ | Classifies vehicle |
| 11 | Understeer gradient (deg/g) | $K_{us} = \left(\dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | Standard industry metric |
| 12 | Steady-state yaw rate gain | $\dfrac{r_{ss}}{\delta} = \dfrac{u}{L + K u^{2}}$ | Steady-state cornering |
| 13 | Characteristic equation | $\lambda^{2} - \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A})\lambda + \det(\mathbf{A}) = 0$ | Eigenvalue solver |
| 14 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{A})}$ | Oscillation rate |
| 15 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = -\dfrac{\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A})}{2 \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{A})}}$ | Oscillation decay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In industry, the same state-space model is embedded into **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** algorithms of production cars. The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are continuously computed at 100 Hz to decide when to apply individual wheel braking during emergency maneuvers.

## 2.3 Why "Lateral Response" is a Critical Engineering Problem

- **Safety:** Determines whether a vehicle spins (oversteer) or pushes wide (understeer) during sharp cornering.
- **Tuning:** Car manufacturers use this simulation to tune the suspension geometry, tire selection, and CG placement.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving algorithms (e.g., lane change maneuvers) rely on these state-space equations to predict vehicle trajectory.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FMVSS and ECE regulations require stability verification for new vehicle platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bicycle Model State-Space Matrices

### Step 1 — Substitute Slip Angle Expressions into the EOM

Starting from the equations of motion and substituting the slip angle definitions:

$$m\dot{v}_y + mur = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \frac{v_y + ar}{u}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\left(-\frac{v_y - br}{u}\right)$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = aC_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \frac{v_y + ar}{u}\right) - bC_{\alpha r}\left(-\frac{v_y - br}{u}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Force Equation

$$m\dot{v}_y + mur = C_{\alpha f}\delta - \frac{C_{\alpha f}}{u}(v_y + ar) - \frac{C_{\alpha r}}{u}(v_y - br)$$

Group the $v_y$ and $r$ terms on the right-hand side:

$$m\dot{v}_y = -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{u}v_y - \left(\frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{u} + mu\right)r + C_{\alpha f}\delta$$

Divide throughout by $m$:

$$\dot{v}_y = -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{mu}v_y + \left[-1 - \frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{mu^2}\right]r + \frac{C_{\alpha f}}{mu}\delta$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Moment Equation

$$I_z \dot{r} = aC_{\alpha f}\delta - \frac{aC_{\alpha f}}{u}(v_y + ar) + \frac{bC_{\alpha r}}{u}(v_y - br)$$

$$I_z \dot{r} = aC_{\alpha f}\delta - \frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{u}v_y - \frac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{u}r$$

Divide by $I_z$:

$$\dot{r} = -\frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{I_z}v_y - \frac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{I_z u}r + \frac{aC_{\alpha f}}{I_z}\delta$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the State-Space Form

In matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\delta$ with $\mathbf{x} = [v_y, \, r]^{T}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{v}_y \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{mu} & -1 - \frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{mu^{2}} \\ -\frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{I_z} & -\frac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{I_z u} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ r \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \frac{C_{\alpha f}}{mu} \\ \frac{aC_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}\delta$$

This is the **complete mathematical model** that the MATLAB simulation integrates numerically at each time step.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Characteristic Equation and Stability Conditions

The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ determine stability. From linear systems theory, the characteristic equation is:

$$\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \lambda^{2} - \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A})\lambda + \det(\mathbf{A}) = 0$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Trace

$$\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A}) = -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{mu} - \frac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{I_z u}$$

Since all terms inside the negative sign are **positive** ($C_{\alpha} > 0$, $m > 0$, etc.), the trace is always **negative** → this is one stability condition.

### Step 2 — Compute the Determinant

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = \frac{(C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r})(a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}) - (aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})^2 - mu^2(aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})}{mI_z u^2}$$

Expanding $(C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r})(a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}) - (aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})^2$:

After algebraic simplification, the numerator reduces to:

$$L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} - mu^2(aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})$$

where $L = a + b$. Therefore:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = \frac{L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} - mu^2(aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})}{mI_z u^2}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Routh–Hurwitz Stability Criteria

For a second-order system, stability requires both coefficients of the characteristic equation to be **positive**:

- **Condition 1:** $-\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A}) > 0$ → always true (verified in Step 1).
- **Condition 2:** $\det(\mathbf{A}) > 0$ → requires:

$$L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r} > mu^2(aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})$$

### Step 4 — Vehicle Classification

| Condition | Classification | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $aC_{\alpha f} > bC_{\alpha r}$ | **Understeer** ($K < 0$) | Stable for ALL speeds; turns less than $\delta$ |
| $aC_{\alpha f} = bC_{\alpha r}$ | **Neutral Steer** | Stable; yaw rate gain $= u/L$ |
| $aC_{\alpha f} < bC_{\alpha r}$ | **Oversteer** ($K > 0$) | Becomes unstable above critical speed $u_{cr}$ |

The **critical speed** for an oversteer vehicle:

$$u_{cr} = \sqrt{\frac{L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}{m(aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r})}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Aligned with Simulation Output)

**Given Data:** $m = 1500$ kg, $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $C_{\alpha f} = C_{\alpha r} = 80\,000$ N/rad, $u = 20$ m/s, step steer $\delta = 0.05$ rad.

### Step 1 — Compute the A-matrix

$$A_{11} = -\frac{80\,000 + 80\,000}{1500 \times 20} = -\frac{160\,000}{30\,000} = -5.333 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$A_{12} = -1 - \frac{(1.2)(80\,000) - (1.5)(80\,000)}{1500 \times 20^{2}} = -1 - \frac{96\,000 - 120\,000}{600\,000} = -1 + 0.04 = -0.96 \text{ m}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$A_{21} = -\frac{96\,000 - 120\,000}{2500} = -\frac{-24\,000}{2500} = +9.6 \text{ m}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$A_{22} = -\frac{(1.44 + 2.25)(80\,000)}{2500 \times 20} = -\frac{3.69 \times 80\,000}{50\,000} = -5.904 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -5.333 & -0.96 \\ +9.6 & -5.904 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the B-matrix

$$B_1 = \frac{80\,000}{1500 \times 20} = 2.667 \text{ m/s}^{2}\text{/rad}, \quad B_2 = \frac{(1.2)(80\,000)}{2500} = 38.4 \text{ rad/s}^{2}\text{/rad}$$

### Step 3 — Eigenvalues (System Poles)

$$\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A}) = -11.237, \quad \det(\mathbf{A}) = (-5.333)(-5.904) - (-0.96)(9.6) = 40.704$$

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-11.237 \pm \sqrt{(-11.237)^{2} - 4(40.704)}}{2} = \frac{-11.237 \pm \sqrt{-36.55}}{2} = -5.618 \pm 3.022j$$

Both eigenvalues have **negative real part** → **Vehicle is STABLE** (understeer class).

### Step 4 — Damping and Frequency

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{40.704} = 6.379 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \zeta = \frac{11.237}{2 \times 6.379} = 0.881$$

Highly damped system: response settles within ~1 second with minimal overshoot.

### Step 5 — Steady-State Yaw Rate

$$K = \frac{1500 \times (1.5 \times 80\,000 - 1.2 \times 80\,000)}{(2.7)^2 \times 80\,000^2} = +7.72 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{2}\text{/m}^{2}$$

Since $K > 0$ (i.e., $aC_{\alpha f} < bC_{\alpha r}$ in the absolute sense, equivalent to understeer index positive in the deg/g convention), the vehicle is **understeered**.

$$r_{ss} = \frac{20}{2.7 + 7.72 \times 10^{-4} \times 400} \times 0.05 = \frac{20}{3.009} \times 0.05 = 0.332 \text{ rad/s}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MATLAB Simulation Flowchart

The procedural flow used in the computational environment to obtain the lateral response is depicted below.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Simulation] --> B[Input Vehicle Parameters]
    B --> C[Define m, a, b, I_z, C_af, C_ar]
    C --> D[Set Constant Longitudinal Speed u]
    D --> E[Compute Slip Angles from Geometry]
    E --> F[Assemble 2x2 System Matrix A]
    F --> G[Assemble 2x1 Input Matrix B]
    G --> H[Define Step Steer Input Delta of t]
    H --> I[Formulate State ODE dot x equals A x plus B u]
    I --> J[Apply Numerical Integration Scheme]
    J --> K[Extract Time History of vy and r]
    K --> L[Compute Steady State Values]
    L --> M[Compute Eigenvalues for Stability]
    M --> N[Plot Yaw Rate Response r versus t]
    N --> O[Plot Sideslip Angle Response beta versus t]
    O --> P[Classify Vehicle as Under Neutral or Oversteer]
    P --> Q[End Simulation]
```

## 4.2 State-Space Block Architecture

The internal architecture of the simulation showing the feedback and feed-forward paths.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Steering Input Delta] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[Input Matrix B 2x1]
    C --> D[Integrator 1 over s]
    D --> E[State Vector x vy and r]
    E --> F[System Matrix A 2x2]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Output Matrix C 2x2]
    G --> H[Output y v and r]
    E --> I[Output Derivative dot v and dot r]
    I --> J[Eigenvalue Analyzer]
    J --> K[Stability Verdict]
```

## 4.3 Free-Body / Functional Block Representation of the Bicycle Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vehicle Body of Mass m] --> B[Coordinate Frame CG]
    B --> C[Front Axle at distance a from CG]
    B --> D[Rear Axle at distance b from CG]
    C --> E[Front Tire Cornering Force C_af times alpha_f]
    D --> F[Rear Tire Cornering Force C_ar times alpha_r]
    E --> G[Lateral Force Summation on Body]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Translational Equation of Motion]
    C --> I[Yaw Moment of arm a]
    D --> J[Yaw Moment of arm b]
    I --> K[Rotational Equation of Motion]
    J --> K
    H --> L[State Space Matrix A and B Assembly]
    K --> L
    L --> M[MATLAB Numerical Solver]
    M --> N[Transient Response Plot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Functional Block Diagrams?** The KTU syllabus emphasizes "MATLAB simulation" — the actual drawing of a 4-wheel vehicle in 2D is complex; the **functional block representation** is the engineering equivalent used in control engineering and ABS/ESC software development.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
> **Define sideslip angle ($\beta$) and tire slip angle ($\alpha$) in vehicle dynamics. How are they related to cornering force generation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Sideslip angle $\beta$** (1 mark): The angle between the vehicle's longitudinal axis (centerline) and the **velocity vector** at the center of gravity. Mathematically, $\beta = \arctan(v_y / u)$. It quantifies the "crab angle" of the body relative to its direction of motion.

- **Tire slip angle $\alpha$** (1 mark): The angle between the wheel's heading direction and the velocity vector of the wheel's contact patch. For the front wheel: $\alpha_f = \delta - (v_y + ar)/u$.

- **Relation to cornering force** (1 mark): Within the linear range ($|\alpha| < 4°$), the lateral force generated is $F_y = C_{\alpha} \cdot \alpha$, where $C_{\alpha}$ is the cornering stiffness. The slip angle is thus the *cause* and the cornering force is the *effect*.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]`
> **What is the stability factor $K$? Using its sign, classify the three possible vehicle handling behaviors and mention the practical significance of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition** (1 mark): The stability factor is $K = \dfrac{m(b C_{\alpha r} - a C_{\alpha f})}{L^{2} C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$, with units of rad·s²/m².

- **Classification** (1.5 marks):
  - $K < 0$ → **Understeer** (turns less than steering; safe and stable for all speeds).
  - $K = 0$ → **Neutral steer** (yaw rate gain $= u/L$).
  - $K > 0$ → **Oversteer** (turns more than steering; becomes unstable above critical speed $u_{cr}$).

- **Practical significance** (0.5 marks): Most modern passenger cars are designed with a slight understeer margin ($K_{us} \approx 1$–$3$ deg/g) to enhance safety and reduce driver workload.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make in this topic include: (i) using the wrong sign convention for $aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}$, (ii) forgetting to square $u$ in the centripetal term of $A_{12}$, (iii) treating the bicycle model as 3-DOF (it is strictly 2-DOF: $v_y$ and $r$), and (iv) computing eigenvalues without verifying that **both** real and imaginary parts are physically meaningful. Each mistake typically costs 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the equations of motion of a vehicle during cornering using the **bicycle model**. Clearly define the slip angles, state the assumptions, and obtain the lateral force and yaw moment equations in terms of $v_y$, $r$, and $\delta$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A passenger car has the following parameters: $m = 1400$ kg, $a = 1.25$ m, $b = 1.45$ m, $I_z = 2200$ kg·m², $C_{\alpha f} = 60\,000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 70\,000$ N/rad. The car is travelling at $u = 25$ m/s and a step steering input of $\delta = 0.04$ rad is applied. Build the state-space matrices, determine the eigenvalues, and comment on the stability.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
1. Small steering and slip angles (linear tire model valid).
2. Constant longitudinal speed $u$ (no longitudinal dynamics).
3. Vehicle is symmetric about the longitudinal $x$-axis.
4. The two front wheels and two rear wheels are each lumped into a single equivalent wheel at the centerline.
5. The road is flat ($\gamma = 0$).

**Slip Angle Definitions** (1 mark):
- Front: $\alpha_f = \delta - \dfrac{v_y + a r}{u}$
- Rear: $\alpha_r = -\dfrac{v_y - b r}{u}$

**Lateral Tire Forces** (1 mark):
- $F_{yf} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f$, $\quad F_{yr} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$

**Newton's Second Law in the Lateral Direction** (2 marks):
Summing lateral forces on the vehicle body (CG frame):
$$m(\dot{v}_y + u r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr} = C_{\alpha f}\alpha_f + C_{\alpha r}\alpha_r$$

Substituting slip angles:
$$m\dot{v}_y + mur = C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \frac{v_y + ar}{u}\right) - \frac{C_{\alpha r}(v_y - br)}{u}$$

Rearranging into the standard form (as shown in Section 3.1) yields the [1,1] and [1,2] elements of matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

**Euler's Equation for Yaw** (2 marks):
Summing moments about the CG:
$$I_z \dot{r} = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr} = a C_{\alpha f}\alpha_f - b C_{\alpha r}\alpha_r$$

Substituting the slip angles:
$$I_z \dot{r} = aC_{\alpha f}\delta - \frac{a^2 C_{\alpha f} + b^2 C_{\alpha r}}{u}r - \frac{aC_{\alpha f} - bC_{\alpha r}}{u}v_y$$

Dividing by $I_z$ gives the [2,1] and [2,2] elements of $\mathbf{A}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the geometric and stability parameters** (1 mark):
$$L = a + b = 1.25 + 1.45 = 2.70 \text{ m}$$
$$aC_{\alpha f} = 1.25 \times 60\,000 = 75\,000 \text{ N}$$
$$bC_{\alpha r} = 1.45 \times 70\,000 = 101\,500 \text{ N}$$
Since $aC_{\alpha f} < bC_{\alpha r}$, the vehicle is **oversteer in the strict sense** (i.e., $K > 0$).

**Step 2: Assemble matrix A** (2 marks):
$$A_{11} = -\frac{60\,000 + 70\,000}{1400 \times 25} = -\frac{130\,000}{35\,000} = -3.714$$
$$A_{12} = -1 - \frac{75\,000 - 101\,500}{1400 \times 625} = -1 - \frac{-26\,500}{875\,000} = -1 + 0.0303 = -0.970$$
$$A_{21} = -\frac{75\,000 - 101\,500}{2200} = -\frac{-26\,500}{2200} = +12.045$$
$$A_{22} = -\frac{(1.25)^2(60\,000) + (1.45)^2(70\,000)}{2200 \times 25} = -\frac{93\,750 + 147\,175}{55\,000} = -4.381$$

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.714 & -0.970 \\ +12.045 & -4.381 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Assemble matrix B** (1 mark):
$$B_1 = \frac{60\,000}{1400 \times 25} = 1.714, \quad B_2 = \frac{1.25 \times 60\,000}{2200} = 34.091$$

$$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.714 \\ 34.091 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Eigenvalue analysis** (2 marks):
$$\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A}) = -8.095, \quad \det(\mathbf{A}) = (-3.714)(-4.381) - (-0.970)(12.045) = 16.275 + 11.684 = 27.959$$

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-8.095 \pm \sqrt{65.53 - 111.84}}{2} = \frac{-8.095 \pm \sqrt{-46.31}}{2} = -4.048 \pm 3.402j$$

**Step 5: Stability comment** (1 mark):
Both eigenvalues have a **negative real part** ($-4.048$). Therefore, the system is **asymptotically stable** at $u = 25$ m/s. The transient response is underdamped with $\omega_n = \sqrt{27.959} = 5.288$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = 4.048 / 5.288 = 0.766$. The vehicle will reach steady-state yaw rate in approximately 1.5 seconds with minimal overshoot.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the aid of a labelled functional block diagram, explain the state-space approach to simulate the lateral dynamic response of an automotive system. Define the state vector, the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$, the input matrix $\mathbf{B}$, and discuss how the **stability of the vehicle** is inferred from the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A mid-size SUV has the following parameters: $m = 2000$ kg, $a = 1.4$ m, $b = 1.6$ m, $I_z = 3500$ kg·m², $C_{\alpha f} = 90\,000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 95\,000$ N/rad. Calculate the **stability factor $K$**, the **understeer gradient in deg/g**, and the **critical speed $u_{cr}$** (if applicable). Classify the vehicle's handling behavior.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — State-Space Approach (7 marks)

**State-space formulation** (2 marks):
The lateral dynamics are described by the canonical form:
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\delta(t)$$
$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\delta(t)$$

with:
- **State vector** $\mathbf{x} = [v_y, \, r]^{T}$ (lateral velocity, yaw rate).
- **Input** $u_{in} = \delta$ (steering angle).
- **System matrix** $\mathbf{A}$: 2×2, encoding vehicle inertia, geometry, and tire stiffness.
- **Input matrix** $\mathbf{B}$: 2×1, encoding the front steering's effect on lateral and yaw dynamics.
