# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) for a vibrating system. How many DOFs does a quarter-car suspension model with vertical bounce and wheel-hop have?**

**Model Answer:**
A Degree of Freedom is the minimum number of independent coordinates required to define the configuration of a vibrating system at any instant. For a quarter-car suspension with vertical bounce of the sprung mass and wheel-hop (unsprung mass vertical motion), the system has **2 DOFs**:
1. Vertical displacement of sprung mass $x_s$
2. Vertical displacement of unsprung mass $x_u$

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define transmissibility. What is its significance in vibration isolation of vehicles?**

**Model Answer:**
Transmissibility (TR) is defined as the ratio of the force transmitted to the foundation to the force applied to the mass:
$$\text{TR} = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Significance:** Transmissibility measures the effectiveness of a suspension in isolating road-induced vibrations from the vehicle body. For TR $< 1$, the suspension attenuates forces transmitted to the cabin, improving ride comfort. The isolation threshold occurs at $r > \sqrt{2}$. Engineers tune suspension stiffness and damping to ensure dominant road frequencies lie in the isolation zone.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A 1-DOF spring-mass-damper system has $m = 20$ kg, $k = 8000$ N/m and $c = 200$ N·s/m.**

#### (a) Determine the natural frequency, damping ratio, and the type of damping. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Natural frequency:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8000}{20}} = \sqrt{400} = 20\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2.** Natural frequency in Hz:
$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3.** Critical damping coefficient:
$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 20 \times 20 = 800\ \text{N·s/m}$$

**Step 4.** Damping ratio:
$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{200}{800} = 0.25$$

**Step 5.** Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Step 6.** Damped natural frequency:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 20\sqrt{0.9375} = 19.36\ \text{rad/s}$$

#### (b) If a harmonic force $F(t) = 500\sin(15t)$ N is applied, find the steady-state amplitude and the phase angle. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Frequency ratio:
$$r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{15}{20} = 0.75$$

**Step 2.** Static deflection:
$$X_{\text{static}} = \dfrac{F_0}{k} = \dfrac{500}{8000} = 0.0625\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3.** Magnification factor:
$$M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Compute each term:
$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375$$
$$2\zeta r = 2(0.25)(0.75) = 0.375$$
$$(1-r^2)^2 = 0.4375^2 = 0.1914$$
$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.375^2 = 0.1406$$
$$\text{Sum} = 0.3320$$
$$\sqrt{0.3320} = 0.5762$$
$$M = \dfrac{1}{0.5762} = 1.7356$$

**Step 4.** Steady-state amplitude:
$$X = M \times X_{\text{static}} = 1.7356 \times 0.0625 = 0.1085\ \text{m} \approx 108.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5.** Phase angle:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.375}{0.4375}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8571) = 40.6°$$

**Step 6.** Final response:
$$x(t) = 0.1085\sin(15t - 40.6°)\ \text{m}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For a 2-DOF system shown below, derive the equation of motion in matrix form. Given $m_1 = 30$ kg, $m_2 = 20$ kg, $k_1 = 6000$ N/m, $k_2 = 8000$ N/m, $k_3 = 4000$ N/m, find the natural frequencies and mode shapes.**

#### (a) Derive the equations of motion in matrix form. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** For mass $m_1$, forces include $k_1$ (to ground), $k_2$ (to $m_2$):
$$m_1\ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 + k_2(x_1 - x_2) = 0$$
$$m_1\ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2.** For mass $m_2$, forces include $k_2$ (to $m_1$) and $k_3$ (to ground):
$$m_2\ddot{x}_2 + k_2(x_2 - x_1) + k_3 x_2 = 0$$
$$m_2\ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0$$

**Step 3.** Matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

#### (b) Find the natural frequencies and mode shapes. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Substitute numerical values:
$$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 0 \\ 0 & 20 \end{bmatrix},\qquad \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 14000 & -8000 \\ -8000 & 12000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2.** Frequency equation: $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2\mathbf{M}) = 0$
$$(14000 - 30\omega^2)(12000 - 20\omega^2) - 8000^2 = 0$$

**Step 3.** Expand:
$$14000 \times 12000 - 14000 \times 20\omega^2 - 30\omega^2 \times 12000 + 30 \times 20\omega^4 - 64 \times 10^6 = 0$$
$$168 \times 10^6 - 280000\,\omega^2 - 360000\,\omega^2 + 600\,\omega^4 - 64 \times 10^6 = 0$$
$$600\,\omega^4 - 640000\,\omega^2 + 104 \times 10^6 = 0$$

**Step 4.** Divide by 200:
$$3\omega^4 - 3200\,\omega^2 + 520000 = 0$$

**Step 5.** Let $u = \omega^2$. Quadratic formula:
$$u = \dfrac{3200 \pm \sqrt{3200^2 - 4 \times 3 \times 520000}}{2 \times 3} = \dfrac{3200 \pm \sqrt{10240000 - 6240000}}{6} = \dfrac{3200 \pm \sqrt{4000000}}{6} = \dfrac{3200 \pm 2000}{6}$$

So:
$$u_1 = \dfrac{1200}{6} = 200,\qquad u_2 = \dfrac{5200}{6} = 866.67$$

**Step 6.** Natural frequencies:
$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{200} = 14.14\ \text{rad/s}\quad (f_1 = 2.25\ \text{Hz})$$
$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{866.67} = 29.44\ \text{rad/s}\quad (f_2 = 4.69\ \text{Hz})$$

**Step 7.** Mode shape ratio: $X_2/X_1 = (k_1 + k_2 - \omega_i^2 m_1)/k_2 = (14000 - 30\omega_i^2)/8000$.

**Mode 1** ($\omega_1^2 = 200$):
$$\dfrac{X_2^{(1)}}{X_1^{(1)}} = \dfrac{14000 - 30(200)}{8000} = \dfrac{14000 - 6000}{8000} = \dfrac{8000}{8000} = +1.0$$
Both masses move **in phase** with equal amplitude.

**Mode 2** ($\omega_2^2 = 866.67$):
$$\dfrac{X_2^{(2)}}{X_1^{(2)}} = \dfrac{14000 - 30(866.67)}{8000} = \dfrac{14000 - 26000}{8000} = \dfrac{-12000}{8000} = -1.5$$
Masses move **out of phase** with magnitude ratio 1.5.

**Step 8.** Normalized mode shape matrices:
$$\mathbf{X}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix},\qquad \mathbf{X}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1.5 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Examiner Pet Peeves):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\omega_n$ from rad/s to Hz** when the question asks for frequency in Hz. KTU boards specifically look for $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$.
> 2. **Sign errors in mode-shape ratio formula.** Always derive the ratio carefully from $(k_1 + k_2 - \omega_i^2 m_1)/k_2$ — using the wrong row of the stiffness matrix gives inverted signs.
> 3. **Confusing magnification factor with transmissibility.** They look similar but are NOT the same: $M = X/X_{\text{static}}$ (displacement ratio), TR $= F_T/F_0$ (force ratio).
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ factor** in $\omega_d$ — many students write $\omega_d = \omega_n$, costing 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the damping regime** ($\zeta < 1$, $=1$, $>1$) after computing it. Examiners expect explicit classification.
> 6. **Using $r = \omega_n/\omega$ instead of $r = \omega/\omega_n$** — this is a classic trap; double-check the definition before plugging in.
> 7. **Forgetting transient + steady-state discussion** in forced vibration. Full marks require explicit mention that the transient decays and only the steady state persists.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute review):**

- **DOF Definition:** Minimum independent coordinates needed to fully define the configuration of a vibrating system.
- **Natural Frequency (undamped):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — single most important quantity in vibration analysis.
- **Frequency Ratio:** $r = \omega/\omega_n$ — the dimensionless parameter governing all forced-vibration formulas.
- **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km}) = c/c_c$ — controls the rate of decay and the peak amplitude.
- **Damped Natural Frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — for $\zeta < 1$ only.
- **Damping Regimes:** $\zeta < 1$ underdamped, $\zeta = 1$ critically damped, $\zeta > 1$ overdamped.
- **Logarithmic Decrement:** $\delta = (2\pi\zeta)/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 2\pi\zeta$ for small $\zeta$ — used to measure $\zeta$ experimentally.
- **Magnification Factor (MF):** $M = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ — peaks at $\omega = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ with $M_{\max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$.
- **Transmissibility (TR):** $\text{TR} = \sqrt{[1+(2\zeta r)^2]/[(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2]}$ — isolation when $r > \sqrt{2}$.
- **Static Deflection:** $X_{\text{static}} = F_0/k$ — used as the reference for MF.
- **Phase Angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}[2\zeta r/(1-r^2)]$ — displacement lags the applied force by $\phi$.
- **2-DOF Eigenvalue Equation:** $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2\mathbf{M}) = 0$ — yields a quadratic in $\omega^2$, giving two natural frequencies.
- **Mode Shape Ratio:** $X_2^{(i)}/X_1^{(i)} = (k_1 + k_2 - \omega_i^2 m_1)/k_2$ — derived from the first row of the eigenvector equation.
- **In-Phase Mode:** The lower natural frequency, masses move together.
- **Out-of-Phase Mode:** The higher natural frequency, masses move oppositely.
- **Modal Superposition:** Any free vibration response is a linear combination of mode shapes with coefficients set by initial conditions.
- **Vehicle Design Targets:** Body bounce $f_n \approx 1$–2 Hz for ride comfort; engine/transmission mounts tuned to keep $r > \sqrt{2}$ for dominant firing frequencies.
- **Numerical Habit:** Always compute $r$ first, then $M$ or TR, then multiply by the static reference to get the actual amplitude.
- **Units Audit:** Every formula's units must balance. $\omega_n$ in rad/s, $\omega_d$ in rad/s, $M$ dimensionless, TR dimensionless, $X$ in metres.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Body Vibrations: Bouncing and Pitching, Doubly Conjugate Points (Only Basic Idea), Body Rolling, Roll Centre and Roll Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Body Vibrations: Bouncing and Pitching, Doubly Conjugate Points (Only Basic Idea), Body Rolling, Roll Centre and Roll Axis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Body Vibrations & Roll Dynamics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bouncing (Heave) Vibration
**Formal Definition:** Bouncing is the rigid-body translation of the vehicle sprung mass along the vertical (z) axis, in which the Centre of Gravity (CG) moves up and down without any rotation. Mathematically, the bounce coordinate is denoted by the scalar displacement $z$ of the CG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bouncing is a 1-DOF pure translation.** It is excited whenever the front and rear wheels are subjected to correlated vertical road inputs.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a person standing on a yoga mat placed on a vibrating washing machine platform. The person's hips (CG) move straight up and down, parallel to the legs — no tilt occurs. That is bouncing.

### 1.2 Pitching Vibration
**Formal Definition:** Pitching is the rigid-body rotation of the sprung mass about a transverse (lateral, y) axis passing through the CG, such that the front of the vehicle moves up while the rear moves down, and vice versa. The coordinate is the small angle $\theta$ about the y-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitching is a 1-DOF pure rotation.** It dominates when the front wheels hit a bump while the rear wheels are on smooth road (or vice versa), creating a moment about the CG.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a ship pitching in head-on waves — the bow goes up as the stern goes down, rotating about a transverse axis. A car's nose-dive under braking and nose-up under acceleration are quasi-static forms of pitching.

### 1.3 Coupled Bounce–Pitch Model (2-DOF)
**Formal Definition:** When both translation and rotation occur simultaneously due to independent road inputs at the front and rear axles, the sprung mass exhibits a 2-DOF coupled system whose natural modes are the **Bounce Mode** (z and θ in phase) and the **Pitch Mode** (z and θ out of phase).

### 1.4 Doubly Conjugate Points (Basic Idea)
**Formal Definition:** A *conjugate point* is a specific location on the vehicle body whose vertical displacement becomes **zero** when the road is excited at one wheel station (front or rear) at a particular frequency. A **Doubly Conjugate Point** is a special location that remains a stationary node **simultaneously for both front and rear excitations** — the body motion at this point is identically zero across the coupled frequency spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> Doubly conjugate points are critically important for **seat placement** and the location of **vibration-sensitive instrumentation** in the vehicle.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a long seesaw with two children sitting on it. There is one point on the seesaw that stays still no matter which child jumps — the *pivot of the moment*, but for vibration it is the *nodal point*.

### 1.5 Body Rolling
**Formal Definition:** Body rolling is the rigid-body rotation of the sprung mass about the **longitudinal (roll) axis** (x-axis), caused by lateral acceleration during cornering, lane changes, or cross-wind disturbances. The roll angle is denoted by $\phi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Rolling is a transverse plane (x–z plane) rotation that occurs in the plane perpendicular to pitch.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A motorcycle leaning into a curve is a controlled roll. A car's body tilting outward when taking a sharp turn is body roll.

### 1.6 Roll Centre and Roll Axis
**Formal Definition:**
- **Roll Centre (RC):** In each axle's vertical (y–z) plane, the Roll Centre is the point in the ground projection about which the wheel (and hence the body) instantaneously rotates when the suspension moves in jounce/rebound. Geometrically, it is the intersection of the lines joining the wheel centre to the **instant centres** of the suspension links.
- **Roll Axis:** The line connecting the **front Roll Centre** and the **rear Roll Centre**. The sprung mass rotates about this 3-D line during body roll.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Roll Axis is the **kinematic axis of rotation** of the sprung mass — analogous to the pitch axis in the side view, but in the front view.

**Standard Metric:** Typical passenger-car Roll Centre heights range from **50 mm to 200 mm** above the road for the front suspension, and **100 mm to 250 mm** for the rear.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2-DOF Bounce–Pitch Model

### Assumptions
- Small oscillations → linearisation valid.
- Sprung mass treated as a rigid body with mass $m$ and pitch mass moment of inertia $I_\theta$.
- Suspension modelled as linear springs (stiffness $k_f, k_r$) — damping neglected in basic formulation.
- Unsprung mass neglected.
- Road inputs $z_f, z_r$ are kinematic (base excitation).

### Degrees of Freedom
- $z(t)$ : vertical displacement of CG (positive **upward**).
- $\theta(t)$ : pitch angle about transverse axis (positive **nose-up**).
- $a, b$ : longitudinal distances from CG to front and rear axle.

### Free-Body Logic Steps

1. **Vertical Force Equilibrium (Newton's 2nd Law):** Sum of all suspension forces on the sprung mass equals $m \ddot{z}$.
2. **Moment Equilibrium (Euler's Equation):** Sum of moments of suspension forces about the CG equals $I_\theta \ddot{\theta}$.
3. **Kinematic Coupling:** Because $\theta$ is small, the vertical displacement of the front body station is $z + a\theta$ (nose-up pitch lifts the front), and the rear station is $z - b\theta$.
4. **Spring Force Law:** Each suspension spring exerts a restoring force proportional to the **relative displacement** between the body station and the road station.

### Significance in Engineering
- The coupled model is the **basis of ride-comfort analysis** at low frequencies (0.5–5 Hz).
- It is used to design suspension stiffness ratios that decouple bounce and pitch.
- It dictates the **optimum suspension stiffness distribution** for an anti-dive/anti-squat geometry.

## 2.2 Doubly Conjugate Points — Basic Concept

### Physical Significance
- In a 2-DOF coupled system, each road input produces a node (zero-motion point) on the body.
- The **front-input conjugate point** is a specific distance $l_1$ from CG; the **rear-input conjugate point** is at distance $l_2$ from CG.
- A **doubly conjugate point** is a unique location (achievable at a specific frequency and stiffness combination) where both $l_1$ and $l_2$ coincide — the point has **zero response to either front or rear road excitation**.
- Practical placement: driver's seat, accelerometer mounting, optical sensor platform.

## 2.3 Body Rolling Kinematics

### Lateral Force Transfer
During cornering with lateral acceleration $a_y$:
- Vertical load transfers from inside wheels to outside wheels.
- Outer suspension compresses, inner suspension extends.
- The body rotates about the Roll Axis by an angle $\phi$.

### Roll Moment Balance
The total overturning moment is balanced by suspension roll stiffness and geometric (RC) reaction.

## 2.4 Roll Centre Geometry — Independent Suspension

For a **double wishbone** suspension:
- Line $\overline{AB}$ is drawn through the upper ball joint A and chassis pivot B.
- Line $\overline{CD}$ is drawn through the lower ball joint C and chassis pivot D.
- The **instant centre** $I$ of the wheel is the intersection of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$.
- The **Roll Centre** $R$ is found by drawing a line from the wheel contact patch $P$ through $I$ until it meets the vehicle's longitudinal centre plane — this intersection is $R$.

For a **MacPherson strut**:
- The strut defines the upper link line.
- The lower control arm defines the lower link.
- Their intersection is the instant centre, and the line from contact patch through this point locates the Roll Centre.

For a **solid (live) axle**:
- The Roll Centre lies at the axle's lateral mid-point, essentially at ground level — this produces a **high roll moment arm** and hence large body roll.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are aligned with the standard 2-DOF vehicle pitch-plane model. Symbols have their usual meanings.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bounce Equation of Motion | $m\ddot{z} + (k_f + k_r)z + (ak_f - bk_r)\theta = k_f z_f + k_r z_r$ | N, m, rad | Vertical dynamic equilibrium |
| Pitch Equation of Motion | $I_\theta \ddot{\theta} + (ak_f - bk_r)z + (a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)\theta = ak_f z_f - bk_r z_r$ | N·m, rad | Rotational dynamic equilibrium |
| Bounce Natural Frequency (decoupled) | $\omega_b = \sqrt{(k_f + k_r) / m}$ | rad/s | Heave mode natural freq. |
| Pitch Natural Frequency (decoupled) | $\omega_p = \sqrt{(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r) / I_\theta}$ | rad/s | Pitch mode natural freq. |
| Bounce–Pitch Decoupling Condition | $a k_f = b k_r$ | N | Makes $\omega_b, \omega_p$ independent |
| Conjugate Point Distance (front input) | $l_f = \dfrac{a B - C}{a A - B}$ where $A=k_f+k_r-m\omega^2$, $B=ak_f-bk_r$, $C=a^2k_f+b^2k_r-I_\theta\omega^2$ | m | Nodal point for front excitation |
| Conjugate Point Distance (rear input) | $l_r = \dfrac{-b B - C}{-b A - B}$ (analogous) | m | Nodal point for rear excitation |
| Doubly Conjugate Point Condition | $l_f = l_r$ at the same $\omega$ | m | Coincident nodal locations |
| Roll Moment (overturning) | $M_{roll} = m a_y h_{cg}$ | N·m | Overturning moment about Roll Axis |
| Roll Rate (static) | $\dfrac{\phi}{a_y} = \dfrac{m h_{cg}}{K_\phi - m g h_{cg}}$ (small-angle) | rad/(m/s²) | Body roll per unit lat. acc. |
| Roll Axis Definition | Line joining front RC and rear RC | — | Kinematic axis of body roll |
| Roll Centre Height (double wishbone) | From contact patch through instant centre to centre plane | m | Geometrically constructed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoupling trick:** Setting $a k_f = b k_r$ eliminates the off-diagonal coupling terms, allowing independent tuning of bounce and pitch natural frequencies — a fundamental concept in suspension design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2-DOF Bounce–Pitch Equations of Motion

### Sign Convention (Fixed Throughout)
- $z$ positive **upward** (bounce displacement of CG).
- $\theta$ positive **nose-up** (pitch).
- $z_f, z_r$ positive **upward** (road input displacement at front and rear).
- $a$ = distance from CG to **front** axle; $b$ = distance from CG to **rear** axle.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Body Stations
The body has a pitch axis through the CG. For a small pitch angle $\theta$:
- Front body station (at distance $a$ forward of CG) rises by $a\theta$ when nose pitches up.
- Rear body station (at distance $b$ behind CG) drops by $b\theta$ when nose pitches up.

Therefore the absolute vertical displacements are:
- Front body station: $z_f^{body} = z + a\theta$
- Rear body station: $z_r^{body} = z - b\theta$

### Step 2 — Suspension Spring Forces
The suspension spring at each axle is compressed (or extended) by the **relative displacement** between the body station and the road. The spring force on the body acts **opposite to the relative displacement**.

Front spring force on body (positive upward):
$$F_f = -k_f \big[(z + a\theta) - z_f\big] = k_f(z_f - z - a\theta)$$

Rear spring force on body (positive upward):
$$F_r = -k_r \big[(z - b\theta) - z_r\big] = k_r(z_r - z + b\theta)$$

**Explanation:** A positive $F_f$ means an upward force. If the body moves up while the road stays put, the spring extends and pulls the body down — hence the negative sign of $k_f(z+a\theta-z_f)$.

### Step 3 — Vertical Force Balance (Newton's 2nd Law)
Sum of forces in the vertical direction equals mass × vertical acceleration:
$$m\ddot{z} = F_f + F_r$$

Substituting the spring forces:
$$m\ddot{z} = k_f(z_f - z - a\theta) + k_r(z_r - z + b\theta)$$

Expanding and rearranging:
$$m\ddot{z} + (k_f + k_r)z + (a k_f - b k_r)\theta = k_f z_f + k_r z_r \qquad \text{(Eq. 1 — Bounce)}$$

### Step 4 — Moment Balance about CG (Euler's Equation)
Moment about the transverse (y) axis = $I_\theta \ddot{\theta}$.

- Front spring force $F_f$ acts at distance $a$ forward of CG. An upward force at a forward point creates a **nose-down** moment about CG, which is **negative** in our sign convention: moment = $-a \cdot F_f$.
- Rear spring force $F_r$ acts at distance $b$ behind CG. An upward force at a rear point creates a **nose-up** moment: moment = $+b \cdot F_r$.

$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} = -a F_f + b F_r$$

Substituting $F_f$ and $F_r$:
$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} = -a \cdot k_f(z_f - z - a\theta) + b \cdot k_r(z_r - z + b\theta)$$

Expanding:
$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} = -a k_f z_f + a k_f z + a^2 k_f \theta + b k_r z_r - b k_r z - b^2 k_r \theta$$

Rearranging:
$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} + (a k_f - b k_r)z + (a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)\theta = a k_f z_f - b k_r z_r \qquad \text{(Eq. 2 — Pitch)}$$

### Step 5 — Matrix Formulation
Equations 1 and 2 are combined into matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & I_\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{z} \\ \ddot{\theta} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_f + k_r & a k_f - b k_r \\ a k_f - b k_r & a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} z \\ \theta \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} k_f & k_r \\ a k_f & -b k_r \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} z_f \\ z_r \end{Bmatrix}$$

This is the canonical 2-DOF vehicle pitch-plane model.

## 3.2 Natural Frequencies (Decoupled Case)

The off-diagonal coupling term $a k_f - b k_r$ vanishes when:
$$a k_f = b k_r \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{b}{a}$$

This is the **bounce–pitch decoupling condition**. Substituting back:

**Bounce (z-direction):** Equation 1 reduces to:
$$m\ddot{z} + (k_f + k_r) z = 0$$

For harmonic solution $z = Z e^{j\omega t}$:
$$(-m\omega^2 + k_f + k_r) Z = 0$$

Therefore the bounce natural frequency is:
$$\boxed{\omega_b = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_f + k_r}{m}}}$$

**Pitch (θ-direction):** Equation 2 reduces to:
$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} + (a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)\theta = 0$$

Therefore the pitch natural frequency is:
$$\boxed{\omega_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r}{I_\theta}}}$$

### Step 6 — General (Coupled) Natural Frequencies
For arbitrary stiffness, the characteristic equation is obtained from $\det([K] - \omega^2 [M]) = 0$:

$$\big(k_f + k_r - m\omega^2\big)\big(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r - I_\theta \omega^2\big) - (a k_f - b k_r)^2 = 0$$

This is a quadratic in $\omega^2$:
$$\omega^4 - \left[\frac{k_f + k_r}{m} + \frac{a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r}{I_\theta}\right]\omega^2 + \frac{(k_f + k_r)(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r) - (a k_f - b k_r)^2}{m I_\theta} = 0$$

The two positive roots give the **coupled bounce and pitch natural frequencies**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Conjugate Point Location (Front-Input Case)

### Setup
Apply a harmonic input at the **front** only: $z_f = Z_f e^{j\omega t}$, $z_r = 0$. Steady-state response: $z = Z e^{j\omega t}$, $\theta = \Theta e^{j\omega t}$.

### Step 1 — Frequency-Domain Equations
Substituting into Eq. 1 and Eq. 2 and cancelling $e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\big(k_f + k_r - m\omega^2\big) Z + (a k_f - b k_r)\Theta = k_f Z_f \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$(a k_f - b k_r) Z + \big(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r - I_\theta \omega^2\big) \Theta = a k_f Z_f \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Define:
- $A = k_f + k_r - m\omega^2$
- $B = a k_f - b k_r$
- $C = a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r - I_\theta \omega^2$
- $\Delta = AC - B^2$ (determinant)

### Step 2 — Solve for $Z$ and $\Theta$
By Cramer's rule:

$$Z = \frac{k_f C - a k_f B}{\Delta} Z_f = \frac{k_f(C - aB)}{\Delta} Z_f$$

$$\Theta = \frac{a k_f A - k_f B}{\Delta} Z_f = \frac{k_f(aA - B)}{\Delta} Z_f$$

### Step 3 — Conjugate Point Condition
A point on the body at distance $l$ forward of the CG has total vertical displacement:
$$u(l) = z + l\theta = Z e^{j\omega t} + l \Theta e^{j\omega t}$$

For zero motion (node), $u(l) = 0$, hence $Z + l \Theta = 0$:

$$\frac{k_f(C - aB)}{\Delta} + l \cdot \frac{k_f(aA - B)}{\Delta} = 0$$

$$C - aB + l(aA - B) = 0$$

Solving for $l$ (the location of the front-input conjugate point, measured forward from CG):
$$\boxed{l_f = \frac{aB - C}{aA - B}}$$

### Step 4 — Rear-Input Conjugate Point
By analogous derivation (excitation at rear, $z_r = Z_r e^{j\omega t}$, $z_f = 0$):
$$\boxed{l_r = \frac{-bB - C}{-bA - B}}$$

measured **rearward** from CG.

### Step 5 — Doubly Conjugate Point (Basic Idea)
A **doubly conjugate point** exists when the same physical location satisfies both nodal conditions:
$$l_f = l_r \quad \text{(at the same excitation frequency } \omega \text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU syllabus, only the *basic idea* is required: a doubly conjugate point is a unique body location that remains stationary for both front and rear road inputs. Its existence depends on a specific combination of $m, I_\theta, k_f, k_r, a, b$ and excitation frequency.

## 3.4 Roll Centre Height — Double Wishbone Derivation (Geometric)

Consider the front suspension in the y–z plane (front view of vehicle).

### Given
- Upper wishbone: chassis pivot at $B$ (height $h_B$, lateral $y_B$), ball joint at $A$ (height $h_A$, lateral $y_A$).
- Lower wishbone: chassis pivot at $D$ (height $h_D$, lateral $y_D$), ball joint at $C$ (height $h_C$, lateral $y_C$).
- Wheel contact patch at $P$ on the ground, lateral position $y_P$.

### Step 1 — Locate the Instant Centre $I$
The instant centre is the intersection of the two wishbone lines $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$:
- Line $AB$: $\dfrac{z - h_A}{y - y_A} = \dfrac{h_B - h_A}{y_B - y_A}$
- Line $CD$: $\dfrac{z - h_C}{y - y_C} = \dfrac{h_D - h_C}{y_D - y_C}$

The intersection point is $(y_I, z_I)$.

### Step 2 — Construct the Roll Centre
The Roll Centre lies on the line from the contact patch $P$ through the instant centre $I$, intersected with the vehicle's longitudinal centre plane ($y = 0$ for one wheel side; for symmetric analysis, take the centre plane).

Parametric form of line $PI$:
$$(y, z) = (1-\lambda)(y_P, 0) + \lambda(y_I, z_I)$$

Setting $y = 0$ (centre plane):
$$\lambda^* = \frac{y_P}{y_P - y_I}$$

Therefore the Roll Centre coordinates are:
$$y_{RC} = 0, \qquad z_{RC} = \lambda^* z_I = \frac{y_P \, z_I}{y_P - y_I}$$

The **Roll Centre height** is:
$$\boxed{h_{RC} = \frac{y_P \, z_I}{y_P - y_I}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Roll Centre height is a purely geometric property of the suspension. It is independent of the spring rate, damping, or vehicle mass.

## 3.5 Roll Rate Derivation (Static)

For steady cornering with lateral acceleration $a_y$:

### Step 1 — Overturning Moment
$$M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg}$$

This moment is reacted by:
- The suspension roll stiffness $K_\phi$ (twist about Roll Axis).
- A geometric moment from the weight shift: $m g \cdot (h_{cg} - h_{RC}) \cdot \sin\phi \approx m g (h_{cg} - h_{RC}) \phi$.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium
$$K_\phi \phi + m g (h_{cg} - h_{RC}) \phi = m a_y h_{cg}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Roll Angle
$$\phi = \frac{m a_y h_{cg}}{K_\phi + m g (h_{cg} - h_{RC})}$$

For small angles and $K_\phi \gg m g (h_{cg} - h_{RC})$:
$$\boxed{\frac{\phi}{a_y} \approx \frac{m h_{cg}}{K_\phi}}$$

**Key insight:** A lower Roll Centre ($h_{RC}$ smaller) increases the *effective* roll stiffness and hence *reduces* body roll — a critical trade-off in suspension geometry design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 2-DOF Bounce–Pitch Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Road Input z_f at Front Axle] --> F1[Front Suspension k_f]
    B[Road Input z_r at Rear Axle] --> F2[Rear Suspension k_r]
    F1 --> C[Body CG: Combined Bounce z and Pitch theta]
    F2 --> C
    C --> D[Output 1: CG Displacement z]
    C --> E[Output 2: Pitch Angle theta]
    C --> F[Front Body Station: z + a theta]
    C --> G[Rear Body Station: z - b theta]
    F --> H[Conjugate Point Check: Z + l Theta = 0]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Doubly Conjugate Point: l_f = l_r]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Roll Centre Construction (Front Suspension)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Wheel Contact Patch P at Ground Level] --> L1[Line P to Instant Centre I]
    I[Instant Centre I: Intersection of Upper and Lower Wishbone Lines] --> L1
    L1 --> RC[Roll Centre R on Vehicle Centre Plane]
    AB[Upper Wishbone Line AB] --> I
    CD[Lower Wishbone Line CD] --> I
    F1[Upper Ball Joint A] --> AB
    F2[Upper Chassis Pivot B] --> AB
    F3[Lower Ball Joint C] --> CD
    F4[Lower Chassis Pivot D] --> CD
    RC --> RA[Front Roll Centre]
    RA --> RAX[Roll Axis: Connect Front RC to Rear RC]
    RAX --> BR[Body Rotates about Roll Axis during Cornering]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Body Roll Dynamics

```mermaid
graph LR
    AY[Lateral Acceleration a_y from Cornering] --> M1[Overturning Moment m*a_y*h_cg]
    M1 --> EQ[Roll Moment Equilibrium]
    KSUS[Suspension Roll Stiffness K_phi] --> EQ
    GMET[Geometric Moment m*g*h_cg - h_rc*phi] --> EQ
    EQ --> PHOUT[Roll Angle phi Output]
    PHOUT --> RB[Body Bank Angle]
    PHOUT --> LS[Load Transfer to Outer Wheels]
    RC[Roll Centre Height h_rc] --> GMET
    CG[Centre of Gravity Height h_cg] --> M1
    CG --> GMET
```

## 4.4 System Architecture — Coupled Bounce–Pitch DOF Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Inputs
        ZF[z_f Front Road]
        ZR[z_r Rear Road]
    end
    subgraph Stiffness Matrix
        K11[k_f + k_r]
        K12[a*k_f - b*k_r Coupling]
        K22[a_squared*k_f + b_squared*k_r]
    end
    subgraph Mass Matrix
        M11[m at CG]
        M22[I_theta about Pitch Axis]
    end
    ZF --> K11
    ZR --> K22
    K11 --> EOM1[Bounce EOM]
    K12 --> EOM1
    K12 --> EOM2[Pitch EOM]
    K22 --> EOM2
    M11 --> EOM1
    M22 --> EOM2
    EOM1 --> OUT1[Output z Bounce]
    EOM2 --> OUT2[Output theta Pitch]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Conjugate Point. What is the significance of Doubly Conjugate Points in vehicle ride comfort? [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **conjugate point** is a specific location on the vehicle sprung mass where the vertical displacement is **identically zero** when the road is excited at a particular wheel station (front or rear) at a given frequency. It arises from the destructive interference between the bouncing translation $z$ and the pitching rotation $\theta$ of the CG.

A **doubly conjugate point** is a single location on the body that remains a stationary node **simultaneously for both front and rear road excitations**. This is significant because:
1. It identifies the **optimum location for the driver's seat** and passengers — minimum transmitted vibration.
2. It guides the **placement of vibration-sensitive instruments** (IMU, accelerometers, cameras, ECUs).
3. It serves as a **design benchmark** for verifying suspension tuning during ride-comfort optimisation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students often confuse *conjugate point* with *centre of percussion* or *instant centre of suspension*. They are completely different concepts — conjugate points are *dynamic nodal points*, not kinematic pivots.

---

### Question 2: Define Roll Centre and Roll Axis. Why is the Roll Centre height important in suspension design? [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Roll Centre (RC)** is the point, in the y–z plane of each axle, about which the wheel (and consequently the body) instantaneously rotates when the suspension deflects. It is geometrically constructed by drawing a line from the wheel contact patch through the instant centre of the suspension links until it meets the vehicle's longitudinal centre plane.

The **Roll Axis** is the line connecting the front Roll Centre to the rear Roll Centre; the sprung mass rotates about this 3-D axis during body roll.

**Importance of RC height:**
- A **lower Roll Centre** provides greater *effective roll stiffness*, reducing body roll angle for a given lateral acceleration.
- It influences the **lateral load transfer distribution** between front and rear axles — critical for handling balance (understeer/oversteer).
- It affects **squat and dive** behaviour under acceleration and braking.
- Typical RC heights are in the range **50–250 mm** above the road for passenger cars.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Many students write "Roll Centre is the centre of gravity of the car" — this is **wrong**. The Roll Centre is a *kinematic point of the suspension*, not a mass property.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Bounce–Pitch Model & Conjugate Points

#### (a) Derive the equations of motion for the 2-DOF bounce–pitch model of a vehicle sprung mass. Clearly state the assumptions and sign convention. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Small oscillations (linearisation valid).
2. Sprung mass is rigid with mass $m$ and pitch inertia $I_\theta$.
3. Suspension modelled as linear springs of stiffness $k_f, k_r$ (damping neglected).
4. Unsprung mass neglected.
5. Road inputs $z_f, z_r$ are kinematic excitations.

**Step 2 — Sign Convention (0.5 Marks):**
- $z$ positive upward (CG bounce).
- $\theta$ positive nose-up (pitch).
- $z_f, z_r$ positive upward.

**Step 3 — Geometry (0.5 Marks):**
- Front body station displacement: $z + a\theta$.
- Rear body station displacement: $z - b\theta$.

**Step 4 — Force Equilibrium (2.5 Marks):**
Vertical force balance on sprung mass:
$$m\ddot{z} = k_f(z_f - z - a\theta) + k_r(z_r - z + b\theta)$$
$$\boxed{m\ddot{z} + (k_f + k_r)z + (ak_f - bk_r)\theta = k_f z_f + k_r z_r}$$

**Step 5 — Moment Equilibrium (2.5 Marks):**
Moment about CG:
$$I_\theta \ddot{\theta} = -a k_f(z_f - z - a\theta) + b k_r(z_r - z + b\theta)$$
$$\boxed{I_\theta \ddot{\theta} + (ak_f - bk_r)z + (a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)\theta = a k_f z_f - b k_r z_r}$$

---

#### (b) For a passenger car with $m = 1500$ kg, $I_\theta = 2500$ kg·m², $a = 1.3$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, find the suspension stiffness values $k_f$ and $k_r$ that decouple the bounce and pitch modes. Also calculate the bounce and pitch natural frequencies assuming $I_\theta = 2500$ kg·m². (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Decoupling Condition (2 Marks):**
The off-diagonal coupling term vanishes when:
$$a k_f = b k_r \implies \frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{1.5}{1.3} = 1.1538$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Reasonable Total Stiffness (1 Mark):**
For passenger cars, typical bounce natural frequency is $\omega_b \approx 1.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 9.42$ rad/s.
$$k_f + k_r = m \omega_b^2 = 1500 \times 88.83 \approx 133{,}245 \text{ N/m}$$

Combined with $k_f = 1.1538 \, k_r$:
$$1.1538 k_r + k_r = 2.1538 k_r = 133{,}245$$
$$k_r = 61{,}855 \text{ N/m}, \quad k_f = 71{,}390 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Bounce Natural Frequency (1 Mark):**
$$\omega_b = \sqrt{\frac{k_f + k_r}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{133{,}245}{1500}} = \sqrt{88.83} \approx 9.42 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_b = \frac{\omega_b}{2\pi} \approx 1.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Pitch Natural Frequency (2 Marks):**
$$\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r}{I_\theta}}$$

Computing the numerator:
$$a^2 k_f = (1.3)^2 \times 71{,}390 = 1.69 \times 71{,}390 = 120{,}649$$
$$b^2 k_r = (1.5)^2 \times 61{,}855 = 2.25 \times 61{,}855 = 139{,}174$$
$$a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r = 259{,}823$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{259{,}823}{2500}} = \sqrt{103.93} \approx 10.19 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_p \approx 1.62 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The decoupled system has $\omega_b \approx 9.42$ rad/s and $\omega_p \approx 10.19$ rad/s, well-separated, with bounce and pitch independent — the design target for comfortable ride.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students forget the factor of $a^2$ and $b^2$ in the pitch stiffness — they write $\omega_p = \sqrt{(k_f + k_r)/I_\theta}$, which has wrong units. The pitch stiffness is the **second moment** of suspension stiffness about the CG, not a simple sum.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Body Rolling & Roll Centre Geometry

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the geometric construction of the Roll Centre for a double wishbone suspension. Why does a solid axle have a much lower Roll Centre? (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Roll Centre (1 Mark):**
The Roll Centre is the point in the vehicle's longitudinal centre plane about which the wheel instantaneously rotates when the suspension moves in jounce/rebound.

**Step 2 — Geometric Construction (4 Marks):**
1. Identify the upper wishbone: locate ball joint $A$ and chassis pivot $B$.
2. Identify the lower wishbone: locate ball joint $C$ and chassis pivot $D$.
3. Draw line $\overline{AB}$ extended.
4. Draw line $\overline{CD}$ extended.
5. Their intersection is the **Instant Centre $I$** of the wheel.
6. Draw a line from the wheel contact patch $P$ through $I$.
7. The intersection of this line with the vehicle's longitudinal centre plane is the **Roll Centre $R$**.

**Step 3 — Why Solid Axle has Lower RC (2 Marks):**
In a solid (live) axle, the axle itself is rigid and locates the wheel. The instant centre is essentially at the axle's lateral mid-point, very close to ground level. The line from the contact patch through this instant centre meets the centre plane almost at the ground itself. Therefore:
- $h_{RC}$ for solid axle is near **zero** (or slightly above ground).
- This produces a **large roll moment arm** $(h_{cg} - h_{RC})$.
- Result: very high body roll during cornering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often confuse the Roll Centre with the **roll axis** of the body, or forget that the Roll Centre is a **kinematic** point, not a force application point. The reaction force from the suspension at the RC is a horizontal force only — the vertical component passes through the contact patch.

---

#### (b) A vehicle of mass $m = 1800$ kg has its CG at height $h_{cg} = 0.6$ m. The front and rear Roll Centre heights are $h_{RC,f} = 0.12$ m and $h_{RC,r} = 0.18$ m respectively. The suspension roll stiffness is $K_\phi = 60{,}000$ N·m/rad. Calculate the steady-state roll angle when the vehicle corners at lateral acceleration $a_y = 0.4g$. Assume the wheelbase is $L = 2.8$ m with CG located at $a = 1.3$ m from the front. (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Roll Centre Height (Weighted) (2 Marks):**
The effective Roll Centre height used in roll calculations is the location along the wheelbase:
$$h_{RC} = \frac{b \cdot h_{RC,f} + a \cdot h_{RC,r}}{a + b}$$

With $a = 1.3$ m, $b = 1.5$ m:
$$h_{RC} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.12 + 1.3 \times 0.18}{2.8} = \frac{0.18 + 0.234}{2.8} = \frac{0.414}{2.8} \approx 0.148 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Overturning Moment (1 Mark):**
$$M_{roll} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{cg} = 1800 \times 0.4 \times 9.81 \times 0.6 = 4237.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Restoring Moment Coefficient (2 Marks):**
$$\Delta K = m g (h_{cg} - h_{RC}) = 1800 \times 9.81 \times (0.6 - 0.148) = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.452 = 7980.9 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Roll Angle (2 Marks):**
$$\phi = \frac{M_{roll}}{K_\phi + \Delta K} = \frac{4237.9}{60{,}000 + 7980.9} = \frac{4237.9}{67{,}980.9} \approx 0.0623 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$\phi \approx 0.0623 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 3.57°$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Optional, 0 Marks, Just for Cross-Check):**
Roll rate: $\phi / a_y = 0.0623 / (0.4 \times 9.81) = 0.0159$ rad/(m/s²) ≈ 0.91°/g — a typical value for a comfort-oriented sedan.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** A common error is using $a_y = 0.4g$ but forgetting to multiply by $g$ to convert to m/s². Always work in SI units: $a_y = 0.4 \times 9.81 = 3.924$ m/s² before substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Body Vibrations & Roll)**

### Bouncing & Pitching
- ✅ **Bouncing = pure vertical translation** $z(t)$ of CG; **Pitching = pure rotation** $\theta(t)$ about the transverse axis.
- ✅ The 2-DOF model couples $z$ and $\theta$ through the off-diagonal term $(a k_f - b k_r)$ in the stiffness matrix.
- ✅ **Decoupling condition:** $a k_f = b k_r$ — set the ratio of suspension stiffnesses equal to the inverse ratio of axle distances from CG.
- ✅ **Bounce natural frequency** (decoupled): $\omega_b = \sqrt{(k_f + k_r)/m}$.
- ✅ **Pitch natural frequency** (decoupled): $\omega_p = \sqrt{(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)/I_\theta}$ — note $a^2$ and $b^2$ factors.
- ✅ For coupled case, the characteristic equation is a **quadratic in $\omega^2$** yielding two positive roots.

### Doubly Conjugate Points
- ✅ **Conjugate point = nodal point** (zero vertical motion) for a single-wheel-station excitation.
- ✅ **Doubly conjugate point = same nodal point** for both front AND rear excitations.
- ✅ Practical use: optimum location for **driver's seat, instrumentation, sensors**.
- ✅ Location depends on the excitation frequency and is given by the formula $l_f = (aB - C)/(aA - B)$.

### Body Rolling
- ✅ **Roll = rotation about longitudinal axis** $\phi(t)$; excited by **lateral acceleration** during cornering.
- ✅ **Overturning moment:** $M_{roll} = m \, a_y \, h_{cg}$.
- ✅ **Roll rate** decreases with higher roll stiffness $K_\phi$ and lower CG height $h_{cg}$.

### Roll Centre & Roll Axis
- ✅ **Roll Centre = geometric intersection** of contact-patch-to-instant-centre line with vehicle centre plane.
- ✅ **Instant Centre = intersection** of upper and lower suspension link lines.
- ✅ **Roll Axis = line connecting front and rear Roll Centres** — the kinematic axis of body rotation.
- ✅ **Lower Roll Centre → more effective roll stiffness** → less body roll, but possibly more bump steer.
- ✅ **Solid axle Roll Centre ≈ ground level**; **independent suspension Roll Centre = 50–250 mm**.
- ✅ For double wishbone: $h_{RC} = (y_P \, z_I)/(y_P - y_I)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Blunders to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **conjugate point** with **centre of percussion**.
> 2. Confusing **Roll Centre** with **Centre of Gravity** — they are entirely different.
> 3. Forgetting $a^2$ and $b^2$ factors in pitch stiffness.
> 4. Using wrong sign convention (bounce up vs. down, nose-up vs. nose-down pitch).
> 5. Treating the Roll Centre as a force point — only horizontal force passes through RC; vertical passes through contact patch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roll Axis and the Vehicle under the Action of Side Forces, Stability against Body Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Roll Axis and the Vehicle under the Action of Side Forces, Stability against Body Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Roll Axis — Formal Definition

The **Roll Axis** of a vehicle is an imaginary straight line (in the longitudinal vertical plane) joining the **front roll center** and the **rear roll center** of the suspension system. It represents the instantaneous axis about which the sprung mass (vehicle body) rotates when subjected to lateral (side) forces during cornering or cross-wind disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The roll axis is generally **inclined** to the horizontal because the front and rear roll centers lie at different heights above the road. This inclination directly governs the **direction of wheel travel** during jounce/rebound, and the magnitude of the **jacking effect** under side loads.

**Roll Center:** A point on the front (or rear) view of the suspension, located in the plane of the wheel, about which the sprung mass rotates instantaneously under a pure lateral force. It is the **instantaneous center of rotation** of the body when only lateral forces act at the tire contact patches.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tilted Hinge"

Imagine you are holding a long wooden plank horizontally and pushing it sideways at the top end. The plank will rotate about some *pivot point* near its base. The lower that pivot point is, the more the plank tilts. The line connecting the front-pivot and rear-pivot of the plank is its **roll axis** — exactly what a vehicle body has.

- If the roll axis is **low and near the ground** → the body rolls only a little, but the wheels are dragged inward (good stability, harsh ride).
- If the roll axis is **high and near the body** → the body rolls a lot, but the wheels move freely (comfortable, but unstable).

> [!NOTE]
> The roll axis is *not* a fixed physical rod. It is a **kinematic construction** that depends on the geometry of the suspension links. As the suspension moves, the roll centers shift, and so does the roll axis.

## 1.3 Vehicle under the Action of Side Forces

When a vehicle negotiates a curve of radius $R$ at speed $v$, the centripetal acceleration $a_y$ acting at the Center of Gravity (CG) produces an apparent **centrifugal force** $F_c$ acting outward. This force is offset from the ground by the CG height $h$, creating a **roll-couples** the body:

$$F_c = m \cdot a_y = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{R} \quad [\text{Newtons}]$$

This lateral force has **two harmful side effects** the chassis engineer must simultaneously control:

| Effect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Body Roll** | Rotation of the body about the roll axis (cosmetic & handling impact) |
| **Load Transfer** | Vertical load shifts from the inside wheels to the outside wheels (tire grip concern) |

## 1.4 Stability against Body Rolling

**Stability against body rolling** refers to the vehicle's ability to resist overturning and maintain tire-road contact when subjected to lateral accelerations. The two key failure modes are:

1. **Rollover Threshold (RST):** The lateral acceleration at which the inside wheels lose contact with the road (zero normal force).
2. **Loss of Control:** The lateral acceleration at which tires saturate and the vehicle slides off the road before rollover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Metric:** The **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** is widely used by regulatory bodies (NHTSA, ISO 3888). It is defined as:
> $$\text{SSF} = \frac{t}{2h}$$
> where $t$ = track width, $h$ = CG height. A higher SSF means a more rollover-resistant vehicle (e.g., SUVs: 1.0–1.3, Sports cars: 1.6–1.9).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of the Rolling Vehicle — Step-by-Step Logic

When a vehicle rounds a curve, the following chain of events occurs at every instant:

1. **Lateral Force Generation:** The tires develop cornering forces $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ at the front and rear contact patches, proportional to slip angles.
2. **Centrifugal Reaction:** The vehicle's mass $m$ resists the change in direction, producing an inertial force $F_c = m \cdot a_y$ acting at the CG, height $h$ above the road.
3. **Roll Couple Formation:** $F_c$ acting at height $h$ generates an overturning moment about the line joining the contact patches:
   $$M_{\text{overturning}} = F_c \cdot h = m \cdot a_y \cdot h$$
4. **Resisting Moment (Suspension):** The suspension springs and anti-roll bars develop a roll-resisting moment, called the **Total Roll Stiffness** $K_\phi$ (in N·m/rad).
5. **Equilibrium Roll Angle:** The body settles at a steady roll angle $\phi$ where the resisting moment equals the overturning moment:
   $$K_\phi \cdot \phi = m \cdot a_y \cdot h$$
6. **Load Transfer:** The roll couple is transmitted to the ground as a **lateral load transfer** $\Delta W$ between inside and outside wheels:
   $$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$$

## 2.2 Why Does the Roll Axis Slope?

The roll axis is generally **inclined** upward at the rear (or front) because:
- The front suspension geometry (e.g., MacPherson strut) often has a roll center near the strut tower — relatively high.
- The rear suspension (e.g., solid axle) may have a roll center closer to the ground.
- An inclined roll axis means that body roll has components of both pure rotation AND vertical translation (jacking), which is generally undesirable for ride height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A low roll axis = good stability but high wheel rate variation. A high roll axis = good ride but excessive body roll. Designers balance this via **anti-roll bars** and **spring tuning**.

## 2.3 Distribution of Load Transfer (Front vs. Rear)

The total load transfer $\Delta W$ is split between the front and rear axles depending on:
- The **longitudinal position of the CG** ($a$ from front, $b$ from rear, $L = a+b$).
- The **roll stiffness distribution** $K_{\phi f} / (K_{\phi f} + K_{\phi r})$.

$$\Delta W_f = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t} \cdot \frac{K_{\phi f}}{K_\phi} - \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot b}{L} \cdot \text{(geometric transfer)}$$

A simpler, often-used split from quasi-static roll analysis:

$$\Delta W_f = \frac{m \cdot a_y}{t} \left( \frac{h \cdot K_{\phi f}}{K_\phi} \right) - \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot b}{L}$$

$$\Delta W_r = \Delta W - \Delta W_f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Understeer/oversteer balance is *highly sensitive* to how load transfer is distributed front-to-rear. Too much rear transfer = oversteer, too little = lazy turn-in.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_y = v^2 / R$ | Lateral (centripetal) acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 2 | $F_c = m \cdot a_y$ | Centrifugal force on vehicle | N |
| 3 | $M_{\text{roll}} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h$ | Overturning roll moment | N·m |
| 4 | $K_\phi = K_{\phi f} + K_{\phi r}$ | Total roll stiffness | N·m/rad |
| 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{K_\phi}$ | Steady-state body roll angle | rad |
| 6 | $\Delta W = \dfrac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$ | Total lateral load transfer | N |
| 7 | $\text{SSF} = t / (2h)$ | Static Stability Factor (Rollover metric) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $a_{y,\text{roll}} = \dfrac{g \cdot t}{2h}$ | Rollover threshold lateral acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 9 | $K_{\phi} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot t^2}{2}$ (single axle) | Roll stiffness from spring rate $K_s$ (per wheel) | N·m/rad |
| 10 | $\phi = \dfrac{\Delta W}{K_\phi / (t/2)} = \dfrac{2 \Delta W \cdot (t/2)}{K_\phi}$ | Alternative form for roll angle | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for KTU:** Lateral force acting on the **outside** of the curve is **positive** ($+y$). Roll moment that tilts the body **outward** (away from curve center) is also **positive**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chassis Tuning:** Race engineers adjust anti-roll bars to shift load transfer front-to-rear, biasing understeer/oversteer.
- **SUV/Cargo Vehicle Design:** A low CG and wide track are mandated for high SSF and rollover safety ratings.
- **Suspension Geometry Optimization:** Placing roll centers at specific heights controls jacking force and bump-steer during cornering.
- **ADAS Systems:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC) algorithms use roll rate sensors and lateral acceleration to predict impending rollover and apply selective braking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Body Roll Angle

**Given:** A vehicle of mass $m$ traveling on a curve of radius $R$ at constant speed $v$. The CG is at height $h$ above the ground and is centered laterally on the track of width $t$. The total roll stiffness is $K_\phi$.

**Find:** The steady-state body roll angle $\phi$ as a function of $a_y$.

**Step 1 — Identify the lateral acceleration.**
The vehicle has a centripetal acceleration directed toward the center of the curve:

$$a_y = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Express the centrifugal inertial force at the CG.**

By D'Alembert's principle, in the vehicle's non-inertial frame, a fictitious centrifugal force acts outward at the CG:

$$F_c = m \cdot a_y = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overturning moment about the roll axis.**
The line joining the contact patches of the outside and inside wheels lies on the ground. The perpendicular distance from the CG to this line is $h$ (the CG height). Hence the overturning moment magnitude is:

$$M_{\text{ov}} = F_c \cdot h = m \cdot a_y \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Write the equilibrium of moments.**
The suspension system (springs + anti-roll bars) provides a resisting moment proportional to the body roll angle $\phi$:

$$M_{\text{resist}} = K_\phi \cdot \phi$$

At steady state:

$$M_{\text{resist}} = M_{\text{ov}}$$

$$K_\phi \cdot \phi = m \cdot a_y \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\phi$.**

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{K_\phi}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $\phi$ is directly proportional to $m$, $a_y$, and $h$ (heavier, faster, or taller vehicles roll more).
- $\phi$ is inversely proportional to $K_\phi$ (stiffer suspensions roll less).
- Typical passenger car values: $\phi \approx 2^\circ\text{–}4^\circ$ at $a_y = 0.3g$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Lateral Load Transfer

**Given:** The same vehicle as in 3.1.

**Find:** The vertical load transferred from the inside wheels to the outside wheels.

**Step 1 — Free-body of the entire vehicle.**
The four tire contact patches exert vertical normal forces on the vehicle. Let $W_o$ = total normal force on outside wheels, $W_i$ = total normal force on inside wheels. We have:

$$W_o + W_i = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Sum moments about the inside tire contact line (a line on the ground).**
The centrifugal force $F_c$ at height $h$ creates a moment. The outside wheels are at track-width distance $t$ from the inside line. The total weight $mg$ at the CG (centered laterally) creates no moment about the roll axis lateral projection (assuming symmetric CG).

$$\sum M_{\text{inside line}} = 0$$

$$W_o \cdot t - m \cdot g \cdot 0 - m \cdot a_y \cdot h = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the outside wheel load.**

$$W_o = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t} + \frac{m \cdot g}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Similarly, for the inside wheels:**

$$W_i = \frac{m \cdot g}{2} - \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$$

**Step 5 — Define load transfer $\Delta W$ as the difference:**

$$\Delta W = W_o - W_i = \frac{2 \cdot m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}$$

For a single axle, the load transfer is half:

$$\boxed{\Delta W_{\text{per axle}} = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget the factor of **2** between "per-axle" and "total-vehicle" load transfer. The KTU Board Examiner's key will always specify which is being asked.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rollover Threshold Lateral Acceleration

**Find:** The lateral acceleration at which the inside wheels lift off the ground ($W_i = 0$).

**Step 1 — Set $W_i = 0$ in the equation from 3.2.**

$$0 = \frac{m \cdot g}{2} - \frac{m \cdot a_{y,\text{roll}} \cdot h}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the critical $a_y$.**

$$\frac{m \cdot a_{y,\text{roll}} \cdot h}{t} = \frac{m \cdot g}{2}$$

$$a_{y,\text{roll}} = \frac{g \cdot t}{2h}$$

**Step 3 — Express in g-units.**

$$\boxed{\frac{a_{y,\text{roll}}}{g} = \frac{t}{2h} = \text{SSF}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the Static Stability Factor definition.** ✓

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- Track width: $t = 1.5$ m
- CG height: $h = 0.6$ m
- Mass: $m = 1500$ kg

$$\text{SSF} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times 0.6} = 1.25$$

$$a_{y,\text{roll}} = 1.25 \times 9.81 = 12.26 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 1.25g$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real vehicles almost never approach the theoretical SSF value because **tire grip is lost long before** the inside wheels lift off. The SSF is purely a **geometric** rollover metric, not a handling one.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Roll Stiffness from Suspension Spring Rate

**Find:** Total roll stiffness $K_\phi$ of an axle with two coil springs of rate $K_s$ each.

**Step 1 — Define roll stiffness.**
Roll stiffness is the resisting moment per unit roll angle of the body about the roll axis. For a small roll $\phi$:

$$M_{\text{resist}} = K_\phi \cdot \phi$$

**Step 2 — Relate body roll to spring compression.**
For small $\phi$, the outside wheel moves up by $(t/2) \cdot \phi$ and the inside wheel moves down by $(t/2) \cdot \phi$. Each spring deflects by $\delta = (t/2) \cdot \phi$.

**Step 3 — Force in each spring.**

$$F_s = K_s \cdot \delta = K_s \cdot \frac{t}{2} \cdot \phi$$

**Step 4 — Resisting moment (force × moment arm $t/2$):**

$$M_{\text{resist}} = 2 \cdot F_s \cdot \frac{t}{2} = 2 \cdot K_s \cdot \frac{t}{2} \cdot \phi \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$M_{\text{resist}} = K_s \cdot \frac{t^2}{2} \cdot \phi$$

**Step 6 — Identify roll stiffness:**

$$\boxed{K_{\phi, \text{axle}} = \frac{K_s \cdot t^2}{2}}$$

For a complete vehicle (front + rear):

$$K_\phi = K_{\phi f} + K_{\phi r} = \frac{K_{sf} \cdot t_f^2}{2} + \frac{K_{sr} \cdot t_r^2}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anti-roll bars (sway bars) add to the effective $K_s$. A sway bar of rate $K_{arb}$ adds an additional $K_{arb}$ to each side's wheel rate for asymmetric vertical motion only (i.e., during roll), so its contribution to $K_\phi$ is $K_{arb} \cdot t^2 / 2$ per axle.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Vehicle Class | CG Height $h$ (m) | Track $t$ (m) | SSF | Typical $a_{y,\text{roll}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sports Car | 0.45 | 1.55 | **1.72** | 1.72g |
| Sedan | 0.55 | 1.50 | **1.36** | 1.36g |
| Compact SUV | 0.65 | 1.55 | **1.19** | 1.19g |
| Full-size SUV | 0.80 | 1.65 | **1.03** | 1.03g |
| Heavy Truck | 1.20 | 2.00 | **0.83** | 0.83g |
| Tall Van | 1.00 | 1.70 | **0.85** | 0.85g |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Body Roll Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph extA[External Inputs]
        v["Vehicle Speed v"]
        R["Curve Radius R"]
        m["Vehicle Mass m"]
        h["CG Height h"]
        t["Track Width t"]
        Kphi["Roll Stiffness K_phi"]
    end

    subgraph extB[Computation Block]
        ay["Lateral Acceleration a_y = v^2 / R"]
        Fc["Centrifugal Force F_c = m * a_y"]
        Mov["Overturning Moment M_ov = F_c * h"]
        phi["Body Roll Angle phi = M_ov / K_phi"]
        dW["Load Transfer dW = m * a_y * h / t"]
        SSF["Static Stability Factor SSF = t / 2h"]
        ar["Rollover Threshold a_roll = g * SSF"]
    end

    subgraph extC[Vehicle Output States]
        body["Body Tilt State"]
        outLoad["Outside Wheel Load: mg/2 + dW"]
        inLoad["Inside Wheel Load: mg/2 - dW"]
        safety["Rollover Risk: a_y vs a_roll"]
    end

    v --> ay
    R --> ay
    m --> Fc
    ay --> Fc
    h --> Mov
    Fc --> Mov
    Mov --> phi
    Kphi --> phi
    Mov --> dW
    t --> dW
    h --> SSF
    t --> SSF
    SSF --> ar

    phi --> body
    dW --> outLoad
    dW --> inLoad
    ay --> safety
    ar --> safety
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Roll Axis Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph frontAxle[Front Axle View]
        f1["Front Left Contact Patch"]
        f2["Front Right Contact Patch"]
        frc["Front Roll Center (Higher)"]
        faxis["Implied Roll Axis Line"]
    end

    subgraph rearAxle[Rear Axle View]
        r1["Rear Left Contact Patch"]
        r2["Rear Right Contact Patch"]
        rrc["Rear Roll Center (Lower)"]
        raxis["Implied Roll Axis Line"]
    end

    subgraph vehicleRoll[Vehicle Body Behavior]
        rollAxis["INCLINED ROLL AXIS"]
        rollDir["Body Rolls Outward"]
        jacking["Jacking Force Component"]
    end

    frc --- faxis
    rrc --- raxis
    faxis --- rollAxis
    raxis --- rollAxis
    rollAxis --> rollDir
    rollAxis --> jacking
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows that the **inclined roll axis** is the geometric consequence of differing front and rear roll center heights. This inclination is a primary source of **jacking force** (vertical force on body during cornering), which is an undesired secondary effect in suspension design.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Vehicle Enters Curve"]
    measure["Measure a_y from IMU"]
    compare1{"a_y less than a_roll?"}
    region1["STABLE ZONE: Body rolls, all wheels grounded"]
    region2["ROLLOVER ZONE: Inside wheels lift off"]
    handling{"a_y greater than tire grip limit?"}
    slide["Tire slide-off (Loss of Control)"]
    stop["Apply ESC Braking Intervention"]

    start --> measure --> compare1
    compare1 -- YES --> region1
    compare1 -- NO --> region2
    region1 --> handling
    handling -- YES --> slide --> stop
    handling -- NO --> region1
```
