# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define roll axis. Explain why it is generally inclined to the horizontal.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The **roll axis** is an imaginary line joining the front and rear roll centers of a vehicle, about which the body rotates under the action of lateral forces.

It is generally inclined because the front and rear roll centers lie at different heights above the road due to differences in suspension geometry (e.g., MacPherson strut front vs. multi-link rear). The inclination produces a **jacking component** during body roll and influences the wheel rate characteristics of the suspension. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. State the significance of the Static Stability Factor (SSF).
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** is the ratio of track width to twice the CG height: $\text{SSF} = t/(2h)$. It is a **geometric** metric that quantifies a vehicle's theoretical resistance to rollover; the lateral acceleration at rollover is $a_{y,\text{roll}} = \text{SSF} \cdot g$. A higher SSF implies a more rollover-resistant vehicle. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**A passenger car has the following data:**
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Track width $t = 1.5$ m
- CG height above ground $h = 0.55$ m
- Distance of CG behind front axle $a = 1.2$ m, wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m
- Total roll stiffness $K_\phi = 60,000$ N·m/rad
- Vehicle is cornering on a curve of radius $R = 80$ m at speed $v = 20$ m/s

#### (a) Calculate the steady-state body roll angle $\phi$ and the total lateral load transfer $\Delta W$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Lateral acceleration.**

$$a_y = \frac{v^2}{R} = \frac{20^2}{80} = \frac{400}{80} = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2: Steady-state body roll angle.**

$$\phi = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{K_\phi} = \frac{1500 \times 5 \times 0.55}{60{,}000}$$

$$\phi = \frac{4125}{60{,}000} = 0.06875 \text{ rad} \approx 3.94^\circ$$

**Step 3: Total lateral load transfer.**

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t} = \frac{1500 \times 5 \times 0.55}{1.5}$$

$$\Delta W = \frac{4125}{1.5} = 2750 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 3.94^\circ, \quad \Delta W = 2750 \text{ N (per side of vehicle)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuation key gives **3 marks** for $\phi$ calculation, **2 marks** for unit conversion to degrees, and **2 marks** for load transfer.

#### (b) Determine the front and rear load transfers, given that the roll stiffness distribution is 60% front, 40% rear. Also compute the Static Stability Factor and comment on rollover safety. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Split roll stiffness.**

$$K_{\phi f} = 0.60 \times 60{,}000 = 36{,}000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$
$$K_{\phi r} = 0.40 \times 60{,}000 = 24{,}000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2: Front load transfer (due to roll couple):**

$$\Delta W_{f,\text{roll}} = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t} \cdot \frac{K_{\phi f}}{K_\phi} = 2750 \times 0.60 = 1650 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Geometric transfer due to lateral force on CG (CG behind front):**

$$\Delta W_{f,\text{geom}} = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot b}{L} \quad \text{where } b = L - a = 2.6 - 1.2 = 1.4 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta W_{f,\text{geom}} = \frac{1500 \times 5 \times 1.4}{2.6} = \frac{10{,}500}{2.6} \approx 4038 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Net front load transfer:**

$$\Delta W_f = \Delta W_{f,\text{roll}} - \Delta W_{f,\text{geom}} = 1650 - 4038 = -2388 \text{ N}$$

Wait — the negative sign indicates the **inside front wheel gains load** while the outside loses load. Re-examining: when the roll-stiffness-based transfer is small relative to the geometric transfer, the front axle can *unload* the outside wheel, creating a condition known as **roll understeer** (or "tight" handling). For the standard KTU convention:

$$\Delta W_f \text{ (net)} = 1650 - 4038 = -2388 \text{ N} \Rightarrow \text{Inside front load increases by } 2388 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Rear load transfer:**

$$\Delta W_r = 2750 - (-2388) = 5138 \text{ N} \quad \text{(outside rear gains load, inside rear loses load)}$$

**Step 6: Static Stability Factor.**

$$\text{SSF} = \frac{t}{2h} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times 0.55} = 1.364$$

**Step 7: Rollover threshold.**

$$a_{y,\text{roll}} = 1.364 \times 9.81 = 13.38 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 1.36g$$

**Step 8: Comment on Safety.**
Since $a_y = 5 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.51g$ is well below the rollover threshold of $1.36g$, the vehicle is in the **stable region** with a healthy safety margin. Tire grip (typical sedan limit ~0.85g) will be exceeded first, so the practical limit is sliding, not rollover.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget the **geometric load transfer term** $\Delta W_{f,\text{geom}} = m a_y b / L$ when computing front/rear splits. The KTU answer key specifically awards **2 marks** for including this term. Skipping it costs full marks on part (b).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**A SUV has the following data:**
- Mass $m = 2200$ kg
- Track width $t = 1.6$ m
- CG height $h = 0.75$ m
- Front and rear roll stiffnesses: $K_{\phi f} = 45,000$ N·m/rad, $K_{\phi r} = 35,000$ N·m/rad
- Curve radius $R = 100$ m, speed $v = 22$ m/s

#### (a) Compute the total roll stiffness, the steady-state roll angle, and verify whether the vehicle is on the verge of rollover. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total roll stiffness.**

$$K_\phi = K_{\phi f} + K_{\phi r} = 45{,}000 + 35{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2: Lateral acceleration.**

$$a_y = \frac{v^2}{R} = \frac{22^2}{100} = \frac{484}{100} = 4.84 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Body roll angle.**

$$\phi = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{K_\phi} = \frac{2200 \times 4.84 \times 0.75}{80{,}000} = \frac{7986}{80{,}000} = 0.0998 \text{ rad} \approx 5.72^\circ$$

**Step 4: Rollover threshold.**

$$\text{SSF} = \frac{t}{2h} = \frac{1.6}{2 \times 0.75} = 1.067$$

$$a_{y,\text{roll}} = 1.067 \times 9.81 = 10.47 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 1.07g$$

**Step 5: Verdict.**

Since $a_y = 4.84 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.49g < a_{y,\text{roll}} = 1.07g$, the vehicle is **NOT on the verge of rollover**. The rollover safety ratio is $1.07/0.49 \approx 2.18$, indicating a comfortable margin. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes confuse $a_y$ (in m/s²) with the g-unit value. Always state both forms. KTU key requires **unit clarity** for 1 mark.

#### (b) Find the load transfer on the outside wheels of front and rear axles, and comment on whether the vehicle tends towards understeer or oversteer. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total load transfer.**

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a_y \cdot h}{t} = \frac{2200 \times 4.84 \times 0.75}{1.6} = \frac{7986}{1.6} = 4991 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Distribution by roll stiffness.**

$$\Delta W_f = \Delta W \cdot \frac{K_{\phi f}}{K_\phi} = 4991 \times \frac{45{,}000}{80{,}000} = 4991 \times 0.5625 = 2807 \text{ N}$$

$$\Delta W_r = \Delta W \cdot \frac{K_{\phi r}}{K_\phi} = 4991 \times \frac{35{,}000}{80{,}000} = 4991 \times 0.4375 = 2184 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Outside wheel normal forces.**

Outside front (per wheel): $W_{of} = \frac{mg}{4} + \frac{\Delta W_f}{2}$ (assuming symmetric 4-wheel loading)

$$W_{of} = \frac{2200 \times 9.81}{4} + \frac{2807}{2} = 5395.5 + 1403.5 = 6799 \text{ N}$$

Outside rear (per wheel):

$$W_{or} = \frac{mg}{4} + \frac{\Delta W_r}{2} = 5395.5 + 1092 = 6487.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Handling comment.**
Since **more load is transferred to the front** ($2807 \text{ N}$ vs $2184 \text{ N}$), the front tires saturate first, producing **understeer tendency** in this cornering regime. If the roll stiffness distribution were reversed (more rear), the vehicle would exhibit oversteer. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students mistakenly conclude "more front transfer = oversteer" (the opposite of truth). The KTU key states explicitly: **front-biased transfer → front tire saturation → understeer**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Roll axis** joins the front and rear roll centers; it is generally **inclined** because the two roll centers lie at different heights.
- [x] **Body roll angle** formula: $\phi = m \cdot a_y \cdot h / K_\phi$. This is the single most important equation in this module.
- [x] **Lateral load transfer** on a vehicle: $\Delta W = m \cdot a_y \cdot h / t$. Per-axle values require an additional **factor of ½** for total, or use the **roll stiffness ratio** for front/rear split.
- [x] **Rollover threshold**: $a_{y,\text{roll}} = g \cdot t / (2h)$. This is a **geometric** limit; tire grip is always lost first in real road conditions.
- [x] **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** = $t / (2h)$ is a **dimensionless** rollover metric; higher is better.
- [x] **Inclined roll axis** causes a **jacking force** during cornering — an undesirable vertical force on the body.
- [x] **Roll stiffness** $K_\phi$ can be increased by stiffer springs, anti-roll bars, or wider track — all reduce $\phi$.
- [x] **Geometric load transfer** ($m a_y b/L$ or $m a_y a/L$) MUST be added to (or subtracted from) the roll-based transfer when computing individual axle loads. Forgetting this is a **top reason for losing marks**.
- [x] **Understeer/Oversteer** depends on **front vs. rear** load transfer: more front → understeer; more rear → oversteer.
- [x] **Anti-roll bar contribution** to roll stiffness: $K_{\phi,\text{bar}} = K_{\text{bar}} \cdot t^2 / 2$ per axle, where $K_{\text{bar}}$ is the effective wheel rate from the bar.
- [x] For KTU problems, **always state units explicitly**: $\phi$ in **rad or degrees**, $\Delta W$ in **N**, $a_y$ in **m/s²** *and* in **g**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Dynamics and Suspension Design for Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Dynamics and Suspension Design for Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicle Dynamics — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Dynamics** is the engineering science that studies the forces, moments, and energy transfers acting on a ground vehicle, and the resulting translational (heave, sway, surge) and rotational (roll, pitch, yaw) motions of the vehicle body relative to a fixed inertial frame.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, vehicle dynamics is studied as a **multi-body mechanical system** consisting of:
- **Sprung mass** (m_s): The vehicle body supported by the suspension.
- **Unsprung mass** (m_u): Wheels, tyres, brake discs, and half-shaft portions.
- **Suspension links**: Springs, dampers, anti-roll bars, control arms.

The discipline is built on the **fundamental equation of motion**:

$$ m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t) $$

where m is mass, c is damping coefficient, k is stiffness, and F(t) is the excitation force from the road.

## 1.2 Suspension Design for Stability — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Suspension Design for Stability** refers to the systematic selection of spring stiffness (K), damping coefficient (c), roll centre height, anti-roll bar stiffness (K_arb), and geometric parameters (track width T, wheelbase L, centre of gravity height h_CG) such that the vehicle resists rollover, maintains tyre-road contact under dynamic cornering/braking, and provides passenger comfort simultaneously.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Drinking Tray" Model

Imagine you are carrying a tray full of water glasses across a bumpy hall. Your **arms act as springs** (absorbing road bumps), your **wrist flexibility acts as a damper** (dissipating oscillation energy), and the **way you hold the tray wide apart** increases stability — much like a wide track width (T) in a car.

- A **stiff arm (high K)** = sporty but jarring ride.
- A **floppy arm (low K)** = smooth ride but unstable cornering.
- **Track width T** = the distance between your hands. Wider T = harder to tip.
- **Centre of gravity height h_CG** = how high the glasses sit. Lower h_CG = more stable.

This is precisely the trade-off suspension engineers face: **Ride ↔ Handling ↔ Stability**.

## 1.4 Key Stability Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** metrics are mandatory to memorize for KTU valuation:
> - **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** = T / (2h_CG) — a dimensionless rollover threshold predictor.
> - **Roll Gradient (°/g)** — body roll angle per unit lateral acceleration.
> - **Roll Centre (RC)** — the instantaneous centre about which the sprung mass rolls.
> - **Wheel Rate (K_w)** — vertical force at the wheel per unit vertical wheel displacement.
> - **Ride Rate (K_r)** — vertical force at the body per unit body displacement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Suspension Geometry — The Two Fundamental Configurations

### A. Dependent Suspension (Solid Axle)
- **Definition**: A rigid beam connects both wheels on an axle; both wheels move together vertically.
- **Advantages**: Robust, simple, low cost, high payload capacity.
- **Disadvantages**: High unsprung mass, poor wheel articulation over uneven surfaces.
- **Use Case**: Trucks, heavy commercial vehicles, off-roaders.

### B. Independent Suspension
- **Definition**: Each wheel moves independently; no mechanical link between left and right wheels.
- **Types Studied in KTU Syllabus**: MacPherson Strut, Double Wishbone, Multi-Link, Semi-Trailing Arm.
- **Advantages**: Lower unsprung mass, better wheel contact, superior ride and handling.
- **Disadvantages**: Higher complexity, cost, and packaging constraints.

## 2.2 Spring and Damper Characteristics — The Force-Velocity-Displacement Trinity

A passive suspension element exerts three orthogonal influences:

1. **Spring (Elastic Element)** — Stores and releases energy. Force is proportional to displacement (Hooke's Law): F = K·x
2. **Damper (Viscous Element)** — Dissipates energy as heat. Force is proportional to velocity: F = c·ẋ
3. **Tyre (Elastic Foundation)** — Stores energy at the road interface; modelled as a stiffness K_t with high damping.

## 2.3 Roll Dynamics — The Physics of Cornering

When a vehicle corners with lateral acceleration **a_y**, an inertia force **m_s · a_y** acts at the CG, producing a **roll moment** M_roll about the roll axis:

$$ M_{roll} = m_s \cdot a_y \cdot h_{CG,roll} $$

This moment is resisted by:
- **Spring roll rate** (K_φ,spring) — contribution from the suspension springs.
- **Anti-roll bar roll rate** (K_φ,ARB) — contribution from torsion bars.

The **total roll stiffness**:

$$ K_\phi = K_{\phi,spring} + K_{\phi,ARB} $$

The **roll gradient** (a key KTU design metric):

$$ \frac{\phi}{a_y} = \frac{m_s \cdot h_{CG,roll}}{K_\phi} \quad \text{[rad·s²/m]} $$

## 2.4 Rollover Threshold — The Stability Limit

> [!WARNING]
> A vehicle **rolls over** when the lateral acceleration creates a rollover moment that exceeds the restoring moment from the tyres. This is a classic **Part B question** topic.

The **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** is the simplest predictor:

$$ SSF = \frac{T}{2 \cdot h_{CG}} $$

The vehicle begins to lift its inner wheels when:

$$ a_{y,rollover} = g \cdot SSF = \frac{g \cdot T}{2 h_{CG}} $$

## 2.5 Anti-Roll Bar Mechanics

An anti-roll bar (ARB) is a **torsion spring** that connects the left and right wheels of an axle via lever arms. When both wheels deflect equally (bump), the bar twists symmetrically — producing **zero net roll resistance**. When wheels deflect differentially (roll), the bar twists asymmetrically — producing **roll resistance only**.

The roll stiffness contribution of an ARB:

$$ K_{\phi,ARB} = \frac{K_{ARB,torsion} \cdot a^2}{2} \cdot \cos^2\theta $$

where **a** is the lever arm length and θ is the installation angle.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **SI Unit** |
|------------|--------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| f_n | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$ | Hz |
| ζ | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{Km}}$ | — |
| K_w | Wheel rate | $K_w = K_s \cdot i^2$ (i = motion ratio) | N/m |
| K_r | Ride rate | $K_r = \dfrac{K_w \cdot K_t}{K_w + K_t}$ | N/m |
| SSF | Static stability factor | $SSF = \dfrac{T}{2 h_{CG}}$ | — |
| a_y,roll | Rollover acceleration | $a_{y,roll} = g \cdot SSF$ | m/s² |
| φ/a_y | Roll gradient | $\dfrac{\phi}{a_y} = \dfrac{m_s h_{CG}}{K_\phi}$ | rad/(m/s²) |
| K_φ | Total roll stiffness | $K_\phi = K_{\phi,spring} + K_{\phi,ARB}$ | N·m/rad |
| ω_d | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion**: When converting roll gradient units, **1 rad ≈ 57.30°**. Examiners frequently test whether students correctly express φ in degrees vs radians.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 Cars**: Use ultra-stiff springs (K ≈ 200,000 N/m) with sophisticated dampers; h_CG is minimized by mounting components low.
- **SUVs (e.g., Mahindra Scorpio)**: Have h_CG ≈ 0.75 m, T ≈ 1.57 m → SSF ≈ 1.05 → rollover threshold ≈ 0.52 g (regulated).
- **Passenger Sedans**: Target roll gradient of 4–6 °/g; SUVs target 5–7 °/g.
- **Commercial Trucks**: Use dependent (I-beam) suspension with helper air springs for load compensation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Wheel Rate from Spring Rate (Motion Ratio Effect)

The **motion ratio (i)** is defined as the ratio of suspension spring deflection to wheel deflection:

$$ i = \frac{\delta_{spring}}{\delta_{wheel}} $$

The force balance at the wheel (assuming quasi-static vertical load):

$$ F_{wheel} = F_{spring} \cdot i $$

Since $F_{spring} = K_s \cdot \delta_{spring}$, we substitute:

$$ F_{wheel} = K_s \cdot \delta_{spring} \cdot i $$

Now use $\delta_{spring} = i \cdot \delta_{wheel}$:

$$ F_{wheel} = K_s \cdot (i \cdot \delta_{wheel}) \cdot i = K_s \cdot i^2 \cdot \delta_{wheel} $$

Therefore, the **wheel rate** is:

$$ K_w = K_s \cdot i^2 $$

**Interpretation**: For a MacPherson strut with i ≈ 0.8, only **64%** of the spring stiffness is transmitted to the wheel — the rest is consumed by the suspension geometry.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Ride Rate Considering Tyre Compliance

When the tyre is modelled as a stiffness K_t in **series** with the suspension spring (K_w at the wheel), the combined vertical stiffness (ride rate) is:

$$ \frac{1}{K_r} = \frac{1}{K_w} + \frac{1}{K_t} $$

Solving for K_r:

$$ K_r = \frac{K_w \cdot K_t}{K_w + K_t} $$

**Numerical Example**: If K_w = 20,000 N/m and K_t = 200,000 N/m:

$$ K_r = \frac{20{,}000 \times 200{,}000}{20{,}000 + 200{,}000} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{220{,}000} = 18{,}181.8 \text{ N/m} $$

**Observation**: The ride rate is **dominated by the smaller stiffness** (the suspension spring). This is why soft suspensions give soft rides even with stiff tyres.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Body Natural Frequency (Quarter-Car Model)

The **quarter-car model** represents one corner of the vehicle as a 2-DOF system:

- Sprung mass m_s (body)
- Unsprung mass m_u (wheel)
- Suspension spring K_s and damper c_s between them
- Tyre spring K_t between m_u and ground

The **equation of motion for the sprung mass** (for free vibration, no road input):

$$ m_s \ddot{x}_s + c_s (\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + K_s (x_s - x_u) = 0 $$

Assuming **stationary wheel** (m_u negligible for body frequency estimation), the body natural frequency is:

$$ f_{n,body} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K_s}{m_s}} $$

For **passenger cars**, target ride frequency: **1.0–1.5 Hz** (human comfort band).
For **sports cars**: **1.5–2.0 Hz** (firmer, more responsive).
For **trucks**: **1.5–2.5 Hz** (with seats as secondary suspension).

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Rollover Threshold from Moment Equilibrium

Consider a vehicle cornering with lateral acceleration **a_y**. Take moments about the **outer wheel contact patch** (the tipping pivot):

**Overturning moment** (inertia force × height):

$$ M_{OT} = m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{CG} $$

**Restoring moment** (gravity × half track):

$$ M_{R} = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{T}{2} $$

At the **rollover threshold**, M_OT = M_R:

$$ m \cdot a_y \cdot h_{CG} = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{T}{2} $$

Solving for the rollover acceleration:

$$ a_{y,roll} = \frac{g \cdot T}{2 h_{CG}} = g \cdot SSF $$

**Worked Example**: A sedan with T = 1.55 m, h_CG = 0.55 m:

$$ SSF = \frac{1.55}{2 \times 0.55} = 1.409 $$

$$ a_{y,roll} = 9.81 \times 1.409 = 13.82 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 1.41\text{ g} $$

This means the sedan can theoretically withstand **1.41 g** lateral acceleration before tipping — well above typical handling limits of 0.8–0.9 g.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Damping Ratio from Suspension Parameters

For a single-DOF mass-spring-damper representing the quarter-car:

$$ \zeta = \frac{c}{2 \sqrt{K \cdot m}} $$

Using **critical damping** c_c = 2√(Km) as reference, **optimal damping ratio for ride** is **ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4** (underdamped but well-controlled). At **ζ = 1**, the system is critically damped — too harsh for ride comfort.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Design Parameter** | **Ride Effect** | **Handling Effect** | **Stability Effect** |
|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| ↑ Spring stiffness K_s | Harsher, higher f_n | Sharper response | Better roll control |
| ↑ Damper coefficient c | Less oscillation | Better transient | Variable |
| ↑ Anti-roll bar K_arb | Minimal change | Sharper cornering | ↑ Roll stiffness |
| ↑ Track width T | None | Better grip | ↑ Rollover threshold |
| ↓ CG height h_CG | None | Lower polar moment | ↑ Stability dramatically |
| ↑ Motion ratio i | Softer at wheel | Reduced wheel rate | Less precise control |
| ↑ Tyre stiffness K_t | Slightly firmer ride | Better road feedback | Better transient response |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Quarter-Car Model — Block Functional Architecture

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```
+----------------+       +-----------------+       +----------------+
|   ROAD INPUT   |       |  UNSPRUNG MASS  |       |  SPRUNG MASS   |
|   (z_r - road  |-------|   m_u - wheel/  |-------|  m_s - vehicle |
|   profile)     | K_t   |   tyre assembly | c_s,   |    body        |
+----------------+       +-----------------+  K_s   +----------------+
        |                        |                      |
        v                        v                      v
   Force from              Vertical motion         Heave acceleration
   tyre deflection         of wheel                of body
                                                       |
                                                       v
                                              +-----------------+
                                              | OUTPUT METRICS  |
                                              | - Body accel    |
                                              | - Roll angle    |
                                              | - Pitch angle   |
                                              | - Ride comfort  |
                                              +-----------------+
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Stability Force/Moment Architecture

```
                 LATERAL FORCE INPUT (a_y from cornering)
                                |
                                v
                  +-----------------------------+
                  |  CENTRIPETAL FORCE F_y =    |
                  |    m * a_y (at CG)          |
                  +-----------------------------+
                                |
                +---------------+----------------+
                |                                |
                v                                v
     +------------------+            +-------------------+
     |  ROLLOVER        |            |  ROLL RESPONSE    |
     |  MOMENT M_OT     |            |  M_roll resisted  |
     |  = m*a_y*h_CG    |            |  by K_phi_total   |
     +------------------+            +-------------------+
              |                                |
              v                                v
     SSF = T/(2*h_CG)             phi/a_y = m_s*h_CG/K_phi
     (rollover threshold)         (roll gradient)
              |                                |
              +----------------+---------------+
                               |
                               v
                  +----------------------------+
                  |  STABILITY OUTCOME:        |
                  |  Vehicle either maintains  |
                  |  tyre contact (stable) or  |
                  |  lifts inner wheels (tip)  |
                  +----------------------------+
```

## 4.4 Anti-Roll Bar Working Principle

```
        LEFT WHEEL                    RIGHT WHEEL
        (bump position)               (normal position)
            |                              |
            v                              v
        +-------+                      +-------+
        |       |                      |       |
        |  WL   |======ARB BAR========|  WR   |
        |       |  (lever arm a)      |       |
        +-------+                      +-------+
              \\                        //
               \\                      //
                \\____________________//
                          |
                          v
                  +---------------+
                  |  TWIST MOMENT |
                  |  T_ARB        |
                  |  = K_arb * delta |
                  +---------------+

          DELTA = (z_L - z_R) -- DIFFERENTIAL DEFLECTION
```

**Operating Principle**:
- **Symmetric bump** (both wheels move up equally) → No net ARB twist → ARB contributes zero roll stiffness.
- **Differential motion** (roll, one wheel up, other down) → ARB twists → Resists roll.

## 4.5 Suspension Kinematic Schematic (Double Wishbone)

```
                 UPPER CONTROL ARM
                  /          \
                 /            \
                /              \
        [UPPER BALL JOINT]  [UPPER BALL JOINT]
                |                |
                |                |
                |   VEHICLE      |
                |    BODY        |
                |   (sprung m)   |
                |                |
        [LOWER BALL JOINT]  [LOWER BALL JOINT]
                \                /
                 \              /
                  \            /
                 LOWER CONTROL ARM
                       |
                  SPRING/DAMPER
                    (coaxial)
                       |
                    WHEEL HUB
                       |
                     TYRE
                       |
                    GROUND
```

**Key Points for KTU Exam**:
- Upper and lower wishbones locate the wheel in 3D space.
- The instantaneous centre of rotation defines the **roll centre height**.
- Spring/damper may be coaxial (simplest) or inclined.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Static Stability Factor (SSF). State its significance in vehicle rollover prediction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Definition (1 Mark)**: Static Stability Factor (SSF) is defined as the ratio of half the vehicle track width (T/2) to the height of the centre of gravity (h_CG) above the road:

$$ SSF = \frac{T}{2 h_{CG}} $$

- **Significance (2 Marks)**:
  1. SSF predicts the **rollover threshold** — the lateral acceleration at which the inner wheels begin to lift.
  2. The rollover acceleration is given by $a_{y,roll} = g \cdot SSF$.
  3. Vehicles with SSF > 1.4 are considered stable; SSF < 1.2 indicates rollover risk under typical cornering.
  4. SUVs and trucks have lower SSF than passenger cars, making them more prone to rollover.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between wheel rate and spring rate. How is the motion ratio used to relate them?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Wheel Rate (K_w)** (1 Mark): The vertical force per unit vertical displacement of the **wheel centre** relative to the road. Units: N/m.
- **Spring Rate (K_s)** (1 Mark): The vertical force per unit compression of the **suspension spring** itself. Units: N/m.
- **Relation via Motion Ratio** (1 Mark): The motion ratio **i = δ_spring / δ_wheel**. Therefore:

$$ K_w = K_s \cdot i^2 $$

A motion ratio of 1.0 means the spring moves the same distance as the wheel; a motion ratio of 0.8 means the spring moves only 80% of the wheel displacement, making the wheel rate 64% of the spring rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A passenger car has the following parameters:
- Sprung mass m_s = 1200 kg
- Wheelbase L = 2.6 m
- Track width T = 1.55 m
- Centre of gravity height h_CG = 0.55 m
- Distance from CG to front axle a = 1.2 m, to rear axle b = 1.4 m

Calculate:
- (i) The **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** and the **rollover lateral acceleration**.
- (ii) The **roll gradient** if the total roll stiffness K_φ = 80,000 N·m/rad.
- (iii) Comment on whether this vehicle satisfies typical regulatory stability norms.

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **function and working principle of an anti-roll bar**. How does it contribute to vehicle stability during cornering?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a)(i) SSF and Rollover Acceleration** (5 Marks):

Using the formula:

$$ SSF = \frac{T}{2 h_{CG}} = \frac{1.55}{2 \times 0.55} = \frac{1.55}{1.10} $$

$$ SSF = 1.409 $$

The rollover lateral acceleration:

$$ a_{y,roll} = g \cdot SSF = 9.81 \times 1.409 $$

$$ a_{y,roll} = 13.82 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 1.41 \text{ g} $$

**(a)(ii) Roll Gradient** (2 Marks):

Using:

$$ \frac{\phi}{a_y} = \frac{m_s \cdot h_{CG}}{K_\phi} = \frac{1200 \times 0.55}{80{,}000} $$

$$ \frac{\phi}{a_y} = \frac{660}{80{,}000} = 8.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad per m/s}^2 $$

Converting to **°/g** (multiply by 9.81 and 57.30):

$$ \frac{\phi}{a_y} = 8.25 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 9.81 = 4.64 \text{ °/g} $$

**(a)(iii) Regulatory Comment** (1 Mark):

The rollover threshold of **1.41 g** is well above the typical handling limit of 0.85 g for passenger cars. The roll gradient of **4.64 °/g** is within the comfort range of 3–5 °/g for sedans. Therefore, the vehicle **satisfies typical regulatory stability norms** (US FMVSS 126 requires SSF > 1.20 for light vehicles).

---

**(b) Anti-Roll Bar Function and Working** (6 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks)**: An anti-roll bar (ARB), also called a sway bar or stabilizer bar, is a **U-shaped or straight torsion bar** mounted transversely across the vehicle chassis, connected to the suspension links (control arms or struts) of both wheels on an axle via lever arms.

**Working Principle (2 Marks)**: 
- The bar acts as a **torsion spring** that resists **differential** vertical motion between the left and right wheels.
- During a **bump** (both wheels move up equally), the bar twists symmetrically → **zero net reaction**.
- During **roll** (one wheel moves up, the other down), the bar twists differentially → **develops a resisting moment** proportional to the relative displacement.
- The moment equation: $M_{ARB} = K_{ARB} \cdot (z_L - z_R)$, where K_ARB is the torsional stiffness of the bar.

**Contribution to Cornering Stability (2 Marks)**:
- During cornering, centrifugal force creates a **roll moment** that tends to lift the inner wheels and load the outer wheels.
- The ARB **redistributes the vertical load** from the outer to the inner wheel, ensuring both wheels maintain adequate grip.
- This improves **lateral stability**, reduces **body roll angle**, and allows **higher cornering speeds** before tyre saturation.
- ARBs do not affect **symmetric** ride motions (heave) — they are selectively stiff for roll only.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A vehicle quarter-car model has the following parameters:
- Sprung mass m_s = 320 kg
- Suspension spring stiffness K_s = 22,000 N/m
- Damper coefficient c = 1500 N·s/m
- Motion ratio i = 0.85

Calculate:
- (i) The **wheel rate** K_w.
- (ii) The **natural frequency** of the sprung mass.
- (iii) The **damping ratio** ζ and comment on the ride comfort.

**(b)** Explain the **quarter-car passive suspension model** with a neat diagram. Derive the equation of motion for the sprung mass and explain the significance of each term.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a)(i) Wheel Rate** (2 Marks):

$$ K_w = K_s \cdot i^2 = 22{,}000 \times (0.85)^2 $$

$$ K_w = 22{,}000 \times 0.7225 = 15{,}895 \text{ N/m} $$

**(a)(ii) Natural Frequency** (3 Marks):

$$ f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K_s}{m_s}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{22{,}000}{320}} $$

$$ f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{68.75} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 8.29 $$

$$ f_n = 1.32 \text{ Hz} $$

**Comment**: This is within the **passenger car comfort range (1.0–1.5 Hz)**.

**(a)(iii) Damping Ratio** (3 Marks):

First, compute the critical damping coefficient:

$$ c_c = 2\sqrt{K_s \cdot m_s} = 2\sqrt{22{,}000 \times 320} $$

$$ c_c = 2\sqrt{7{,}040{,}000} = 2 \times 2653.3 = 5306.6 \text{ N·s/m} $$

Now compute ζ:

$$ \zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{5306.6} = 0.283 $$

**Comment on Ride Comfort**: A damping ratio of **ζ ≈ 0.28** falls in the **optimal ride range (0.2–0.4)**. The vehicle is **underdamped**, allowing controlled oscillation that decays quickly. This provides a **good balance between ride comfort and road feel**.

---

**(b) Quarter-Car Model Explanation** (6 Marks):

**Diagram (2 Marks)**:

```
     Sprung Mass m_s
     ______________
     |            |
     |     z_s    |
     |____________|      Suspension: K_s, c_s
     |            |     |
     |     z_u    |=====+
     |____________|     |
        (O)             |  Tyre: K_t
        TYRE            |
       =======+==========+
              |
           ROAD
          (z_r input)
```

**Equation of Motion (2 Marks)**: For the sprung mass m_s subjected to suspension spring force K_s and damper force c_s, the equation of motion is:

$$ m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + K_s (z_s - z_u) = 0 $$

**Significance of Each Term (2 Marks)**:
- $m_s \ddot{z}_s$: Inertia force of the body resisting acceleration.
- $c_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$: Damping force proportional to **relative velocity** between body and wheel.
- $K_s (z_s - z_u)$: Spring force proportional to **relative displacement** (suspension travel).
- The right-hand side is **zero** because road input z_r enters through the tyre force on m_u, not directly on m_s.

**Engineering Insight**: This 2-DOF model predicts two natural frequencies: **body bounce mode** (~1–2 Hz) and **wheel hop mode** (~10–15 Hz). Good suspension design isolates the body from both.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Suspension & Stability:**
> 
> 1. **Units Mismatch (–2 marks)**: Students frequently mix rad and degrees when computing roll gradient. Always convert: **1 rad = 57.30°**, and remember **1 g = 9.81 m/s²**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Square in Wheel Rate (–1 mark)**: The wheel rate formula is $K_w = K_s \cdot i^2$, NOT $K_w = K_s \cdot i$. Many students lose marks by omitting the square.
> 
> 3. **Wrong CG Height Usage (–2 marks)**: For rollover, use **h_CG (CG height from ground)**. For roll gradient, sometimes a separate **h_CG,roll (height above roll axis)** is given — do not confuse them.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the Damping Ratio Comment (–1 mark)**: Always provide a qualitative comment after numerical calculations. KTU examiners reward **interpretation**, not just numbers.
> 
> 5. **Missing the ARB Symmetry Insight (–2 marks)**: In the anti-roll bar question, students often forget to state that ARBs do **not** resist symmetric bump motions — this is a critical conceptual point worth 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Bullet Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- **Suspension Purpose**: Isolate the body from road disturbances (ride comfort) while maintaining tyre-road contact (handling/stability).
- **Two Masses**: Sprung mass (body) and unsprung mass (wheel/tyre assembly) — each has distinct dynamics.
- **Three Elements**: Spring (K_s), Damper (c_s), Anti-roll bar (K_ARB). Each serves a unique function.
- **Wheel Rate Formula**: $K_w = K_s \cdot i^2$ — always square the motion ratio.
- **Ride Rate Formula**: $K_r = \dfrac{K_w K_t}{K_w + K_t}$ — series combination rule.
- **Natural Frequency Target**: 1.0–1.5 Hz for cars, 1.5–2.5 Hz for trucks.
- **Damping Ratio Target**: ζ ≈ 0.2–0.4 for ride; ζ ≈ 0.5–0.7 for handling-biased vehicles.
- **Rollover Threshold**: $a_{y,roll} = g \cdot SSF = \dfrac{g \cdot T}{2 h_{CG}}$.
- **Roll Gradient**: $\dfrac{\phi}{a_y} = \dfrac{m_s h_{CG}}{K_\phi}$ — lower is better.
- **SSF Norms**: SSF > 1.4 = stable; SSF < 1.2 = rollover-prone.
- **Anti-Roll Bar**: Resists differential motion only; zero effect on symmetric bump.
- **Suspension Types**: Independent (MacPherson, Double Wishbone) for cars; Dependent (Solid Axle, Leaf Spring) for trucks.
- **Tyre Stiffness**: K_t ≈ 200,000–300,000 N/m (much higher than suspension K_s).
- **Stability Trade-off**: Comfort ↔ Handling ↔ Safety — design is always a compromise.
- **Formula Mastery**: Memorize SSF, K_w, K_r, f_n, ζ — these five formulas cover 80% of Part B questions.
- **Diagram Skill**: Always draw a **free-body diagram** with springs as coils, dampers as dashpots, and masses as rectangles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Analysis of Passive, Semi-Active and Active Suspension Using Quarter Car, Half Car and Full Car Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Analysis of Passive, Semi-Active and Active Suspension Using Quarter Car, Half Car and Full Car Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Suspension System - Formal Definition

An **Automotive Suspension System** is the mechanism that physically links a vehicle's body (sprung mass) to its wheels (unsprung mass). Its primary engineering functions are:

1. **Road Holding (Tire-to-Ground Contact):** Maintaining continuous tire contact with the road surface for steering control and braking efficiency.
2. **Ride Comfort (Isolation):** Attenuating vibrations and shock loads transmitted from road irregularities to the vehicle occupants.
3. **Load Carrying:** Supporting static and dynamic loads imposed by cargo and passengers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Suspension design is fundamentally a multi-objective optimization problem — the engineer must trade-off **ride comfort** against **road-holding capability** against **suspension working space (packaging)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Suspension Systems

| Type | Actuation | Energy Source | Key Feature |
|------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Passive** | None (fixed `ks`, `cs`) | None | Simple, cheap, no external power |
| **Semi-Active** | Variable damper only (or variable stiffness) | External electrical (sensor + controller) | Real-time adjustable damping, no external energy source for force generation |
| **Active** | Active force actuator (hydraulic, electromagnetic, pneumatic) | External (typically 1–10 kW) | Can both dissipate AND add energy; provides "sky-hook" capability |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy - Plain English Intuition

**Imagine a backpacker carrying a heavy backpack on a rocky mountain trail:**

- **Passive Suspension** is like the backpacker's feet just absorbing shocks through their ankles — the muscles react, but with fixed biomechanical properties. If the path is gentle, walking is easy. If the path is very rough, the backpacker gets thrown around — there's no way to change response in real-time.

- **Semi-Active Suspension** is like wearing shock-absorbing boots with adjustable valves — the backpacker can tighten or loosen the valves based on the trail ahead, but cannot push upward; only resist downward. Energy can only be dissipated, never added.

- **Active Suspension** is like a backpacker with small thrusters on the backpack that fire automatically — actively pushing UP when the trail dips DOWN, keeping the load nearly stationary. Energy is added to the system, providing the smoothest possible ride.

## 1.4 Vehicle Models - Hierarchy of Complexity

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) in suspension modeling:**
> - **Quarter-Car Model:** 2-DOF (bounce of sprung mass + bounce of unsprung mass)
> - **Half-Car Model:** 3 to 4-DOF (bounce, pitch, ± wheel hop) — captures longitudinal symmetry
> - **Full-Car Model:** 7-DOF (bounce, pitch, roll, 4× wheel hop) — captures all rigid-body modes

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Standard ISO 2631)

The three conflicting design objectives are quantified as:

- **Sprung Mass Acceleration (¨zs):** Direct measure of ride comfort (units: m/s², weighted RMS via ISO 2631)
- **Suspension Working Space / Deflection (zs − zu):** Limits packaging; must remain within `f_s,max` (typically 0.05–0.15 m)
- **Tire Deflection / Dynamic Load (zt − q):** Affects road holding; must stay within `f_t,max` (typically 0.02–0.05 m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quarter Car Model - Foundation

The **quarter-car (2-DOF) model** is the cornerstone of suspension analysis. It models ONE corner of the vehicle, with two lumped masses:

- **ms** = Sprung mass (1/4 of vehicle body mass)
- **mu** = Unsprung mass (wheel, tire, brake, suspension arm)
- **ks** = Suspension spring stiffness
- **cs** = Suspension damping coefficient
- **kt** = Tire vertical stiffness
- **q(t)** = Road input disturbance (elevation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Modeling Assumptions (KTU Convention):**
> 1. Vertical motion only (no longitudinal/lateral coupling)
> 2. Small-angle, linear regime
> 3. Tire modeled as linear spring (no damping in tire)
> 4. Road profile `q(t)` is the only exogenous input
> 5. Sprung mass CG is directly above the suspension strut

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Setup

**Step 1 — Identify the system as a 2-DOF spring-mass-damper network:**
The sprung mass `ms` is connected to the unsprung mass `mu` through a parallel combination of `ks` and `cs`. The unsprung mass `mu` is connected to the road `q(t)` through tire stiffness `kt`.

**Step 2 — Define the generalized coordinates:**
- `zs(t)` = vertical displacement of sprung mass (absolute)
- `zu(t)` = vertical displacement of unsprung mass (absolute)

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law** in the vertical direction for each mass.

**Step 4 — Recognize the three transfer-function outputs of interest:**
1. Sprung mass acceleration: `¨zs(s) / Q(s)` → ride comfort
2. Suspension deflection: `[Zs(s) − Zu(s)] / Q(s)` → suspension travel
3. Tire deflection: `[Zu(s) − Q(s)] / Q(s)` → road holding

## 2.3 Equations of Motion (Quarter Car)

For the **Sprung Mass** (free body: suspension spring + damper pull it toward `zu`):

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

For the **Unsprung Mass** (free body: tire spring pushes up, suspension pulls down):

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = k_s(z_s - z_u) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_t(z_u - q)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quarter Car)

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Transfer Function | Units |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------|-------|
| Natural frequency (sprung) | `ω_n` | $\sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}}$ | rad/s |
| Damping ratio | `ζ` | $\dfrac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$ | dimensionless |
| Critical damping | `c_cr` | $2\sqrt{k_s m_s}$ | N·s/m |
| Optimal damping ratio (passive) | `ζ_opt` | $\approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ | dimensionless |
| Sprung/Unsprung mass ratio | `r` | $\dfrac{m_s}{m_u}$ (typically 7–12) | dimensionless |
| Tire stiffness ratio | `ρ` | $\dfrac{k_t}{k_s}$ (typically 8–10) | dimensionless |
| Ride rate | — | $\dfrac{m_s g}{\delta_{static}}$ | N/m |
| Frequency ratio (tire to suspension) | `f` | $\dfrac{\omega_t}{\omega_s} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_t / m_u}{k_s / m_s}}$ | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Transfer Function Derivation (Sprung Mass Acceleration)

Taking the Laplace transform of the equations of motion (with zero initial conditions):

$$m_s s^2 Z_s = -k_s(Z_s - Z_u) - c_s s(Z_s - Z_u)$$

$$m_u s^2 Z_u = k_s(Z_s - Z_u) + c_s s(Z_s - Z_u) - k_t(Z_u - Q)$$

Rearranging into matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
(k_s + c_s s + m_s s^2) Z_s - (k_s + c_s s) Z_u &= 0 \\
-(k_s + c_s s) Z_s + (k_s + c_s s + k_t + m_u s^2) Z_u &= k_t Q
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for `Zs/Q` using Cramer's rule yields the **suspension travel transfer function**, and the **sprung mass acceleration transfer function** is obtained by multiplying by `s²`:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\ddot{Z}_s(s)}{Q(s)} = \dfrac{s^2 \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}}$$

where the characteristic polynomial `D(s)` is:

$$D(s) = m_s m_u s^4 + (m_s c_s + m_u c_s) s^3 + (m_s k_s + m_s k_t + m_u k_s) s^2 + c_s k_t s + k_s k_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule (KTU 2024 High-Yield):** For optimum passive ride, the damping ratio should be chosen so that the **low-frequency resonance** (≈ 1 Hz, body bounce) is **well-damped** and the **high-frequency wheel-hop resonance** (≈ 10–15 Hz) is **lightly damped**. This is the famous "skyhook" tradeoff.

## 2.6 Active vs. Semi-Active Force Actuator Models

In the **Active Suspension** case, an additional force `Fa(t)` is applied between `ms` and `mu`:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + F_a$$

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = k_s(z_s - z_u) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_t(z_u - q) - F_a$$

In the **Semi-Active** case, the damping coefficient `cs` becomes a **time-varying control variable**:

$$c_s(t) = c_{s,\min} \cdot (1 + u(t)), \quad u(t) \in [0, u_{\max}]$$

This is equivalent to modifying only the dissipative term — energy is dissipated, never added.

## 2.7 Half-Car and Full-Car Model State Vectors

| Model | DOF | State Vector `x` | Inputs `u` |
|-------|-----|------------------|------------|
| Quarter car | 2 | `[zs, ˙zs, zu, ˙zu]^T` | `[q]^T` |
| Half car | 4 | `[zb, ˙zb, θ, ˙θ, zf, ˙zf, zr, ˙zr]^T` | `[qf, qr]^T` |
| Full car | 7 | `[zb, ˙zb, θ, ˙θ, φ, ˙φ, z1, ˙z1, z2, ˙z2, z3, ˙z3, z4, ˙z4]^T` | `[q1, q2, q3, q4]^T` |

Where:
- `zb` = body bounce (vertical CG displacement)
- `θ` = body pitch angle
- `φ` = body roll angle
- `z1, z2, z3, z4` = vertical displacements at the four wheel stations (FL, FR, RL, RR)
- `qf, qr` = front and rear road inputs (half car)
- `q1, q2, q3, q4` = four corner road inputs (full car)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation — Quarter Car 2-DOF State-Space Model

### Step 3.1.1: Define the State Vector

Let the four state variables be the positions and velocities of the two masses:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} z_s \\ \dot{z}_s \\ z_u \\ \dot{z}_u \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3.1.2: Express Velocities (Trivial Definitions)

The first and third rows of `˙x` are simply the velocities:

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{z}_s = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_3 = \dot{z}_u = x_4$$

### Step 3.1.3: Solve the EOM for Accelerations

From the EOM for `ms`:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u) - c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

Solving for `¨zs`:

$$\ddot{z}_s = -\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}(z_s - z_u) - \dfrac{c_s}{m_s}(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\dfrac{k_s}{m_s} x_1 + \dfrac{k_s}{m_s} x_3 - \dfrac{c_s}{m_s} x_2 + \dfrac{c_s}{m_s} x_4$$

From the EOM for `mu`:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = k_s(z_s - z_u) + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - k_t(z_u - q)$$

Solving for `¨zu`:

$$\ddot{z}_u = \dfrac{k_s}{m_u}(z_s - z_u) + \dfrac{c_s}{m_u}(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - \dfrac{k_t}{m_u}(z_u - q)$$

$$\dot{x}_4 = \dfrac{k_s}{m_u} x_1 - \dfrac{k_s + k_t}{m_u} x_3 + \dfrac{c_s}{m_u} x_2 - \dfrac{c_s}{m_u} x_4 + \dfrac{k_t}{m_u} q$$

### Step 3.1.4: Assemble the State-Space Form `˙x = Ax + Bu`

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \\ \dot{x}_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -\dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & -\dfrac{c_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{c_s}{m_s} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \dfrac{k_s}{m_u} & \dfrac{c_s}{m_u} & -\dfrac{k_s + k_t}{m_u} & -\dfrac{c_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \dfrac{k_t}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} q$$

This is the **canonical KTU quarter-car state-space form** that students must reproduce from memory in the exam.

### Step 3.1.5: Output Equation (Ride Comfort Output)

For sprung mass acceleration output `y = ¨zs`:

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & -\dfrac{c_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{c_s}{m_s} \end{bmatrix} x + [0] \cdot q$$

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation — Half Car 4-DOF Model

### Step 3.2.1: Coordinate System & Generalized Coordinates

The half car treats the body as a **rigid bar** of mass `mb` with moment of inertia `Iy` about the lateral axis (pitch). Coordinates:

- `zb` = vertical displacement of body CG
- `θ` = pitch angle (positive: nose up)
- `z_f` = front unsprung mass displacement
- `z_r` = rear unsprung mass displacement

**Geometric Parameters:**
- `a` = distance from CG to front axle
- `b` = distance from CG to rear axle
- `L = a + b` = wheelbase
- `m_f` = front unsprung mass
- `m_r` = rear unsprung mass
- `k_sf`, `c_sf` = front suspension spring/damping
- `k_sr`, `c_sr` = rear suspension spring/damping
- `k_tf`, `k_tr` = front/rear tire stiffness
- `q_f`, `q_r` = front/rear road inputs

### Step 3.2.2: Vertical Displacement of Body at Front and Rear

The body displacement at the front and rear suspension pickup points is, by small-angle pitch:

$$z_{b,f} = z_b - a \theta, \quad z_{b,r} = z_b + b \theta$$

### Step 3.2.3: Equation of Motion — Bounce (Translation, ΣFz = 0)

Vertical force balance on the body:

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b = -k_{sf}(z_{b,f} - z_f) - c_{sf}(\dot{z}_{b,f} - \dot{z}_f) - k_{sr}(z_{b,r} - z_r) - c_{sr}(\dot{z}_{b,r} - \dot{z}_r)$$

Substituting `z_{b,f}` and `z_{b,r}`:

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b = -k_{sf}(z_b - a\theta - z_f) - c_{sf}(\dot{z}_b - a\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_f) - k_{sr}(z_b + b\theta - z_r) - c_{sr}(\dot{z}_b + b\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_r)$$

### Step 3.2.4: Equation of Motion — Pitch (Rotation, ΣM_CG = 0)

Taking moments about the CG (positive nose-up):

$$I_y \ddot{\theta} = a \left[ k_{sf}(z_b - a\theta - z_f) + c_{sf}(\dot{z}_b - a\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_f) \right] - b \left[ k_{sr}(z_b + b\theta - z_r) + c_{sr}(\dot{z}_b + b\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_r) \right]$$

### Step 3.2.5: Equation of Motion — Front Unsprung Mass

$$m_f \ddot{z}_f = k_{sf}(z_b - a\theta - z_f) + c_{sf}(\dot{z}_b - a\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_f) - k_{tf}(z_f - q_f)$$

### Step 3.2.6: Equation of Motion — Rear Unsprung Mass

$$m_r \ddot{z}_r = k_{sr}(z_b + b\theta - z_r) + c_{sr}(\dot{z}_b + b\dot{\theta} - \dot{z}_r) - k_{tr}(z_r - q_r)$$

### Step 3.2.7: State-Space Form (8-state, 2-input)

Let `x = [z_b, ˙z_b, θ, ˙θ, z_f, ˙z_f, z_r, ˙z_r]^T` and `u = [q_f, q_r]^T`. The compact matrix form is:

$$\dot{x} = A_{hc} x + B_{hc} u$$

with `A_hc` being an 8×8 matrix and `B_hc` being 8×2.

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation — Full Car 7-DOF Model

### Step 3.3.1: Generalized Coordinates

- `zb` = body bounce at CG
- `θ` = pitch (about y-axis)
- `φ` = roll (about x-axis)
- `z1, z2, z3, z4` = vertical displacement at FL, FR, RL, RR wheel stations

### Step 3.3.2: Body Displacement at Each Wheel Station

With small-angle assumption and track width `t`, wheelbase `L`:

- $z_{b,FL} = z_b - a\theta + \dfrac{t}{2}\phi$
- $z_{b,FR} = z_b - a\theta - \dfrac{t}{2}\phi$
- $z_{b,RL} = z_b + b\theta + \dfrac{t}{2}\phi$
- $z_{b,RR} = z_b + b\theta - \dfrac{t}{2}\phi$

### Step 3.3.3: Three Rigid-Body EOMs (Bounce, Pitch, Roll)

**Bounce (Vertical):**

$$m_b \ddot{z}_b = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} \left[ k_{si}(z_{b,i} - z_i) + c_{si}(\dot{z}_{b,i} - \dot{z}_i) \right]$$

**Pitch (Rotation about y-axis):**

$$I_y \ddot{\theta} = a\left[F_{sFL} + F_{sFR}\right] - b\left[F_{sRL} + F_{sRR}\right]$$

**Roll (Rotation about x-axis):**

$$I_x \ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{t}{2}\left[F_{sFL} - F_{sFR} + F_{sRL} - F_{sRR}\right]$$

where $F_{si} = k_{si}(z_{b,i} - z_i) + c_{si}(\dot{z}_{b,i} - \dot{z}_i)$ is the suspension force at corner `i`.

### Step 3.3.4: Four Unsprung Mass EOMs

For each corner `i ∈ {1,2,3,4}`:

$$m_{u,i} \ddot{z}_i = k_{si}(z_{b,i} - z_i) + c_{si}(\dot{z}_{b,i} - \dot{z}_i) - k_{ti}(z_i - q_i)$$

### Step 3.3.5: Final State-Space

The 7-DOF full car yields a **14-state, 4-input** system:

$$x = [z_b, \dot{z}_b, \theta, \dot{\theta}, \phi, \dot{\phi}, z_1, \dot{z}_1, z_2, \dot{z}_2, z_3, \dot{z}_3, z_4, \dot{z}_4]^T$$

$$\dot{x} = A_{fc} x + B_{fc} u, \quad u = [q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4]^T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The full-car model is essential for analyzing **brake dive**, **acceleration squat**, and **roll dynamics** — phenomena that cannot be captured in a quarter-car or half-car model.

## 3.4 Active Suspension Control — Sky-Hook Damping (Derivation)

The **sky-hook** controller places a virtual damper between the sprung mass and an imaginary "sky" (inertial reference). The control law is:

$$F_a = -c_{sky} \dot{z}_s$$

Substituting into the sprung-mass EOM with passive `cs = 0`:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + c_{sky} \dot{z}_s = -k_s(z_s - z_u)$$

This **critically decouples** the sprung mass from the road, giving the best possible ride comfort. However, full sky-hook requires external energy — it is **active only**.

For **semi-active** emulation of sky-hook, the control law is:

$$c_s(t) = \begin{cases} c_{\max} & \text{if } \dot{z}_s \cdot (z_s - z_u) > 0 \quad \text{(damper extends)} \\ c_{\min} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is the **"on-off" sky-hook** algorithm, achievable in real time with MR dampers.

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A quarter car has `ms = 250 kg`, `mu = 40 kg`, `ks = 20,000 N/m`, `cs = 1,000 N·s/m`, `kt = 200,000 N/m`. The car traverses a step road of height `0.05 m`. Find:
(a) Body natural frequency
(b) Wheel-hop natural frequency
(c) Damping ratio

**Solution:**

**(a) Body Bounce Frequency:**

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{20{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944 \text{ rad/s} \Rightarrow f_s \approx 1.42 \text{ Hz}$$

**(b) Wheel-Hop Frequency:**

$$\omega_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_t + k_s}{m_u}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{220{,}000}{40}} = \sqrt{5500} \approx 74.16 \text{ rad/s} \Rightarrow f_t \approx 11.8 \text{ Hz}$$

**(c) Damping Ratio:**

$$\zeta = \dfrac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}} = \dfrac{1000}{2\sqrt{20{,}000 \times 250}} = \dfrac{1000}{4472.1} \approx 0.224$$
