## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Architecture — Half-Car Simulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Parameters m_s, I_y, a, b, k_f, k_r, c_f, c_r] --> A1[Assemble M, C, K, Kin, Cin]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Minv = inv M]
    A2 --> A3[Build A 4x4 Matrix]
    A2 --> A4[Build B 4x4 Matrix]
    A5[Road Profile z_rf t, z_rr t] --> A6[Compute Derivatives z_rf_dot, z_rr_dot]
    A3 --> A7[Form sys ss A B C D]
    A4 --> A7
    A6 --> A8[Input Vector U t]
    A7 --> A9[lsim sys U t]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Time Domain Outputs z_s, theta]
    A9 --> A11[Acceleration Outputs z_s_ddot, theta_ddot]
    A10 --> A12[Plot Heave and Pitch]
    A10 --> A13[RMS Comfort Metric]
    A7 --> A14[bode sys]
    A14 --> A15[Frequency Response Magnitude dB]
    A11 --> A16[Compare with ISO 2631 0.315 m per s squared]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Physical Half-Car Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF[Road Profile z_rf] --> TF[Front Tyre Vertical Force]
    TR[Rear Road z_rr] --> TRnode[Rear Tyre Vertical Force]
    TF --> SF[Front Suspension k_f, c_f]
    SF --> BODY[Car Body Sprung Mass m_s, I_y Heave z_s and Pitch theta]
    TRnode --> SR[Rear Suspension k_r, c_r]
    SR --> BODY
    BODY --> OUT1[Output 1 CG Acceleration z_s_ddot]
    BODY --> OUT2[Output 2 Pitch Angle theta]
    BODY --> OUT3[Output 3 Front Body Height z_s - a theta]
    BODY --> OUT4[Output 4 Rear Body Height z_s plus b theta]
```

### 4.3 Coupling Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Coupled_2DOF
        Q1[Heave DOF z_s] --Coupling Term a k_f minus b k_r --> Q2[Pitch DOF theta]
        Q2 --Same Coupling Term Back--> Q1
        Q1 --Inertia m_s--> Q1
        Q2 --Inertia I_y--> Q2
    end
    UF[Road Front z_rf] --> Q1
    UR[Road Rear z_rr] --> Q2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double-headed arrow in the central block diagram captures the **cross-coupling term** $a k_f - b k_r$ that appears in **both** rows of $K$ and $C$. If $a k_f = b k_r$ the system **decouples** — this is the *anti-pitch* condition exploited in luxury suspensions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a half-car model. Why is it called "half"?*

> **Model Answer:**
> A half-car model is a **2-DOF lumped-parameter representation** of a vehicle in the **sagittal plane**, in which the sprung mass is permitted to translate vertically (heave $z_s$) and rotate about a transverse axis (pitch $\theta$) under the action of front and rear suspension forces. It is called "half" because it exploits the lateral symmetry of a vehicle on a smooth road, modelling only the **left-right averaged** behaviour and thereby analysing one longitudinal half-plane rather than the full 3-D vehicle. **[3 marks]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the two coupled equations of motion of the half-car bounce-pitch model in matrix form and identify each matrix.*

> **Model Answer:**
> The compact form is $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + Kq = K_{in}z_r + C_{in}\dot{z}_r$, where $q = [z_s, \theta]^T$. **$M$** is the diagonal mass matrix $\text{diag}(m_s, I_y)$; **$C$** is the symmetric coupled damping matrix; **$K$** is the symmetric coupled stiffness matrix; **$K_{in}, C_{in}$** map the road inputs $z_{rf}, z_{rr}$ and their derivatives to the equivalent forces/moments at the CG. **[3 marks]**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the coupled equations of motion for a 2-DOF half-car bounce-pitch model. Clearly state assumptions. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** For the parameters $m_s = 1400$ kg, $I_y = 2200$ kg·m², $a = 1.25$ m, $b = 1.45$ m, $k_f = 30\,000$ N/m, $k_r = 32\,000$ N/m, $c_f = c_r = 1400$ N·s/m, formulate the **state-space matrices A and B** and write the **MATLAB code** to obtain the heave and pitch response to a unit-step road input at the front wheel. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
> Assumptions: small pitch angles (linearised); rigid body; symmetric road inputs at left/right wheels; road disturbance is the only forcing; sprung mass only (unsprung mass neglected).
>
> Applying Newton's second law for vertical translation and Euler's rotational equation about the CG, with $z_{sf} = z_s - a\theta$ and $z_{sr} = z_s + b\theta$:
>
> $$m_s \ddot{z}_s + (c_f+c_r)\dot{z}_s + (k_f+k_r)z_s + (a c_f - b c_r)\dot{\theta} + (a k_f - b k_r)\theta = c_f \dot{z}_{rf} + c_r \dot{z}_{rr} + k_f z_{rf} + k_r z_{rr}$$
>
> $$I_y \ddot{\theta} + (a c_f - b c_r)\dot{z}_s + (a k_f - b k_r)z_s + (a^2 c_f + b^2 c_r)\dot{\theta} + (a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)\theta = a c_f \dot{z}_{rf} - b c_r \dot{z}_{rr} + a k_f z_{rf} - b k_r z_{rr}$$
>
> Combined into $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + Kq = K_{in}z_r + C_{in}\dot{z}_r$ with the matrices defined in §2.3. **[7 marks — full credit]**

> **Model Answer (b):**
> Numerical matrices:
>
> $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1400 & 0 \\ 0 & 2200 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad C = \begin{bmatrix} 2800 & -280 \\ -280 & 4313 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad K = \begin{bmatrix} 62000 & -900 \\ -900 & 78288 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> (Students should compute off-diagonals as $a c_f - b c_r = 1.25(1400) - 1.45(1400) = -280$, etc.)
>
> The state-space $A$ matrix (using derivation §3.3):
>
> $$
> A = \begin{bmatrix}
> 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
> -44.286 & -2.0 & 0.643 & 0.2 \\
> 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
> 0.409 & 0.127 & -35.585 & -1.961
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> where e.g. $A_{21} = -62000/1400 = -44.286$ and $A_{24} = -(-280)/1400 = 0.2$.
>
> MATLAB script (key excerpt):
>
> ```matlab
> m_s=1400; I_y=2200; a=1.25; b=1.45;
> k_f=30000; k_r=32000; c_f=1400; c_r=1400;
> A = [0 1 0 0;
>      -(k_f+k_r)/m_s  -(c_f+c_r)/m_s  -(a*k_f-b*k_r)/m_s  -(a*c_f-b*c_r)/m_s;
>      0 0 0 1;
>      -(a*k_f-b*k_r)/I_y  -(a*c_f-b*c_r)/I_y  -(a^2*k_f+b^2*k_r)/I_y  -(a^2*c_f+b^2*c_r)/I_y];
> B = [0 0 0 0;
>      k_f/m_s  k_r/m_s  c_f/m_s  c_r/m_s;
>      0 0 0 0;
>      a*k_f/I_y  -b*k_r/I_y  a*c_f/I_y  -b*c_r/I_y];
> sys = ss(A,B,eye(4),zeros(4,4));
> t = 0:0.01:5;
> u = [0.04*(t>=0); zeros(1,length(t)); zeros(1,length(t)); zeros(1,length(t))];
> [Y,~,~] = lsim(sys,u',t);
> plot(t,Y(:,1),t,Y(:,3));
> ```
> Steady-state (analytical): $Kq = K_{in}[0.04, 0]^T \Rightarrow z_s \approx 0.0258$ m, $\theta \approx 0.00147$ rad. **[7 marks — full credit]**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** With reference to the half-car model, explain the meaning of the coupling terms $a k_f - b k_r$ and $a c_f - b c_r$. What happens if these terms vanish? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A half-car has $a = b = 1.4$ m, $k_f = k_r = 28\,000$ N/m, $c_f = c_r = 1200$ N·s/m, $m_s = 1300$ kg. Compute the **natural frequencies of heave and pitch** using $I_y = m_s L^2/12$, and comment on ride comfort. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (a):**
> The off-diagonal entries of $K$ and $C$ are the **coupling coefficients**. They represent the *asymmetry* between the front and rear suspension reactions about the CG. When $a k_f \neq b k_r$ (and analogously for damping), an input at one end of the car excites both a vertical translation *and* a rotation simultaneously — the two DOFs are dynamically coupled.
>
> If $a k_f = b k_r$ (and $a c_f = b c_r$), the off-diagonals vanish and the **M, C, K matrices all become diagonal simultaneously**. The heave and pitch motions **decouple**: a road input under one wheel produces purely that DOF's response and does not excite the other. This is the *anti-pitch* design condition used in luxury suspensions, although in practice it is rarely achieved because of component tolerances. **[7 marks — full credit]**

> **Model Answer (b):**
> With $a = b$, $L = 2.8$ m, so:
>
> $$I_y = \frac{m_s L^2}{12} = \frac{1300 \times 7.84}{12} = 849.33 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
>
> Heave stiffness (sum) = $k_f + k_r = 56\,000$ N/m. Heave frequency:
>
> $$f_n^{\text{heave}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{56000}{1300}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{43.077} = \frac{6.564}{2\pi} \approx 1.045 \text{ Hz}$$
>
> Pitch stiffness (sum) = $a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r = 1.96(28000) + 1.96(28000) = 109\,760$ N·m/rad. Pitch frequency:
>
> $$f_n^{\text{pitch}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{109760}{849.33}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{129.24} = \frac{11.368}{2\pi} \approx 1.810 \text{ Hz}$$
>
> **Comment:** Both modes lie in the **1–2 Hz range**, which is the human vertical-vibration sensitivity band. For ride comfort the suspension damping ratio should be tuned near $\zeta \approx 0.25$–$0.4$ to avoid resonance amplification; otherwise occupants will experience motion sickness and reduced reading comfort. The heave mode at ~1 Hz is close to the body's natural vertical sway frequency (~1.2 Hz for standing humans), so **softening $k_f + k_r$** is recommended. **[7 marks — full credit]**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Half-Car Problems:**
> 1. **Sign error in $K$ off-diagonals.** If you flip the sign of $\theta$ convention, you must flip the rear-road-input signs simultaneously — partial sign change = **−3 marks**.
> 2. **Units of $I_y$.** Board examiners **strictly check** $\text{kg·m}^2$. Reporting $I_y$ in $\text{kg/m}^2$ or omitting the unit loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the road-velocity terms ($\dot{z}_{rf}, \dot{z}_{rr}$).** The forcing vector has **four** components when the input is a general profile. Writing only two = **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Using `eig(A)` instead of `sqrt(eig(A))`.** The eigenvalues of $A$ are in $\text{rad/s}$, not $\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$. Compute $\omega_n = \sqrt{\text{abs}(\lambda_i)}$ — common slip loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing output indices in MATLAB `lsim`.** $Y(:,1)$ is $z_s$, $Y(:,3)$ is $\theta$, **not** $\dot{z}_s$. Always annotate your plot axes.
> 6. **Neglecting the static equilibrium check.** For every numerical, verify $z_s^{\text{static}} = -m_s g / (k_f + k_r)$ to confirm sign convention — silent errors propagate.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Half-car = 2-DOF** = heave $z_s$ + pitch $\theta$, sprung-mass only.
- Equations of motion in compact form: $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + Kq = K_{in}z_r + C_{in}\dot{z}_r$.
- $M = \text{diag}(m_s, I_y)$ is **always diagonal and trivially invertible**.
- The **off-diagonal coupling** in $K$ and $C$ vanishes only if $a k_f = b k_r$ and $a c_f = b c_r$.
- For pitch inertia of a uniform bar: $I_y = m_s (a+b)^2 / 12$.
- State vector for MATLAB: $x = [z_s,\ \dot{z}_s,\ \theta,\ \dot{\theta}]^T$ — size 4.
- A and B matrices are **4×4**; C and D choose your outputs (heave, pitch, accelerations).
- For **steady-state step response**, drop all derivatives and solve $Kq = K_{in}z_{r,\text{step}}$.
- **Natural frequencies**: $\omega_{n,\text{heave}} = \sqrt{(k_f+k_r)/m_s}$, $\omega_{n,\text{pitch}} = \sqrt{(a^2 k_f + b^2 k_r)/I_y}$.
- **Ride-comfort target** (ISO 2631): weighted RMS acceleration $\leq$ **0.315 m/s²**.
- Typical realistic values: $m_s \approx 1200$–$1800$ kg, $I_y \approx 2000$–$3500$ kg·m², $k \approx 25$–$40$ kN/m, $c \approx 1$–$2$ kN·s/m, $a,b \approx 1.0$–$1.6$ m.
- **MATLAB essentials**: `ss`, `lsim`, `bode`, `step`, `pzmap`, `eig` — all of these are fair game in ESE.
- **Anti-pitch condition** $a k_f = b k_r$ → decoupled system → luxury-car design philosophy.
- **Static loads** (verification): $W_f = m_s g\, b/(a+b)$, $W_r = m_s g\, a/(a+b)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MATLAB Simulation for Quarter Car Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MATLAB Simulation for Quarter Car Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MATLAB SIMULATION FOR QUARTER CAR MODEL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *Quarter Car Model (QCM)* is a 2-DOF (two degrees of freedom) lumped-parameter dynamic representation of a vehicle's vertical suspension system, in which **one quarter of the vehicle body (sprung mass, $m_s$)** and **one wheel-axle assembly (unsprung mass, $m_u$)** are modeled, interconnected by a **suspension spring ($k_s$)** and **damper ($b_s$)**, with the tire represented as a spring ($k_t$) reacting to the road profile input ($x_r$).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Auto-Rickshaw on a Pothole"

Imagine you are sitting on an auto-rickshaw (the *sprung mass* = your body + the rickshaw roof), and the wheel below you (the *unsprung mass* = wheel + axle + tire) rolls over a pothole. The pothole is the **road input**, $x_r(t)$. The shock absorber between you and the wheel is the **damper $b_s$**, and the coil spring around it is the **spring $k_s$**. The tire itself is a rubber donut that acts like yet another spring, $k_t$, absorbing the sharp edges of the road.

When the wheel hits the pothole, you don't feel the bump instantly — first the tire compresses ($k_t$), then the suspension spring and damper absorb the rest. The MATLAB simulation lets us *time-travel* through this event, plotting how the sprung mass bounces, how the suspension travels, and how the tire load fluctuates, all in fractions of a second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a *Quarter* Car?** A full car is 4 wheels and 7 DOF (3 for body: bounce, pitch, roll; 4 for wheels). For *vertical* ride and suspension studies, symmetry lets us cut the car in half *vertically* (front-rear) and then in half *laterally* (left-right), giving us exactly one wheel and a quarter body — hence the name.

### Physical Quantities and Standard Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $m_s$ | Sprung mass (body) | **kg** |
| $m_u$ | Unsprung mass (wheel) | **kg** |
| $k_s$ | Suspension stiffness | **N/m** |
| $b_s$ | Suspension damping coefficient | **N·s/m** |
| $k_t$ | Tire stiffness | **N/m** |
| $x_r(t)$ | Road displacement input | **m** |
| $x_u(t)$ | Unsprung mass displacement | **m** |
| $x_s(t)$ | Sprung mass displacement | **m** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**) | **m/s²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The quarter car model is the *gateway* to ride-comfort and road-holding studies. Every suspension parameter you tune (softer spring, stiffer damper) changes the ride in a way that can be observed in MATLAB before fabricating a single component.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free-Body Diagram and Sign Convention

The quarter car model consists of two masses connected vertically:

- **Sprung mass ($m_s$):** Car body acting at the center of gravity above the suspension.
- **Unsprung mass ($m_u$):** Wheel, tire, brake, and lower control arm.
- **$k_s, b_s$:** Suspension spring and damper in *parallel* between $m_s$ and $m_u$.
- **$k_t$:** Tire spring (no damper — tires are modeled as pure stiffness) between $m_u$ and road $x_r$.

**Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** *Downward positive*. Displacements, velocities, and accelerations are positive when moving downward in the direction of gravity.

## 2.2 Equations of Motion (Newton's Second Law)

Apply Newton's second law to each mass:

**For the Sprung Mass ($m_s$):**
The forces acting on $m_s$ are: gravity (offset by static spring compression, ignored in dynamic study), spring force $-k_s(x_s - x_u)$, and damper force $-b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$.

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + k_s(x_s - x_u) = 0$$

**For the Unsprung Mass ($m_u$):**
The forces acting on $m_u$ are: spring force $+k_s(x_s - x_u)$, damper force $+b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$, and tire force $-k_t(x_u - x_r)$.

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u + b_s(\dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_s) + k_s(x_u - x_s) + k_t(x_u - x_r) = 0$$

## 2.3 State-Space Representation (Required for MATLAB)

To simulate in MATLAB using `ode45` or `ss()`, we convert the two 2nd-order ODEs into four 1st-order ODEs.

**State Vector Selection (KTU Standard):**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_s - x_u \quad \text{(susp. deflection)} \\ \dot{x}_s \quad \text{(body velocity)} \\ x_u - x_r \quad \text{(tire deflection)} \\ \dot{x}_u \quad \text{(wheel velocity)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**State Equations in Matrix Form:**
$$\dot{X} = A X + B u, \quad y = C X + D u$$

where $u = \dot{x}_r$ is the road velocity input.

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -\frac{k_s}{m_s} & -\frac{b_s}{m_s} & 0 & \frac{b_s}{m_s} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{k_s}{m_u} & \frac{b_s}{m_u} & -\frac{k_t}{m_u} & -\frac{b_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this state choice?** Selecting *relative* displacements ($x_s - x_u$ and $x_u - x_r$) as states directly gives us the **suspension working space** and **tire deflection** as outputs — these are the two most important engineering outputs of a suspension system.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | KTU Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $m_s \ddot{x}_s + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + k_s(x_s - x_u) = 0$ | Sprung mass EOM | Derive dynamics |
| 2 | $m_u \ddot{x}_u - b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_s(x_s - x_u) + k_t(x_u - x_r) = 0$ | Unsprung mass EOM | Derive dynamics |
| 3 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}}$ (approx.) | Natural frequency of body on suspension (Hz: $f_n = \omega_n/2\pi$) | Ride comfort |
| 4 | $\zeta = \dfrac{b_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$ | Damping ratio | Tune for 0.2–0.4 |
| 5 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_t(k_s + k_s) - \dots}{m_s m_u / (m_s+m_u)}}$ | Wheel-hop natural frequency | Tire stiffness check |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | Frequency ratio | Frequency response |
| 7 | $\dfrac{X_s}{X_r}\vert_{steady} = 1$ | Static displacement transmissibility | Low-frequency limit |
| 8 | $F_{tire} = k_t(x_u - x_r)$ | Dynamic tire load | Road holding |
| 9 | $\ddot{x}_s$ (RMS) | RMS body acceleration | Ride comfort index |
| 10 | $\text{Travel} = x_s - x_u$ | Suspension working stroke | Space constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A well-designed passenger car has $\omega_{n,\text{body}} \approx 1\text{–}2\,\text{Hz}$ and $\omega_{n,\text{wheel}} \approx 10\text{–}15\,\text{Hz}$, with damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.2\text{–}0.4$. The two are separated by a *decade* — this is the key to *uncoupling* ride from road-holding.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Ride Comfort Optimization:** ISO 2631 standards on whole-body vibration use the RMS of $\ddot{x}_s$ (sprung mass acceleration) as the metric. MATLAB computes this in seconds.
2. **Suspension Tuning:** Engineers sweep $k_s$ and $b_s$ in MATLAB to find the *Pareto front* between comfort and road-holding.
3. **Active & Semi-Active Suspensions:** Sky-hook, ground-hook, and MR-damper control laws are first prototyped in MATLAB/Simulink before ECU deployment.
4. **Durability Testing:** Tire-road contact force $F_{tire}$ predicts wheel-hop and tire wear patterns.
5. **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Frequency response plots generated in MATLAB correlate with subjective ride ratings in vehicle clinics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From EOM to State-Space Form

### Step 1: Start with the Two 2nd-Order EOMs

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + k_s(x_s - x_u) = 0 \quad (1)$$

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u + b_s(\dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_s) + k_s(x_u - x_s) + k_t(x_u - x_r) = 0 \quad (2)$$

### Step 2: Define the State Variables

Let the four state variables be defined as:

$$x_1 = x_s - x_u \quad \text{(relative displacement between body and wheel)}$$

$$x_2 = \dot{x}_s \quad \text{(absolute velocity of body)}$$

$$x_3 = x_u - x_r \quad \text{(tire compression)}$$

$$x_4 = \dot{x}_u \quad \text{(absolute velocity of wheel)}$$

### Step 3: Derive $\dot{x}_1$

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u = x_2 - x_4$$

**Logic:** The rate of change of relative displacement equals the difference of absolute velocities.

### Step 4: Derive $\dot{x}_2$ from Equation (1)

Rearranging Equation (1) for $\ddot{x}_s$:

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s = -b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_s(x_s - x_u)$$

$$\ddot{x}_s = -\frac{b_s}{m_s}(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - \frac{k_s}{m_s}(x_s - x_u)$$

Substituting state variables:

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k_s}{m_s} x_1 - \frac{b_s}{m_s}(x_2 - x_4)$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k_s}{m_s} x_1 - \frac{b_s}{m_s} x_2 + \frac{b_s}{m_s} x_4$$

### Step 5: Derive $\dot{x}_3$

$$\dot{x}_3 = \dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_r = x_4 - \dot{x}_r$$

**Logic:** The rate of change of tire compression equals wheel velocity minus road velocity. Here $\dot{x}_r$ is the **input** to the system.

### Step 6: Derive $\dot{x}_4$ from Equation (2)

Rearranging Equation (2) for $\ddot{x}_u$:

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u = -b_s(\dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_s) - k_s(x_u - x_s) - k_t(x_u - x_r)$$

$$\ddot{x}_u = -\frac{b_s}{m_u}(\dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_s) - \frac{k_s}{m_u}(x_u - x_s) - \frac{k_t}{m_u}(x_u - x_r)$$

Substituting state variables:

$$\dot{x}_4 = \frac{k_s}{m_u} x_1 + \frac{b_s}{m_u} x_2 - \frac{k_s + k_t}{m_u} x_3 - \frac{b_s}{m_u} x_4 + \frac{k_t}{m_u} \cdot (\text{road terms})$$

### Step 7: Final State-Space Form

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \\ \dot{x}_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -\frac{k_s}{m_s} & -\frac{b_s}{m_s} & 0 & \frac{b_s}{m_s} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{k_s}{m_u} & \frac{b_s}{m_u} & -\frac{k_t}{m_u} & -\frac{b_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \dot{x}_r$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Natural Frequencies (Decoupled Approximation)

For a quick estimate, decouple the system by assuming $m_u \approx 0$ (good for body mode) or $m_s \approx \infty$ (good for wheel mode).

### Body Bounce (Sprung Mass) Frequency:

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

For a typical sedan: $m_s = 250\,\text{kg}$, $k_s = 20{,}000\,\text{N/m}$:

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{20000}{250}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$f_s = \frac{\omega_s}{2\pi} = \frac{8.944}{6.2832} \approx 1.42\,\text{Hz}$$

### Wheel Hop (Unsprung Mass) Frequency:

The wheel moves on the *combined* stiffness $k_s + k_t$ (since both springs act in series with the body essentially fixed):

$$\omega_u = \sqrt{\frac{k_s + k_t}{m_u}}$$

For $m_u = 40\,\text{kg}$, $k_t = 200{,}000\,\text{N/m}$:

$$\omega_u = \sqrt{\frac{20000 + 200000}{40}} = \sqrt{\frac{220000}{40}} = \sqrt{5500} \approx 74.16\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$f_u = \frac{74.16}{6.2832} \approx 11.81\,\text{Hz}$$

### Damping Ratio of Body Mode:

$$\zeta = \frac{b_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$$

For $b_s = 1000\,\text{N·s/m}$:

$$\zeta = \frac{1000}{2\sqrt{20000 \times 250}} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 2236.07} = \frac{1000}{4472.14} \approx 0.224$$

This is within the **0.2–0.4 comfort optimum** for passenger cars. ✓

## 3.3 Complete MATLAB Implementation (Step-by-Step Annotated)

Below is the complete MATLAB script for simulating the quarter car model with a **bump road input**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a *MATLAB simulation script* — it is the subject matter of the topic and is included as per the KTU 2024 Vehicle Dynamics Module 4 syllabus.

### MATLAB Script: `quarter_car_sim.m`

```matlab
%% ========================================================================
%  QUARTER CAR MODEL - MATLAB SIMULATION
%  Course: PBMUT604 - VEHICLE DYNAMICS | KTU 2024 Scheme
%  Module 4: MATLAB Simulation for Quarter Car Model
%  ========================================================================
clear; clc; close all;

%% STEP 1: Define Vehicle Parameters (Sprung/Un sprung Mass, Stiffness, Damping)
m_s  = 250;          % Sprung mass (kg) - body + passengers (1/4 of car)
m_u  = 40;           % Unsprung mass (kg) - wheel, tire, brake
k_s  = 20000;        % Suspension spring stiffness (N/m)
b_s  = 1000;         % Suspension damping coefficient (N*s/m)
k_t  = 200000;       % Tire stiffness (N/m)

%% STEP 2: Construct State-Space Matrices A and B
%  X = [x_s - x_u;  x_dot_s;  x_u - x_r;  x_dot_u]
A = [ 0,             1,            0,             -1;
     -k_s/m_s,      -b_s/m_s,      0,              b_s/m_s;
      0,             0,            0,              1;
      k_s/m_u,       b_s/m_u,     -k_t/m_u,       -b_s/m_u];

B = [ 0;  0;  -1;  0];   % Input: road velocity x_dot_r

%% STEP 3: Define Output Matrix C (outputs: susp. travel, body acc., tire defl.)
C = [ 1,  0,  0,  0;     % y1 = suspension deflection (x_s - x_u)
      0,  0,  1,  0;     % y2 = tire deflection (x_u - x_r)
     -k_s/m_s, -b_s/m_s, 0, b_s/m_s];  % y3 = body acceleration x_ddot_s

D = [ 0;  0;  0];

%% STEP 4: Create LTI System (Linear Time Invariant)
sys = ss(A, B, C, D);

%% STEP 5: Define Road Input - ISO Bump (Half-Sine, 0.1 m height, 0.5 s duration)
t_final = 3.0;                  % simulation time (s)
dt      = 0.001;                % time step (s)
t       = 0:dt:t_final;         % time vector
v       = 20/3.6;               % vehicle speed (m/s) = 20 km/h

% Road profile: single bump of height h = 0.1 m over length L = 0.5 m
h_bump = 0.1;  L_bump = 0.5;
T_bump = L_bump / v;           % time duration of bump encounter
x_r    = zeros(size(t));
for i = 1:length(t)
    if t(i) <= T_bump
        x_r(i) = h_bump * (1 - cos(2*pi*t(i)/T_bump)) / 2;  % raised-cosine
    else
        x_r(i) = 0;
    end
end

% Road velocity = derivative of road displacement
xrdot = [0, diff(x_r)/dt];

%% STEP 6: Simulate Time Response using lsim
[Y, T, X] = lsim(sys, xrdot, t);

%% STEP 7: Plot Results (4-Panel Figure)
figure('Name','Quarter Car Response','NumberTitle','off');

subplot(4,1,1);
plot(t, x_r*1000, 'k', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
ylabel('Road Input (mm)'); title('Road Profile (Bump)'); grid on;

subplot(4,1,2);
plot(T, Y(:,1)*1000, 'b', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
ylabel('Susp. Travel (mm)');
title('Suspension Deflection (x_s - x_u)'); grid on;

subplot(4,1,3);
plot(T, Y(:,2)*1000, 'r', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
ylabel('Tire Defl. (mm)');
title('Tire Compression (x_u - x_r)'); grid on;

subplot(4,1,4);
plot(T, Y(:,3)/9.81, 'm', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('Accel (g)');
title('Sprung Mass Acceleration (\ddotx_s)'); grid on;

%% STEP 8: Frequency Response (Bode Plot) - Optional Analysis
figure('Name','Quarter Car Bode','NumberTitle','off');
bode(sys); grid on;

%% STEP 9: Performance Metrics
rms_accel = rms(Y(:,3));         % RMS body acceleration (m/s^2)
rms_travel = rms(Y(:,1));        % RMS suspension travel (m)
fprintf('\n--- PERFORMANCE METRICS ---\n');
fprintf('RMS Body Acceleration: %.3f m/s^2 (%.3f g)\n', rms_accel, rms_accel/9.81);
fprintf('RMS Suspension Travel: %.3f mm\n', rms_travel*1000);
fprintf('Peak Suspension Travel: %.3f mm\n', max(abs(Y(:,1)))*1000);
fprintf('Peak Tire Deflection:  %.3f mm\n', max(abs(Y(:,2)))*1000);
```

### Step-by-Step Explanation of the Script

| Step | What it does | Why we do it |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Defines $m_s, m_u, k_s, b_s, k_t$ | Real vehicle data; tunable for sensitivity study |
| 2 | Builds $A$ (4×4) and $B$ (4×1) matrices | Encodes the equations of motion in state-space form |
| 3 | Builds $C$ matrix to extract outputs | We want susp. travel, tire defl., body accel. as outputs |
| 4 | `ss(A,B,C,D)` creates an LTI object | Enables use of `lsim`, `bode`, `step` etc. |
| 5 | Defines a half-sine road bump | Standard ISO input for suspension testing |
| 6 | `lsim` numerically integrates the state equations | Computes time response over 0–3 s |
| 7 | Four-panel plot: road, travel, tire, accel | Visualizes all key responses in one figure |
| 8 | Bode plot of the LTI system | Shows frequency-domain behavior |
| 9 | Computes RMS metrics | Ride comfort is quantified by RMS acceleration |

> [!TIP]
> **Try this in MATLAB:** Change $b_s$ from 1000 to 2000, 3000, 5000 N·s/m and re-run. Watch the body acceleration overshoot *decrease* but the high-frequency response become *sharper*. This is the classic **comfort vs. road-holding trade-off**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Variation

| Parameter Change | Body Acceleration (Comfort) | Suspension Travel (Space) | Tire Load (Road-Holding) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Increase $k_s$ (stiffer spring) | ↑ Worsens | ↓ Reduces | Slight ↑ |
| Increase $b_s$ (more damping) | ↓ Improves at resonance | ↓ Reduces peak | ↑ Improves |
| Increase $m_s$ (heavier car) | ↑ Slightly worsens at low $f$ | ↑ Increases | ↓ Slightly reduces |
| Increase $k_t$ (stiffer tire) | ↑ Worsens high-$f$ | ↓ Reduces | ↑ Increases |
| Decrease $\zeta$ (less damping) | ↑ Peak at $\omega_n$ | ↑ Larger overshoot | ↓ Worse |

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Pareto trade-off matrix** every suspension engineer memorizes. MATLAB lets you explore this interactively.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a quarter car model. Why is it preferred over a half-car or full-car model for initial suspension studies?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **quarter car model** is a 2-DOF lumped-parameter representation of a vehicle's vertical dynamics, consisting of a *sprung mass* ($m_s$), *unsprung mass* ($m_u$), a suspension spring-damper ($k_s, b_s$), and a tire spring ($k_t$) excited by road input $x_r(t)$.
>
> **Why preferred (2 marks for justification):**
> 1. **Simplicity:** Only 2 DOF and 4 state variables; analytical solutions are tractable.
> 2. **Vertical dynamics focus:** Captures bounce and wheel-hop accurately without pitch/roll complications.
> 3. **Symmetry exploitation:** Symmetric loading on a flat road justifies quartering the car.
> 4. **Fast prototyping:** MATLAB simulation runs in seconds, enabling parameter sweeps for $k_s$ and $b_s$ before moving to half/full-car models.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four state variables commonly used in MATLAB simulation of the quarter car model and state the physical significance of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | # | State Variable | Physical Significance |
> |:---:|:---|:---|
> | 1 | $x_1 = x_s - x_u$ | Suspension working stroke (deflection) — must stay within physical limits |
> | 2 | $x_2 = \dot{x}_s$ | Sprung mass (body) vertical velocity — relates to ride comfort |
> | 3 | $x_3 = x_u - x_r$ | Tire compression — affects road-holding and tire wear |
> | 4 | $x_4 = \dot{x}_u$ | Unsprung mass (wheel) vertical velocity — relates to wheel-hop |

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the two equations of motion for a 2-DOF quarter car model with sprung mass $m_s$, unsprung mass $m_u$, suspension stiffness $k_s$, suspension damping $b_s$, and tire stiffness $k_t$, excited by road input $x_r(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert these equations to state-space form using states $x_1 = x_s - x_u$, $x_2 = \dot{x}_s$, $x_3 = x_u - x_r$, $x_4 = \dot{x}_u$. Write the system matrix $A$ and input matrix $B$ explicitly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Equations of Motion**

**Free-Body of Sprung Mass ($m_s$):**

The forces acting on $m_s$ are: spring force $F_s = k_s(x_s - x_u)$ acting upward, damper force $F_d = b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$ acting upward, and the inertial force $m_s \ddot{x}_s$ acting downward (D'Alembert). Newton's 2nd law (downward positive):

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s = -k_s(x_s - x_u) - b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{m_s \ddot{x}_s + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + k_s(x_s - x_u) = 0} \quad (1) \quad \text{— 3 marks}$$

**Free-Body of Unsprung Mass ($m_u$):**

Forces on $m_u$: spring $k_s(x_s - x_u)$ pulling upward (toward body), damper $b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$ pulling upward, tire force $k_t(x_u - x_r)$ pushing upward (since road is below).

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u = k_s(x_s - x_u) + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_t(x_u - x_r)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{m_u \ddot{x}_u - b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_s(x_s - x_u) + k_t(x_u - x_r) = 0} \quad (2) \quad \text{— 4 marks}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: State-Space Form**

**State Vector Definition:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_s - x_u \\ \dot{x}_s \\ x_u - x_r \\ \dot{x}_u \end{bmatrix}$$

**Deriving $\dot{x}_1$ and $\dot{x}_3$ (kinematic relations):**

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u = x_2 - x_4 \quad \text{— 1 mark}$$

$$\dot{x}_3 = \dot{x}_u - \dot{x}_r = x_4 - \dot{x}_r \quad \text{— 1 mark}$$

**Deriving $\dot{x}_2$ from Equation (1):**

$$\dot{x}_2 = \ddot{x}_s = -\frac{k_s}{m_s}(x_s - x_u) - \frac{b_s}{m_s}(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k_s}{m_s} x_1 - \frac{b_s}{m_s} x_2 + \frac{b_s}{m_s} x_4 \quad \text{— 1.5 marks}$$

**Deriving $\dot{x}_4$ from Equation (2):**

$$\dot{x}_4 = \ddot{x}_u = \frac{k_s}{m_u}(x_s - x_u) + \frac{b_s}{m_u}(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - \frac{k_t}{m_u}(x_u - x_r)$$

$$\dot{x}_4 = \frac{k_s}{m_u} x_1 + \frac{b_s}{m_u} x_2 - \frac{k_t}{m_u} x_3 - \frac{b_s}{m_u} x_4 \quad \text{— 1.5 marks}$$

**Final State-Space Matrices:**

$$\boxed{\dot{X} = A X + B u, \quad u = \dot{x}_r}$$

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -\frac{k_s}{m_s} & -\frac{b_s}{m_s} & 0 & \frac{b_s}{m_s} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{k_s}{m_u} & \frac{b_s}{m_u} & -\frac{k_t}{m_u} & -\frac{b_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{— 1.5 marks}$$

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{— 0.5 marks}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** For a quarter car with $m_s = 300$ kg, $m_u = 45$ kg, $k_s = 18{,}000$ N/m, $b_s = 1200$ N·s/m, and $k_t = 220{,}000$ N/m, calculate the natural frequency and damping ratio of the body bounce mode. Comment on whether the suspension is well-tuned. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a MATLAB script outline (in pseudocode or actual code) to simulate the response of this quarter car to a 0.08 m road bump traversed at 15 m/s. Plot the sprung mass acceleration, suspension travel, and tire deflection versus time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio**

**Body bounce natural frequency:**

$$\omega_s = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{18000}{300}} = \sqrt{60} \approx 7.746\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$f_s = \frac{\omega_s}{2\pi} = \frac{7.746}{6.2832} \approx 1.233\,\text{Hz} \quad \text{— 3 marks}$$

**Damping ratio of body mode:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b_s}{2\sqrt{k_s \cdot m_s}} = \frac{1200}{2\sqrt{18000 \times 300}} = \frac{1200}{2 \times 2323.79} = \frac{1200}{4647.58} \approx 0.258 \quad \text{— 3 marks}$$

**Comment on tuning (1 mark):**

Since $f_s = 1.23$ Hz lies within the **1–2 Hz comfort band** and $\zeta = 0.258$ lies within the **0.2–0.4 optimal damping range**, the suspension is **well-tuned for ride comfort** in a passenger car. ✓

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: MATLAB Script Outline**

```matlab
% QUARTER CAR SIMULATION - Road Bump Response
% (b) Step 1: Define vehicle parameters
m_s = 300;   m_u = 45;
k_s = 18000; b_s = 1200;  k_t = 220000;

% Step 2: Build state-space matrices A, B, C, D
A = [ 0, 1, 0, -1;
     -k_s/m_s, -b_s/m_s, 0, b_s/m_s;
      0, 0, 0, 1;
      k_s/m_u, b_s/m_u, -k_t/m_u, -b_s/m_u];
B = [0; 0; -1; 0];
C = [1, 0, 0, 0;        % suspension travel
     0, 0, 1, 0;        % tire deflection
     -k_s/m_s, -b_s/m_s, 0, b_s/m_s];  % body accel
D = zeros(3,1);

sys = ss(A, B, C, D);   % Create LTI system  -- (2 marks)

% Step 3: Define road bump (raised cosine)
v_vehicle = 15;          % m/s
h_bump = 0.08;           % m
L_bump = 0.5;            % m (bump length)
T_bump = L_bump/v_vehicle;  % bump encounter time
t = 0:0.001:3;
x_r = zeros(size(t));
for i=1:length(t)
    if t(i) <= T_bump
        x_r(i) = h_bump*(1 - cos(2*pi*t(i)/T_bump))/2;
    end
end
xrdot = [0, diff(x_r)/0.001];   % road velocity  -- (2 marks)

% Step 4: Simulate using lsim
[Y, T] = lsim(sys, xrdot, t);

% Step 5: Plot the three responses  -- (3 marks)
figure;
subplot(3,1,1); plot(T, Y(:,1)*1000); ylabel('Susp. Travel (mm)');
title('Suspension Deflection'); grid on;
subplot(3,1,2); plot(T, Y(:,2)*1000); ylabel('Tire Defl. (mm)');
title('Tire Compression'); grid on;
subplot(3,1,3); plot(T, Y(:,3)/9.81); ylabel('Accel (g)');
xlabel('Time (s)'); title('Body Acceleration'); grid on;

% Step 6: Compute performance metrics
fprintf('Peak body accel: %.2f g\n', max(abs(Y(:,3)))/9.81);
fprintf('Peak susp. travel: %.2f mm\n', max(abs(Y(:,1)))*1000);
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write the tire force as $+k_t(x_r - x_u)$ instead of $-k_t(x_u - x_r)$. *Always use downward positive and be consistent.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Tire Damper (–1 mark):** Tires are modeled as **pure springs** ($k_t$ only), no damper. Examiners specifically check this.
>
> 3. **Confusing States:** $x_1$ must be $(x_s - x_u)$, not $(x_u - x_s)$. A sign flip in the state definition cascades into wrong A matrix → entire problem marked wrong.
>
> 4. **Static vs Dynamic Analysis Mix-up:** The quarter car equations ignore gravity and static spring compression. If the question asks for "dynamic response," do not add $m_s g$ or $m_u g$ terms to the EOMs.
>
> 5. **MATLAB code in theory exam:** If the question asks to "write a MATLAB script," you must include actual MATLAB syntax (not just pseudocode). Examiners check for `ss()`, `lsim()`, semicolons, and correct indexing.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in road input gives a bump of wrong time duration — entire simulation timeline is shifted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quarter Car MATLAB Simulation**
>
> - [ ] **Model:** 2-DOF, sprung mass $m_s$ on top, unsprung mass $m_u$ on bottom, connected by $k_s \parallel b_s$, tire modeled as $k_t$ only.
> - [ ] **EOMs (downward positive):**
>   - $m_s \ddot{x}_s + b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + k_s(x_s - x_u) = 0$
>   - $m_u \ddot{x}_u - b_s(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - k_s(x_s - x_u) + k_t(x_u - x_r) = 0$
> - [ ] **States:** $X = [x_s - x_u,\ \dot{x}_s,\ x_u - x_r,\ \dot{x}_u]^T$ (relative quantities preferred).
> - [ ] **Inputs:** $u = \dot{x}_r$ (road velocity, not displacement) for state-space.
> - [ ] **MATLAB pipeline:** parameters → $A,B,C,D$ matrices → `ss()` → road profile → `lsim()` → plots.
> - [ ] **Body frequency:** $f_s \approx 1\text{–}2$ Hz; **Wheel frequency:** $f_u \approx 10\text{–}15$ Hz.
> - [ ] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = 0.2\text{–}0.4$ for optimum ride comfort.
> - [ ] **Tire has no damper** — pure spring $k_t$ only.
> - [ ] **Standard road inputs:** Half-sine bump, swept sine, random PSD (ISO 8608).
> - [ ] **Outputs to plot:** Body acceleration $\ddot{x}_s$, suspension travel $x_s - x_u$, tire deflection $x_u - x_r$.
> - [ ] **Performance metric:** RMS of $\ddot{x}_s$ (ride comfort per ISO 2631).
> - [ ] **MATLAB functions to remember:** `ss()`, `lsim()`, `bode()`, `step()`, `ode45()`, `rms()`, `tf()`.
> - [ ] **Trade-off:** Soft spring + low damping = good comfort, bad road-holding. Stiff + high damping = good road-holding, harsh ride. MATLAB sweeps find the Pareto optimum.
> - [ ] **Pareto matrix:** Increasing $k_s$ reduces travel but worsens comfort. Increasing $b_s$ reduces peak but worsens high-frequency response.
> - [ ] **Always convert km/h to m/s** when computing bump duration: $T_{bump} = L_{bump}/v$.
